Nice — I’ll do two things:

Show concrete Java code that demonstrates conflict resolution when two interfaces provide the same default method (and also show how a superclass method wins).

Explain real JDK examples (why JDK designers provided an Interface + an Abstract skeletal class), using List / AbstractList and Comparable / Comparator — and list the practical differences and rationale.

1) Conflict resolution — runnable Java examples
Case A — two interfaces with the same default method: you must resolve the conflict
interface A {
    default void hello() { System.out.println("A.hello"); }
}
interface B {
    default void hello() { System.out.println("B.hello"); }
}

// Class implements both A and B -> must override hello()
class C implements A, B {
    @Override
    public void hello() {
        // you pick which default impl to call, or provide your own
        A.super.hello();          // call A's default
        B.super.hello();          // call B's default
        System.out.println("C.hello");
    }
}

// Demo
public class Demo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        C c = new C();
        c.hello();
    }
}


Output

A.hello
B.hello
C.hello


Key: A.super.hello() and B.super.hello() let you explicitly invoke a particular interface default method.

Case B — class inherits a concrete method from a superclass and also implements an interface default

Rule: class (superclass) method wins over interface default methods — you don’t need to override.

class SuperClass {
    public void hello() { System.out.println("SuperClass.hello"); }
}

interface I {
    default void hello() { System.out.println("I.hello"); }
}

class D extends SuperClass implements I {
    // no override needed — SuperClass.hello() wins
}

public class Demo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        D d = new D();
        d.hello();   // calls SuperClass.hello
    }
}


Output

SuperClass.hello

Case C — subinterface provides its own default (resolves conflict)
interface X { default void run() { System.out.println("X.run"); } }
interface Y { default void run() { System.out.println("Y.run"); } }
interface XY extends X, Y {
    @Override
    default void run() { 
        // XY decides the rule; implementer just inherits XY.run
        System.out.println("XY.run (resolved)");
    }
}
class Impl implements XY {}
// Impl.run() prints XY.run

2) Real JDK examples & why designers used Interface vs Abstract (with differences)

I'll cover two classical JDK pairs: List vs AbstractList and Comparable vs Comparator. After that I list the general "why" differences using these examples.

A. List (interface) vs AbstractList (abstract class)

What they are in practice

List (interface): pure contract — describes behaviors (ordered collection, allow duplicates, positional access).

Declares methods like get(int), add(E), remove(int), listIterator(), size().

Multiple, unrelated types can implement List.

AbstractList (abstract class): skeletal implementation of List.

Provides default implementations for many List methods (e.g., addAll, equals, hashCode, indexOf, iterator implementations).

Maintains helper fields/logics such as modCount (for fail-fast iterators) and reduces implementer boilerplate.

Leaves core methods abstract (e.g., get(int), size()), and possibly set/add/remove depending on mutability.

Why JDK designers used both

List defines the contract — any class claiming to be a List must meet the API. It's small, flexible, and allows multiple inheritance of type (implementing several interfaces).

AbstractList eases implementers: you only implement the minimal subset (like get and size), and AbstractList supplies the rest. This reduces bugs and duplication across multiple List implementations.

Example benefit: ArrayList extends AbstractList (actually ArrayList extends AbstractList -> it reuses many methods) — implementers don't have to re-implement equals, hashCode, iterators, etc.

Practical differences (List vs AbstractList)

List: no fields permitted, no constructor; a pure type contract.

AbstractList: can have fields (e.g., modCount), constructors, protected helper methods — used to provide common functionality.

Analogy: List = what a list is, AbstractList = helpful base class to build a list.

B. Comparable vs Comparator

What they are in practice

Comparable<T> (interface): defines the natural ordering for objects — int compareTo(T other) implemented on the class itself.

Example: String implements Comparable<String> — natural lexicographic order.

Used by Collections.sort(list) when no explicit comparator provided.

Comparator<T> (interface; functional): separate strategy object that defines an ordering between two objects — int compare(T a, T b).

Many static and default helper methods (Comparator.comparing, thenComparing, reversed) make composition easy.

Can be passed to sort methods to provide multiple different orderings.

Why JDK designers used both

Comparable ties ordering to the class itself — appropriate when a single natural order exists (e.g., numbers, strings).

Comparator is the strategy pattern — allows many orderings without changing the class; suitable for external ordering or multiple different orderings.

Comparator became a very rich functional interface (Java 8+) with default/static helpers — encouraging composition, lambda use, and reuse.

Practical differences

Comparable method: int compareTo(T o) — instance method on the object.

Comparator method: int compare(T o1, T o2) — separate object/lambda.

Use Comparable when a single canonical order exists; use Comparator to implement other orders or when you cannot modify the class.

C. Other JDK pairs showing the same pattern

JDK often provides an Interface + Abstract pair for many families:

Map (interface) + AbstractMap (abstract class)

Set + AbstractSet

Queue + AbstractQueue
This is a consistent design pattern: contract (interface) + skeletal implementation (abstract class) for implementer convenience and consistency (see Effective Java suggestions and JDK design).

3) Summary — every practical difference (quick checklist)

Use this checklist when deciding or understanding JDK design:

Does the API only describe operations (no state)? → Interface.

Example: List, Map, Comparable, Runnable.

Do implementers benefit from shared code or fields to reduce boilerplate? → Abstract class.

Example: AbstractList, AbstractMap.

Need multiple behaviors mixed into one class? → Interface (supports multiple inheritance of type).

Need to store instance fields / provide constructors? → Abstract class (interfaces can't have instance fields or constructors).

Do you want lambdas/functional use? → Interface (functional), e.g., Comparator, Predicate.

Do you need to evolve an API without breaking existing implementers?

Interfaces can add default methods to grant backward-compatible behavior (Java 8), but adding new abstract methods still breaks implementers. Abstract classes let you add protected helpers that subclasses can use — still risky.

If JDK designers gave both: interface = contract, abstract = convenience / skeletal implementation.

4) Short code cheat sheet for conflict resolution & calling defaults
// call a specific interface default:
InterfaceA.super.methodName(args);

// class method wins over interface defaults (no override necessary)
class My extends SuperClass implements I1 { /* inherits SuperClass.method */ }

// if multiple interfaces have same default, your class must override:
class MustResolve implements I1, I2 {
  public void method() {
    I1.super.method(); // or custom impl
  }
}




---------------------


🌟 SOLID Principles — Interview Style Table
Principle	Meaning (short)	Why it matters	Bad Example (violates principle)	Good Example (follows principle)
S — Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)	A class should have only one reason to change (one responsibility).	Keeps code modular, easier to maintain, test, and extend.	A UserService that handles login, database CRUD, email sending, and logging.	Split into UserRepository (DB), AuthService (login), EmailService (email).
O — Open/Closed Principle (OCP)	Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification.	Add new behavior without changing existing code → safer upgrades.	DiscountCalculator with if(type == "Diwali") … else if(type == "NewYear") …	Define DiscountStrategy interface → implement DiwaliDiscount, NewYearDiscount.
L — Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)	Subclasses should be substitutable for their base class (no broken behavior).	Prevents surprises when using polymorphism.	Square extends Rectangle, but setting width/height breaks area formula.	Shape interface with getArea(), implement separately in Rectangle, Square.
I — Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)	Don’t force clients to depend on methods they don’t use.	Avoids “fat” interfaces.	Worker interface with work(), eat(), sleep(). A Robot doesn’t eat/sleep.	Split into Workable, Feedable, Sleepable → Robot only implements Workable.
D — Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)	High-level modules shouldn’t depend on low-level modules — both should depend on abstractions.	Improves flexibility & testability (use interfaces, not concrete).	PaymentService directly creates PaypalPayment object.	PaymentService depends on PaymentProcessor (interface). Pass PaypalPayment or StripePayment at runtime.
🌟 Example mini-code snippets (ready for interviews)
Single Responsibility Principle
// ❌ Bad: too many responsibilities
class Report {
   void generate() { ... }
   void print() { ... }
   void email() { ... }
}

// ✅ Good: separate responsibilities
class ReportGenerator { void generate() {...} }
class ReportPrinter { void print() {...} }
class ReportEmailer { void email() {...} }

Open/Closed Principle
// ❌ Bad: modification needed for new discounts
class Discount {
   double getDiscount(String type) {
      if (type.equals("Diwali")) return 0.1;
      else if (type.equals("NewYear")) return 0.2;
      return 0;
   }
}

// ✅ Good: extendable without modification
interface DiscountStrategy { double getDiscount(); }
class DiwaliDiscount implements DiscountStrategy { public double getDiscount() { return 0.1; } }
class NewYearDiscount implements DiscountStrategy { public double getDiscount() { return 0.2; } }

Liskov Substitution Principle
// ❌ Bad: Square breaks Rectangle behavior
class Rectangle {
   void setWidth(int w) {...}
   void setHeight(int h) {...}
}
class Square extends Rectangle { ... } // breaks substitution

// ✅ Good: use common interface
interface Shape { int getArea(); }
class Rectangle implements Shape { ... }
class Square implements Shape { ... }

Interface Segregation Principle
// ❌ Bad: Robot forced to implement eat/sleep
interface Worker { void work(); void eat(); void sleep(); }

// ✅ Good: segregated interfaces
interface Workable { void work(); }
interface Feedable { void eat(); }
interface Sleepable { void sleep(); }

class Robot implements Workable { public void work() {...} }

Dependency Inversion Principle
// ❌ Bad: high-level depends on low-level
class PaymentService {
   PaypalPayment payment = new PaypalPayment();
   void pay() { payment.process(); }
}

// ✅ Good: high-level depends on abstraction
interface PaymentProcessor { void process(); }
class PaypalPayment implements PaymentProcessor { public void process() {...} }
class StripePayment implements PaymentProcessor { public void process() {...} }

class PaymentService {
   private PaymentProcessor processor;
   PaymentService(PaymentProcessor p) { this.processor = p; }
   void pay() { processor.process(); }
}


✅ This table + examples makes SOLID easy to explain in interviews:

Start with short meaning

Then give a 1-liner bad example

Finish with how you’d fix it


Common starting point (use this EXACT snippet for every principle)
class OrderService {
    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        validate(d);            // validation
        saveToDatabase(d);      // persistence
        chargePayment(d);       // payment
        sendEmail(d);           // notification
        generateReport(d);      // reporting
    }
}

1) SRP — Single Responsibility Principle

Without SRP (original)

class OrderService {
    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        validate(d);            // validation
        saveToDatabase(d);      // persistence
        chargePayment(d);       // payment
        sendEmail(d);           // notification
        generateReport(d);      // reporting
    }
}


With SRP (extract responsibilities)

class Validator { void validate(OrderData d) { /* validation logic */ } }
class Repository { void save(OrderData d) { /* persist */ } }
class PaymentService { void chargePayment(OrderData d) { /* payment */ } }
class Notifier { void sendEmail(OrderData d) { /* notify */ } }
class Reporter { void generateReport(OrderData d) { /* reporting */ } }

class OrderService {
    Validator validator;
    Repository repo;
    PaymentService payment;
    Notifier notifier;
    Reporter reporter;

    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        validator.validate(d);
        repo.save(d);
        payment.chargePayment(d);
        notifier.sendEmail(d);
        reporter.generateReport(d);
    }
}


Comment: SRP: each class now has a single responsibility. OrderService only orchestrates.

2) OCP — Open/Closed Principle

Without OCP (original)

class OrderService {
    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        validate(d);
        saveToDatabase(d);
        chargePayment(d);
        sendEmail(d);
        generateReport(d);
    }
}


With OCP (make behaviors extensible via strategy interfaces)

interface DiscountStrategy { double apply(OrderData d, double subtotal); }
interface NotificationStrategy { void notify(Customer c, String message); }
interface ReportStrategy { void record(OrderData d); }

class OrderService {
    List<DiscountStrategy> discounts;         // new strategies can be added
    NotificationStrategy notifier;
    ReportStrategy reporter;

    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        validate(d);
        double subtotal = computeSubtotal(d);
        double totalDiscount = discounts.stream().mapToDouble(s -> s.apply(d, subtotal)).sum();
        double finalAmount = subtotal - totalDiscount;
        saveToDatabase(d);
        chargePaymentWithAmount(d, finalAmount);
        notifier.notify(d.customer, "Order placed");
        reporter.record(d);
    }
}


Comment: OCP: to add new discount or notification behavior, create a new DiscountStrategy or NotificationStrategy — no changes to OrderService.

3) LSP — Liskov Substitution Principle

Without LSP (original)

class OrderService {
    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        validate(d);
        saveToDatabase(d);
        chargePayment(d);       // assumes chargePayment behaves in a certain way
        sendEmail(d);
        generateReport(d);
    }
}


With LSP (define stable contracts for substitutable components)

class PaymentResult { boolean success; String error; }

interface PaymentProcessor {
    PaymentResult charge(OrderData d);
}

class StripeProcessor implements PaymentProcessor {
    public PaymentResult charge(OrderData d) {
        return new PaymentResult(true, null);
    }
}

class FaultyProcessor implements PaymentProcessor {
    public PaymentResult charge(OrderData d) {
        // MUST return PaymentResult, not throw unpredictable exceptions
        return new PaymentResult(false, "card declined");
    }
}

class OrderService {
    PaymentProcessor payment;

    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        validate(d);
        saveToDatabase(d);
        PaymentResult r = payment.charge(d);  // safe: all implementations return PaymentResult
        if (!r.success) handleFailure(r.error);
        sendEmail(d);
        generateReport(d);
    }
}


Comment: LSP: any PaymentProcessor can be substituted because they all honor the same PaymentResult contract — no surprises like unexpected exceptions or changed semantics.

4) ISP — Interface Segregation Principle

Without ISP (original)

interface BigService {
    void validate(OrderData d);
    void saveToDatabase(OrderData d);
    void chargePayment(OrderData d);
    void sendEmail(OrderData d);
    void generateReport(OrderData d);
}
// Many implementers forced to implement irrelevant methods


With ISP (split into focused interfaces)

interface Validator { void validate(OrderData d); }
interface Repository { void save(OrderData d); }
interface PaymentProcessor { PaymentResult charge(OrderData d); }
interface Notifier { void sendEmail(OrderData d); }
interface Reporter { void generateReport(OrderData d); }

// Implementers implement only what they need
class EmailNotifier implements Notifier {
    public void sendEmail(OrderData d) { /* send */ }
}
// RobotMailService doesn't implement Repository, etc.

class OrderService {
    Validator v; Repository repo; PaymentProcessor p; Notifier n; Reporter r;
    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        v.validate(d);
        repo.save(d);
        p.charge(d);
        n.sendEmail(d);
        r.generateReport(d);
    }
}


Comment: ISP: clients/implementers implement only the small interfaces they require instead of a fat BigService.

5) DIP — Dependency Inversion Principle

Without DIP (original)

class OrderService {
    SqlRepository repo = new SqlRepository();     // concrete
    StripePaymentService payment = new StripePaymentService(); // concrete

    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        validate(d);
        repo.save(d);          // direct dependency on concrete classes
        payment.chargePayment(d);
        sendEmail(d);
        generateReport(d);
    }
}


With DIP (depend on abstractions; inject implementations)

interface Repository { void save(OrderData d); }
interface PaymentProcessor { PaymentResult charge(OrderData d); }

class OrderService {
    private final Repository repo;                 // depends on abstraction
    private final PaymentProcessor payment;        // depends on abstraction

    OrderService(Repository repo, PaymentProcessor payment) {
        this.repo = repo;
        this.payment = payment;
    }

    void placeOrder(OrderData d) {
        validate(d);
        repo.save(d);                              // concrete swapped at runtime
        PaymentResult r = payment.charge(d);
        if (!r.success) handleFailure(r.error);
        sendEmail(d);
        generateReport(d);
    }
}

// Wiring (composition root)
Repository repoImpl = new SqlRepository();                // concrete created at startup
PaymentProcessor payImpl = new StripeProcessor();
OrderService svc = new OrderService(repoImpl, payImpl);


Comment: DIP: OrderService depends only on interfaces; concrete implementations are injected, making swapping/mocking easy.

Short recap (1-line each)

SRP: Pull validate, save, charge, notify, report into separate classes — OrderService orchestrates only.

OCP: Replace inlined behavior with strategy interfaces (DiscountStrategy, NotificationStrategy) so you can extend without modifying.

LSP: Define clear contracts (PaymentResult) so all processors can be substituted safely.

ISP: Break a fat interface into many small ones so implementers only implement what they need.

DIP: Depend on Repository/PaymentProcessor interfaces and inject concrete implementations at startup.

If you want, I can now:

Produce a single concise file (Markdown) containing the 5 before/after code blocks as shown (clean, copy-paste ready), or

Generate one runnable Java example that shows the refactor applied end-to-end (with small stubs), or

Draw a simple diagram linking OrderService to the small interfaces.

Tell me which and I’ll produce it in the same clean format.


Design Patter(23)------------------------


📘 Design Patterns — Table with Examples
Category	Pattern	Definition	General Example	OrderService Example
Creational	Singleton	Ensures only one instance of a class exists.	One DB connection pool across the app.	OrderIdGenerator ensures unique IDs globally.
	Factory Method	Creates objects without specifying exact class.	ShapeFactory returns Circle/Square.	PaymentProcessorFactory returns Stripe or PayPal processor.
	Abstract Factory	Factory of factories; creates related objects.	GUI factory for Windows vs Mac widgets.	NotificationFactory creates EmailService + SMSService together.
	Builder	Constructs complex objects step by step.	Building a Car with engine, wheels, seats.	InvoiceBuilder constructs detailed invoices (header, items, totals).
	Prototype	Creates object by cloning an existing one.	Cloning a document template.	Clone an Order object to retry payment without re-entering details.
Structural	Adapter	Converts one interface into another.	Power plug adapter (US → EU).	DHLAdapter adapts DHL API to ShippingProvider interface.
	Bridge	Decouples abstraction from implementation.	Remote control works with different TVs.	OrderService (abstraction) with different PaymentProcessor implementations (bridge).
	Composite	Treats individual and group of objects uniformly.	Folders contain files/subfolders.	OrderItem (single product) vs BundleItem (set of products).
	Decorator	Adds behavior dynamically without modifying code.	Coffee + sugar + milk decorators.	FraudCheckDecorator adds fraud detection to PaymentProcessor.
	Facade	Provides a simplified interface to complex subsystems.	HomeTheaterFacade controls projector, speakers, DVD.	OrderFacade wraps payment, inventory, and notification calls.
	Flyweight	Shares objects to save memory.	Reusing character objects in text rendering.	Reuse Product metadata across multiple OrderItems.
	Proxy	Controls access to another object.	Virtual proxy loads images lazily.	CachedOrderRepository is a proxy around DB repository.
Behavioral	Chain of Responsibility	Passes request through chain of handlers.	Support tickets escalated to manager if unresolved.	DiscountChain → StudentDiscount → SeasonalDiscount → LoyaltyDiscount.
	Command	Encapsulates requests as objects.	Undo/redo in a text editor.	CancelOrderCommand, ShipOrderCommand encapsulate order actions.
	Interpreter	Defines grammar for interpreting expressions.	Math expression evaluator.	OrderFilterInterpreter parses search queries like “amount > 100 AND status=PAID”.
	Iterator	Sequentially access elements without exposing structure.	for-each loops.	OrderIterator loops through paginated orders.
	Mediator	Centralizes communication between objects.	Air traffic control mediates planes.	OrderMediator coordinates between Payment, Inventory, and Shipping.
	Memento	Captures and restores object state.	Undo in a text editor.	Save Order state before applying discount → rollback if invalid.
	Observer	Notifies dependents automatically on state change.	Weather app updates subscribers.	OrderPlacedEvent notifies InventoryService, NotificationService.
	State	Alters behavior when internal state changes.	Vending machine states: idle, dispensing.	Order state: New → Paid → Shipped → Delivered.
	Strategy	Encapsulates interchangeable algorithms.	Sorting with bubble/quick/merge sort.	PricingStrategy: FlatRate, SeasonalDiscount, DynamicPricing.
	Template Method	Defines skeleton, subclasses override steps.	Cooking recipe: boil → add ingredients → serve.	OrderProcessor defines process; subclasses vary payment/shipping.
	Visitor	Adds new operations without modifying objects.	Tax calculator visiting items.	TaxVisitor applies VAT/GST rules to OrderItem.
	
	
	
	Great — I’ll give you **one compact, focused pseudocode example per GoF design pattern (23 total)** using the `OrderService` domain.
Each pattern block includes:

* a **one-line purpose**,
* which **OOP concept(s)** it highlights (encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction),
* a **collection/generics** touch where it naturally fits (e.g., `List<Order>`, `Map<String, PaymentProcessor>`),
* and **Java-like pseudocode** showing how it plugs into `OrderService`.

I’ll keep each example short and practical so you can read/paste/adapt quickly. Tell me to expand any pattern into full code/tests/diagrams after this.

---

# 1 — Singleton

**Purpose:** single shared instance. — *OOP:* encapsulation (single instance)
**Collections/generics:** none (singleton often holds collections).

```java
// OrderIdGenerator (Singleton)
class OrderIdGenerator {
  private static OrderIdGenerator instance;
  private AtomicLong counter = new AtomicLong(0);
  private OrderIdGenerator() {}
  public static synchronized OrderIdGenerator getInstance() {
    if(instance == null) instance = new OrderIdGenerator();
    return instance;
  }
  public String nextId() { return "ORD-" + counter.incrementAndGet(); }
}

// Usage in OrderService
class OrderService {
  void placeOrder(Order order) {
    order.id = OrderIdGenerator.getInstance().nextId();
    // ...
  }
}
```

---

# 2 — Factory Method

**Purpose:** subclass decides which concrete to create. — *OOP:* polymorphism, abstraction
**Collections/generics:** `Map<String, PaymentProcessor>` factory registry.

```java
interface PaymentProcessor { Receipt pay(Order o); }

abstract class PaymentProcessorFactory {
  abstract PaymentProcessor create(String type);
}

class DefaultPaymentProcessorFactory extends PaymentProcessorFactory {
  PaymentProcessor create(String type) {
    if(type.equals("STRIPE")) return new StripeProcessor();
    if(type.equals("PAYPAL")) return new PayPalProcessor();
    throw new IllegalArgumentException();
  }
}

// OrderService uses factory
class OrderService {
  private PaymentProcessorFactory factory;
  void setFactory(PaymentProcessorFactory f) { this.factory = f; }
  void pay(Order o, String type) {
    PaymentProcessor p = factory.create(type);
    p.pay(o);
  }
}
```

---

# 3 — Abstract Factory

**Purpose:** family of related objects. — *OOP:* abstraction, encapsulation
**Collections/generics:** returns collections of related objects.

```java
interface NotificationFactory {
  EmailService createEmailService();
  SMSService createSMSService();
}

class AWSNotificationFactory implements NotificationFactory {
  EmailService createEmailService() { return new SESMailer(); }
  SMSService createSMSService() { return new PinpointSMS(); }
}

// OrderService uses factory to get all notification channels
class OrderService {
  NotificationFactory notifFactory;
  void notifyOrderPlaced(Order o) {
    EmailService e = notifFactory.createEmailService();
    SMSService s = notifFactory.createSMSService();
    e.send(o);
    s.send(o);
  }
}
```

---

# 4 — Builder

**Purpose:** step-by-step complex object construction. — *OOP:* encapsulation, fluent API
**Collections/generics:** builder accepts `List<OrderItem>` generic collection.

```java
class Invoice {
  String id; String customer; List<OrderItem> items; Money total;
}

class InvoiceBuilder {
  private Invoice inv = new Invoice();
  InvoiceBuilder forCustomer(String c) { inv.customer = c; return this; }
  InvoiceBuilder addItems(List<OrderItem> items) { inv.items = items; return this; }
  Invoice build() { inv.total = calcTotal(inv.items); return inv; }
}

// Use in OrderService
class OrderService {
  Invoice createInvoice(Order o) {
    return new InvoiceBuilder()
              .forCustomer(o.customerId)
              .addItems(o.items) // List<OrderItem>
              .build();
  }
}
```

---

# 5 — Prototype

**Purpose:** clone existing object. — *OOP:* polymorphism (clone), encapsulation
**Collections/generics:** clone `Order` to make retry copies.

```java
class Order implements Cloneable {
  String id; List<OrderItem> items;
  Order clone() { Order c = new Order(); c.items = new ArrayList<>(this.items); /*deep copy*/ return c; }
}

// Use in retry path
class OrderService {
  void retryPayment(Order o) {
    Order copy = o.clone();
    // modify copy for retry without touching original
  }
}
```

---

# 6 — Adapter

**Purpose:** convert one interface to another. — *OOP:* composition, abstraction
**Collections/generics:** map of adapters `Map<String, ShippingAdapter>` .

```java
interface ShippingProvider { TrackingInfo ship(Order o); }
class DHLApi { /* vendor API */ TrackingResponse sendParcel(Parcel p){} }

class DHLAdapter implements ShippingProvider {
  DHLApi api = new DHLApi();
  TrackingInfo ship(Order o) {
    Parcel p = toParcel(o);
    TrackingResponse r = api.sendParcel(p);
    return toTrackingInfo(r);
  }
}

// OrderService uses ShippingProvider
class OrderService {
  Map<String, ShippingProvider> providers;
  void ship(Order o, String providerKey) { providers.get(providerKey).ship(o); }
}
```

---

# 7 — Bridge

**Purpose:** decouple abstraction from implementation. — *OOP:* abstraction + implementation separation
**Collections/generics:** abstraction uses generic `PaymentProcessorImpl`.

```java
// Abstraction
abstract class Payment {
  protected PaymentGateway gateway;
  Payment(PaymentGateway g) { this.gateway = g; }
  abstract Receipt pay(Order o);
}
// Implementations of gateway
interface PaymentGateway { Response charge(PaymentRequest r); }

// Concrete abstractions
class StandardPayment extends Payment {
  Receipt pay(Order o) { return gateway.charge(reqFrom(o)); }
}
// Plug different gateways without changing Payment classes.
```

---

# 8 — Composite

**Purpose:** treat individual and group uniformly. — *OOP:* polymorphism, recursion
**Collections/generics:** `List<OrderComponent>` generic.

```java
interface OrderComponent { Money getPrice(); }
class OrderItem implements OrderComponent {
  Money getPrice() { return price; }
}
class Bundle implements OrderComponent {
  List<OrderComponent> children = new ArrayList<>();
  Money getPrice() {
    Money sum = 0;
    for(OrderComponent c : children) sum += c.getPrice();
    return sum;
  }
}
// OrderService calculates total over List<OrderComponent>
```

---

# 9 — Decorator

**Purpose:** add behavior at runtime. — *OOP:* composition, polymorphism
**Collections/generics:** stack/decorator chain with generics `T extends PaymentProcessor`.

```java
interface PaymentProcessor { Receipt pay(Order o); }
class BaseProcessor implements PaymentProcessor { public Receipt pay(Order o){ /*...*/ } }

abstract class PaymentProcessorDecorator implements PaymentProcessor {
  protected PaymentProcessor delegate;
  PaymentProcessorDecorator(PaymentProcessor d){ this.delegate = d; }
  Receipt pay(Order o) { return delegate.pay(o); }
}

class FraudCheckDecorator extends PaymentProcessorDecorator {
  Receipt pay(Order o){
    if(isFraud(o)) throw new FraudException();
    return super.pay(o);
  }
}

// Build chain:
PaymentProcessor proc = new FraudCheckDecorator(new LoggingDecorator(new BaseProcessor()));
```

---

# 10 — Facade

**Purpose:** simple unified interface to complex subsystems. — *OOP:* encapsulation, abstraction
**Collections/generics:** may aggregate collections internally.

```java
class OrderFacade {
  PaymentProcessor payment;
  InventoryService inventory;
  NotificationService notifier;

  public void placeOrder(Order o) {
    inventory.reserve(o.items); // List<OrderItem>
    payment.pay(o);
    notifier.notify(o);
  }
}
// OrderService or API calls OrderFacade.placeOrder(o)
```

---

# 11 — Flyweight

**Purpose:** share common state to save memory. — *OOP:* sharing, encapsulation
**Collections/generics:** `Map<String, ProductMeta>` flyweight pool.

```java
class ProductMeta { String name; String description; } // intrinsic
class ProductMetaFactory {
  Map<String, ProductMeta> pool = new HashMap<>();
  ProductMeta get(String productId) {
    return pool.computeIfAbsent(productId, id -> loadMeta(id));
  }
}
// OrderItem references shared ProductMeta rather than duplicating.
```

---

# 12 — Proxy

**Purpose:** control access, lazy load, caching. — *OOP:* composition, encapsulation
**Collections/generics:** cache map `Map<String, Order>` in proxy.

```java
interface OrderRepository { Order find(String id); void save(Order o); }

class OrderRepositoryProxy implements OrderRepository {
  private OrderRepository real;
  private Map<String, Order> cache = new HashMap<>();
  Order find(id) {
    if(cache.containsKey(id)) return cache.get(id);
    Order o = real.find(id);
    cache.put(id, o);
    return o;
  }
}
```

---

# 13 — Chain of Responsibility

**Purpose:** pass request along chain of handlers. — *OOP:* polymorphism, decoupling
**Collections/generics:** `List<DiscountHandler>` generic chain.

```java
interface DiscountHandler { void handle(Order o); void setNext(DiscountHandler h); }

class StudentDiscount implements DiscountHandler { /*...*/ }
class SeasonalDiscount implements DiscountHandler { /*...*/ }

class DiscountChain {
  List<DiscountHandler> handlers = new ArrayList<>();
  void apply(Order o) { for(DiscountHandler h : handlers) h.handle(o); }
}
```

---

# 14 — Command

**Purpose:** encapsulate actions as objects (undo/redo). — *OOP:* encapsulation, polymorphism
**Collections/generics:** `Stack<Command>` for undo history.

```java
interface Command { void execute(); void undo(); }

class PlaceOrderCommand implements Command {
  OrderService svc; Order o;
  void execute(){ svc.placeOrder(o); }
  void undo(){ svc.cancelOrder(o.id); }
}

Stack<Command> history = new Stack<>();
history.push(new PlaceOrderCommand(svc, o)); history.peek().execute();
```

---

# 15 — Interpreter

**Purpose:** evaluate expressions in a grammar. — *OOP:* abstraction, recursion
**Collections/generics:** parse tokens into `List<Token>`.

```java
// Simple order filter grammar: expr -> field op value
interface Expr { boolean eval(Order o); }
class AndExpr implements Expr { Expr left, right; boolean eval(o) { return left.eval(o) && right.eval(o); } }
// Parser turns "amount > 100 AND status=PAID" into Expr tree, then eval against List<Order>
List<Order> filterOrders(List<Order> orders, Expr expr) {
  return orders.stream().filter(o -> expr.eval(o)).collect(Collectors.toList());
}
```

---

# 16 — Iterator

**Purpose:** sequential traversal without exposing internals. — *OOP:* encapsulation, polymorphism
**Collections/generics:** `Iterator<Order>` over paginated results.

```java
class OrderPagination implements Iterable<Order> {
  PageFetcher fetcher;
  Iterator<Order> iterator() {
    return new Iterator<Order>() {
      Queue<Order> buffer = new LinkedList<>();
      boolean hasNext(){ refillIfEmpty(); return !buffer.isEmpty(); }
      Order next(){ return buffer.poll(); }
      void refillIfEmpty(){ if(buffer.isEmpty()) buffer.addAll(fetcher.fetchNextPage()); }
    };
  }
}
```

---

# 17 — Mediator

**Purpose:** central mediator coordinates colleagues. — *OOP:* decoupling, encapsulation
**Collections/generics:** mediator keeps `List<Colleague>` and routes messages.

```java
interface Mediator { void notify(Component c, Event e); }
class OrderMediator implements Mediator {
  PaymentService payment; InventoryService inventory; NotificationService notifier;
  void notify(Component c, Event e) {
    if(e.type == ORDER_PLACED) { inventory.reserve(e.order); payment.charge(e.order); notifier.notify(e.order); }
  }
}
```

---

# 18 — Memento

**Purpose:** capture and restore object state without exposing internals. — *OOP:* encapsulation
**Collections/generics:** `Stack<Memento<Order>>` for undo history.

```java
class OrderMemento { private final Order snapshot; OrderMemento(Order o){ snapshot = deepCopy(o); } Order get(){ return deepCopy(snapshot); } }
class OrderCaretaker { Stack<OrderMemento> history = new Stack<>(); void save(Order o){ history.push(new OrderMemento(o)); } Order restore(){ return history.pop().get(); } }
```

---

# 19 — Observer

**Purpose:** publish/subscribe for state changes. — *OOP:* decoupling, polymorphism
**Collections/generics:** `List<Observer<OrderEvent>>` generic subscribers.

```java
interface Observer<T> { void onEvent(T e); }
class EventBus {
  Map<Class<?>, List<Observer<?>>> subs = new HashMap<>();
  <T> void subscribe(Class<T> type, Observer<T> obs) { subs.computeIfAbsent(type, k->new ArrayList<>()).add(obs); }
  void publish(Object e) { List<Observer<?>> list = subs.get(e.getClass()); for(Observer o : list) o.onEvent(e); }
}
// OrderService publishes new OrderPlacedEvent to notify Inventory, Billing, Email
```

---

# 20 — State

**Purpose:** allow object behavior to change with internal state. — *OOP:* polymorphism, encapsulation
**Collections/generics:** `Map<OrderStateType, OrderState>` mapping.

```java
interface OrderState { void pay(OrderContext ctx); void ship(OrderContext ctx); }
class NewState implements OrderState { void pay(ctx){ ctx.setState(new PaidState()); } }
class OrderContext { OrderState state; void pay(){ state.pay(this); } void setState(OrderState s){ state = s; } }
```

---

# 21 — Strategy

**Purpose:** interchangeable algorithms selected at runtime. — *OOP:* polymorphism, encapsulation
**Collections/generics:** `Map<String, PricingStrategy>` registry.

```java
interface PricingStrategy { Money calculate(Order o); }
class FlatRateStrategy implements PricingStrategy { Money calculate(Order o) { ... } }
class OrderService {
  Map<String, PricingStrategy> strategies;
  Money computePrice(Order o, String strategyKey) { return strategies.get(strategyKey).calculate(o); }
}
```

---

# 22 — Template Method

**Purpose:** skeleton algorithm in base class; subclasses override steps. — *OOP:* inheritance, polymorphism
**Collections/generics:** base template operates over `List<OrderItem>`.

```java
abstract class OrderProcessor {
  final void process(Order o) {
    validate(o);
    calculate(o);
    persist(o);
    postProcess(o);
  }
  abstract void validate(Order o);
  abstract void calculate(Order o);
  void persist(Order o) { repo.save(o); } // shared
  void postProcess(Order o) {}
}
class ExpressOrderProcessor extends OrderProcessor {
  void validate(Order o) { /* extra checks */ }
  void calculate(Order o) { /* special rates */ }
}
```

---

# 23 — Visitor

**Purpose:** add operations to object structure without modifying classes. — *OOP:* double-dispatch, separation of concerns
**Collections/generics:** iterate `List<OrderItem>` and accept visitor.

```java
interface ItemVisitor { void visit(BookItem b); void visit(SoftwareItem s); }
interface OrderItem { void accept(ItemVisitor v); }
class TaxVisitor implements ItemVisitor {
  void visit(BookItem b) { // tax rules for book }
  void visit(SoftwareItem s) { // tax for software }
}
for(OrderItem it : order.items) it.accept(new TaxVisitor());
```

## 1) Singleton — `OrderIdGenerator`

```java
// Purpose: single shared instance for order IDs
// OOP: encapsulation; SOLID: SRP (single job), use interface wrapper for DIP in real code

class OrderIdGenerator {
    private static OrderIdGenerator instance;
    private long counter = 0;
    private OrderIdGenerator(){}
    public static synchronized OrderIdGenerator getInstance(){
        if(instance == null) instance = new OrderIdGenerator();
        return instance;
    }
    public synchronized String nextId(){ return "ORD-" + (++counter); }
}

class SingletonMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        OrderIdGenerator g1 = OrderIdGenerator.getInstance();
        OrderIdGenerator g2 = OrderIdGenerator.getInstance();
        System.out.println(g1.nextId()); // ORD-1
        System.out.println(g2.nextId()); // ORD-2
        System.out.println(g1 == g2);    // true
    }
}
```

## 2) Factory Method — `PaymentFactory`

```java
// Purpose: let subclasses/factories decide concrete product
// OOP: polymorphism; SOLID: OCP (extend processors), DIP (depend on interface)

interface PaymentProcessor { String pay(String orderId); }

class StripeProcessor implements PaymentProcessor {
    public String pay(String orderId){ return "StripeReceipt:" + orderId; }
}
class PayPalProcessor implements PaymentProcessor {
    public String pay(String orderId){ return "PayPalReceipt:" + orderId; }
}

abstract class PaymentFactory {
    abstract PaymentProcessor create(String type);
}
class DefaultPaymentFactory extends PaymentFactory {
    PaymentProcessor create(String type){
        if("STRIPE".equals(type)) return new StripeProcessor();
        if("PAYPAL".equals(type)) return new PayPalProcessor();
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown");
    }
}

class FactoryMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        PaymentFactory f = new DefaultPaymentFactory();
        PaymentProcessor p = f.create("STRIPE");
        System.out.println(p.pay("ORD-10"));
    }
}
```

## 3) Abstract Factory — `NotificationFactory`

```java
// Purpose: family of related objects (email+sms)
// OOP: abstraction; SOLID: OCP, DIP

interface EmailService { void send(String to, String body); }
interface SmsService { void send(String to, String body); }

class SESMail implements EmailService { public void send(String to,String body){ System.out.println("SES mail to " + to); } }
class PinpointSms implements SmsService { public void send(String to,String body){ System.out.println("Pinpoint SMS to " + to); } }

interface NotificationFactory {
    EmailService email();
    SmsService sms();
}
class AWSNotificationFactory implements NotificationFactory {
    public EmailService email(){ return new SESMail(); }
    public SmsService sms(){ return new PinpointSms(); }
}

class AbstractFactoryMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        NotificationFactory nf = new AWSNotificationFactory();
        nf.email().send("user@example.com","Order placed");
        nf.sms().send("+911234","Order placed");
    }
}
```

## 4) Builder — `InvoiceBuilder`

```java
// Purpose: step-by-step construction for complex object
// OOP: encapsulation; SOLID: SRP (builder separate), OCP

class OrderItem { String name; int qty; double price; OrderItem(String n,int q,double p){name=n;qty=q;price=p;} }
class Invoice { String id; String customer; java.util.List<OrderItem> items; double total; }

class InvoiceBuilder {
    private Invoice inv = new Invoice();
    public InvoiceBuilder id(String id){ inv.id = id; return this; }
    public InvoiceBuilder customer(String c){ inv.customer = c; return this; }
    public InvoiceBuilder items(java.util.List<OrderItem> items){ inv.items = new java.util.ArrayList<>(items); return this; }
    public Invoice build(){
        inv.total = inv.items.stream().mapToDouble(i -> i.price * i.qty).sum();
        return inv;
    }
}

class BuilderMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        java.util.List<OrderItem> items = java.util.List.of(new OrderItem("Book",2,150.0));
        Invoice inv = new InvoiceBuilder().id("INV-1").customer("Alice").items(items).build();
        System.out.println(inv.id + " total=" + inv.total);
    }
}
```

## 5) Prototype — `Order.clone()`

```java
// Purpose: clone objects to avoid expensive creation
// OOP: polymorphism/clone; SOLID: SRP (cloning separate behavior)

class OrderItem implements Cloneable {
    String sku; int qty;
    OrderItem(String s,int q){ sku=s; qty=q; }
    public OrderItem clone(){ return new OrderItem(sku, qty); }
}
class Order implements Cloneable {
    String id; java.util.List<OrderItem> items = new java.util.ArrayList<>();
    Order(String id){ this.id = id; }
    void add(OrderItem i){ items.add(i); }
    public Order clone(){
        Order o = new Order(this.id + "-clone");
        for(OrderItem it : items) o.add(it.clone());
        return o;
    }
}

class PrototypeMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        Order o = new Order("ORD-1"); o.add(new OrderItem("BOOK",1));
        Order o2 = o.clone();
        System.out.println(o.id);   // ORD-1
        System.out.println(o2.id);  // ORD-1-clone
    }
}
```

## 6) Adapter — `DHLAdapter`

```java
// Purpose: adapt vendor API to our interface
// OOP: composition; SOLID: DIP (we depend on our interface), OCP (add adapters)

interface ShippingProvider { String ship(String orderId); }
class DHLApi { String sendParcel(String payload){ return "DHL-TN-100"; } }

class DHLAdapter implements ShippingProvider {
    DHLApi api = new DHLApi();
    public String ship(String orderId){
        String payload = "parcel for " + orderId; // adapt to DHL
        return api.sendParcel(payload);
    }
}

class AdapterMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        ShippingProvider prov = new DHLAdapter();
        System.out.println("Tracking: " + prov.ship("ORD-20"));
    }
}
```

## 7) Bridge — `Payment` + `PaymentGateway`

```java
// Purpose: separate abstraction from implementation so both vary
// OOP: abstraction+implementation separation; SOLID: DIP, OCP

interface PaymentGateway { String charge(double amount); }
class StripeGateway implements PaymentGateway { public String charge(double a){ return "stripe-rx"; } }
class PayPalGateway implements PaymentGateway { public String charge(double a){ return "paypal-rx"; } }

abstract class Payment {
    protected PaymentGateway gateway;
    Payment(PaymentGateway g){ this.gateway = g; }
    abstract String pay(double amount);
}
class CardPayment extends Payment {
    CardPayment(PaymentGateway g){ super(g); }
    String pay(double amount){ return gateway.charge(amount); }
}

class BridgeMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        Payment p1 = new CardPayment(new StripeGateway());
        System.out.println(p1.pay(100.0));
        Payment p2 = new CardPayment(new PayPalGateway());
        System.out.println(p2.pay(200.0));
    }
}
```

## 8) Composite — `OrderComponent`

```java
// Purpose: treat items and bundles uniformly
// OOP: polymorphism; SOLID: LSP (components interchangeable), SRP

interface OrderComponent { double price(); }
class OrderItem implements OrderComponent {
    String name; double price;
    OrderItem(String n,double p){ name=n; price=p; }
    public double price(){ return price; }
}
class Bundle implements OrderComponent {
    java.util.List<OrderComponent> parts = new java.util.ArrayList<>();
    public void add(OrderComponent c){ parts.add(c); }
    public double price(){ return parts.stream().mapToDouble(OrderComponent::price).sum(); }
}

class CompositeMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        OrderItem a = new OrderItem("Pen", 10.0);
        OrderItem b = new OrderItem("Notebook", 50.0);
        Bundle bundle = new Bundle(); bundle.add(a); bundle.add(b);
        System.out.println("Bundle price: " + bundle.price());
    }
}
```

## 9) Decorator — `FraudCheckDecorator`

```java
// Purpose: add behavior dynamically
// OOP: composition; SOLID: OCP (extend behavior), DIP

interface PaymentProcessor { String pay(String orderId); }
class BaseProcessor implements PaymentProcessor {
    public String pay(String orderId){ return "ok:" + orderId; }
}
abstract class ProcessorDecorator implements PaymentProcessor {
    protected PaymentProcessor delegate;
    ProcessorDecorator(PaymentProcessor d){ this.delegate = d; }
    public String pay(String orderId){ return delegate.pay(orderId); }
}
class FraudCheckDecorator extends ProcessorDecorator {
    FraudCheckDecorator(PaymentProcessor d){ super(d); }
    public String pay(String orderId){
        // simple fraud check
        if(orderId.contains("FRAUD")) throw new RuntimeException("Fraud");
        return super.pay(orderId);
    }
}

class DecoratorMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        PaymentProcessor p = new FraudCheckDecorator(new BaseProcessor());
        System.out.println(p.pay("ORD-30"));
    }
}
```

## 10) Facade — `OrderFacade`

```java
// Purpose: simplified unified interface to multiple subsystems
// OOP: encapsulation; SOLID: SRP (facade single API)

class InventoryService { void reserve(String orderId){ System.out.println("reserve:"+orderId); } }
class PaymentService { void charge(String orderId){ System.out.println("charge:"+orderId); } }
class NotificationService { void notify(String orderId){ System.out.println("notify:"+orderId); } }

class OrderFacade {
    InventoryService inv = new InventoryService();
    PaymentService pay = new PaymentService();
    NotificationService note = new NotificationService();
    void placeOrder(String orderId){
        inv.reserve(orderId);
        pay.charge(orderId);
        note.notify(orderId);
    }
}

class FacadeMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        OrderFacade facade = new OrderFacade();
        facade.placeOrder("ORD-40");
    }
}
```

## 11) Flyweight — `ProductMetaFactory`

```java
// Purpose: share intrinsic state to save memory
// OOP: sharing; SOLID: SRP (factory handles pooling)

class ProductMeta { String id; String name; ProductMeta(String id,String n){ this.id=id; this.name=n; } }

class ProductMetaFactory {
    private java.util.Map<String, ProductMeta> pool = new java.util.HashMap<>();
    public ProductMeta get(String id){
        return pool.computeIfAbsent(id, k -> new ProductMeta(k, "Name-"+k));
    }
}

class FlyweightMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        ProductMetaFactory f = new ProductMetaFactory();
        ProductMeta m1 = f.get("P1");
        ProductMeta m2 = f.get("P1");
        System.out.println(m1 == m2); // true -> shared
    }
}
```

## 12) Proxy — `OrderRepositoryProxy`

```java
// Purpose: control access/caching to real repository
// OOP: composition; SOLID: DIP (depend on interface), SRP

interface OrderRepository { String find(String id); void save(String id, String data); }
class RealOrderRepository implements OrderRepository {
    public String find(String id){ return "DB:"+id; }
    public void save(String id, String data){ System.out.println("saved " + id); }
}
class OrderRepositoryProxy implements OrderRepository {
    private OrderRepository real;
    private java.util.Map<String,String> cache = new java.util.HashMap<>();
    OrderRepositoryProxy(OrderRepository real){ this.real = real; }
    public String find(String id){
        if(cache.containsKey(id)) return cache.get(id);
        String r = real.find(id);
        cache.put(id, r);
        return r;
    }
    public void save(String id, String data){ real.save(id, data); cache.put(id, data); }
}

class ProxyMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        OrderRepository repo = new OrderRepositoryProxy(new RealOrderRepository());
        System.out.println(repo.find("ORD-50"));
        System.out.println(repo.find("ORD-50")); // cached
    }
}
```

---

## 13) Chain of Responsibility — `DiscountChain`

```java
// Purpose: pass request through handlers (discounts pipeline)
// OOP: polymorphism; SOLID: OCP (add handlers), SRP

class OrderContext { String id; double total; OrderContext(String id,double t){this.id=id;this.total=t;} }

interface DiscountHandler { void handle(OrderContext ctx); }

class StudentDiscount implements DiscountHandler {
    public void handle(OrderContext ctx){ if(ctx.id.startsWith("STU")) ctx.total *= 0.9; }
}
class SeasonalDiscount implements DiscountHandler {
    public void handle(OrderContext ctx){ ctx.total *= 0.95; }
}

class ChainMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        java.util.List<DiscountHandler> handlers = java.util.List.of(new StudentDiscount(), new SeasonalDiscount());
        OrderContext ctx = new OrderContext("STU-100", 1000);
        for(DiscountHandler h : handlers) h.handle(ctx);
        System.out.println("Final total " + ctx.total); // 1000 * 0.9 * 0.95
    }
}
```

---

## 14) Command — `PlaceOrderCommand`

```java
// Purpose: encapsulate actions as objects (undo/redo)
// OOP: encapsulation; SOLID: SRP (commands isolate action)

class OrderServiceSimple {
    void place(String id){ System.out.println("placed " + id); }
    void cancel(String id){ System.out.println("cancel " + id); }
}
interface Command { void execute(); void undo(); }
class PlaceOrderCommand implements Command {
    OrderServiceSimple svc; String id;
    PlaceOrderCommand(OrderServiceSimple s, String id){ this.svc = s; this.id = id; }
    public void execute(){ svc.place(id); }
    public void undo(){ svc.cancel(id); }
}

class CommandMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        OrderServiceSimple svc = new OrderServiceSimple();
        Command cmd = new PlaceOrderCommand(svc, "ORD-60");
        java.util.Stack<Command> history = new java.util.Stack<>();
        cmd.execute(); history.push(cmd);
        // undo
        history.pop().undo();
    }
}
```

## 15) Interpreter — `OrderFilterInterpreter`

```java
// Purpose: parse and evaluate domain-specific expressions
// OOP: recursion/abstraction; SOLID: SRP (parser separate), OCP (add expr types)

interface Expr { boolean eval(OrderContext o); }
class AmountGreater implements Expr {
    double threshold;
    AmountGreater(double t){ threshold = t; }
    public boolean eval(OrderContext o){ return o.total > threshold; }
}
class AndExpr implements Expr {
    Expr a,b; AndExpr(Expr a,Expr b){this.a=a;this.b=b;}
    public boolean eval(OrderContext o){ return a.eval(o) && b.eval(o); }
}

class InterpreterMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        OrderContext o = new OrderContext("ORD-70", 500);
        Expr expr = new AndExpr(new AmountGreater(100), new AmountGreater(200)); // effectively >200
        System.out.println(expr.eval(o)); // true if >200
    }
}
```

---

## 16) Iterator — `PagedOrderIterable`

```java
// Purpose: hide traversal (pagination) details
// OOP: encapsulation; SOLID: SRP (iterator only traverses)

class PageFetcher { int page=0; java.util.List<String> fetchNext(){
    page++; if(page==1) return java.util.List.of("ORD-1","ORD-2");
    if(page==2) return java.util.List.of("ORD-3"); return java.util.List.of();
}}
class PagedOrderIterable implements Iterable<String> {
    PageFetcher fetcher;
    PagedOrderIterable(PageFetcher f){ this.fetcher=f; }
    public java.util.Iterator<String> iterator(){
        return new java.util.Iterator<>() {
            java.util.Queue<String> buf = new java.util.LinkedList<>();
            public boolean hasNext(){ refill(); return !buf.isEmpty(); }
            public String next(){ refill(); return buf.poll(); }
            private void refill(){ if(buf.isEmpty()){ buf.addAll(fetcher.fetchNext()); } }
        };
    }
}

class IteratorMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        PagedOrderIterable it = new PagedOrderIterable(new PageFetcher());
        for(String id : it) System.out.println("Got " + id);
    }
}
```

## 17) Mediator — `OrderMediator`

```java
// Purpose: centralize communications between services
// OOP: decoupling; SOLID: SRP (mediator handles coordination)

class Inventory { void reserve(String o){ System.out.println("reserve " + o); } }
class Payment { void charge(String o){ System.out.println("charge " + o); } }
class Notifier { void notify(String o){ System.out.println("notify " + o); } }

class OrderMediator {
    Inventory inv = new Inventory(); Payment pay = new Payment(); Notifier note = new Notifier();
    void orderPlaced(String orderId){
        inv.reserve(orderId);
        pay.charge(orderId);
        note.notify(orderId);
    }
}

class MediatorMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        OrderMediator m = new OrderMediator();
        m.orderPlaced("ORD-80");
    }
}
```

## 18) Memento — `OrderMemento`

```java
// Purpose: snapshot and restore object state (undo)
// OOP: encapsulation; SOLID: SRP (caretaker stores mementos)

class OrderState { String id; double total; OrderState(String i,double t){id=i;total=t;} }
class Order {
    OrderState state;
    Order(String id,double t){ state = new OrderState(id,t); }
    OrderState save(){ return new OrderState(state.id, state.total); }
    void restore(OrderState s){ this.state = s; }
}

class MementoMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        Order o = new Order("ORD-90", 1000);
        OrderState snap = o.save();
        // apply discount
        o.state.total = 900;
        System.out.println("After discount " + o.state.total);
        o.restore(snap);
        System.out.println("Restored " + o.state.total);
    }
}
```

---

## 19) Observer — `EventBus`

```java
// Purpose: publish/subscribe pattern for domain events
// OOP: decoupling; SOLID: OCP (add observers), DIP

interface Observer<T>{ void onEvent(T e); }
class OrderPlacedEvent { String id; OrderPlacedEvent(String id){ this.id=id; } }

class EventBus {
    private java.util.Map<Class<?>, java.util.List<Observer<?>>> subs = new java.util.HashMap<>();
    public <T> void subscribe(Class<T> t, Observer<T> o){
        subs.computeIfAbsent(t, k->new java.util.ArrayList<>()).add(o);
    }
    public <T> void publish(T e){
        java.util.List<Observer<?>> list = subs.getOrDefault(e.getClass(), java.util.List.of());
        for(Observer obs : list) ((Observer<T>)obs).onEvent(e);
    }
}

class InventoryObserver implements Observer<OrderPlacedEvent>{
    public void onEvent(OrderPlacedEvent e){ System.out.println("Inventory reserves for " + e.id); }
}
class ObserverMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        EventBus bus = new EventBus();
        bus.subscribe(OrderPlacedEvent.class, new InventoryObserver());
        bus.publish(new OrderPlacedEvent("ORD-100"));
    }
}
```

---

## 20) State — `OrderState` transitions

```java
// Purpose: change behavior when object's state changes
// OOP: polymorphism; SOLID: SRP (states handle their behaviors)

interface OrderState { void pay(OrderContext ctx); void ship(OrderContext ctx); }
class NewState implements OrderState {
    public void pay(OrderContext ctx){ System.out.println("paid"); ctx.setState(new PaidState()); }
    public void ship(OrderContext ctx){ throw new IllegalStateException("Not paid"); }
}
class PaidState implements OrderState {
    public void pay(OrderContext ctx){ System.out.println("already paid"); }
    public void ship(OrderContext ctx){ System.out.println("shipped"); ctx.setState(new ShippedState()); }
}
class ShippedState implements OrderState {
    public void pay(OrderContext ctx){ System.out.println("can't pay after shipped"); }
    public void ship(OrderContext ctx){ System.out.println("already shipped"); }
}
class OrderContext {
    OrderState state;
    OrderContext(){ state = new NewState(); }
    void setState(OrderState s){ this.state = s; }
    void pay(){ state.pay(this); }
    void ship(){ state.ship(this); }
}

class StateMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        OrderContext ctx = new OrderContext();
        ctx.pay(); // paid
        ctx.ship(); // shipped
    }
}
```

---

## 21) Strategy — `PricingStrategy`

```java
// Purpose: interchangeable algorithms for pricing
// OOP: polymorphism; SOLID: OCP, DIP

interface PricingStrategy { double compute(double base); }
class FlatRate implements PricingStrategy { public double compute(double b){ return b; } }
class Seasonal implements PricingStrategy { public double compute(double b){ return b * 0.9; } }

class StrategyMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        java.util.Map<String,PricingStrategy> map = java.util.Map.of("flat", new FlatRate(), "season", new Seasonal());
        double price = map.get("season").compute(100.0);
        System.out.println("Price = " + price);
    }
}
```

## 22) Template Method — `OrderProcessor`

```java
// Purpose: define skeleton of algorithm, subclasses override steps
// OOP: inheritance; SOLID: SRP (processor skeleton), OCP (override steps)

abstract class OrderProcessor {
    public final void process(String orderId){
        validate(orderId);
        calculate(orderId);
        persist(orderId);
        postProcess(orderId);
    }
    protected abstract void validate(String orderId);
    protected abstract void calculate(String orderId);
    protected void persist(String orderId){ System.out.println("persist " + orderId); }
    protected void postProcess(String orderId){}
}
class ExpressProcessor extends OrderProcessor {
    protected void validate(String orderId){ System.out.println("express validate " + orderId); }
    protected void calculate(String orderId){ System.out.println("express calc " + orderId); }
}

class TemplateMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        OrderProcessor p = new ExpressProcessor();
        p.process("ORD-120");
    }
}
```

---

## 23) Visitor — `TaxVisitor`

```java
// Purpose: add operations to object structure without modifying classes
// OOP: double-dispatch; SOLID: OCP (add new visitors), SRP (visitor has operation)

interface ItemVisitor { void visit(BookItem b); void visit(SoftwareItem s); }
interface OrderItem { void accept(ItemVisitor v); }

class BookItem implements OrderItem {
    double price; BookItem(double p){ price = p; }
    public void accept(ItemVisitor v){ v.visit(this); }
}
class SoftwareItem implements OrderItem {
    double price; SoftwareItem(double p){ price = p; }
    public void accept(ItemVisitor v){ v.visit(this); }
}

class TaxVisitor implements ItemVisitor {
    double tax = 0;
    public void visit(BookItem b){ tax += b.price * 0.05; }      // example tax
    public void visit(SoftwareItem s){ tax += s.price * 0.18; }
    public double getTax(){ return tax; }
}

class VisitorMain {
    public static void main(String[] args){
        java.util.List<OrderItem> items = java.util.List.of(new BookItem(100), new SoftwareItem(200));
        TaxVisitor v = new TaxVisitor();
        for(OrderItem it : items) it.accept(v);
        System.out.println("Total tax = " + v.getTax());
    }
}
```

### Final notes

* Each snippet includes a `Main` demo and shows the minimal wiring to run the pattern.
* All examples are intentionally compact — in production code:

  * replace `System.out.println` with proper logging,
  * add interfaces and DI where applicable (to respect DIP),
  * write unit tests, and
  * add error handling and deep copies where required.
* If you want, I can:

  * Convert any single pattern into **full Java** (with package/imports, real types, unit tests),
  * Provide a **single multi-pattern `OrderService` example** that composes several patterns together (Facade + Strategy + Observer + Decorator), or
  * Generate a **downloadable PDF cheat-sheet** containing all snippets.

Which next?


Nice — here’s a single, self-contained **Java-style** example that composes **Facade + Strategy + Observer + Decorator** around an `OrderService` domain.
It’s intentionally compact and runnable-like so you can paste into a Java file and run (you’ll just need to add package/imports if you want).

What you get:

* `OrderFacade` — **Facade** that exposes a simple `placeOrder()` API.
* `PricingStrategy` — **Strategy** to pick pricing algorithms.
* `PaymentProcessor` + `LoggingDecorator` + `FraudDecorator` — **Decorator** chain for payments.
* `EventBus` + subscribers — **Observer** pattern for domain events (Inventory, Notifications, Billing).
* `main()` that wires everything, demonstrates runtime behavior, and prints output.

I also annotate each class with which pattern / OOP / SOLID principle it demonstrates.

---

```java
// Single-file Java-style pseudocode demo: Facade + Strategy + Observer + Decorator
// Paste into a file like MultiPatternOrderDemo.java (add package/imports if needed)

import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicLong;

// --- Domain models ---
class OrderItem {
    String sku;
    int qty;
    double price;
    OrderItem(String sku, int qty, double price) { this.sku = sku; this.qty = qty; this.price = price; }
}

class Order {
    String id;
    String customerEmail;
    List<OrderItem> items = new ArrayList<>();
    double total; // computed
    Order(String id, String customerEmail) { this.id = id; this.customerEmail = customerEmail; }
    void addItem(OrderItem it) { items.add(it); }
}

// --- Observer/Event Bus (Observer pattern) ---
// OOP: decoupling via publish/subscribe
// SOLID: OCP (we can add observers), DIP (depend on Observer interface)
interface Event {}
class OrderPlacedEvent implements Event { final Order order; OrderPlacedEvent(Order o){ this.order=o;} }

interface Observer<T extends Event> { void onEvent(T e); }

class EventBus {
    private final Map<Class<?>, List<Observer<?>>> subs = new HashMap<>();
    public <T extends Event> void subscribe(Class<T> type, Observer<T> obs) {
        subs.computeIfAbsent(type, k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(obs);
    }
    @SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
    public <T extends Event> void publish(T e) {
        List<Observer<?>> list = subs.getOrDefault(e.getClass(), Collections.emptyList());
        for (Observer<?> o : list) {
            ((Observer<T>) o).onEvent(e);
        }
    }
}

// Example subscribers
class InventorySubscriber implements Observer<OrderPlacedEvent> {
    public void onEvent(OrderPlacedEvent e) {
        System.out.println("[Inventory] Reserving items for order " + e.order.id);
    }
}
class NotificationSubscriber implements Observer<OrderPlacedEvent> {
    public void onEvent(OrderPlacedEvent e) {
        System.out.println("[Notification] Sending confirmation to " + e.order.customerEmail);
    }
}
class BillingSubscriber implements Observer<OrderPlacedEvent> {
    public void onEvent(OrderPlacedEvent e) {
        System.out.println("[Billing] Recording transaction for " + e.order.id + " amount " + e.order.total);
    }
}

// --- Pricing Strategy (Strategy pattern) ---
// OOP: polymorphism (interchangeable algorithms)
// SOLID: OCP (add new strategies), DIP
interface PricingStrategy { double computeTotal(List<OrderItem> items); }
class FlatPricing implements PricingStrategy {
    public double computeTotal(List<OrderItem> items) {
        return items.stream().mapToDouble(i -> i.price * i.qty).sum();
    }
}
class SeasonalPricing implements PricingStrategy {
    public double computeTotal(List<OrderItem> items) {
        double base = items.stream().mapToDouble(i -> i.price * i.qty).sum();
        return base * 0.9; // 10% off seasonal
    }
}

// --- Payment Processor + Decorators (Decorator pattern) ---
// OOP: composition & polymorphism
// SOLID: OCP (add behaviors via decorators), DIP (code depends on PaymentProcessor interface)
interface PaymentProcessor {
    String pay(Order order) throws Exception;
}

class StripePaymentProcessor implements PaymentProcessor {
    public String pay(Order order) throws Exception {
        // simulate processing
        System.out.println("[Stripe] Charging amount: " + order.total);
        return "STRIPE-RECEIPT-" + order.id;
    }
}

// Logging decorator
class LoggingPaymentDecorator implements PaymentProcessor {
    private final PaymentProcessor delegate;
    LoggingPaymentDecorator(PaymentProcessor delegate) { this.delegate = delegate; }
    public String pay(Order order) throws Exception {
        System.out.println("[LoggingDecorator] About to charge order " + order.id);
        String r = delegate.pay(order);
        System.out.println("[LoggingDecorator] Payment result: " + r);
        return r;
    }
}

// Fraud check decorator
class FraudCheckPaymentDecorator implements PaymentProcessor {
    private final PaymentProcessor delegate;
    FraudCheckPaymentDecorator(PaymentProcessor delegate) { this.delegate = delegate; }
    private boolean isFraud(Order o) {
        // simple rule: if any sku contains "FRAUD" treat as fraud
        return o.items.stream().anyMatch(it -> it.sku.contains("FRAUD"));
    }
    public String pay(Order order) throws Exception {
        if (isFraud(order)) {
            System.out.println("[FraudCheck] Fraud detected for order " + order.id);
            throw new RuntimeException("Payment blocked due to fraud");
        }
        return delegate.pay(order);
    }
}

// --- Order Facade (Facade pattern) ---
// OOP: encapsulation, provides a simple API
// SOLID: SRP (facade orchestrates), DIP (depends on abstractions like PricingStrategy, PaymentProcessor)
class OrderFacade {
    private final PricingStrategy pricing;
    private final PaymentProcessor payment;
    private final EventBus eventBus;
    private final AtomicLong persistedOrderCounter = new AtomicLong(0); // simulate persistence id

    OrderFacade(PricingStrategy pricing, PaymentProcessor payment, EventBus bus) {
        this.pricing = pricing;
        this.payment = payment;
        this.eventBus = bus;
    }

    /**
     * placeOrder - high-level API that orchestrates:
     *  1) compute price (Strategy)
     *  2) persist/order id creation (simulated)
     *  3) charge via PaymentProcessor (Decorator chain may intercept)
     *  4) publish OrderPlacedEvent (Observer)
     */
    public void placeOrder(Order order) {
        // 1. compute total via strategy
        order.total = pricing.computeTotal(order.items);
        System.out.println("[Facade] Computed total = " + order.total);

        // 2. "persist" (simulate)
        order.id = "ORD-" + persistedOrderCounter.incrementAndGet();
        System.out.println("[Facade] Persisted order with id " + order.id);

        // 3. attempt payment
        try {
            String receipt = payment.pay(order);
            System.out.println("[Facade] Payment succeeded: " + receipt);
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            System.out.println("[Facade] Payment failed for order " + order.id + ": " + ex.getMessage());
            // In real app: rollback / compensation / publish failure event
            return;
        }

        // 4. publish event for other subsystems (Observer)
        eventBus.publish(new OrderPlacedEvent(order));
    }
}

// --- Main wiring ---
public class MultiPatternOrderDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1) Event bus + subscribers (Observer)
        EventBus bus = new EventBus();
        bus.subscribe(OrderPlacedEvent.class, new InventorySubscriber());
        bus.subscribe(OrderPlacedEvent.class, new NotificationSubscriber());
        bus.subscribe(OrderPlacedEvent.class, new BillingSubscriber());

        // 2) Pricing strategy selection (Strategy)
        PricingStrategy pricing = new SeasonalPricing();           // try FlatPricing or SeasonalPricing

        // 3) Payment processor + decorators (Decorator)
        PaymentProcessor baseProcessor = new StripePaymentProcessor();
        PaymentProcessor logged = new LoggingPaymentDecorator(baseProcessor);
        PaymentProcessor withFraud = new FraudCheckPaymentDecorator(logged); // fraud check wraps logging+base

        // 4) Facade wiring (Facade)
        OrderFacade facade = new OrderFacade(pricing, withFraud, bus);

        // Create an order
        Order order = new Order(null, "alice@example.com");
        order.addItem(new OrderItem("BOOK-1", 1, 200.0));
        order.addItem(new OrderItem("PEN-2", 3, 10.0));

        // Place order
        System.out.println("=== Place normal order ===");
        facade.placeOrder(order);

        // Create a fraudulent order to see decorators in action
        Order fraudulent = new Order(null, "badguy@example.com");
        fraudulent.addItem(new OrderItem("FRAUD-99", 1, 1000.0));

        System.out.println("\n=== Place fraudulent order ===");
        facade.placeOrder(fraudulent);
    }
}
```

---

## What this demonstrates (concise)

* **Facade** (`OrderFacade`) gives a single method `placeOrder()` that hides all the orchestration and complexity of pricing, persistence, payment, and event publishing.
* **Strategy** (`PricingStrategy`) lets you change how totals are computed (flat vs seasonal) without changing `OrderFacade`.
* **Decorator** (`LoggingPaymentDecorator`, `FraudCheckPaymentDecorator`) composes cross-cutting payment behaviors (logging, fraud checks) around a concrete `PaymentProcessor` (Stripe).
* **Observer** (`EventBus` + subscribers) decouples subsystems — inventory, notifications, and billing subscribe to order events and react independently.
* **OOP concepts used:** encapsulation (facade and services), polymorphism (strategy & processor interfaces), composition (decorators & adapters), abstraction (interfaces).
* **SOLID links:**

  * **SRP:** Facade has one job (orchestration); decorators separate concerns.
  * **OCP:** Add new pricing/payer/observers without modifying existing code.
  * **DIP:** High-level modules depend on abstractions (interfaces), not concrete implementations.
  * **ISP & LSP:** Interfaces are focused and implementations conform to contracts.

---

## How to run / adapt

* Paste into a Java file (`MultiPatternOrderDemo.java`) and compile/run. You may need to add `package` and imports.
* Swap `PricingStrategy` implementations to see different totals.
* Change decorator order (wrap fraud before logging vs vice versa) to see different behaviors.
* Add more observers for analytics, audit, etc. — they’ll be invoked automatically when events are published.

* Produce a **sequence diagram** or **UML** showing the run-time interactions.
* Expand the example to include **sagas/compensation** for failed payments.

public class FlatPricing implements PricingStrategy {

        return items.stream().mapToDouble(i -> i.price * i.qty).sum();
    }
}

package com.example.orderservice.pricing;

import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderItem;

public class SeasonalPricing implements PricingStrategy {
    @Override
    public double computeTotal(List<OrderItem> items) {
        double base = items.stream().mapToDouble(i -> i.price * i.qty).sum();
        return base * 0.90; // 10% off
    }

package com.example.orderservice.payment;

import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;

public interface PaymentProcessor {

package com.example.orderservice.payment;

import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component("stripeProcessor")
public class StripePaymentProcessor implements PaymentProcessor {
    @Override
    public String pay(Order order) throws Exception {
        // simulate external call
        System.out.println("[Stripe] Charging: " + order.total);
        return "STRIPE-RECEIPT-" + order.id;
    }
}

payment/LoggingPaymentDecorator.java
package com.example.orderservice.payment;

import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;

public class LoggingPaymentDecorator implements PaymentProcessor {
    private final PaymentProcessor delegate;
    public LoggingPaymentDecorator(PaymentProcessor delegate){ this.delegate = delegate; }
    @Override
    public String pay(Order order) throws Exception {
        System.out.println("[Logging] Before pay for " + order.id);
        String r = delegate.pay(order);
        System.out.println("[Logging] After pay: " + r);
        return r;
    }
}

payment/FraudCheckPaymentDecorator.java
package com.example.orderservice.payment;

import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;

public class FraudCheckPaymentDecorator implements PaymentProcessor {
    private final PaymentProcessor delegate;
    public FraudCheckPaymentDecorator(PaymentProcessor delegate){ this.delegate = delegate; }
    private boolean isFraud(Order o){
        return o.items.stream().anyMatch(it -> it.sku.contains("FRAUD"));
    }
    @Override
    public String pay(Order order) throws Exception {
        if(isFraud(order)){
            System.out.println("[Fraud] Detected for " + order.id);
            throw new RuntimeException("Fraud detected");
        }
        return delegate.pay(order);
    }
}
