event/SimpleEventBus.java
package com.example.orderservice.event;

import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap;
import java.util.function.Consumer;

@Component
public class SimpleEventBus {
    private final Map<Class<?>, List<Consumer<?>>> subs = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    public <T> void subscribe(Class<T> clazz, Consumer<T> handler){
        subs.computeIfAbsent(clazz, k -> Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<>())).add(handler);
    }
    @SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
    public <T> void publish(T event){
        List<Consumer<?>> list = subs.getOrDefault(event.getClass(), Collections.emptyList());
        for(Consumer c : list) ((Consumer<T>)c).accept(event);
    }
}

saga/OrderSaga.java (the saga orchestrator with compensation)
package com.example.orderservice.saga;

import com.example.orderservice.event.SimpleEventBus;
import com.example.orderservice.inventory.InventoryService;
import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;
import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderPlacedEvent;
import com.example.orderservice.notification.NotificationService;
import com.example.orderservice.payment.PaymentProcessor;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicLong;

/**
 * Saga orchestrates steps and compensates on failures:
 * Steps:
 *  1) reserve inventory
 *  2) charge payment
 *  3) publish OrderPlacedEvent
 *
 * Compensation on payment failure:
 *  - release inventory
 *  - send failure notification
 */
@Component
public class OrderSaga {
    private final InventoryService inventory;
    private final PaymentProcessor payment;
    private final SimpleEventBus bus;
    private final NotificationService notification;
    private final AtomicLong idGen = new AtomicLong(0);

    public OrderSaga(InventoryService inventory,
                     PaymentProcessor payment,
                     SimpleEventBus bus,
                     NotificationService notification) {
        this.inventory = inventory;
        this.payment = payment;
        this.bus = bus;
        this.notification = notification;
    }

    public void executeSaga(Order order) {
        // simulate persistence id assignment
        order.id = "ORD-" + idGen.incrementAndGet();
        try {
            // Step 1: reserve inventory
            inventory.reserve(order);

            // Step 2: charge payment
            String receipt = payment.pay(order);
            System.out.println("[Saga] payment success: " + receipt);

            // Step 3: publish event so other subscribers (billing, notification) act
            bus.publish(new OrderPlacedEvent(order));
            System.out.println("[Saga] published OrderPlacedEvent for " + order.id);
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            // Compensation: release inventory, notify failure
            System.out.println("[Saga] payment failed: " + ex.getMessage() + " - compensating...");
            try {
                inventory.release(order);
            } catch (Exception releaseEx) {
                System.out.println("[Saga] failed to release inventory: " + releaseEx.getMessage());
            }
            notification.sendFailure(order, ex.getMessage());
        }
    }
}

facade/OrderFacade.java
package com.example.orderservice.facade;

import com.example.orderservice.event.SimpleEventBus;
import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;
import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderPlacedEvent;
import com.example.orderservice.payment.PaymentProcessor;
import com.example.orderservice.pricing.PricingStrategy;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicLong;

/**
 * Facade orchestrates a simpler API over lower-level components.
 * Here it is NOT the saga orchestrator — the facade can call saga or orchestrator.
 */
@Service
public class OrderFacade {
    private final PricingStrategy pricing;
    private final PaymentProcessor payment;
    private final SimpleEventBus bus;
    private final AtomicLong persistedOrderCounter = new AtomicLong(0);

    public OrderFacade(PricingStrategy pricing,
                       PaymentProcessor payment,
                       SimpleEventBus bus) {
        this.pricing = pricing;
        this.payment = payment;
        this.bus = bus;
    }

    public void placeOrder(Order order){
        order.total = pricing.computeTotal(order.items);
        order.id = "ORD-" + persistedOrderCounter.incrementAndGet();
        System.out.println("[Facade] computed total: " + order.total);
        try {
            String receipt = payment.pay(order);
            System.out.println("[Facade] payment successful: " + receipt);
            bus.publish(new OrderPlacedEvent(order));
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            System.out.println("[Facade] payment failed: " + ex.getMessage());
            // In simple facade case we won't compensate here; saga does compensation in more advanced flow
        }
    }
}

4) Tests (src/test/java/...)
OrderFacadeTest.java
package com.example.orderservice;

import com.example.orderservice.event.SimpleEventBus;
import com.example.orderservice.facade.OrderFacade;
import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;
import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderItem;
import com.example.orderservice.payment.PaymentProcessor;
import com.example.orderservice.pricing.FlatPricing;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import org.mockito.Mockito;

import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicBoolean;

import static org.mockito.Mockito.*;

public class OrderFacadeTest {
    @Test
    void placeOrder_success_publishesEvent() throws Exception {
        FlatPricing pricing = new FlatPricing();
        PaymentProcessor payment = mock(PaymentProcessor.class);
        when(payment.pay(any())).thenReturn("OK-RECEIPT");

        SimpleEventBus bus = new SimpleEventBus();
        AtomicBoolean published = new AtomicBoolean(false);
        bus.subscribe(com.example.orderservice.model.OrderPlacedEvent.class, e -> published.set(true));

        OrderFacade facade = new OrderFacade(pricing, payment, bus);

        Order order = new Order(null, "alice@example.com");
        order.addItem(new OrderItem("BK",1,100.0));
        facade.placeOrder(order);

        verify(payment, times(1)).pay(any());
        assert published.get();
    }

    @Test
    void placeOrder_paymentFailure_noEvent() throws Exception {
        FlatPricing pricing = new FlatPricing();
        PaymentProcessor payment = mock(PaymentProcessor.class);
        when(payment.pay(any())).thenThrow(new RuntimeException("card declined"));

        SimpleEventBus bus = new SimpleEventBus();
        AtomicBoolean published = new AtomicBoolean(false);
        bus.subscribe(com.example.orderservice.model.OrderPlacedEvent.class, e -> published.set(true));

        OrderFacade facade = new OrderFacade(pricing, payment, bus);
        Order order = new Order(null, "bob@example.com");
        order.addItem(new OrderItem("BK",1,100.0));
        facade.placeOrder(order);

        verify(payment, times(1)).pay(any());
        assert !published.get();
    }
}

OrderSagaTest.java
package com.example.orderservice;

import com.example.orderservice.event.SimpleEventBus;
import com.example.orderservice.inventory.InventoryService;
import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;
import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderItem;
import com.example.orderservice.notification.NotificationService;
import com.example.orderservice.payment.PaymentProcessor;
import com.example.orderservice.saga.OrderSaga;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import org.mockito.Mockito;

import static org.mockito.Mockito.*;

public class OrderSagaTest {
    @Test
    void saga_success() throws Exception {
        InventoryService inv = mock(InventoryService.class);
        PaymentProcessor payment = mock(PaymentProcessor.class);
        when(payment.pay(any())).thenReturn("R1");
        SimpleEventBus bus = new SimpleEventBus();
        NotificationService notif = mock(NotificationService.class);

        OrderSaga saga = new OrderSaga(inv, payment, bus, notif);
        Order o = new Order(null, "u@example.com");
        o.addItem(new OrderItem("X",1,10));

        saga.executeSaga(o);

        verify(inv, times(1)).reserve(any());
        verify(payment, times(1)).pay(any());
        // event published — subscribers none in test; ensure no compensation
        verify(inv, never()).release(any());
        verify(notif, never()).sendFailure(any(), any());
    }

    @Test
    void saga_paymentFailure_compensate() throws Exception {
        InventoryService inv = mock(InventoryService.class);
        PaymentProcessor payment = mock(PaymentProcessor.class);
        when(payment.pay(any())).thenThrow(new RuntimeException("card fail"));
        SimpleEventBus bus = new SimpleEventBus();
        NotificationService notif = mock(NotificationService.class);

        OrderSaga saga = new OrderSaga(inv, payment, bus, notif);
        Order o = new Order(null, "u@example.com");
        o.addItem(new OrderItem("X",1,10));

        saga.executeSaga(o);

        verify(inv, times(1)).reserve(any());
        verify(payment, times(1)).pay(any());
        verify(inv, times(1)).release(any());
        verify(notif, times(1)).sendFailure(any(), any());
    }
}

5) How to build & run

From project root:

# build
mvn clean package

# run the Spring Boot app
mvn spring-boot:run

# run tests
mvn test


When running the app, you can optionally add a CommandLineRunner bean to create sample orders at startup; the above project has the components wired and you can create a small runner to demo.

6) PlantUML sequence diagram

Save the following as order_saga_sequence.puml and render with PlantUML (VSCode PlantUML extension, online renderer, or plantuml.jar):

@startuml
title Order Saga: Reserve -> Pay -> Publish/Compensate

participant Client
participant OrderSaga
participant InventoryService
participant PaymentProcessor
participant EventBus
participant NotificationService

Client -> OrderSaga : executeSaga(order)
OrderSaga -> InventoryService : reserve(order)
InventoryService --> OrderSaga : reserved

OrderSaga -> PaymentProcessor : pay(order)
alt payment success
    PaymentProcessor --> OrderSaga : receipt
    OrderSaga -> EventBus : publish(OrderPlacedEvent)
    EventBus -> BillingSubscriber : onEvent(...)
    EventBus -> NotificationSubscriber : onEvent(...)
    EventBus -> InventorySubscriber : onEvent(...)
else payment failure
    PaymentProcessor --> OrderSaga : exception
    OrderSaga -> InventoryService : release(order)  // compensate
    InventoryService --> OrderSaga : released
    OrderSaga -> NotificationService : sendFailure(order, reason)
end

OrderSaga --> Client : return (success/failure)
@enduml


Render it and you’ll see the saga orchestration and the compensation path.

7) Notes / Explanation of patterns & SOLID used

Facade: OrderFacade gives a simple placeOrder() for simple flows; OrderSaga is the advanced orchestrator that handles compensation. Facade obeys SRP by being the orchestration entry point.

Strategy: PricingStrategy allows swapping pricing algorithms without changing orchestration (OCP, DIP).

Decorator: Logging & fraud-check are decorators wrapping a PaymentProcessor, enabling cross-cutting behaviors without modifying core processor (OCP).

Observer: SimpleEventBus decouples producers from consumers — add subscribers later without changing producers (OCP, DIP).

Saga/Compensation: OrderSaga demonstrates a simple orchestrator that performs steps and compensates when later steps fail — a practical approach for distributed transactions in microservices.

8) Next steps / Improvements (optional)

Replace SimpleEventBus with Spring ApplicationEventPublisher for native Spring eventing.

Use Spring Profiles + configuration to wire a real payment provider or a mock for tests.

Persist orders + saga state into a DB (e.g., using Spring Data JPA).

Expand saga to distributed services (use Kafka/RabbitMQ to publish steps) and implement retry/backoff + idempotency.

Add more unit & integration tests (Testcontainers for DB, WireMock for external APIs).

Add metrics, logging (SLF4J), structured logs, and exception handling strategies.



Nice — I converted the event plumbing to use Spring’s native event system (ApplicationEventPublisher / @EventListener) and added a real startup runner (CommandLineRunner) that demonstrates the full flow at application startup.

Below you’ll find the minimal set of modified/new files (ready to paste into the Maven project from earlier). I kept package names com.example.orderservice. After these changes you can run the Spring Boot app and watch the event listeners fire automatically.

Summary of changes

Removed SimpleEventBus.

OrderPlacedEvent is now a simple POJO (published through ApplicationEventPublisher).

Subscribers are Spring beans annotated with @EventListener.

OrderSaga (or OrderFacade) uses ApplicationEventPublisher to publish events.

Added a CommandLineRunner (StartupRunner) that creates orders and demonstrates both success and failure paths at app startup.

Files to update / add
1) model/OrderPlacedEvent.java (POJO event)
package com.example.orderservice.model;

public record OrderPlacedEvent(Order order) { }


This is the event payload class — Spring can publish any object as an event.

2) saga/OrderSaga.java — publish with ApplicationEventPublisher
package com.example.orderservice.saga;

import com.example.orderservice.inventory.InventoryService;
import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;
import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderPlacedEvent;
import com.example.orderservice.notification.NotificationService;
import com.example.orderservice.payment.PaymentProcessor;
import org.springframework.context.ApplicationEventPublisher;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicLong;

@Component
public class OrderSaga {
    private final InventoryService inventory;
    private final PaymentProcessor payment;
    private final ApplicationEventPublisher publisher;
    private final NotificationService notification;
    private final AtomicLong idGen = new AtomicLong(0);

    public OrderSaga(InventoryService inventory,
                     PaymentProcessor payment,
                     ApplicationEventPublisher publisher,
                     NotificationService notification) {
        this.inventory = inventory;
        this.payment = payment;
        this.publisher = publisher;
        this.notification = notification;
    }

    public void executeSaga(Order order) {
        // assign id (simulate persistence)
        order.id = "ORD-" + idGen.incrementAndGet();
        try {
            // Step 1: reserve inventory
            inventory.reserve(order);

            // Step 2: charge payment
            String receipt = payment.pay(order);
            System.out.println("[Saga] payment success: " + receipt);

            // Step 3: publish event (Spring event)
            publisher.publishEvent(new OrderPlacedEvent(order));
            System.out.println("[Saga] published OrderPlacedEvent for " + order.id);
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            // Compensation: release inventory, send failure notification
            System.out.println("[Saga] payment failed: " + ex.getMessage() + " - compensating...");
            try {
                inventory.release(order);
            } catch (Exception releaseEx) {
                System.out.println("[Saga] failed to release inventory: " + releaseEx.getMessage());
            }
            notification.sendFailure(order, ex.getMessage());
        }
    }
}

3) event → removed SimpleEventBus (delete that class). Use Spring events instead.
4) Subscribers (listeners) — using @EventListener
inventory/InventoryListener.java
package com.example.orderservice.inventory;

import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderPlacedEvent;
import org.springframework.context.event.EventListener;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
public class InventoryListener {

    @EventListener
    public void onOrderPlaced(OrderPlacedEvent evt) {
        // react to event
        System.out.println("[InventoryListener] Reserving items for " + evt.order().id());
        // In real app: inventory.reserve(order)
    }
}

notification/NotificationListener.java
package com.example.orderservice.notification;

import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderPlacedEvent;
import org.springframework.context.event.EventListener;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
public class NotificationListener {

    @EventListener
    public void onOrderPlaced(OrderPlacedEvent evt) {
        System.out.println("[NotificationListener] Sending confirmation to " + evt.order().customerEmail());
        // real app: notificationService.sendConfirmation(order)
    }
}

billing/BillingListener.java
package com.example.orderservice.billing;

import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderPlacedEvent;
import org.springframework.context.event.EventListener;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
public class BillingListener {

    @EventListener
    public void onOrderPlaced(OrderPlacedEvent evt) {
        System.out.println("[BillingListener] Recording transaction for " + evt.order().id() + " amount " + evt.order().total());
        // real app: create billing record
    }
}

5) payment remain same (decorators + implementation) — but ensure StripePaymentProcessor is a Spring bean (we had @Component("stripeProcessor") earlier). No changes needed.
6) facade/OrderFacade.java — optional: can publish events through ApplicationEventPublisher too (I keep saga to orchestrate, but showing both is fine). If you want facade to publish, inject ApplicationEventPublisher and call publisher.publishEvent(...) as in saga.

(If you used previous OrderFacade, you can leave it as-is — it used SimpleEventBus before; now remove SimpleEventBus wiring or switch it to ApplicationEventPublisher.)

Here’s an example snippet for OrderFacade if you want it to publish:

package com.example.orderservice.facade;

import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;
import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderPlacedEvent;
import com.example.orderservice.payment.PaymentProcessor;
import com.example.orderservice.pricing.PricingStrategy;
import org.springframework.context.ApplicationEventPublisher;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicLong;

@Service
public class OrderFacade {
    private final PricingStrategy pricing;
    private final PaymentProcessor payment;
    private final ApplicationEventPublisher publisher;
    private final AtomicLong persistedOrderCounter = new AtomicLong(0);

    public OrderFacade(PricingStrategy pricing,
                       PaymentProcessor payment,
                       ApplicationEventPublisher publisher) {
        this.pricing = pricing;
        this.payment = payment;
        this.publisher = publisher;
    }

    public void placeOrder(Order order){
        order.total = pricing.computeTotal(order.items);
        order.id = "ORD-" + persistedOrderCounter.incrementAndGet();
        System.out.println("[Facade] computed total: " + order.total);
        try {
            String receipt = payment.pay(order);
            System.out.println("[Facade] payment successful: " + receipt);
            publisher.publishEvent(new OrderPlacedEvent(order));
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            System.out.println("[Facade] payment failed: " + ex.getMessage());
        }
    }
}

7) StartupRunner — CommandLineRunner that demonstrates the flow on startup

Create StartupRunner.java in package com.example.orderservice:

package com.example.orderservice;

import com.example.orderservice.model.Order;
import com.example.orderservice.model.OrderItem;
import com.example.orderservice.saga.OrderSaga;
import com.example.orderservice.facade.OrderFacade;
import org.springframework.boot.CommandLineRunner;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

/**
 * Demonstrates:
 *  - placing a normal order via Saga (with compensation)
 *  - placing a fraudulent order to trigger compensation
 */
@Component
public class StartupRunner implements CommandLineRunner {

    private final OrderSaga saga;
    private final OrderFacade facade;

    public StartupRunner(OrderSaga saga, OrderFacade facade) {
        this.saga = saga;
        this.facade = facade;
    }

    @Override
    public void run(String... args) throws Exception {
        System.out.println("=== STARTUP DEMO: Order Saga + Spring Events ===");

        // Normal order via Saga (with compensation logic inside saga)
        Order normal = new Order(null, "alice@example.com");
        normal.addItem(new OrderItem("BOOK-1", 1, 200.0));
        normal.addItem(new OrderItem("PEN-2", 3, 10.0));
        System.out.println("\n--- Executing Saga for normal order ---");
        saga.executeSaga(normal);

        // Fraudulent order to show compensation (fraud decorator will throw)
        Order fraud = new Order(null, "scammer@example.com");
        fraud.addItem(new OrderItem("FRAUD-XYZ", 1, 1000.0));
        System.out.println("\n--- Executing Saga for fraudulent order (expect compensation) ---");
        saga.executeSaga(fraud);

        // Also show using the simple facade (non-saga) that publishes events
        Order quick = new Order(null, "bob@example.com");
        quick.addItem(new OrderItem("MAG-5", 2, 50.0));
        System.out.println("\n--- Using OrderFacade (simple) to place quick order ---");
        facade.placeOrder(quick);

        System.out.println("\n=== DEMO COMPLETE ===");
    }
}

8) Wiring / Configuration notes

Make sure StripePaymentProcessor is a Spring bean (we had @Component("stripeProcessor") earlier).

For the decorators (LoggingPaymentDecorator, FraudCheckPaymentDecorator) you can either:

Build the decorator chain in a @Configuration class and expose a single PaymentProcessor bean, or

Use @Primary bean definitions or factory bean that wraps the concrete processor.

Example PaymentConfig to wire decorators:

package com.example.orderservice.config;

import com.example.orderservice.payment.*;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;

@Configuration
public class PaymentConfig {

    @Bean
    public PaymentProcessor paymentProcessorStripe(StripePaymentProcessor stripe) {
        // wrap stripe with logging + fraud
        return new FraudCheckPaymentDecorator(new LoggingPaymentDecorator(stripe));
    }
}


This exposes a PaymentProcessor bean (the decorated one) which will be injected into OrderSaga and OrderFacade.

mvn clean package

mvn spring-boot:run — you’ll see console output showing:

Saga reserving inventory

Payment processing / fraud detection

Event listeners reacting (InventoryListener, NotificationListener, BillingListener)

Compensation path on fraud

Example console excerpt (approx)
=== STARTUP DEMO: Order Saga + Spring Events ===

--- Executing Saga for fraudulent order (expect compensation) ---

--- Using OrderFacade (simple) to place quick order ---

[Stripe] Charging: 100.0
[Facade] payment successful: STRIPE-RECEIPT-ORD-3
[InventoryListener] Reserving items for ORD-3
[NotificationListener] Sending confirmation to bob@example.com
[BillingListener] Recording transaction for ORD-3 amount 100.0

=== DEMO COMPLETE ===

10) Tests

Your existing tests (OrderSagaTest, OrderFacadeTest) work the same; replace SimpleEventBus usage with ApplicationEventPublisher where applicable or mock the event publisher (@Mock ApplicationEventPublisher) and verify publishEvent() was called.

Example snippet for mocking publisher in tests:

ApplicationEventPublisher publisher = mock(ApplicationEventPublisher.class);
OrderFacade facade = new OrderFacade(new FlatPricing(), paymentMock, publisher);
...
verify(publisher, times(1)).publishEvent(any(OrderPlacedEvent.class));

11) Next suggestions

Replace console System.out.println with SLF4J (logger.info) for production quality logs.

Add async listeners (@Async) if listeners do slow work; also configure @EnableAsync.

Persist orders and saga state (DB) to survive crashes / restarts; consider a durable messaging system for long-running sagas.

Add @Transactional boundaries if you have DB writes in steps (but note distributed transactions require sagas or two-phase commit).



Java 5 to 17------------------------------------------


Java Version	Release Year	Type	Stable (LTS)?	Key Notes
Java 5 (1.5)	2004	Major	❌ Not LTS	Introduced Generics, Annotations, Enums
Java 6 (1.6)	2006	Major	❌ Not LTS	Compiler & scripting updates
Java 7	2011	Major	❌ Not LTS	try-with-resources, NIO.2
Java 8	2014	LTS	✅ Yes	Lambdas, Streams, Date/Time API
Java 9	2017	Feature	❌ Not LTS	JPMS (modules), jshell
Java 10	2018	Feature	❌ Not LTS	var keyword
Java 11	2018	LTS	✅ Yes	HTTP Client, var in lambda, removed Java EE
Java 12	2019	Feature	❌ Not LTS	Switch expressions (preview)
Java 13	2019	Feature	❌ Not LTS	Text blocks (preview)
Java 14	2020	Feature	❌ Not LTS	Records (preview), instanceof pattern matching
Java 15	2020	Feature	❌ Not LTS	Sealed classes (preview), hidden classes
Java 16	2021	Feature	❌ Not LTS	Records (final), strong encapsulation
Java 17	2021	LTS	✅ Yes	Sealed classes, pattern matching (instanceof)



Perfect 👍 Let’s go step by step. I’ll keep it sample-level, not overwhelming, but with real project / MNC context so you see how each version shaped enterprise Java.

Java 5 (2004 – Not LTS)

🔑 Major Features:

Generics (List<String> instead of List)

Annotations (@Override, @Deprecated)

Enums (enum Status { ACTIVE, INACTIVE })

Varargs (print(String... values))

💼 Impact in projects:

Generics made type-safe collections (no more Object casting mess).

Annotations laid the foundation of Spring & Hibernate (e.g., @Autowired, @Entity).

Enums allowed clean state handling (e.g., OrderStatus).

📌 MNC Tricky Q:

“Why do frameworks like Spring rely heavily on annotations introduced in Java 5?”
👉 Because annotations allowed metadata-driven programming, reducing XML config clutter.

Java 6 (2006 – Not LTS)

🔑 Major Features:

JDBC 4.0 auto-loading of drivers.

javax.script (scripting API for JS/others).

Compiler tool API (javax.tools).

💼 Impact in projects:

JDBC 4.0 made database access easier (no manual Class.forName("Driver")).

Boosted IDE/compiler tooling → improved dev productivity.

📌 MNC Tricky Q:

“Why was JDBC 4.0 a big step for ORM tools like Hibernate?”
👉 Because ORM frameworks depend on JDBC drivers, and auto-loading simplified DB connectivity.

Java 7 (2011 – Not LTS)

🔑 Major Features:

Try-with-resources (try(FileReader fr = new FileReader("a.txt")) {})

Diamond operator (List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();)

NIO.2 (java.nio.file.*)

Multi-catch (catch(IOException | SQLException e))

💼 Impact in projects:

Try-with-resources eliminated resource leaks (DB, sockets, files).

NIO.2 gave better file system handling → used in large-scale batch processing.

📌 MNC Tricky Q:

“How does try-with-resources improve code reliability compared to finally blocks?”
👉 It auto-closes resources in correct order, even if exceptions occur, reducing leaks.

Java 8 (2014 – ✅ LTS)

🔑 Major Features:

Lambdas (list.forEach(x -> System.out.println(x)))

Streams API (list.stream().filter(x -> x>10).collect(...))

Functional Interfaces (Predicate<T>, Function<T,R>)

Default & Static methods in interfaces.

New Date/Time API (LocalDate, ZonedDateTime)

Optional<T>

💼 Impact in projects:

Revolutionized Java → functional style coding.

Spring 5+ internally optimized with lambdas & streams.

Date/Time API fixed the old Date & Calendar nightmares.

📌 MNC Tricky Q:

“Why are streams better than loops?”
👉 Streams allow declarative, parallelized processing; loops are imperative and harder to optimize.

Java 9 (2017 – Not LTS)

🔑 Major Features:

JPMS (Java Platform Module System → module-info.java)

jshell (REPL for Java)

Reactive Streams API

💼 Impact in projects:

Modules gave strong encapsulation but broke many old libraries → adoption was low.

jshell useful for quick prototyping (like Python REPL).

📌 MNC Tricky Q:

“Why did enterprises avoid JPMS in Java 9?”
👉 Migration pain: existing jars weren’t modular → broke classpath-based applications.

Java 10 (2018 – Not LTS)

🔑 Major Features:

var for local variable type inference.

💼 Impact in projects:

Cleaner syntax but only syntactic sugar.

Still restricted (only local variables, not fields/params).

📌 MNC Tricky Q:

“Does var make Java dynamically typed?”
👉 No. It’s still statically typed, type inferred at compile time.

Java 11 (2018 – ✅ LTS)

🔑 Major Features:

var in lambda params.

New HTTP Client (non-blocking, async).

Removed Java EE & CORBA modules.

String API additions (isBlank(), lines(), repeat()).

💼 Impact in projects:

Many enterprises still migrating from 8 → 11.

HTTP Client simplified REST calls (replacing HttpURLConnection).

📌 MNC Tricky Q:

“Why was removal of Java EE modules controversial in 11?”
👉 Because frameworks (like JAX-WS, JAXB) broke; enterprises had to add external dependencies.

Java 12–16 (Short-term releases)

🔑 Preview/Incubator Features:

Switch expressions (switch(value) -> ...)

Text blocks (""" multiline """)

Records (record User(String name, int age))

Pattern matching (if(obj instanceof String s))

💼 Impact in projects:

Developers got modern syntax sugar.

But enterprises often skipped → waited for 17 (LTS).

Java 17 (2021 – ✅ LTS)

🔑 Major Features:

Sealed classes (sealed class Shape permits Circle, Square)

Pattern matching for instanceof.

Strong encapsulation of JDK internals.

Records finalized.

💼 Impact in projects:

Enterprise adoption rising (8 → 11 → 17 migration path).

Sealed classes improve domain modeling (DDD).

📌 MNC Tricky Q:

“How do sealed classes differ from enums?”
👉 Enums represent fixed values, sealed classes represent restricted type hierarchies with richer behavior.

✅ Stable LTS choices for enterprises: Java 8, Java 11, Java 17.
Most MNCs are still between 8 & 11, new projects target 17.



🚀 Java 5 → Java 8: Topics Overview
Java Version	Key Topics / Features	Explanation & Impact
Java 5 (2004)	- Generics
- Annotations
- Enums
- Varargs
- Enhanced for-loop
- Autoboxing/Unboxing	🔹 Generics made Collections type-safe: List<String> instead of raw List.
🔹 Annotations introduced metadata (@Override, later used by Spring/Hibernate).
🔹 Enums replaced constant ints → better type safety.
🔹 Varargs simplified method arguments (print(String... args)).
🔹 Enhanced for simplified iteration (for(String s : list)).
🔹 Autoboxing bridged primitives ↔ wrapper classes (int <-> Integer).
Java 6 (2006)	- JDBC 4.0 (auto driver loading)
- Scripting API (javax.script)
- Compiler API (javax.tools)
- Minor Collection & Performance updates	🔹 JDBC 4.0: No need for Class.forName() for DB drivers.
🔹 Scripting API: Run JavaScript or Groovy inside Java.
🔹 Compiler API: Compile Java from inside Java programs.
🔹 Mostly stability release → enterprises adopted heavily.
Java 7 (2011)	- Diamond operator (List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();)
- Multi-catch exception (`catch(IOException	SQLException e)`)
- try-with-resources (auto-close resources)
- NIO.2 (new file API)
- Binary literals & underscores in numbers
Java 8 (2014 — LTS)	- Lambda expressions
- Streams API
- Functional interfaces (Predicate, Function)
- Default & Static methods in interfaces
- New Date/Time API (java.time)
- Optional<T>
- Nashorn JavaScript Engine
- CompletableFuture & Concurrency updates	🔹 Lambdas enabled functional style (list.forEach(x -> ...)).
🔹 Streams API simplified data processing (filter/map/reduce).
🔹 Functional interfaces enabled lambdas & method references.
🔹 Default methods → evolve interfaces without breaking old code.
🔹 java.time API replaced old Date & Calendar.
🔹 Optional → safer null handling.
🔹 Nashorn: JS engine (later removed in Java 15).
🔹 CompletableFuture improved async programming.
🎯 Mapping to Your Topics (Collections, OOP, REST)
Area	Java 5	Java 6	Java 7	Java 8
Collections	Generics introduced → type-safe List<String>
Enhanced for-loop	Minor improvements	Diamond operator simplified generics
try-with-resources for safe closing of iterators/resources	Streams API revolutionized collections (map, filter, reduce)
OOP	Enums, Annotations, Varargs, Autoboxing	Mostly stable	Multi-catch, Diamond operator improve OOP ergonomics	Default & static methods in interfaces
Functional programming support (lambdas)
REST / APIs	Servlets (Servlet 2.5) + JAX-RPC style
Annotations laid foundation (@WebService, @WebServlet)	JAX-WS + JAXB built into JDK
Scripting useful for API scripting	NIO.2 improved file upload/download
try-with-resources for DB/file cleanup	Spring MVC & JAX-RS fully leverage lambdas, streams, Optional, async APIs


✅ Stable enterprise versions in this range:

Java 5 → big leap (Generics, Annotations, Enums).

Java 6 → stable, widely used in enterprises.

Java 7 → many enterprises skipped (short-lived), some adopted.

Java 8 → massive adoption, still widely used today.




Java 5 


📘 Java 5 Topics Table (2004)
Category	Topic	Explanation (Java 5 Impact)
Core Language	Generics	Major feature of Java 5. Enables type safety: List<String> instead of raw List. Removes need for manual casting. Implemented with type erasure → at runtime generics info is gone.
	Enhanced for-loop	Simplified iteration: for(String s : list). Works on arrays and Iterable. Prevents common off-by-one errors.
	Varargs	Define methods with variable arguments: print(String... args). Behind the scenes it’s an array.
	Autoboxing/Unboxing	Automatic conversion between primitives and wrapper types: int x = new Integer(5); Integer y = 10;. Helps with collections but introduces subtle performance/memory issues.
	Enums	Strongly typed enumerations: enum Status { NEW, PROCESSING, DONE }. Much safer than int constants; can have fields, methods, constructors.
Collections	Generics with Collections	Now collections are type-safe: List<String> list = new ArrayList<String>();. Compile-time safety avoids ClassCastException.
	Fail-fast Iterators	Iterators throw ConcurrentModificationException if structure modified during iteration. Introduced consistently with generics.
	Concurrent Collections (java.util.concurrent)	Added in 1.5: ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList, BlockingQueue, etc. Essential for multi-threaded programs.
	EnumSet & EnumMap	Special collections optimized for enum keys. Very fast and memory efficient.
OOP	Annotations (Metadata)	New feature in Java 5. Built-in annotations: @Override, @Deprecated, @SuppressWarnings. Frameworks (Spring, Hibernate, JUnit 4) rely heavily on annotations.
	Generics in OOP	Generic classes/interfaces (class Box<T>) allow flexible, type-safe OOP design.
	Covariant return types	A method overriding another can return a more specific type. Improves code readability and safety.
Concurrency	Executor Framework	ExecutorService, Executors.newFixedThreadPool(). Standardized thread pool management.
	Callable & Future	Asynchronous tasks that return results. Replaces manually managing threads.
	Locks & Conditions	ReentrantLock, Condition objects introduced. Provide finer control compared to synchronized.
	Atomic Variables	AtomicInteger, AtomicReference. Lock-free thread-safe programming.
	Synchronizers	New tools: CountDownLatch, CyclicBarrier, Semaphore, Exchanger. Useful for coordinating threads.
I/O & Networking	java.util.Scanner	New utility for easier input parsing from strings/files/streams. Example: Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);.
	Formatter / printf	New printf-style formatting (System.out.printf("Hello %s", name)).
	I/O Enhancements	Performance improvements, charset handling improvements.
JVM / Advanced	Java Memory Model Update	Fixed in Java 5. Before, volatile and final semantics were broken. After fix: double-checked locking with volatile is safe.
	Metadata & Reflection	Reflection API updated to access annotations at runtime. Foundation for modern frameworks.
	Enums as Singleton	Recommended singleton pattern: enum Singleton { INSTANCE; }. Guarantees single instance + thread-safe.
Servlets / REST (Java EE 5)	Servlet 2.5 API	Works with Java 5. Introduced @WebServlet annotations (optional) but most config still in web.xml.
	JAX-WS / JAXB (in JDK 1.6, but influenced in Java 5 EE)	Java 5 core didn’t ship JAX-WS, but annotations in Java 5 enabled declarative web services later.
	Framework foundation	Spring 2.x and Hibernate 3 started to leverage Java 5 annotations (e.g., @Entity, @Autowired).
	
	
	
	
	🔑 Key Takeaways for Java 5

Biggest leap since Java’s beginning → Generics, Annotations, Enums, Concurrency utilities.

Set the foundation of modern Java frameworks (Spring/Hibernate heavily depend on annotations + reflection).

Fixed the Java Memory Model → safe multithreading with volatile, final, and java.util.concurrent.

Enterprises migrated quickly because of type safety + concurrency support.

Interviewers often ask about type erasure, fail-fast iterators, volatile semantics, annotations in frameworks.


------------------

Java 5 (1.5) — Generics, enums, annotations, varargs, enhanced for, java.util.concurrent basics
// Java5_Generics_Enum_Annotation.java  (Java 5 compatible)
import java.util.*;

@interface MyAnn { String value(); }                // Java 5 annotation

@MyAnn("example")
enum Status { NEW, RUNNING, DONE }               // Java 5 enum with values

public class Java5_Generics_Enum_Annotation {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Generics + enhanced for-loop
        List<String> list = new ArrayList<String>();
        list.add("apple"); list.add("banana");
        for (String s : list) {
            System.out.println("Item: " + s);
        }

        // Varargs (Java 5)
        printAll("one", "two", "three");

        // java.util.concurrent introduced in 1.5 (simple demo)
        java.util.concurrent.ArrayBlockingQueue<String> q =
            new java.util.concurrent.ArrayBlockingQueue<String>(2);
        try {
            q.put("a"); q.put("b");
            System.out.println("Queue head: " + q.take());
        } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
    }

    public static void printAll(String... items) {
        for (String it : items) System.out.println("vararg: " + it);
    }
}


Notes: Java 5 brought generics (compile-time type safety via type erasure), rich enums, annotations and java.util.concurrent (Executors, Future, locks, etc.).

Java 6 — JDBC 4.0, Scripting API, Compiler API (mostly incremental)
// Java6_URLConn_and_Scripting.java  (targets Java 6)
import javax.script.*;
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;

public class Java6_URLConn_and_Scripting {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Simple scripting: run a tiny JavaScript snippet using ScriptEngine (Nashorn is later)
        ScriptEngineManager mgr = new ScriptEngineManager();
        ScriptEngine js = mgr.getEngineByName("JavaScript"); // Rhino or engine supported by JVM
        if (js != null) {
            Object res = js.eval("var x = 10; x + 5;");
            System.out.println("JS eval result: " + res);
        } else {
            System.out.println("No JS engine available in this JVM.");
        }

        // JDBC 4.0 convenience: automatic driver loading means you usually don't need Class.forName("com...Driver")
        // (Can't demo DB here; note explained.)
    }
}


Notes: Java 6 smoothed tooling, added javax.script useful for quick scripts inside Java, and improved JDBC driver loading (JDBC 4.0).

Java 7 — Diamond <>, try-with-resources, multi-catch, NIO.2
// Java7_TryWithResources_Diamond_NIO.java  (Java 7)
import java.io.*;
import java.nio.file.*;

public class Java7_TryWithResources_Diamond_NIO {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Diamond operator: no need to repeat generics on RHS
        java.util.List<String> list = new java.util.ArrayList<>();

        // Try-with-resources: auto-close streams
        Path p = Paths.get("example.txt");
        try (BufferedWriter bw = Files.newBufferedWriter(p)) {
            bw.write("hello Java7");
        }

        // Multi-catch example
        try {
            int x = Integer.parseInt("not-int");
        } catch (NumberFormatException | NullPointerException ex) {
            System.out.println("parsed failed: " + ex.getClass().getSimpleName());
        }
    }
}


Notes: try-with-resources dramatically reduces resource-leak bugs. NIO.2 improved file APIs (Paths, Files, file attributes).

Java 8 — Lambdas, Streams, Functional Interfaces, Optional, Date/Time API, default methods
// Java8_Lambda_Stream_Optional_DateTime.java  (Java 8)
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;
import java.time.*;

public class Java8_Lambda_Stream_Optional_DateTime {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Lambda + stream: filter, map, collect
        List<String> names = Arrays.asList("Anna", "Bob", "Alex", "Cory");
        List<String> aNames = names.stream()
            .filter(s -> s.startsWith("A"))
            .map(String::toUpperCase)
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println(aNames); // [ANNA, ALEX]

        // Optional to avoid direct null checks
        Optional<String> maybe = Optional.ofNullable(null);
        System.out.println("present? " + maybe.isPresent());

        // java.time API
        LocalDate now = LocalDate.now();
        System.out.println("today: " + now);

        // Default method on interface (example)
        Runnable r = () -> System.out.println("run via lambda");
        r.run();
    }
}


Notes: Java 8 is a turning point — lambdas & streams enable functional-style collection processing; java.time fixes old date woes.

Java 9 — Modules (JPMS), JShell, factory methods, private interface methods
// Java9_ModuleFactoryAndPrivateInterface.java  (Java 9)
// Note: module-info.java is needed to fully compile modules; here we show factory method use and private interface
import java.util.*;

interface IExample {
    static void hello() { System.out.println("static in interface"); }
    private static String helper() { return "helper"; } // private interface method (Java9)
}

public class Java9_ModuleFactory {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Factory methods for immutable collections (Java 9)
        List<String> fixed = List.of("a", "b", "c"); // unmodifiable list
        System.out.println("fixed: " + fixed);
        IExample.hello();
        // Note: JShell (REPL) is an interactive tool added in Java 9 (not shown here)
    }
}


Notes: Java 9 introduces modules (requires module-info.java), immutable collection factories (List.of), and interface helper/private methods.

Java 10 — Local variable type inference (var)
// Java10_VarExample.java  (Java 10)
import java.util.*;

public class Java10_VarExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var list = new ArrayList<String>(); // compiler infers ArrayList<String>
        list.add("x"); list.add("y");
        for (var s : list) System.out.println(s); // var for loop variable
        // Note: var is only local variable type inference (not fields or method params)
    }
}


Notes: var reduces local variable verbosity but does not change static typing semantics.

Java 11 — Standard HTTP Client, string helpers, LTS
// Java11_HTTPClient_StringHelpers.java  (Java 11)
import java.net.http.*;
import java.net.*;
import java.io.*;
import java.time.*;

public class Java11_HTTPClient_StringHelpers {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // New HTTP client (simpler than HttpURLConnection)
        HttpClient client = HttpClient.newHttpClient();
        HttpRequest req = HttpRequest.newBuilder()
            .uri(URI.create("https://postman-echo.com/get"))
            .GET()
            .build();
        HttpResponse<String> resp = client.send(req, HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString());
        System.out.println("Status: " + resp.statusCode());

        // String helpers
        String s = "  hello\nworld  ";
        System.out.println("lines count: " + s.lines().count());
        System.out.println("isBlank? " + s.isBlank());
    }
}


Notes: Java 11 standardized the modern HTTP client, and added useful String API methods. Java 11 is LTS.

Java 12 → 14 (selected features) — Switch expressions, Text blocks, Records (preview → final)
// Java14_Switch_TextBlock_Record.java  (Java 14 targeted)
import java.util.*;

public class Java14_Features {
    // Text block ("""...""") and record are Java 14+ (preview/final depending on update)
    // Using record:
    record Person(String name, int age) {} // Java 14+ feature (compact data class)

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Person p = new Person("Alice", 30);
        System.out.println("record: " + p.name() + " age:" + p.age());

        // Switch expression (Java 14 final)
        String day = "MON";
        int num = switch (day) {
            case "MON", "TUE" -> 1;
            case "WED" -> 2;
            default -> 0;
        };
        System.out.println("num: " + num);

        // Text block example (Java 13+)
        String json = """
            { "name": "Alice",
              "age": 30
            }
            """;
        System.out.println(json);
    }
}


Notes: Java 12–14 added modern syntax improvements: switch expressions, text blocks, and record types to reduce DTO boilerplate.

Java 15 → 16 — Sealed classes (preview → final in 17), records finalized in 16
// Java16_SealedPattern.java  (Java 16+ preview/Java17 final)
public class Java16_Sealed {
    // Example of a sealed hierarchy (syntax finalized in Java 17):
    // public sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle {}
    // public final class Circle implements Shape { double r; }
    // public final class Rectangle implements Shape { double w,h; }

    // For demo we show pattern matching notion (instanceof pattern is Java 16+)
    Object o = "hello";
    // if (o instanceof String s) { System.out.println(s.toUpperCase()); } // Java 16+ syntax
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Object o = "hello";
        if (o instanceof String) {
            String s = (String) o; // pre-pattern form
            System.out.println(s.toUpperCase());
        }
    }
}


Notes: Sealed classes let you restrict which types can extend a class or implement an interface (useful for closed hierarchies in domain models). Pattern matching for instanceof reduces boilerplate casts.

Java 17 (LTS) — Sealed classes final, pattern matching improvements, strong encapsulation
// Java17_Sealed_Record_Pattern.java  (Java 17)
public class Java17_Example {
    // Sealed + records (Java 17 final)
    public static sealed interface Expr permits Val, Add {}
    public static record Val(int v) implements Expr {}
    public static record Add(Expr left, Expr right) implements Expr {}

    public static int eval(Expr e) {
        if (e instanceof Val v) return v.v();      // pattern matching (Java 17)
        if (e instanceof Add add) return eval(add.left()) + eval(add.right());
        throw new IllegalStateException("unknown");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Expr expr = new Add(new Val(2), new Val(3));
        System.out.println("eval: " + eval(expr)); // eval: 5
    }
}


Notes: Java 17 is LTS and stabilizes sealed classes and pattern matching. Records + sealed + pattern matching cleanly express ADT-like domain models (useful in DDD).

Quick mapping: OOP / Interfaces / Collections evolution (short recap)

Interfaces

Java 5: abstract methods only.

Java 8: default & static methods.

Java 9: private methods in interfaces.

Java 17: full set (and can be used with sealed types).

Collections

Java 5: generics, EnumSet/EnumMap, concurrent collections added.

Java 7: NIO.2 file utilities.

Java 8: Streams, Collectors, Optional.

Java 9: factory methods List.of, Map.of.

Java 16/17: convenience/immutability collectors, toList() improvements.

Concurrency

Java 5: core concurrency (Executors, ConcurrentHashMap, locks, synchronizers).

Java 7: ForkJoinPool.

Java 8: CompletableFuture, parallel streams.

Java 9: Flow (Reactive Streams API).

Great — we’ll start with Java 5 only (all examples are Java 5–compatible).
Below are 12 focused examples that demonstrate the major Java 5 features. Each example is a single runnable main class (or a compact set of classes in one block), contains inline comments, and ends with a 1–2 line interview/notes tip. Copy-paste each into a .java file and run with a Java 5+ JVM.

Java 5 — key features covered

Generics, enhanced for loop, enums, annotations, varargs, autoboxing/unboxing, java.util.concurrent (Executors, Callable, Future, BlockingQueue), WeakHashMap, EnumSet/EnumMap, LinkedHashMap LRU, volatile + double-checked locking, reflection basics, Scanner and printf.

1) Generics + Enhanced for — simple list iteration
// Java5_01_GenericsFor.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java5_01_GenericsFor {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Generics and enhanced for-loop (introduced in Java 5)
        List<String> names = new ArrayList<String>();
        names.add("Alice");
        names.add("Bob");
        names.add("Charlie");

        for (String name : names) {                 // enhanced for
            System.out.println("Name: " + name);    // no casts required
        }
    }
}


Note: generics give compile-time type-safety; at runtime types are erased (type erasure). Interviewers often ask about type erasure.

2) Enum with methods — richer enums
// Java5_02_EnumExample.java
public class Java5_02_EnumExample {
    // Enums can have fields, constructors & methods in Java 5
    enum Status {
        NEW(0), RUNNING(1), DONE(2);
        private final int code;
        Status(int c) { this.code = c; }
        public int code() { return code; }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Status s = Status.RUNNING;
        System.out.println("Status: " + s + " code=" + s.code());
    }
}


Note: enums replace int constants; they’re typesafe and can implement interfaces.

3) Annotations — definition + usage (@MyAnno, @Override example)
// Java5_03_AnnotationExample.java
import java.lang.annotation.*;
import java.lang.reflect.*;

// Define a simple annotation type (Java 5)
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
@Target(ElementType.TYPE)
@interface MyInfo {
    String author();
    String desc() default "";
}

@MyInfo(author="nikhi", desc="demo")
class AnnotatedClass { }

public class Java5_03_AnnotationExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Read annotation via reflection
        MyInfo info = AnnotatedClass.class.getAnnotation(MyInfo.class);
        System.out.println("Author: " + info.author() + ", desc: " + info.desc());
    }
}


Note: Annotations + reflection are the foundation for frameworks like Spring/Hibernate.

4) Varargs + autoboxing/unboxing
// Java5_04_VarargsAutoboxing.java
public class Java5_04_VarargsAutoboxing {
    // varargs (String...) introduced in Java 5
    public static int sum(Integer... nums) {      // wrappers demonstrate autoboxing
        int total = 0;
        for (Integer n : nums) {
            total += n.intValue();               // unboxing, or just `total += n;`
        }
        return total;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Sum: " + sum(1, 2, 3, 10)); // autoboxing of ints -> Integer
    }
}


Note: autoboxing convenient but can introduce performance and null pitfalls.

5) java.util.concurrent — ExecutorService + Callable + Future
// Java5_05_ExecutorCallableFuture.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class Java5_05_ExecutorCallableFuture {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ExecutorService exec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(2);

        Callable<String> c = new Callable<String>() {
            public String call() {
                return "result-from-task";
            }
        };

        Future<String> f = exec.submit(c);      // submit Callable -> Future
        System.out.println("Task returned: " + f.get()); // blocks until done

        exec.shutdown();
    }
}


Note: java.util.concurrent introduced modern concurrency primitives. Be prepared to discuss thread pools and resource cleanup.

6) BlockingQueue producer-consumer (ArrayBlockingQueue)
// Java5_06_BlockingQueueExample.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class Java5_06_BlockingQueueExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        final ArrayBlockingQueue<String> q = new ArrayBlockingQueue<String>(2);

        // Producer thread
        Thread prod = new Thread(new Runnable() {
            public void run() {
                try {
                    q.put("one");
                    q.put("two");
                    // the next put will block until consumer takes
                    q.put("three");
                    System.out.println("Producer done");
                } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
            }
        });

        // Consumer thread
        Thread cons = new Thread(new Runnable() {
            public void run() {
                try {
                    Thread.sleep(200); // simulate delay
                    System.out.println("Consumed: " + q.take());
                    Thread.sleep(200);
                    System.out.println("Consumed: " + q.take());
                    Thread.sleep(200);
                    System.out.println("Consumed: " + q.take());
                } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
            }
        });

        prod.start(); cons.start();
        prod.join(); cons.join();
    }
}


Note: BlockingQueue gives natural back-pressure for producer/consumer.

7) ConcurrentHashMap (basic use)
// Java5_07_ConcurrentHashMapDemo.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class Java5_07_ConcurrentHashMapDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ConcurrentHashMap<String, Integer> chm = new ConcurrentHashMap<String, Integer>();
        chm.put("a", 1);
        chm.put("b", 2);
        System.out.println("Value a=" + chm.get("a"));
        // safe concurrent reads/writes without locking the whole map
    }
}


Note: Java 5 ConcurrentHashMap used segmented locking (good concurrency characteristics). Interviewers ask about concurrent map semantics.

8) WeakHashMap for caches where keys should be GC-able
// Java5_08_WeakHashMapDemo.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java5_08_WeakHashMapDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Map<Object, String> cache = new WeakHashMap<Object, String>();
        Object key = new Object();
        cache.put(key, "value");
        System.out.println("Before GC: size=" + cache.size()); // 1

        key = null;
        System.gc();
        Thread.sleep(100);
        System.out.println("After GC (may be 0): size=" + cache.size());
    }
}


Note: WeakHashMap entries are removed when keys are no longer strongly reachable — useful for metadata caches tied to key lifetime.

9) EnumSet and EnumMap — highly efficient enum-based collections
// Java5_09_EnumSetEnumMap.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java5_09_EnumSetEnumMap {
    enum Color { RED, GREEN, BLUE }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        EnumSet<Color> set = EnumSet.of(Color.RED, Color.BLUE);
        System.out.println("EnumSet: " + set);

        EnumMap<Color, String> map = new EnumMap<Color, String>(Color.class);
        map.put(Color.GREEN, "go");
        System.out.println("EnumMap: " + map);
    }
}


Note: EnumSet and EnumMap are very memory- and time-efficient for enum keys.

10) LinkedHashMap LRU cache pattern (override removeEldestEntry)
// Java5_10_LRUCache.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java5_10_LRUCache {
    static class LRUCache<K,V> extends LinkedHashMap<K,V> {
        private final int capacity;
        LRUCache(int capacity) {
            super(capacity, 0.75f, true); // access-order
            this.capacity = capacity;
        }
        protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry<K,V> eldest) {
            return size() > capacity;
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LRUCache<Integer,String> cache = new LRUCache<Integer,String>(2);
        cache.put(1, "one");
        cache.put(2, "two");
        cache.get(1);                // access 1 -> becomes recently used
        cache.put(3, "three");       // evicts 2
        System.out.println("Keys: " + cache.keySet()); // [1,3]
    }
}


Note: not thread-safe — in an MNC use Collections.synchronizedMap wrapper or a concurrent cache library.

11) Double-checked locking singleton (volatile semantics fixed in Java 5)
// Java5_11_SingletonDCL.java
public class Java5_11_SingletonDCL {
    private static volatile Java5_11_SingletonDCL instance;
    private Java5_11_SingletonDCL() { }

    public static Java5_11_SingletonDCL getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            synchronized (Java5_11_SingletonDCL.class) {
                if (instance == null) {
                    instance = new Java5_11_SingletonDCL();
                }
            }
        }
        return instance;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Java5_11_SingletonDCL s = Java5_11_SingletonDCL.getInstance();
        System.out.println("Singleton: " + s);
    }
}


Note: Before Java 5 the memory model made this pattern unsafe; Java 5 fixed volatile semantics so DCL is correct.

12) Reflection basics — load class, method invoke
// Java5_12_ReflectionDemo.java
import java.lang.reflect.*;

public class Java5_12_ReflectionDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Using reflection to call java.lang.System.currentTimeMillis()
        Class<?> sysClass = Class.forName("java.lang.System");
        Method m = sysClass.getMethod("currentTimeMillis", new Class[0]);
        Object val = m.invoke(null, new Object[0]); // static method -> null instance
        System.out.println("currentTimeMillis via reflection: " + val);
    }
}


Note: reflection is powerful but slower and used by frameworks for dependency injection and annotations processing.

Quick interview-style recap (Java 5)

Why generics use type erasure? (Backward compatibility.)

What changed in the Java Memory Model in Java 5? (Fixes making volatile semantics stronger; DCL safe.)

When to use ConcurrentHashMap vs Collections.synchronizedMap? (CHM for high concurrency; synchronized wrapper for simple mutual exclusion.)

Why prefer EnumSet/EnumMap for enums? (Performance & memory).

Pitfalls of autoboxing? (null -> NullPointerException, identity vs equality for cached wrapper ints).

📘 Java 5 Parameter → Expression Examples
🔹 1. Generics (Collections)

Parameter: Why generics?
Expression:

// Java5_GenericsDemo.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java5_GenericsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Before Java 5 (raw type) - unsafe
        List raw = new ArrayList();
        raw.add("string");
        raw.add(10);  // compiles, but unsafe cast later

        // Java 5 Generics
        List<String> safe = new ArrayList<String>();
        safe.add("hello");
        // safe.add(10); // compile error

        for (String s : safe) { // no cast required
            System.out.println(s.toUpperCase());
        }
    }
}


📝 Interview note: Java 5 implemented generics with type erasure, so at runtime types are removed.

🔹 2. Enhanced for-loop

Parameter: Iterating collections/arrays easily.
Expression:

// Java5_ForEachDemo.java
public class Java5_ForEachDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String[] arr = {"one", "two", "three"};

        // Classic for
        for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
            System.out.println(arr[i]);
        }

        // Enhanced for (Java 5)
        for (String s : arr) {
            System.out.println(s);
        }
    }
}


📝 Note: Works with arrays and anything Iterable.

🔹 3. Enums

Parameter: Replace int constants with type-safe enums.
Expression:

// Java5_EnumDemo.java
public class Java5_EnumDemo {
    enum Direction {
        NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Direction d = Direction.NORTH;
        System.out.println("Chosen: " + d);

        // switch on enum
        switch (d) {
            case NORTH: System.out.println("Go up"); break;
            case SOUTH: System.out.println("Go down"); break;
        }
    }
}


📝 Note: Enums in Java 5 are full classes — can have fields, methods, constructors.

🔹 4. Annotations

Parameter: Metadata attached to code.
Expression:

// Java5_AnnotationDemo.java
import java.lang.annotation.*;

@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
@Target(ElementType.METHOD)
@interface MyAnno {
    String value();
}

public class Java5_AnnotationDemo {
    @MyAnno("custom info")
    public void doWork() { }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        MyAnno anno = Java5_AnnotationDemo.class
            .getMethod("doWork").getAnnotation(MyAnno.class);
        System.out.println("Annotation value = " + anno.value());
    }
}


📝 Note: Core built-ins introduced: @Override, @Deprecated, @SuppressWarnings.

🔹 5. Varargs

Parameter: Pass variable number of arguments.
Expression:

// Java5_VarargsDemo.java
public class Java5_VarargsDemo {
    static void printAll(String... words) {
        for (String w : words) {
            System.out.println(w);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        printAll("a", "b", "c"); // any number of args
    }
}


📝 Note: Behind the scenes, it’s just an array.

🔹 6. Autoboxing / Unboxing

Parameter: Convert primitives ↔ wrappers automatically.
Expression:

// Java5_AutoboxingDemo.java
public class Java5_AutoboxingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Integer x = 10;      // autoboxing
        int y = x;           // unboxing
        System.out.println(x + y);
    }
}


📝 Pitfall: Identity (==) may fail due to caching (Integer caches -128 to 127).

🔹 7. ExecutorService + Future

Parameter: Run tasks in thread pool.
Expression:

// Java5_ExecutorFutureDemo.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class Java5_ExecutorFutureDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ExecutorService service = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(2);

        Future<Integer> result = service.submit(new Callable<Integer>() {
            public Integer call() {
                return 42;
            }
        });

        System.out.println("Got: " + result.get());
        service.shutdown();
    }
}


📝 Note: Introduced in java.util.concurrent (1.5).

🔹 8. BlockingQueue

Parameter: Producer/consumer with back-pressure.
Expression:

// Java5_BlockingQueueDemo.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class Java5_BlockingQueueDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        BlockingQueue<String> queue = new ArrayBlockingQueue<String>(1);

        new Thread(new Runnable() {
            public void run() {
                try {
                    queue.put("task");
                    System.out.println("Produced");
                } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
            }
        }).start();

        System.out.println("Consumed: " + queue.take());
    }
}


📝 Note: Blocks automatically when full/empty.

🔹 9. ConcurrentHashMap

Parameter: Thread-safe map.
Expression:

// Java5_ConcurrentHashMapDemo.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class Java5_ConcurrentHashMapDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ConcurrentHashMap<String,Integer> map = new ConcurrentHashMap<String,Integer>();
        map.put("a", 1);
        map.put("b", 2);
        System.out.println(map);
    }
}


📝 Note: Java 5 version used segmented locks → better concurrency.

🔹 10. WeakHashMap

Parameter: Auto-remove entries when key GC’ed.
Expression:

// Java5_WeakHashMapDemo.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java5_WeakHashMapDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Map<Object,String> cache = new WeakHashMap<Object,String>();
        Object key = new Object();
        cache.put(key, "val");
        System.out.println("Before GC: " + cache.size());
        key = null;
        System.gc(); Thread.sleep(100);
        System.out.println("After GC: " + cache.size());
    }
}


📝 Note: Often used for caches tied to external object lifetimes.

🔹 11. EnumSet & EnumMap

Parameter: Efficient enums in collections.
Expression:

// Java5_EnumSetMapDemo.java
import java.util.*;

enum Day { MON, TUE, WED }

public class Java5_EnumSetMapDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        EnumSet<Day> weekend = EnumSet.of(Day.SAT, Day.SUN); // compile error - no SAT
        // Correction: Use valid enum values
        EnumSet<Day> set = EnumSet.of(Day.MON, Day.WED);
        System.out.println(set);

        EnumMap<Day,String> map = new EnumMap<Day,String>(Day.class);
        map.put(Day.TUE, "meeting");
        System.out.println(map);
    }
}


📝 Note: O(1) lookups, internally use bit vectors for sets.

🔹 12. Singleton with volatile (DCL safe after JMM fix)

Parameter: Thread-safe singleton.
Expression:

// Java5_SingletonDemo.java
public class Java5_SingletonDemo {
    private static volatile Java5_SingletonDemo instance;

    private Java5_SingletonDemo() { }

    public static Java5_SingletonDemo getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            synchronized (Java5_SingletonDemo.class) {
                if (instance == null) {
                    instance = new Java5_SingletonDemo();
                }
            }
        }
        return instance;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(Java5_SingletonDemo.getInstance());
    }
}


📝 Note: Java 5 fixed memory model so this is correct.

✅ That’s Java 5 in parameter → expression style (12 examples).
Each shows concept → runnable snippet → explanation.


Perfect 👍 Let’s move to **Java 6 (2006)**.
Java 6 wasn’t a “big bang” like Java 5 or Java 8 — it was mostly a **stability and tooling release** — but still introduced important things that MNCs test in interviews:

* **Scripting API (`javax.script`)**
* **Compiler API (`javax.tools`)**
* **JDBC 4.0 auto driver loading**
* **`NavigableSet`/`NavigableMap`** (TreeSet/TreeMap enhancements)
* **Desktop API** (open files, browse URLs)
* **Pluggable annotations processing (APT → JSR 269)**
* **Improvements to `Console`, Swing, Web Services (JAX-WS, JAXB)**

I’ll use the **parameter → expression** style (concept → runnable code with `main` → explanation).

---

# 📘 Java 6 Parameter → Expression Examples

---

## 🔹 1. Scripting API — run JavaScript from Java

**Parameter:** Execute scripts inside Java program.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java6_ScriptingDemo.java
import javax.script.*;

public class Java6_ScriptingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // ScriptEngineManager introduced in Java 6
        ScriptEngineManager manager = new ScriptEngineManager();
        ScriptEngine engine = manager.getEngineByName("JavaScript"); // Rhino (in Java 6)

        if (engine != null) {
            Object result = engine.eval("var x = 5; var y = 7; x + y;");
            System.out.println("Script result = " + result); // 12
        } else {
            System.out.println("No JS engine found!");
        }
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Useful for embedding scripting. In Java 8 it switched to **Nashorn**.

---

## 🔹 2. Compiler API (`javax.tools`)

**Parameter:** Compile `.java` code dynamically.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java6_CompilerDemo.java
import javax.tools.*;
import java.util.*;

public class Java6_CompilerDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // JavaCompiler API introduced in Java 6
        JavaCompiler compiler = ToolProvider.getSystemJavaCompiler();
        if (compiler == null) {
            System.out.println("No compiler available (JRE instead of JDK?)");
        } else {
            int res = compiler.run(null, null, null, "Java6_ScriptingDemo.java");
            System.out.println("Compilation exit code: " + res);
        }
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Lets apps like IDEs (Eclipse, IntelliJ) compile Java source at runtime.

---

## 🔹 3. JDBC 4.0 — auto driver loading

**Parameter:** No need for `Class.forName("com.mysql.jdbc.Driver")`.
**Expression (pseudo — requires DB driver JAR):**

```java
// Java6_JDBCDemo.java
import java.sql.*;

public class Java6_JDBCDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // In Java 5: Class.forName("com.mysql.jdbc.Driver"); // required
        // In Java 6: not needed if driver JAR has META-INF/services/java.sql.Driver

        Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection("jdbc:h2:mem:testdb", "sa", "");
        Statement st = conn.createStatement();
        st.execute("CREATE TABLE test(id INT, name VARCHAR)");
        st.execute("INSERT INTO test VALUES(1,'Alice')");
        ResultSet rs = st.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM test");
        while (rs.next()) {
            System.out.println("Row: " + rs.getInt("id") + " " + rs.getString("name"));
        }
        conn.close();
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* JDBC 4.0 simplified DB access. Interviewers may ask about this.

---

## 🔹 4. NavigableMap / NavigableSet

**Parameter:** More control over sorted maps/sets.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java6_NavigableMapDemo.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java6_NavigableMapDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        NavigableMap<Integer, String> map = new TreeMap<Integer, String>();
        map.put(1, "one"); map.put(3, "three"); map.put(5, "five");

        System.out.println("Lower than 3: " + map.lowerEntry(3));
        System.out.println("Ceiling of 2: " + map.ceilingEntry(2));
        System.out.println("Descending keys: " + map.descendingKeySet());
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Interviewers may ask difference between `SortedMap` vs `NavigableMap`.

---

## 🔹 5. Desktop API

**Parameter:** Open browser or file from Java.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java6_DesktopDemo.java
import java.awt.Desktop;
import java.net.URI;

public class Java6_DesktopDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        if (Desktop.isDesktopSupported()) {
            Desktop desk = Desktop.getDesktop();
            desk.browse(new URI("https://www.oracle.com/java/"));
        } else {
            System.out.println("Desktop API not supported");
        }
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Java can now interact with host OS desktop features.

---

## 🔹 6. Console API

**Parameter:** Securely read from console.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java6_ConsoleDemo.java
public class Java6_ConsoleDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        java.io.Console cons = System.console();
        if (cons != null) {
            String name = cons.readLine("Enter name: ");
            char[] pwd = cons.readPassword("Enter password: ");
            System.out.println("Hello, " + name + " (password hidden)");
        } else {
            System.out.println("No console available (IDE run?)");
        }
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* `Console.readPassword()` doesn’t echo input — good for security.

---

## 🔹 7. Pluggable Annotation Processing (APT → JSR 269)

**Parameter:** Frameworks can process annotations at compile-time.
**Expression (conceptual):**

```java
// Java6_AnnotationProcessingDemo.java
// (Real demo requires creating an annotation processor class registered via META-INF/services)
// Example usage: compile-time code generation frameworks like Lombok, JPA metamodel generators
```

📝 *Note:* Java 6 replaced `apt` tool with standardized annotation processing API. Foundation of Lombok, MapStruct, etc.

---

## 🔹 8. Web Services (JAX-WS / JAXB)

**Parameter:** Built into JDK (removed in Java 11).
**Expression (conceptual):**

```java
// Example: @WebService public class HelloService { public String sayHi(String n){ return "Hi " + n; } }
// Can be deployed in Java 6 with wsgen/wsimport tools.
```

📝 *Note:* In Java 6–8 era, SOAP web services were native; now usually done with REST + JSON.

---

# ✅ Summary of Java 6 Features

* **Core:** No new language syntax, but **Desktop API, Console, NavigableMap/Set**.
* **Tooling:** Compiler API, Scripting API.
* **DB:** JDBC 4.0 auto-loading.
* **Enterprise:** JAX-WS, JAXB (annotations-based web services).
* **Annotations:** Compile-time processing standardized (APT → JSR 269).
