👉 Do you want me to now continue with **Java 7** in the same **parameter → expression** style (main class + comments + interview notes)?



Perfect 👍 We finished **Java 5** and **Java 6**.
Now let’s continue with **Java 7 (2011)**.

Java 7 is bigger than 6 — it introduced **Project Coin** (small language changes), **NIO.2** (new file API), and **Fork/Join framework** for concurrency.

---

# 📘 Java 7 Parameter → Expression Examples

---

## 🔹 1. Diamond Operator (`<>`)

**Parameter:** Avoid repeating generic types.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java7_DiamondDemo.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java7_DiamondDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Before: List<String> list = new ArrayList<String>();
        List<String> list = new ArrayList<>(); // diamond operator
        list.add("a"); list.add("b");
        System.out.println(list);
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Compiler infers type on RHS.

---

## 🔹 2. Multi-catch Exception

**Parameter:** Catch multiple exceptions in one block.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java7_MultiCatchDemo.java
public class Java7_MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            String s = null;
            System.out.println(s.length());
        } catch (NullPointerException | ArithmeticException ex) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + ex.getClass().getSimpleName());
        }
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Can’t catch exceptions that have parent-child relation in one line.

---

## 🔹 3. Try-with-Resources

**Parameter:** Automatically close resources.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java7_TryWithResourcesDemo.java
import java.io.*;

public class Java7_TryWithResourcesDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Resource must implement AutoCloseable (FileReader, BufferedReader do)
        try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("test.txt"))) {
            System.out.println("First line: " + br.readLine());
        } // auto-closes
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Replaces try-finally boilerplate.

---

## 🔹 4. NIO.2 — Paths & Files API

**Parameter:** Better file operations.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java7_NIO2Demo.java
import java.nio.file.*;

public class Java7_NIO2Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Path p = Paths.get("sample.txt");

        // Create file
        Files.write(p, "Hello Java7".getBytes());

        // Read file
        String content = new String(Files.readAllBytes(p));
        System.out.println(content);

        // Walk directory
        Path dir = Paths.get(".");
        Files.walk(dir, 1).forEach(System.out::println);
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* NIO.2 unified file handling across platforms.

---

## 🔹 5. Switch on String

**Parameter:** Use `String` in switch-case.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java7_SwitchOnString.java
public class Java7_SwitchOnString {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String cmd = "start";
        switch (cmd) {
            case "start": System.out.println("Starting..."); break;
            case "stop": System.out.println("Stopping..."); break;
            default: System.out.println("Unknown");
        }
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Compiles to `String.hashCode()` + equals checks.

---

## 🔹 6. Numeric literals — binary and underscores

**Parameter:** Easier number representation.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java7_BinaryUnderscore.java
public class Java7_BinaryUnderscore {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int bin = 0b1010;      // binary literal
        int million = 1_000_000; // underscores for readability
        System.out.println("bin=" + bin + ", million=" + million);
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Underscores can’t be at start/end of number.

---

## 🔹 7. ForkJoin Framework

**Parameter:** Parallel recursive tasks.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java7_ForkJoinDemo.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

class SumTask extends RecursiveTask<Integer> {
    private final int[] arr; private final int start, end;
    SumTask(int[] arr, int start, int end) { this.arr = arr; this.start=start; this.end=end; }

    protected Integer compute() {
        if (end - start <= 3) { // small range -> compute directly
            int sum=0; for(int i=start;i<end;i++) sum+=arr[i]; return sum;
        }
        int mid = (start+end)/2;
        SumTask left = new SumTask(arr,start,mid);
        SumTask right = new SumTask(arr,mid,end);
        left.fork(); // async
        return right.compute() + left.join();
    }
}

public class Java7_ForkJoinDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] arr = {1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8};
        ForkJoinPool pool = new ForkJoinPool();
        int result = pool.invoke(new SumTask(arr,0,arr.length));
        System.out.println("Sum=" + result);
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Foundation of parallel streams (Java 8).

---

## 🔹 8. Suppressed Exceptions (with try-with-resources)

**Parameter:** Get hidden exceptions.
**Expression:**

```java
// Java7_SuppressedException.java
class MyRes implements AutoCloseable {
    public void close() { throw new RuntimeException("close failed"); }
}

public class Java7_SuppressedException {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try (MyRes r = new MyRes()) {
            throw new RuntimeException("work failed");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Main ex: " + e.getMessage());
            for (Throwable sup : e.getSuppressed()) {
                System.out.println("Suppressed: " + sup.getMessage());
            }
        }
    }
}
```

📝 *Note:* Useful for debugging multiple failure causes.

---

# ✅ Java 7 Summary

* **Language:** diamond `<>`, try-with-resources, multi-catch, binary literals, underscores, string in switch.
* **API:** NIO.2 (major), suppressed exceptions.
* **Concurrency:** ForkJoin framework.
* **Enterprise:** Better resource mgmt, exception handling.

---

👉 Next: Shall I continue with **Java 8** in the same **parameter → expression** style (many runnable `main` examples + comments)?


Nice — we’ll build Java **8** from scratch using the **parameter → expression** teaching pattern. For each *parameter* (concept) I'll give a **short explanation**, then a **compact, runnable Java 8 `main` example** (complete class), and a **one-line note** about pitfalls / interview angle. These are Java 8–compatible (use lambdas, streams, `java.time`, `Optional`, default methods, etc.).

I'll cover the most important Java 8 topics you’ll need: functional interfaces, lambdas, method references, streams (many flavors), collectors, parallel streams and pitfalls, `Optional`, `Date/Time` API, `CompletableFuture`, primitive streams, `Comparator` helpers, `Map` new ops (`merge`, `computeIfAbsent`), default & static methods in interfaces, `Base64`, Nashorn sketch, repeating annotations, and `Spliterator`/stream internals.

---

# Java 8 — Parameter → Expression reference (each is a runnable `main`)

## 1) Parameter: Functional Interface (foundation)

**Goal:** Single-abstract-method interface for lambdas.

```java
// Java8_01_FunctionalInterface.java
@FunctionalInterface
interface Greeter {
    void greet(String name);
}

public class Java8_01_FunctionalInterface {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Greeter g = (name) -> System.out.println("Hello, " + name);
        g.greet("Alice"); // prints Hello, Alice
    }
}
```

Note: `@FunctionalInterface` not required but documents intent; used by lambdas.

---

## 2) Parameter: Lambda Expression (syntax & capture)

**Goal:** Replace anonymous classes for SAM types.

```java
// Java8_02_LambdaBasics.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java8_02_LambdaBasics {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> names = Arrays.asList("Anna","Bob","Carl");
        names.forEach(n -> System.out.println(n.toUpperCase()));
    }
}
```

Note: Lambdas capture effectively-final variables from outer scope.

---

## 3) Parameter: Method Reference (shorter lambda)

**Goal:** Use method references where possible.

```java
// Java8_03_MethodReference.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java8_03_MethodReference {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> list = Arrays.asList("a","b","c");
        list.forEach(System.out::println); // method reference
    }
}
```

Note: three forms — `Class::static`, `instance::method`, `Class::instanceMethod` (on first param).

---

## 4) Parameter: `Predicate`, `Function`, `Consumer`, `Supplier` (java.util.function)

**Goal:** Standard functional types.

```java
// Java8_04_FunctionalTypes.java
import java.util.function.*;

public class Java8_04_FunctionalTypes {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Predicate<String> isEmpty = s -> s == null || s.isEmpty();
        Function<String,Integer> len = String::length;
        Consumer<String> printer = System.out::println;
        Supplier<String> supplier = () -> "hello";

        System.out.println(isEmpty.test(""));
        System.out.println(len.apply("abc"));
        printer.accept(supplier.get());
    }
}
```

Note: Useful for composing behavior and building reusable APIs.

---

## 5) Parameter: Streams — basic pipeline (filter → map → collect)

**Goal:** Declarative data processing.

```java
// Java8_05_StreamBasic.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java8_05_StreamBasic {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> names = Arrays.asList("alice","bob","anna","charlie");
        List<String> aNames = names.stream()
            .filter(s -> s.startsWith("a"))
            .map(String::toUpperCase)
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println(aNames); // [ALICE, ANNA]
    }
}
```

Note: Streams are single-use; pipeline has source → intermediate ops → terminal op.

---

## 6) Parameter: Primitive streams (`IntStream`, `LongStream`, `DoubleStream`)

**Goal:** Avoid boxing overhead for primitives.

```java
// Java8_06_PrimitiveStream.java
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java8_06_PrimitiveStream {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int sum = IntStream.rangeClosed(1, 10).sum();
        double avg = DoubleStream.of(1.0,2.0,3.0).average().orElse(0.0);
        System.out.println(sum + ", avg=" + avg);
    }
}
```

Note: Use primitive streams for performance-critical numeric pipelines.

---

## 7) Parameter: Collectors — groupingBy / partitioningBy / joining / counting

**Goal:** Aggregate stream results.

```java
// Java8_07_Collectors.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java8_07_Collectors {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> names = Arrays.asList("anna","bob","alex","brian");

        Map<Character, List<String>> byFirst = names.stream()
            .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(s -> s.charAt(0)));

        Map<Boolean, List<String>> partitioned = names.stream()
            .collect(Collectors.partitioningBy(s -> s.length() <= 4));

        String joined = names.stream().collect(Collectors.joining(", "));

        System.out.println(byFirst);
        System.out.println(partitioned);
        System.out.println(joined);
    }
}
```

Note: `groupingBy` returns map; can combine with downstream collectors (counting, mapping, etc.).

---

## 8) Parameter: Collector to Map with merge function

**Goal:** Convert stream to a Map handling duplicate keys.

```java
// Java8_08_ToMapMerge.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java8_08_ToMapMerge {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> words = Arrays.asList("apple","axe","boat","banana");
        Map<Character, Integer> counts = words.stream()
            .collect(Collectors.toMap(w -> w.charAt(0), w -> 1, (a,b) -> a + b));
        System.out.println(counts); // counts by first letter
    }
}
```

Note: third arg is merge function to resolve key collisions.

---

## 9) Parameter: flatMap (flatten nested structures)

**Goal:** Turn `Stream<Stream<T>>` → `Stream<T>`.

```java
// Java8_09_FlatMap.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java8_09_FlatMap {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<List<Integer>> nested = Arrays.asList(Arrays.asList(1,2), Arrays.asList(3,4));
        List<Integer> flat = nested.stream()
            .flatMap(List::stream)
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println(flat); // [1,2,3,4]
    }
}
```

Note: Useful for nested collections or tokenizing strings.

---

## 10) Parameter: Reduction (reduce)

**Goal:** Combine elements into single value.

```java
// Java8_10_Reduce.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java8_10_Reduce {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int product = Arrays.asList(1,2,3,4).stream()
            .reduce(1, (a,b) -> a * b);
        System.out.println("product=" + product);
    }
}
```

Note: Provide identity and accumulator; for parallel use combiner overload.

---

## 11) Parameter: Parallel streams & pitfalls

**Goal:** Run pipelines in parallel (speed vs correctness tradeoffs).

```java
// Java8_11_ParallelStreamPitfall.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java8_11_ParallelStreamPitfall {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> result = Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<Integer>());
        IntStream.range(0, 1000).parallel().forEach(i -> result.add(i)); // potentially correct due to synchronizedList but order unpredictable
        System.out.println("size=" + result.size());
    }
}
```

Note: Avoid shared mutable state; use thread-safe collectors or `Collectors.toList()` (not guaranteeing order in parallel).

---

## 12) Parameter: `Optional<T>` usage patterns

**Goal:** Avoid `null` and `NPE`s.

```java
// Java8_12_OptionalDemo.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java8_12_OptionalDemo {
    public static Optional<String> findName(boolean present) {
        return present ? Optional.of("Alice") : Optional.empty();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String name = findName(true).orElse("Unknown");
        findName(false).ifPresent(n -> System.out.println("found: " + n));
        System.out.println(name);
    }
}
```

Note: Don’t overuse `Optional` in fields — favor return types.

---

## 13) Parameter: New Date/Time API (`java.time`)

**Goal:** Work with immutable, thread-safe date/time.

```java
// Java8_13_JavaTimeDemo.java
import java.time.*;

public class Java8_13_JavaTimeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LocalDate today = LocalDate.now();
        LocalDate nextMonth = today.plusMonths(1);
        LocalDate parsed = LocalDate.parse("2020-08-20");
        System.out.println(today + " -> " + nextMonth + " parsed:" + parsed);

        ZonedDateTime zdt = ZonedDateTime.now(ZoneId.of("Asia/Kolkata"));
        System.out.println(zdt);
    }
}
```

Note: Prefer `java.time` over `Date`/`Calendar`.

---

## 14) Parameter: DateTimeFormatter (format & parse)

**Goal:** Thread-safe formatting/parsing.

```java
// Java8_14_DateTimeFormatter.java
import java.time.*;
import java.time.format.*;

public class Java8_14_DateTimeFormatter {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LocalDateTime now = LocalDateTime.now();
        DateTimeFormatter fmt = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("dd-MMM-yyyy HH:mm");
        String out = now.format(fmt);
        LocalDateTime parsed = LocalDateTime.parse(out, fmt);
        System.out.println(out);
    }
}
```

Note: `DateTimeFormatter` is immutable and thread-safe.

---

## 15) Parameter: `CompletableFuture` (async composition)

**Goal:** Non-blocking async workflows and composition.

```java
// Java8_15_CompletableFuture.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class Java8_15_CompletableFuture {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            sleep(200);
            return "hello";
        }).thenApply(s -> s + " world")
          .thenAccept(System.out::println)
          .get(); // block only for demo
    }

    static void sleep(long ms) { try { Thread.sleep(ms); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); } }
}
```

Note: Powerful for composing async calls without blocking threads for orchestration.

---

## 16) Parameter: `Comparator` helpers (comparing, thenComparing, reversed)

**Goal:** Build readable comparators.

```java
// Java8_16_ComparatorHelpers.java
import java.util.*;
import static java.util.Comparator.*;

public class Java8_16_ComparatorHelpers {
    static class Person { String name; int age; Person(String n,int a){name=n;age=a;} public String toString(){return name+":"+age;} }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Person> people = Arrays.asList(new Person("A",30), new Person("B",25), new Person("C",30));
        people.sort(comparing((Person p) -> p.age).thenComparing(p -> p.name));
        people.forEach(System.out::println);
    }
}
```

Note: Method references and `Comparator` static methods make code concise.

---

## 17) Parameter: Map new operations (`computeIfAbsent`, `merge`)

**Goal:** Concise, atomic map patterns.

```java
// Java8_17_MapOps.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java8_17_MapOps {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Map<String, List<String>> map = new HashMap<>();
        // computeIfAbsent: create list if missing
        map.computeIfAbsent("k", k -> new ArrayList<>()).add("v1");
        System.out.println(map);

        // merge: sum counts
        Map<String,Integer> counts = new HashMap<>();
        counts.merge("a", 1, Integer::sum);
        counts.merge("a", 2, Integer::sum);
        System.out.println(counts); // a->3
    }
}
```

Note: These methods help avoid explicit null checks and race-prone get/put patterns (but still need concurrent map for multithreaded access).

---

## 18) Parameter: Default & Static methods in Interfaces

**Goal:** Extend interfaces without breaking implementors.

```java
// Java8_18_DefaultStaticInterface.java
public class Java8_18_DefaultStaticInterface {
    interface Logger {
        default void log(String s) { System.out.println("LOG: " + s); }
        static void info(String s) { System.out.println("INFO: " + s); }
    }

    static class MyClass implements Logger { }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MyClass().log("hello");
        Logger.info("app started");
    }
}
```

Note: Use wisely to avoid confusing multiple inheritance of behavior.

---

## 19) Parameter: `Base64` encoder/decoder

**Goal:** Built-in Base64 handling.

```java
// Java8_19_Base64.java
import java.util.*;
import java.nio.charset.*;

public class Java8_19_Base64 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String plain = "hello";
        String enc = Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(plain.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8));
        String dec = new String(Base64.getDecoder().decode(enc), StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
        System.out.println(enc + " -> " + dec);
    }
}
```

Note: Replaces common third-party utilities for simple encoding tasks.

---

## 20) Parameter: `Spliterator` & stream parallelism tuning (advanced reference)

**Goal:** Understand stream internals (splitting, characteristics).

```java
// Java8_20_SpliteratorBulk.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.Spliterator;

public class Java8_20_SpliteratorBulk {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i=0;i<100;i++) list.add(i);
        Spliterator<Integer> sp = list.spliterator();
        Spliterator<Integer> sp2 = sp.trySplit(); // split for parallel processing
        sp.forEachRemaining(System.out::print);
        System.out.println();
        if (sp2 != null) sp2.forEachRemaining(System.out::print);
    }
}
```

Note: Custom collections should implement `Spliterator` well for efficient parallel streams.

---

## 21) Parameter: Repeating Annotations (meta)

**Goal:** Apply same annotation multiple times.

```java
// Java8_21_RepeatingAnnotation.java
import java.lang.annotation.*;

@Repeatable(Roles.class)
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
@interface Role { String value(); }
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
@interface Roles { Role[] value(); }

@Role("dev")
@Role("admin")
class User {}

public class Java8_21_RepeatingAnnotation {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Role[] roles = User.class.getAnnotationsByType(Role.class);
        for (Role r : roles) System.out.println(r.value());
    }
}
```

Note: Useful for meta-data where multiplicity is required.

---

## 22) Parameter: Nashorn JS engine (script integration)

**Goal:** Run JS in JVM (Java 8's Nashorn).

```java
// Java8_22_NashornDemo.java
import javax.script.*;

public class Java8_22_NashornDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ScriptEngine engine = new ScriptEngineManager().getEngineByName("nashorn");
        Object res = engine.eval("var x = 10; x * 2;");
        System.out.println("Nashorn result: " + res);
    }
}
```

Note: Nashorn was powerful but later removed; useful for scripting integration.

---

## 23) Parameter: Stream side-effects — do NOT mutate shared state

**Goal:** Show what *not* to do and safe alternative.

```java
// Java8_23_StreamSideEffects.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java8_23_StreamSideEffects {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> list = IntStream.range(0,1000).boxed().collect(Collectors.toList());
        // unsafe: shared ArrayList add in parallel stream (bad)
        List<Integer> bad = Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<>());
        list.parallelStream().forEach(bad::add); // works but ordering & performance issues
        // safe: use collector
        List<Integer> good = list.parallelStream().collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println("sizes: " + bad.size() + " / " + good.size());
    }
}
```

Note: Prefer built-in collectors for thread-safe accumulation.

---

## 24) Parameter: Custom Collector (advanced)

**Goal:** Build a custom collector (example: concatenation with delimiter).

```java
// Java8_24_CustomCollector.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;
import java.util.function.*;

public class Java8_24_CustomCollector {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Collector<CharSequence, StringBuilder, String> joiningCollector =
            Collector.of(StringBuilder::new,
                         StringBuilder::append,
                         (a,b)-> { a.append(b); return a; },
                         StringBuilder::toString);

        String result = Stream.of("a","b","c").collect(joiningCollector);
        System.out.println(result);
    }
}
```

Note: Custom collectors allow optimized parallel accumulation.

---

## 25) Parameter: Streams + I/O (Files.lines)

**Goal:** Use streams to process file lines efficiently.

```java
// Java8_25_FilesLinesStream.java
import java.nio.file.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java8_25_FilesLinesStream {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Path p = Paths.get("somefile.txt");
        try (Stream<String> lines = Files.lines(p)) {
            long count = lines.filter(l -> l.contains("ERROR")).count();
            System.out.println("errors: " + count);
        }
    }
}
```

Note: `Files.lines` uses lazy IO — close the stream or use try-with-resources.

# Wrap-up & next steps

This set gives you a compact **parameter → expression** reference for the core Java 8 features. Each example is runnable and focused: copy into separate `.java` files and execute on Java 8 (or newer).

If you want, I can:

* Expand any **single parameter** (e.g., Streams) into **basic → simple → advanced → tough → MNC** examples with explanations and interview Q\&A.
* Produce a **small exercise set** (problems + solutions) for practicing Java 8 streams & concurrency.
* Move on to **Java 9 → 17** with the same pattern.

Which Java 8 topic should I deep-dive into first? (Streams? CompletableFuture? Date/Time? Optional & best practices?)



Part A — Exercise set: Java 8 Streams & Concurrency

Each problem is followed by a solution. I keep code Java 8-compatible and compact so you can paste into files and run.

Exercises — Problems

(Streams Basic) Given List<String> names, return a List<String> of names that start with "A" (case-insensitive) and sorted alphabetically, upper-cased.

(Streams Mapping) Given a List<Person> (Person has name and age), return the average age of people whose name length > 3. Handle empty case gracefully.

(Streams Grouping) Given List<String> words, produce a Map<Integer, List<String>> grouping words by their length.

(Streams Reduce) Given List<Integer> nums, compute product of all numbers using reduce. If empty, return 1.

(Streams FlatMap) Given List<String> sentences, produce a list of unique words (split on whitespace), lower-cased, sorted.

(Parallel Streams Pitfall) Given a very large List<Integer>, compute sum of squares in parallel safely and measure time vs sequential.

(Collectors Advanced) Given List<Transaction> (with userId and amount), produce Map<Long, Double> of total amounts per user using Collectors.toMap and a merge function.

(Concurrency + Streams) Implement a small producer-consumer using BlockingQueue<String> where producer streams lines from an array into the queue, and consumer reads and prints them. Use ExecutorService with two threads.

(CompletableFuture + Streams) Given List<String> urls, use CompletableFuture.supplyAsync to fetch content length for each URL in parallel (simulate fetch with sleep + random length) and produce a Map<String,Integer> of url→length when all are complete.

(Concurrency Challenge) Given a shared Map<String,Integer>, multiple threads should increment counts for random keys; implement safe concurrent updates using ConcurrentHashMap and compute method. Run N threads and show final totals.

Exercises — Solutions

For brevity I show each solution as a single Java class with main. Replace or move classes to separate files if you prefer.

Problem 1 — filter / map / sort (Basic)
// Ex1_FilterMapSort.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Ex1_FilterMapSort {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> names = Arrays.asList("alice","Bob","ALAN","ann","aaron","Charlie");
        List<String> result = names.stream()
            .filter(s -> s != null && s.toLowerCase().startsWith("a"))
            .map(String::toUpperCase)
            .sorted()
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println(result); // [ALAN, AARON, ALICE, ANN]
    }
}


Why: filter + map + sorted + collect — standard pipeline.

Problem 2 — average with OptionalDouble (Simple)
// Ex2_AverageAge.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Ex2_AverageAge {
    static class Person { String name; int age; Person(String n,int a){name=n;age=a;} }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Person> people = Arrays.asList(new Person("John",25), new Person("Alex",40), new Person("Ana",22));
        OptionalDouble avg = people.stream()
            .filter(p -> p.name.length() > 3)
            .mapToInt(p -> p.age)
            .average();
        System.out.println("Average: " + (avg.isPresent() ? avg.getAsDouble() : 0.0));
    }
}


Why: mapToInt and OptionalDouble handle empty streams.

Problem 3 — groupingBy (Simple)
// Ex3_GroupByLength.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Ex3_GroupByLength {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> words = Arrays.asList("apple","bat","cat","art","banana","ant");
        Map<Integer, List<String>> grouped = words.stream()
            .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(String::length));
        System.out.println(grouped);
    }
}


Why: Collectors.groupingBy is powerful for bucketing.

Problem 4 — reduce (Basic)
// Ex4_ProductReduce.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Ex4_ProductReduce {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> nums = Arrays.asList(2,3,4);
        int product = nums.stream().reduce(1, (a,b) -> a * b);
        System.out.println("Product: " + product); // 24
    }
}


Why: identity 1 ensures empty -> 1.

Problem 5 — flatMap unique words (Simple)
// Ex5_UniqueWords.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Ex5_UniqueWords {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> sentences = Arrays.asList("Hello world", "hello Java", "world of Java");
        List<String> words = sentences.stream()
            .flatMap(s -> Arrays.stream(s.split("\\s+")))
            .map(w -> w.toLowerCase().replaceAll("[^a-z]",""))
            .distinct()
            .sorted()
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println(words); // [hello, java, of, world]
    }
}


Why: flatMap flattens nested streams; distinct() + sorted() tidy output.

Problem 6 — parallel streams safe usage (Advanced)
// Ex6_ParallelSumOfSquares.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;
public class Ex6_ParallelSumOfSquares {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> big = IntStream.rangeClosed(1, 5_000_000).boxed().collect(Collectors.toList());

        long t1 = System.currentTimeMillis();
        long sumSeq = big.stream().mapToLong(i -> (long)i * i).sum();
        long seqTime = System.currentTimeMillis() - t1;

        long t2 = System.currentTimeMillis();
        long sumPar = big.parallelStream().mapToLong(i -> (long)i * i).sum();
        long parTime = System.currentTimeMillis() - t2;

        System.out.println("Seq sum=" + sumSeq + " time=" + seqTime + "ms");
        System.out.println("Par sum=" + sumPar + " time=" + parTime + "ms");
    }
}


Why / Pitfall: Use mapToLong primitive pipeline to avoid boxing overhead. Parallel streams help on multi-core but may be slower for small lists or if non-associative or side-effectful ops used.

Problem 7 — Collectors.toMap with merge (Advanced)
// Ex7_TransactionTotals.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Ex7_TransactionTotals {
    static class Transaction { long userId; double amount; Transaction(long u,double a){userId=u;amount=a;} }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Transaction> txs = Arrays.asList(new Transaction(1,10.0), new Transaction(2,5.0), new Transaction(1,7.5));
        Map<Long, Double> totals = txs.stream()
            .collect(Collectors.toMap(
                tx -> tx.userId,
                tx -> tx.amount,
                (a,b) -> a + b // merge amounts if same key
            ));
        System.out.println(totals); // {1=17.5, 2=5.0}
    }
}


Why: Merge function handles duplicate keys.

Problem 8 — Producer/Consumer using BlockingQueue (Concurrency)
// Ex8_ProducerConsumer.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;
public class Ex8_ProducerConsumer {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        final BlockingQueue<String> q = new ArrayBlockingQueue<>(5);
        Runnable producer = () -> {
            try {
                for (int i=1;i<=10;i++) {
                    q.put("msg-" + i);
                    System.out.println("Produced msg-" + i);
                }
                q.put("DONE");
            } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        };
        Runnable consumer = () -> {
            try {
                String s;
                while (!"DONE".equals(s = q.take())) {
                    System.out.println("Consumed " + s);
                }
                System.out.println("Consumer done");
            } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        };

        ExecutorService ex = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(2);
        ex.submit(producer);
        ex.submit(consumer);

        ex.shutdown();
        ex.awaitTermination(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS);
    }
}


Why: BlockingQueue coordinates producer/consumer without manual locking.

Problem 9 — CompletableFuture simulate parallel fetch (Advanced)
// Ex9_CompletableFutureFetch.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Ex9_CompletableFutureFetch {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        List<String> urls = Arrays.asList("u1","u2","u3","u4");
        ExecutorService ex = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);
        List<CompletableFuture<Map.Entry<String,Integer>>> futures = urls.stream()
            .map(url -> CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
                try { Thread.sleep(200 + new Random().nextInt(200)); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
                int len = url.length() * 10 + new Random().nextInt(100); // simulate length
                return new AbstractMap.SimpleEntry<>(url, len);
            }, ex))
            .collect(Collectors.toList());

        Map<String,Integer> result = CompletableFuture.allOf(futures.toArray(new CompletableFuture[0]))
            .thenApply(v -> futures.stream()
                .map(CompletableFuture::join)
                .collect(Collectors.toMap(Map.Entry::getKey, Map.Entry::getValue)))
            .get();

        System.out.println(result);
        ex.shutdown();
    }
}


Why: CompletableFuture composes async tasks. Use allOf + join to aggregate results.

Problem 10 — ConcurrentHashMap compute increments (Concurrency)
// Ex10_ConcurrentMapIncrement.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class Ex10_ConcurrentMapIncrement {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Integer> map = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
        List<String> keys = Arrays.asList("A","B","C");

        int threads = 10;
        ExecutorService ex = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(threads);
        for (int t=0;t<threads;t++) {
            ex.submit(() -> {
                Random r = new Random();
                for (int i=0;i<1000;i++) {
                    String k = keys.get(r.nextInt(keys.size()));
                    map.compute(k, (kk, v) -> v == null ? 1 : v + 1);
                }
            });
        }
        ex.shutdown();
        ex.awaitTermination(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS);
        System.out.println(map);
    }
}


Why: compute is atomic for ConcurrentHashMap, avoiding explicit locks.

Part B — Deep dive: Streams (parameter → expression) across levels

We’ll expand Streams into Basic → Simple → Advanced → Tough → MNC. Each level: runnable example, compact explanation, and interview Q&A.

Level 1 — Basic

Concept (parameter): Create a stream from a collection, filter, map, collect.
Expression:

// Stream_Level1_Basic.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Stream_Level1_Basic {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> items = Arrays.asList("apple","banana","avocado","berry");
        List<String> filtered = items.stream()
            .filter(s -> s.startsWith("a"))
            .map(String::toUpperCase)
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println(filtered); // [APPLE, AVOCADO]
    }
}


Explanation: single-threaded pipeline: source → intermediate ops (filter/map) → terminal op (collect).

Interview Q&A

Q: Are streams reusable? — A: No, streams are single-use; after a terminal operation they are consumed.

Q: Difference between intermediate and terminal operations? — A: Intermediate ops (map/filter) are lazy and build pipeline; terminal ops trigger processing.

Level 2 — Simple

Concept: numeric streams and primitive specializations to avoid boxing.
Expression:

// Stream_Level2_Primitive.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Stream_Level2_Primitive {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int sum = IntStream.of(1,2,3,4,5).sum();
        double avg = DoubleStream.of(2.0,4.0,6.0).average().orElse(0.0);
        List<Integer> squares = IntStream.rangeClosed(1,5).map(i -> i*i).boxed().collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println(sum + ", avg=" + avg + ", squares=" + squares);
    }
}


Explanation: IntStream/LongStream/DoubleStream have primitive ops and terminal methods.

Interview Q&A

Q: Why prefer primitive streams? — A: Avoid boxing/unboxing overhead and memory churn; better performance.

Q: How to convert IntStream to Stream<Integer>? — A: use .boxed().

Level 3 — Advanced

Concept: groupings, downstream collectors, partitioning, multi-level collectors.
Expression:

// Stream_Level3_CollectorsAdvanced.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Stream_Level3_CollectorsAdvanced {
    static class Person { String name; int age; Person(String n,int a){name=n;age=a;} public String toString(){return name+":"+age;} }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Person> people = Arrays.asList(new Person("A",30), new Person("B",25), new Person("C",30), new Person("D",25));
        Map<Integer, List<Person>> byAge = people.stream().collect(Collectors.groupingBy(p -> p.age));
        Map<Integer, Long> countsByAge = people.stream().collect(Collectors.groupingBy(p->p.age, Collectors.counting()));
        Map<Integer, Optional<String>> eldestNameByAge = people.stream()
            .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(p->p.age,
                Collectors.mapping(p->p.name, Collectors.maxBy(Comparator.naturalOrder()))));
        System.out.println(byAge);
        System.out.println(countsByAge);
        System.out.println(eldestNameByAge);
    }
}


Explanation: downstream collectors allow combination (groupingBy + counting, mapping, maxBy).

Interview Q&A

Q: How to get top N per group with streams? — A: Use groupingBy then in downstream collector use collectingAndThen with sorted/limit or a custom collector (or use toCollection then sort & trim).

Q: Is groupingBy parallel safe? — A: groupingByConcurrent exists for concurrent grouping; default groupingBy is not optimized for concurrent updates.

Level 4 — Tough

Concept: parallel streams correctness & performance — stateless, associative operations, ordering.
Expression:

// Stream_Level4_ParallelAndStatefulPitfalls.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Stream_Level4_ParallelAndStatefulPitfalls {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> data = IntStream.range(0,1000000).boxed().collect(Collectors.toList());

        // BAD: mutable shared state inside parallel stream (race, non-deterministic)
        List<Integer> bad = Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<>());
        data.parallelStream().forEach(bad::add); // may work but ordering unpredictable & performance poor

        // GOOD: use thread-safe collector
        List<Integer> good = data.parallelStream().collect(Collectors.toList());

        System.out.println("sizes: bad=" + bad.size() + " good=" + good.size());
    }
}


Explanation: avoid side-effects in stream ops; use built-in collectors and stateless lambda functions. For parallel streams, ensure operations are associative and non-interfering.

Interview Q&A

Q: What makes an operation safe for parallel streams? — A: Operations must be stateless, non-interfering (not modify source), and functions (accumulators/combiners) must be associative and thread-safe.

Q: When would you avoid parallel streams? — A: When tasks are IO-bound, when ordered results are required and costly, or when tasks have shared mutable state.

Level 5 — MNC (Enterprise)

Concept: building a scalable pipeline: streaming large files, backpressure considerations, memory limits, instrumenting stream performance, choosing between DB and in-memory streaming for very large data.

Expression: Example: streaming a large file, tokenizing, counting words, but using bounded parallelism and efficient collectors.

// Stream_Level5_MNC.java
import java.nio.file.*;
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Stream_Level5_MNC {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Path path = Paths.get("large.txt"); // assume large file
        // Using a bounded thread pool for CPU work
        ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors());

        try (Stream<String> lines = Files.lines(path)) {
            Map<String, Long> freq = lines
                .parallel() // careful: Files.lines splits by IO; parallel may be OK depending on FS
                .flatMap(line -> Arrays.stream(line.split("\\s+")))
                .map(w -> w.replaceAll("[^a-zA-Z]","").toLowerCase())
                .filter(w -> !w.isEmpty())
                .collect(Collectors.groupingByConcurrent(w -> w, Collectors.counting()));

            System.out.println("Top 10 words: ");
            freq.entrySet().stream()
                .sorted(Map.Entry.<String,Long>comparingByValue(Comparator.reverseOrder()))
                .limit(10)
                .forEach(System.out::println);
        } finally {
            pool.shutdown();
        }
    }
}


Explanation & MNC tips:

For truly huge data, stream from the database with pagination or use specialized stream processing (Kafka, Spark) — don't keep everything in memory.

Use groupingByConcurrent for parallel aggregation on multi-core.

Monitor CPU, memory, and GC; add metrics (timers, counters) around stream stages.

Use batching and backpressure (e.g., flow-control at source) to avoid resource exhaustion.

Interview Q&A

Q: How to handle counting words in 100GB text file? — A: Use distributed processing (Hadoop/Spark) or stream + external store (Redis) + sharding. Avoid in-memory collection. Use map-reduce pattern.

Q: How to instrument streams in production? — A: Insert timers and counters before/after heavy stages; wrap expensive ops in monitored CompletableFuture or use peek() carefully for metrics (avoid side effects that alter semantics).

Quick checklist: Streams best practices (cheat sheet)

Prefer stateless lambdas; avoid side-effects.

Use primitive streams when dealing with numbers.

Use Collectors not manual accumulation for parallel streams.

Use groupingByConcurrent / toConcurrentMap for concurrency.

Measure before converting sequential → parallel; parallelism helps for CPU-bound, large datasets, not IO-bound or small datasets.

Avoid peek() for business logic — use for debugging/metrics only if you know it won’t alter behavior.



Intro — why CompletableFuture?

CompletableFuture provides composable async tasks (non-blocking coordination, composition, error handling). It’s the core building block for modern async code before full reactive stacks.

Level — BASIC

Parameter: Create an async task, get result, simple composition (thenApply, thenAccept).

Expression

// CF_Basic.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class CF_Basic {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // run on common ForkJoinPool
        CompletableFuture<String> cf = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            sleep(300);
            return "hello";
        });

        // synchronous transformation when previous completes
        CompletableFuture<String> transformed = cf.thenApply(s -> s.toUpperCase());

        // terminal action
        transformed.thenAccept(s -> System.out.println("Result: " + s));

        // block to keep JVM alive for demo (in real apps avoid blocking)
        System.out.println("Blocking get(): " + transformed.get()); // may throw checked exceptions
    }

    static void sleep(long ms) { try { Thread.sleep(ms); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); } }
}


Explanation / Best practice

supplyAsync runs supplier on ForkJoinPool.commonPool by default.

thenApply transforms result synchronously in the same thread that completed the previous stage (unless thenApplyAsync is used).

Prefer non-blocking composition; avoid get()/join() in async pipelines.

Interview Q&A

Q: What’s the difference between thenApply and thenApplyAsync?
A: thenApply runs the mapper in the thread completing previous stage; thenApplyAsync submits the mapper to the ForkJoinPool (or supplied executor).

Q: What does supplyAsync return?
A: CompletableFuture<T> representing eventual result of supplier.

Level — SIMPLE

Parameter: thenCompose (flatMap for futures), thenCombine (combine two futures), exceptionally / handle.

Expression

// CF_Simple.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class CF_Simple {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // simulate fetching userId, then fetching profile by id (dependent calls)
        CompletableFuture<Integer> idFuture = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            sleep(200);
            return 42;
        });

        // sequential composition: fetch profile after id => thenCompose
        CompletableFuture<String> profileFuture = idFuture.thenCompose(id ->
            CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
                sleep(200);
                return "User#" + id + " Profile";
            })
        );

        // parallel tasks + combine results
        CompletableFuture<String> newsFuture = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> "TopNews");
        CompletableFuture<String> combined = profileFuture.thenCombine(newsFuture, (profile, news) ->
            profile + " | " + news
        );

        // handle exceptions gracefully
        CompletableFuture<String> safe = combined.exceptionally(ex -> "fallback-profile");

        System.out.println(safe.get());
    }

    static void sleep(long ms) { try { Thread.sleep(ms); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); } }
}


Explanation / Best practice

Use thenCompose when next async depends on previous result (avoids nested futures).

Use thenCombine/thenAcceptBoth when two independent futures need combining.

Use exceptionally for fallback; handle gives both result and exception.

Interview Q&A

Q: When to use thenCompose vs thenCombine?
A: thenCompose chains dependent async ops (A → B(A)). thenCombine merges two independent futures’ results.

Q: How do you handle exceptions in a pipeline?
A: exceptionally, handle, whenComplete — choose exceptionally for fallback value, handle to transform result or exception, and whenComplete for side-effects.

Level — ADVANCED

Parameter: Aggregate many futures (allOf, anyOf), custom executors, join() vs get(), avoiding blocking thread pool starvation.

Expression

// CF_Advanced.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class CF_Advanced {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ExecutorService ioPool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4); // use dedicated pool for blocking IO

        List<String> urls = Arrays.asList("u1","u2","u3","u4");
        List<CompletableFuture<Integer>> futures = urls.stream()
            .map(url -> CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchLength(url), ioPool))
            .collect(Collectors.toList());

        // allOf returns CF<Void> — convert to List of results
        CompletableFuture<Void> all = CompletableFuture.allOf(futures.toArray(new CompletableFuture[0]));
        CompletableFuture<List<Integer>> allResults = all.thenApply(v ->
            futures.stream().map(CompletableFuture::join).collect(Collectors.toList())
        );

        List<Integer> lengths = allResults.get(); // blocking here for demo
        System.out.println("lengths: " + lengths);

        ioPool.shutdown();
    }

    static int fetchLength(String url) {
        sleep(200);
        return url.length() * 10;
    }

    static void sleep(long ms) { try { Thread.sleep(ms); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); } }
}


Explanation / Best practice

Use dedicated executor for blocking operations — commonPool may get exhausted causing deadlocks.

allOf returns CompletableFuture<Void>; to get typed results map futures and join() inside thenApply.

join() rethrows exceptions as CompletionException (unchecked), while get() throws checked exceptions.

Interview Q&A

Q: Why provide your own executor for supplyAsync?
A: If tasks block (IO, DB), using common pool can starve it; providing a bounded thread pool avoids interference.

Q: Difference join() vs get()?
A: join() throws unchecked CompletionException; get() throws checked ExecutionException/InterruptedException.

Level — TOUGH

Parameter: Timeouts, cancellation, retry with backoff, exception propagation, avoiding deadlock and resource leak scenarios.

Expression

// CF_Tough.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.*;

public class CF_Tough {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ExecutorService ex = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(2);

        CompletableFuture<String> work = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            sleep(2000); // long-running
            return "OK";
        }, ex);

        // Timeout using a scheduled timeout future and applyToEither
        ScheduledExecutorService scheduler = Executors.newSingleThreadScheduledExecutor();
        CompletableFuture<String> timeout = new CompletableFuture<>();
        scheduler.schedule(() -> timeout.completeExceptionally(new TimeoutException("Timed out")), 1, TimeUnit.SECONDS);

        CompletableFuture<String> result = work.applyToEither(timeout, s -> s)
            .exceptionally(exn -> {
                System.out.println("Exception in pipeline: " + exn);
                return "DEFAULT";
            });

        System.out.println("Result: " + result.get()); // will return DEFAULT due to timeout
        // cancellation example
        boolean cancel = work.cancel(true); // may interrupt executing thread
        System.out.println("Cancelled work: " + cancel);

        ex.shutdown(); scheduler.shutdown();
    }

    static void sleep(long ms) { try { Thread.sleep(ms); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); } }
}


Explanation / Best practice

Java 8 has no built-in orTimeout — use a scheduled future to completeExceptionally or applyToEither.

Canceling a CompletableFuture attempts to cancel underlying task only if task cooperatively checks interrupts.

Always shut down executors to avoid thread leaks.

Interview Q&A

Q: How to implement a timeout for a CompletableFuture in Java 8?
A: Create a scheduled task that completesExceptionally a complementary future after timeout and race it with the original future using applyToEither or acceptEither.

Q: Does cancel(true) guarantee interrupting the underlying work?
A: No — it sends interrupt to thread if running, but the task must cooperatively respond to interrupts.

Level — MNC (Enterprise-grade patterns)

Parameter: Robust async pipelines: retries with exponential backoff, circuit-breaker integration (concept), tracing/propagation of request context, metrics, idempotency and resource cleanup, bulkheading, timeouts & fallback strategies.

Expression (retry + fallback sketch)

// CF_MNC.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.*;
import java.util.function.*;

public class CF_MNC {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ExecutorService ioPool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(8);
        // example remote call simulation that fails intermittently
        Supplier<CompletableFuture<String>> remoteCall = () ->
            CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> unreliableNetworkCall(), ioPool);

        CompletableFuture<String> withRetry = retry(remoteCall, 3, 200, ioPool)
            .exceptionally(ex -> {
                // fallback
                System.out.println("All retries failed: " + ex);
                return "DEFAULT_RESULT";
            });

        System.out.println("Final: " + withRetry.get());
        ioPool.shutdown();
    }

    static String unreliableNetworkCall() {
        // simulate 50% failure
        if (new Random().nextBoolean()) throw new RuntimeException("transient failure");
        return "OK-" + System.currentTimeMillis();
    }

    // Simple retry returning a CompletableFuture using recursion
    static <T> CompletableFuture<T> retry(Supplier<CompletableFuture<T>> taskSupplier, int retries, long backoffMs, ScheduledExecutorService scheduler) {
        ExecutorService exec = Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor();
        // wrapper that schedules retries via scheduler
        CompletableFuture<T> promise = new CompletableFuture<>();
        runWithRetry(taskSupplier, retries, backoffMs, scheduler, promise);
        exec.shutdown();
        return promise;
    }

    static <T> void runWithRetry(Supplier<CompletableFuture<T>> supplier, int retries, long backoffMs, ScheduledExecutorService scheduler, CompletableFuture<T> promise) {
        try {
            supplier.get().whenComplete((res, ex) -> {
                if (ex == null) {
                    promise.complete(res);
                } else {
                    if (retries > 0) {
                        // schedule retry
                        scheduler.schedule(() -> runWithRetry(supplier, retries - 1, backoffMs * 2, scheduler, promise), backoffMs, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
                    } else {
                        promise.completeExceptionally(ex);
                    }
                }
            });
        } catch (Exception e) {
            if (retries > 0) {
                scheduler.schedule(() -> runWithRetry(supplier, retries - 1, backoffMs * 2, scheduler, promise), backoffMs, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
            } else {
                promise.completeExceptionally(e);
            }
        }
    }
}


(Note: above sketch uses ScheduledExecutorService externally; in real systems integrate with libraries like Resilience4j for circuit-breaker/retry/timeout patterns.)

Enterprise best practices

Bulkhead: isolate expensive/unstable services into bounded thread pools (separate executor per external system).

Circuit breaker: fail fast on repeated failures to avoid cascading. Use libraries (Resilience4j, Hystrix-like) integrated into async flow.

Context propagation: carry trace IDs and user context across async boundaries — wrap Runnable/Callable or use thread-context propagation utilities.

Metrics & tracing: instrument start/end of CompletableFuture chains; capture latencies and failure counts.

Idempotency: ensure retried operations are safe or deduped server-side.

Resource cleanup: ensure executors are shut down and future cancellations free resources.

Interview Q&A

Q: How to avoid thread starvation with CompletableFuture pipelines?
A: Don’t block commonPool threads. Use dedicated, bounded pools for blocking IO; use non-blocking IO where possible; avoid calling get() inside pipeline tasks.

Q: How to propagate tracing context across async boundaries?
A: Capture current trace context and wrap Runnable/Supplier to set the context when executing; or use framework support (e.g., OpenTracing/OpenTelemetry hooks).

Q: When to prefer CompletableFuture over reactive streams?
A: CompletableFuture is great for ad hoc async composition of finite operations; reactive streams are better when you need backpressure and streaming data processing at scale.

Quick Cheatsheet — Common Methods

supplyAsync(Supplier) — start async supplier.

thenApply(fn) / thenApplyAsync(fn, executor) — sync/async transform.

thenCompose(fn) — chain futures (flatten).

thenCombine(future, fn) — combine independent futures.

allOf(...) / anyOf(...) — aggregate futures.

exceptionally(fn) / handle(fn) / whenComplete(fn) — error handling.

complete(value) / completeExceptionally(ex) — externally complete a CF.

cancel(true) — attempt cancel.

Small Practice Exercises (CompletableFuture)

Use thenCompose to chain three dependent async tasks: auth → fetchProfile → fetchPermissions.

Use allOf to fetch several mock URLs in parallel and return a map url→result.

Implement orTimeout semantics in Java 8 using ScheduledExecutorService (race original vs timeout future).

Build a retry wrapper that retries a failing CompletableFuture supplier up to N times with exponential backoff.

Show a deadlock: execute a supplyAsync that calls get() on another future that uses the same single-threaded executor — explain why it deadlocks.



Exercise 1 — Chain three dependent async tasks: auth → fetchProfile → fetchPermissions

Goal: show thenCompose for dependent async calls (avoid nested futures).

// CF_Exercise1_ChainDependent.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class CF_Exercise1_ChainDependent {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Executor for clarity (could use commonPool)
        ExecutorService ex = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);

        // Step 1: authenticate -> returns userId (async)
        CompletableFuture<Integer> auth = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            sleep(200);
            System.out.println("[auth] succeeded -> userId=123");
            return 123;
        }, ex);

        // Step 2: fetchProfile(userId) returns profile string
        // Step 3: fetchPermissions(profile) returns permissions string
        CompletableFuture<String> pipeline =
            auth.thenCompose(userId ->
                CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
                    sleep(150);
                    String profile = "Profile(user=" + userId + ")";
                    System.out.println("[fetchProfile] for user " + userId + " -> " + profile);
                    return profile;
                }, ex)
            ).thenCompose(profile ->
                CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
                    sleep(120);
                    String perms = "PERMS(for=" + profile + ")";
                    System.out.println("[fetchPermissions] -> " + perms);
                    return perms;
                }, ex)
            );

        // final result
        String result = pipeline.get(); // blocking only for demo
        System.out.println("Pipeline result: " + result);

        ex.shutdown();
    }

    static void sleep(long ms) {
        try { Thread.sleep(ms); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
    }
}


Explanation: thenCompose flattens dependent CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<T>> into CompletableFuture<T>. Use thenCompose when next async step depends on previous result.

Interview note: Don’t use thenApply when the mapper returns a CompletableFuture — that yields nested futures and is awkward to manage.

Exercise 2 — allOf to fetch several mock URLs in parallel and return a Map<String,String>

Goal: run N tasks in parallel, wait for all, then aggregate results.

// CF_Exercise2_AllOfToMap.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class CF_Exercise2_AllOfToMap {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ExecutorService ex = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(6);
        List<String> urls = Arrays.asList("urlA", "urlB", "urlC", "urlD");

        List<CompletableFuture<AbstractMap.SimpleEntry<String, String>>> futures =
            urls.stream()
                .map(url -> CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchMock(url), ex)
                    .thenApply(content -> new AbstractMap.SimpleEntry<>(url, content)))
                .collect(Collectors.toList());

        // allOf -> CompletableFuture<Void>
        CompletableFuture<Void> all = CompletableFuture.allOf(futures.toArray(new CompletableFuture[0]));

        // When all complete, collect results
        CompletableFuture<Map<String, String>> allResults = all.thenApply(v ->
            futures.stream()
                .map(CompletableFuture::join) // safe because all completed
                .collect(Collectors.toMap(Map.Entry::getKey, Map.Entry::getValue))
        );

        Map<String, String> resultMap = allResults.get(); // blocking for demo
        System.out.println("Results: " + resultMap);

        ex.shutdown();
    }

    static String fetchMock(String url) {
        sleep(120 + new Random().nextInt(200));
        return "content-of-" + url;
    }

    static void sleep(long ms) {
        try { Thread.sleep(ms); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
    }
}


Explanation: allOf gives a single future representing completion of all tasks. Map typed results by joining original futures after allOf completes.

Interview note: Avoid blocking threads unnecessarily. For streaming aggregation without collecting all in memory, consider streaming results as they arrive (e.g., CompletableFuture.anyOf or reactive streaming).

Exercise 3 — Implement orTimeout semantics (Java 8) using ScheduledExecutorService

Goal: create a helper that returns a new CF that fails if original does not complete before the timeout.

// CF_Exercise3_OrTimeout.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.function.*;

public class CF_Exercise3_OrTimeout {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ScheduledExecutorService scheduler = Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(1);
        ExecutorService ex = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(2);

        // Long-running task that may time out
        CompletableFuture<String> original = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            sleep(2000); // 2s
            return "OK";
        }, ex);

        // Wrap with timeout 1s
        CompletableFuture<String> withTimeout = orTimeout(original, 1, TimeUnit.SECONDS, scheduler);

        try {
            System.out.println("Result: " + withTimeout.get());
        } catch (ExecutionException ee) {
            System.out.println("Completed exceptionally: " + ee.getCause());
        }

        scheduler.shutdown();
        ex.shutdown();
    }

    // helper: returns a future that completes with original result OR times out with TimeoutException
    public static <T> CompletableFuture<T> orTimeout(CompletableFuture<T> original, long timeout, TimeUnit unit, ScheduledExecutorService scheduler) {
        final CompletableFuture<T> timeoutFuture = new CompletableFuture<>();
        ScheduledFuture<?> scheduled = scheduler.schedule(() -> timeoutFuture.completeExceptionally(new TimeoutException("Timed out after " + timeout + " " + unit)), timeout, unit);
        // whichever completes first wins; cancel scheduled if original completes
        return original.applyToEither(timeoutFuture, Function.identity())
                .whenComplete((res, ex) -> scheduled.cancel(false));
    }

    static void sleep(long ms) {
        try { Thread.sleep(ms); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
    }
}


Explanation: We race the original future against a scheduled future that completes exceptionally. applyToEither returns the winner. We cancel the scheduled task when done to avoid leaks.

Interview note: In Java 9+ you have orTimeout and completeOnTimeout built-in. In Java 8 use this pattern.

Exercise 4 — Retry wrapper that retries a failing CompletableFuture supplier up to N times with exponential backoff

Goal: robust retry with backoff using ScheduledExecutorService. The wrapper returns a CompletableFuture<T>.

// CF_Exercise4_RetryWrapper.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.function.*;

public class CF_Exercise4_RetryWrapper {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ScheduledExecutorService scheduler = Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(1);
        ExecutorService ex = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);

        Supplier<CompletableFuture<String>> unreliable = () ->
            CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> unreliableCall(), ex);

        CompletableFuture<String> result = retry(unreliable, 3, 200, scheduler);
        try {
            System.out.println("Final result: " + result.get());
        } catch (ExecutionException ee) {
            System.out.println("All retries failed: " + ee.getCause());
        }

        scheduler.shutdown();
        ex.shutdown();
    }

    // Simulate flaky call: 60% chance to fail
    static String unreliableCall() {
        if (Math.random() < 0.6) throw new RuntimeException("transient error");
        return "SUCCESS";
    }

    public static <T> CompletableFuture<T> retry(Supplier<CompletableFuture<T>> supplier, int maxRetries, long initialBackoffMillis, ScheduledExecutorService scheduler) {
        CompletableFuture<T> promise = new CompletableFuture<>();
        runAttempt(supplier, maxRetries, initialBackoffMillis, scheduler, promise);
        return promise;
    }

    private static <T> void runAttempt(Supplier<CompletableFuture<T>> supplier, int remaining, long backoffMs, ScheduledExecutorService scheduler, CompletableFuture<T> promise) {
        try {
            supplier.get().whenComplete((res, ex) -> {
                if (ex == null) {
                    promise.complete(res);
                } else {
                    if (remaining > 0) {
                        // schedule next attempt after backoff
                        scheduler.schedule(() -> runAttempt(supplier, remaining - 1, backoffMs * 2, scheduler, promise), backoffMs, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
                    } else {
                        promise.completeExceptionally(ex);
                    }
                }
            });
        } catch (Throwable t) {
            if (remaining > 0) {
                scheduler.schedule(() -> runAttempt(supplier, remaining - 1, backoffMs * 2, scheduler, promise), backoffMs, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
            } else {
                promise.completeExceptionally(t);
            }
        }
    }
}


Explanation: Each failed attempt schedules the next with doubled backoff. Use durable scheduler to avoid blocking task threads. In production, prefer libraries (Resilience4j) for circuit breaker + retry.

Interview note: Ensure idempotency of retried operations — otherwise retries can cause duplicate side-effects.

Exercise 5 — Deadlock demonstration: single-thread executor + blocking .get() inside task

Goal: show how blocking inside a task that submits/waits on another task on the same single-thread executor causes deadlock.

// CF_Exercise5_DeadlockDemo.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class CF_Exercise5_DeadlockDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ExecutorService single = Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor();

        // future2 is scheduled on the same single-thread executor
        CompletableFuture<String> future2 = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            sleep(500);
            return "Result2";
        }, single);

        // future1 runs on same executor and blocks waiting for future2 -> deadlock
        CompletableFuture<String> future1 = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            // This call blocks the single thread until future2 completes, but future2 can't run because thread is busy -> deadlock
            try {
                System.out.println("[future1] waiting for future2...");
                String r = future2.get(); // blocking call inside same single-thread executor
                return "future1 got -> " + r;
            } catch (Exception e) {
                throw new RuntimeException(e);
            }
        }, single);

        // Attempt to get with timeout so demo terminates
        try {
            System.out.println("future1 result: " + future1.get(2, TimeUnit.SECONDS));
        } catch (TimeoutException te) {
            System.out.println("Timed out -> deadlock observed (single-thread pool blocked)");
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            System.out.println("Exception: " + ex);
        } finally {
            single.shutdownNow();
        }
    }

    static void sleep(long ms) {
        try { Thread.sleep(ms); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
    }
}


Explanation: future1 occupies the only thread in the executor and calls .get() on future2, which cannot run because the thread is blocked — classic deadlock. Avoid blocking calls inside task threads of limited executors. Use non-blocking composition (thenCompose) or use separate executors for dependent tasks.

Interview note: This is a common gotcha — explain how thenCompose avoids deadlock and show how using different executors or non-blocking composition fixes it.

Short Recap & Best Practices

Use thenCompose for dependent async steps and thenCombine for independent ones.

Do not block in async tasks running on shared/common or bounded pools — prefer non-blocking composition.

Provide dedicated executors for blocking IO; don't overload ForkJoinPool.commonPool with blocking ops.

Use ScheduledExecutorService to implement timeouts or backoff in Java 8.

Prefer frameworks (Resilience4j, Hystrix-style libs) for production-grade retry/circuit-breaker semantics and metrics.


Basic — core types and simple operations
Parameter: LocalDate, LocalTime, LocalDateTime

What: Immutable date / time types with no timezone info. Use them for local calendar/time operations (birthdates, daily schedules).

// DT_Basic_LocalTypes.java
import java.time.*;

public class DT_Basic_LocalTypes {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LocalDate date = LocalDate.of(2025, 9, 12);
        LocalTime time = LocalTime.of(14, 30, 15);
        LocalDateTime dt = LocalDateTime.of(date, time);

        System.out.println("date: " + date);           // 2025-09-12
        System.out.println("time: " + time);           // 14:30:15
        System.out.println("datetime: " + dt);         // 2025-09-12T14:30:15

        // arithmetic
        System.out.println("plusDays: " + date.plusDays(3));
        System.out.println("minusHours: " + dt.minusHours(2));
    }
}


Note: Local types have no zone; they represent local wall-clock values.

Interview Q&A

Q: When would you use LocalDate vs Instant?
A: LocalDate for date-only values (birthday). Instant for machine time (timestamp, logging) independent of timezone.

Parameter: Instant (epoch-point in time)

What: A machine timestamp (UTC-based), good for persistence and interop with epoch millis.

// DT_Basic_Instant.java
import java.time.*;

public class DT_Basic_Instant {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Instant now = Instant.now();
        long epochMillis = now.toEpochMilli();
        Instant later = now.plusSeconds(3600);

        System.out.println("now: " + now);
        System.out.println("epoch ms: " + epochMillis);
        System.out.println("later: " + later);
    }
}


Note: Use Instant to store timestamps (persist as long or ISO instant string).

Interview Q&A

Q: How to convert Instant to LocalDateTime?
A: LocalDateTime.ofInstant(instant, ZoneId.of("Asia/Kolkata")).

Simple — formatting/parsing and timezones
Parameter: DateTimeFormatter (format & parse)

What: Thread-safe formatter for date/time to/from text.

// DT_Simple_Formatter.java
import java.time.*;
import java.time.format.*;

public class DT_Simple_Formatter {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LocalDateTime dt = LocalDateTime.of(2025, 9, 12, 14, 30, 15);
        DateTimeFormatter fmt = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("dd-MMM-yyyy HH:mm:ss");
        String text = dt.format(fmt);
        LocalDateTime parsed = LocalDateTime.parse(text, fmt);

        System.out.println("formatted: " + text);
        System.out.println("parsed: " + parsed);
    }
}


Note: DateTimeFormatter instances are immutable and safe for concurrent use.

Interview Q&A

Q: Are SimpleDateFormat and DateTimeFormatter both thread-safe?
A: SimpleDateFormat is not thread-safe. DateTimeFormatter is thread-safe.

Parameter: ZonedDateTime & ZoneId (time zone conversions)

What: Zoned date-time (wall-clock + zone rules). Convert between zones.

// DT_Simple_ZonedDateTime.java
import java.time.*;

public class DT_Simple_ZonedDateTime {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LocalDateTime local = LocalDateTime.of(2025, 3, 30, 2, 30); // ambiguous/invalid in some zones
        ZoneId ist = ZoneId.of("Asia/Kolkata");
        ZoneId ny = ZoneId.of("America/New_York");

        ZonedDateTime zIst = ZonedDateTime.of(local, ist);
        ZonedDateTime zNy = zIst.withZoneSameInstant(ny); // same instant, different wall time

        System.out.println("IST: " + zIst);
        System.out.println("NY (same instant): " + zNy);
    }
}


Note: Use withZoneSameInstant to map the same instant to a different zone; withZoneSameLocal preserves local time and changes the instant.

Interview Q&A

Q: Difference between withZoneSameInstant and withZoneSameLocal?
A: withZoneSameInstant converts instant to another time zone keeping the instant; withZoneSameLocal keeps wall-clock fields and interprets them in the new zone (changes instant).

Advanced — periods, durations, adjusters, parsing complex formats
Parameter: Duration vs Period

What: Duration for time-based amounts (seconds/nanos), Period for date-based amounts (years, months, days).

// DT_Advanced_DurationPeriod.java
import java.time.*;

public class DT_Advanced_DurationPeriod {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Duration d = Duration.ofHours(5).plusMinutes(30);
        Period p = Period.of(1, 2, 3); // 1 year, 2 months, 3 days

        LocalDate date = LocalDate.of(2025, 1, 31);
        LocalDate newDate = date.plus(p); // adds months/years/days
        LocalDateTime dt = LocalDateTime.now().plus(d); // adds hours/minutes

        System.out.println("Duration: " + d);
        System.out.println("Period: " + p);
        System.out.println("newDate: " + newDate);
    }
}


Note: Beware adding months to end-of-month dates; behavior follows calendar semantics.

Interview Q&A

Q: Why not use Duration for months/years?
A: Months/years vary in length—use Period for date-based arithmetic.

Parameter: TemporalAdjusters and custom adjuster (next working day)

What: adjust dates using built-in or custom logic.

// DT_Advanced_TemporalAdjuster.java
import java.time.*;
import java.time.temporal.*;
import java.util.*;

public class DT_Advanced_TemporalAdjuster {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LocalDate today = LocalDate.of(2025, 9, 12); // Friday
        LocalDate nextMon = today.with(TemporalAdjusters.next(DayOfWeek.MONDAY));
        System.out.println("Next Monday: " + nextMon);

        // custom adjuster: next working day (skip Saturday/Sunday)
        TemporalAdjuster nextWorkingDay = (temporal) -> {
            LocalDate d = LocalDate.from(temporal);
            DayOfWeek dw = d.getDayOfWeek();
            if (dw == DayOfWeek.FRIDAY) return d.plusDays(3);
            if (dw == DayOfWeek.SATURDAY) return d.plusDays(2);
            return d.plusDays(1);
        };
        System.out.println("Next work day: " + today.with(nextWorkingDay));
    }
}


Note: You can implement complex business rules here (holidays, region calendars).

Interview Q&A

Q: How to express "first business day of next month"?
A: Combine TemporalAdjusters.firstDayOfNextMonth() then apply a next-working-day adjuster.

Parameter: DateTimeFormatterBuilder — flexible parsing with optional parts

What: Build parsers that accept optional components or multiple formats.

// DT_Advanced_FormatterBuilder.java
import java.time.*;
import java.time.format.*;

public class DT_Advanced_FormatterBuilder {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        DateTimeFormatter formatter = new DateTimeFormatterBuilder()
            .appendPattern("yyyy-MM-dd")
            .optionalStart()
            .appendPattern("'T'HH:mm[:ss]")
            .optionalEnd()
            .toFormatter();

        LocalDate d1 = LocalDate.parse("2025-09-12", formatter);
        LocalDateTime dt2 = LocalDateTime.parse("2025-09-12T14:30", formatter);
        System.out.println(d1);
        System.out.println(dt2);
    }
}


Note: Good for tolerating multiple incoming formats.

Interview Q&A

Q: How to parse either yyyy-MM-dd or yyyy/MM/dd?
A: Use DateTimeFormatterBuilder with appendOptional variants or attempt parsing with multiple formatters sequentially.

Tough — DST, ambiguous/invalid times, ZoneRules
Parameter: DST ambiguous and invalid local times

What: When clocks jump forward or back, some local times are invalid (gap) or ambiguous (overlap). ZonedDateTime handles them via ZoneResolver.

// DT_Tough_DSTAmbiguity.java
import java.time.*;
import java.time.zone.*;
import java.util.*;

public class DT_Tough_DSTAmbiguity {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ZoneId london = ZoneId.of("Europe/London");
        // In 2015-03-29 clocks moved forward at 01:00 -> 01:00-02:00 skipped (example)
        LocalDateTime spring = LocalDateTime.of(2015, 3, 29, 1, 30);

        // trying to create ZonedDateTime may throw or resolve - use ofLocal/strict/lenient
        ZonedDateTime zdt = ZonedDateTime.ofLocal(spring, london, null); // will adjust to later offset
        System.out.println("Zoned (ofLocal): " + zdt);

        // Overlap example (clocks back)
        LocalDateTime fall = LocalDateTime.of(2015, 10, 25, 1, 30);
        ZoneRules rules = london.getRules();
        List<ZoneOffset> offsets = rules.getValidOffsets(fall);
        System.out.println("Valid offsets for " + fall + " : " + offsets);
        if (offsets.size() == 2) {
            // ambiguous: pick earlier or later offset
            ZonedDateTime earlier = ZonedDateTime.of(fall, london, offsets.get(0));
            ZonedDateTime later = ZonedDateTime.of(fall, london, offsets.get(1));
            System.out.println("Earlier: " + earlier + ", Later: " + later);
        }
    }
}


Note: Always consider DST transitions for scheduling tasks; use ZoneRules to inspect.

Interview Q&A

Q: What happens if you schedule a job at 2:30 AM on DST-forward day?
A: The local time may not exist; scheduler must decide: skip, run at next valid time, or run at equivalent instant in UTC—document policy.

Parameter: withZoneSameInstant vs withZoneSameLocal demonstration (subtle)

What: Convert ZonedDateTime keeping instant vs keeping wall-clock fields.

// DT_Tough_ZoneConversion.java
import java.time.*;

public class DT_Tough_ZoneConversion {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ZonedDateTime zLondon = ZonedDateTime.of(LocalDateTime.of(2025,9,12,10,0), ZoneId.of("Europe/London"));
        ZonedDateTime zNYSameInstant = zLondon.withZoneSameInstant(ZoneId.of("America/New_York"));
        ZonedDateTime zNYSameLocal = zLondon.withZoneSameLocal(ZoneId.of("America/New_York"));

        System.out.println("London: " + zLondon);
        System.out.println("NY same instant: " + zNYSameInstant);
        System.out.println("NY same local (different instant): " + zNYSameLocal);
    }
}


Note: Use sameInstant when you want same moment across zones (most common for timestamps).

Interview Q&A

Q: Which should you use when showing a scheduled meeting to a user in their timezone?
A: Use withZoneSameInstant to convert the stored instant to the user's zone for display.

MNC — enterprise patterns, storage, testing, scheduling, best practices
Parameter: Persisting times — store as Instant / UTC or OffsetDateTime / ISO-8601

What: Best practice: store points in time as UTC/epoch or ISO-8601 with offset; store local date-times only when the value is inherently local (e.g., birthday).

// DT_MNC_Persistence.java
import java.time.*;
import java.sql.*;

public class DT_MNC_Persistence {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Instant now = Instant.now();
        // Persist as epoch millis
        long epoch = now.toEpochMilli();
        System.out.println("Epoch millis: " + epoch);

        // For JDBC: convert Instant <-> java.sql.Timestamp
