        Timestamp ts = Timestamp.from(now);
        Instant back = ts.toInstant();
        System.out.println("Timestamp via JDBC: " + ts + " -> " + back);

        // Or persist ISO string
        String iso = now.toString(); // e.g., 2025-09-12T07:30:00Z
        System.out.println("ISO instant: " + iso);
    }
}


Best practice: Use DB type TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE / timestamptz when available; otherwise store epoch millis or ISO-8601 with offset.

Interview Q&A

Q: Should you store event time as LocalDateTime in DB?
A: Only if the event is truly local and zone-free (like a store opening time). For instant events, store Instant/UTC or timestamp with timezone.

Parameter: Clock — testability & injection

What: Inject Clock to make time-testable (use Clock.fixed in tests).

// DT_MNC_ClockTestability.java
import java.time.*;

public class DT_MNC_ClockTestability {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Clock systemClock = Clock.systemUTC();
        Clock fixedClock = Clock.fixed(Instant.parse("2020-01-01T10:00:00Z"), ZoneId.of("UTC"));

        System.out.println("Now (system): " + LocalDateTime.now(systemClock));
        System.out.println("Now (fixed): " + LocalDateTime.now(fixedClock));
    }
}


Best practice: Pass Clock into services rather than calling Instant.now() statically — improves testability and determinism.

Interview Q&A

Q: How would you write a unit test for time-dependent logic?
A: Inject a Clock that you control (Clock.fixed or Clock.offset) and verify logic deterministically.

Parameter: Scheduling with ScheduledExecutorService using ZonedDateTime

What: Calculate delay between now and target zoned time (consider DST) and schedule task.

// DT_MNC_ScheduleZonedTask.java
import java.time.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class DT_MNC_ScheduleZonedTask {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ScheduledExecutorService scheduler = Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(2);

        ZoneId zone = ZoneId.of("Europe/London");
        ZonedDateTime now = ZonedDateTime.now(zone);
        ZonedDateTime nextRun = now.withHour(14).withMinute(0).withSecond(0);
        if (!nextRun.isAfter(now)) nextRun = nextRun.plusDays(1);

        long delaySec = Duration.between(now.toInstant(), nextRun.toInstant()).getSeconds();
        System.out.println("Scheduling task to run in " + delaySec + " seconds at " + nextRun);

        scheduler.schedule(() -> System.out.println("Task run at " + ZonedDateTime.now(zone)), delaySec, TimeUnit.SECONDS);

        scheduler.shutdown();
    }
}


Note: Recompute next run after execution because DST may change next day; or use frameworks (Quartz) that handle calendar semantics.

Interview Q&A

Q: How to reliably schedule daily task at 2:00 AM in local time accounting DST?
A: Compute next run using ZonedDateTime each time (or use scheduler that supports cron with timezone), and schedule single-shot tasks; reschedule after run.

Parameter: Timezone DB updates & operational concerns

What: Time zone rules change (tzdb updates). Keep JVM tzdb up-to-date (JVM updates or use DstRuleUpdater or depend on OS TZDB). Be aware of DB timezone consistency across services.

Interview Q&A

Q: How to handle a timezone database change that affects scheduled jobs?
A: Deploy updated JVM/timezone DB, re-evaluate scheduled tasks and persisted local datetimes, and have incident plan to adjust schedules.

Extra: Interoperability with legacy java.util.Date and Calendar

Conversions:

// DT_Interop_DateCalendar.java
import java.time.*;
import java.util.*;

public class DT_Interop_DateCalendar {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Date legacy = new Date();
        Instant instant = legacy.toInstant();
        LocalDateTime ldt = LocalDateTime.ofInstant(instant, ZoneId.systemDefault());

        // back
        Date back = Date.from(instant);

        // Calendar
        Calendar cal = Calendar.getInstance();
        Instant fromCal = cal.toInstant();

        System.out.println("legacy->instant->ldt: " + ldt);
        System.out.println("back: " + back);
    }
}


Note: Prefer java.time, use conversion when integrating with old APIs.

Final Best Practices (cheat-sheet)

Store instants in UTC (epoch millis or ISO-8601 with Z) for persistence; convert to user zone for display.

Use java.time types — they are immutable and thread-safe.

Inject Clock for testable time-dependent code.

Handle DST & ambiguous times explicitly — inspect ZoneRules when scheduling.

Prefer Duration for time spans and Period for calendar spans.

Avoid LocalDateTime for timestamps unless the domain requires zone-less representation.

Use DateTimeFormatter (thread-safe) instead of SimpleDateFormat.

Update timezone DB when OS/JVM tzdb updates are published.

For enterprise scheduling & calendar rules, consider robust schedulers (Quartz) or stream processors.

Document your storage format (epoch ms vs ISO) and timezone assumptions.

Summary Interview Q&A (pick 6 high-value ones)

Q: Why java.time over Date/Calendar?
A: It's immutable, thread-safe, clearer API, separates concerns (LocalDate/LocalTime/Instant/ZonedDateTime), and fixes many legacy bugs.

Q: Store UTC or local?
A: Store UTC (Instant) or timestamp-with-zone. Convert to local zones for display.

Q: What is the difference between Duration and Period?
A: Duration measures time-based amounts in seconds/nanos; Period measures date-based amounts in years/months/days.

Q: How to handle DST gaps when scheduling?
A: Use ZoneRules to inspect valid offsets; decide policy (skip/run next valid/shift by amount), or use libraries that manage these policies.

Q: Are DateTimeFormatters thread-safe?
A: Yes — they are immutable and thread-safe (unlike SimpleDateFormat).

Q: How to test time-dependent code?
A: Inject Clock and use Clock.fixed/Clock.offset in tests for deterministic behavior.


Java 9 — Parameter → Expression (Basic → MNC reference slab)
1) Parameter: JPMS — Modules (module-info.java)

What: Java Platform Module System (JPMS). Breaks big monolithic classpath into modules with module-info.java describing requires / exports. Improves encapsulation and startup.

Expression (module descriptor snippet — not a single-file runnable):

// module-info.java  (placed at module root, not inside a .java main file)
module com.example.app {
    requires com.example.lib;      // depends on another module
    exports com.example.app.api;   // public API packages
}


Explanation / MNC note: Modules require building with --module-path or using javac/java module options. Migration: large codebases often start by modularizing only a few modules (use automatic modules or jlink for runtime images). Interviewers ask about split packages, encapsulation, and effects on reflection.

2) Parameter: Factory methods for collections (List.of, Set.of, Map.of, Map.ofEntries)

What: Concise immutable collection creation.

Expression:

// Java9_01_FactoryCollections.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java9_01_FactoryCollections {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> list = List.of("a", "b", "c");          // immutable list
        Set<Integer> set = Set.of(1,2,3);
        Map<String,Integer> map = Map.of("k1",1, "k2",2);    // up to 10 pairs
        Map<String,Integer> map2 = Map.ofEntries(
            Map.entry("a",1),
            Map.entry("b",2),
            Map.entry("c",3)
        ); // for >10 entries

        System.out.println(list);
        try {
            list.add("x"); // throws UnsupportedOperationException
        } catch (UnsupportedOperationException e) {
            System.out.println("immutable");
        }
    }
}


Note: Collections are immutable (no null elements for Set.of/List.of/Map.of). Interview Q: why prefer of over Arrays.asList? (immutability, better performance, smaller footprint).

3) Parameter: Private methods in interfaces

What: Helper methods inside interfaces that are not part of public API.

Expression:

// Java9_02_PrivateInterfaceMethods.java
public class Java9_02_PrivateInterfaceMethods {
    interface Greeting {
        default void hello() {
            System.out.println(greet("World"));
        }
        private static String greet(String who) {     // private helper (Java 9)
            return "Hello, " + who;
        }
    }

    static class Impl implements Greeting {}

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Impl().hello();
    }
}


Note: Private instance/private static methods let you refactor default methods safely without exposing helpers.

4) Parameter: JShell (REPL) — interactive evaluation

What: Read–Eval–Print Loop for Java; great for quick experiments and learning.

Expression (usage hint):

$ jshell
jshell> int x = 5;
x ==> 5
jshell> List.of("a","b").forEach(System.out::println);


Note: JShell runs interactively (not a .java class). Interviewers like to see familiarity; it’s great for prototyping.

5) Parameter: ProcessHandle & enhanced Process API

What: Inspect and manage OS processes (ps-like capabilities) and improved Process API.

Expression:

// Java9_03_ProcessHandleDemo.java
import java.util.*;
public class Java9_03_ProcessHandleDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ProcessHandle current = ProcessHandle.current();
        System.out.println("PID: " + current.pid());
        current.info().command().ifPresent(cmd -> System.out.println("Command: " + cmd));

        // List children (if any)
        current.children().forEach(ph -> System.out.println("Child pid: " + ph.pid()));
    }
}


Note: Useful for tooling, monitoring. ProcessHandle allows getting info(), pid(), descendants(), and destroying processes.

6) Parameter: Streams API additions — takeWhile, dropWhile, and overloaded iterate

What: New short-circuiting stream ops and better iterate.

Expression:

// Java9_04_StreamAdditions.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java9_04_StreamAdditions {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> nums = Arrays.asList(2,4,6,7,8,10);

        List<Integer> taken = nums.stream().takeWhile(n -> n % 2 == 0).collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println("takeWhile even prefix: " + taken); // [2,4,6]

        List<Integer> dropped = nums.stream().dropWhile(n -> n % 2 == 0).collect(Collectors.toList());
        System.out.println("dropWhile even prefix: " + dropped); // [7,8,10]

        // iterate with predicate (Java 9)
        Stream<Integer> s = Stream.iterate(1, n -> n <= 10, n -> n + 1);
        System.out.println(s.collect(Collectors.toList())); // 1..10
    }
}


Note: takeWhile/dropWhile are useful for ordered streams and for early termination. Interview Q: behavior on unordered streams — takeWhile/dropWhile are only meaningful for ordered streams.

7) Parameter: Optional improvements (Optional.or, ifPresentOrElse?`)

What: Java 9 added Optional.or (supplier-based fallback) and a few helper improvements (some methods like ifPresentOrElse were later; careful).

Expression (Optional.or):

// Java9_05_OptionalOr.java
import java.util.*;

public class Java9_05_OptionalOr {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Optional<String> a = Optional.empty();
        Optional<String> b = a.or(() -> Optional.of("fallback")); // Java 9: or(Supplier)
        System.out.println(b.get()); // fallback
    }
}


Note: Use or to lazily supply a fallback Optional. (Be aware which convenience methods exist in which release — e.g., ifPresentOrElse came in Java 9? Actually ifPresentOrElse arrived in Java 9 as well — you can use it similarly.)

8) Parameter: StackWalker — better stack walking API

What: Replace slow Thread.getStackTrace() or Throwable stack traces with a flexible API that supports lazy access and efficient walking.

Expression:

// Java9_06_StackWalkerDemo.java
import java.lang.StackWalker;
public class Java9_06_StackWalkerDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        StackWalker walker = StackWalker.getInstance(StackWalker.Option.RETAIN_CLASS_REFERENCE);
        walker.forEach(frame -> System.out.println(frame.getClassName() + "#" + frame.getMethodName() + ":" + frame.getLineNumber()));
    }
}


Note: StackWalker supports filters, depths, and avoids creating full stack trace arrays; good for logging/tracing.

9) Parameter: Flow API (Reactive Streams) — basic publisher/subscriber

What: Standard java.util.concurrent.Flow interfaces (Publisher, Subscriber, Subscription, Processor) to support reactive streams interoperability.

Expression (minimal conceptual snippet):

// Java9_07_FlowSimple.java
import java.util.concurrent.Flow;
import java.util.concurrent.SubmissionPublisher;

public class Java9_07_FlowSimple {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        SubmissionPublisher<String> publisher = new SubmissionPublisher<>();
        Flow.Subscriber<String> subscriber = new Flow.Subscriber<String>() {
            private Flow.Subscription sub;
            public void onSubscribe(Flow.Subscription subscription) { this.sub = subscription; subscription.request(1); }
            public void onNext(String item) { System.out.println("Got " + item); sub.request(1); }
            public void onError(Throwable t) { t.printStackTrace(); }
            public void onComplete() { System.out.println("Done"); }
        };
        publisher.subscribe(subscriber);
        publisher.submit("hello");
        publisher.submit("world");
        publisher.close();
        Thread.sleep(100); // wait for async delivery
    }
}


Note: SubmissionPublisher helps publish items; Flow interfaces are low-level building blocks for reactive libraries (RxJava, Reactor).

10) Parameter: Process API improvements — ProcessHandle.Info and children

(covered above in #5 but can note Process API improvements like PID, children, and onExit returning CompletableFuture)

Expression (onExit):

// Java9_08_ProcessOnExit.java
import java.util.concurrent.*;
public class Java9_08_ProcessOnExit {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        ProcessBuilder pb = new ProcessBuilder("echo", "hello");
        Process p = pb.start();
        // onExit returns CompletableFuture<Process>
        p.onExit().thenAccept(proc -> System.out.println("Process exited with: " + proc.exitValue())).get();
    }
}


Note: onExit() useful to compose process lifecycle into async flows.

11) Parameter: Compact Strings (JEP 254) — implementation detail

What: JVM optimization: stores String internally as byte array + coder (latin1/utf16) to save memory; not directly visible as API change but improved runtime memory use.

Expression: No direct API; mention in enterprise considerations.

MNC note: Memory footprint reduced for many applications; good to mention in performance discussions.

12) Parameter: jlink — custom runtime images

What: Build a trimmed runtime image containing only required modules (smaller JVMs for deployment).

Expression (usage hint, not runnable in .java):

$ jlink --module-path jmods --add-modules com.example.app --output my-image


Note: Useful for creating minimal container-friendly images and reducing attack surface.

Java 9 — Quick Interview Q&A (high-value)

Q: What problems did JPMS solve?
A: Strong encapsulation of internals, reliable configuration (modules replace fragile classpath), smaller runtime images, clearer API boundaries.

Q: What pitfalls when modularizing a large app?
A: Split packages, reflection access (you may need opens/exports), third-party libraries may not be modular (use automatic modules), migration complexity.

Q: When to use List.of(...) vs new ArrayList<>(...)?
A: List.of for small immutable fixed data; ArrayList if you need mutability or null elements.

Q: What’s Stream.takeWhile semantics?
A: It takes elements while predicate is true — it stops at the first false; meaningful only for ordered streams.


Java 10 — Key Parameters (features) → Expressions (examples + notes)
1) Parameter: Local-variable type inference (var)

What: You can use var for local variable declarations so the compiler infers the type. It’s not dynamic typing — the variable still has a static type inferred at compile time. var is allowed only for local variables (including index variables of enhanced for), try-with-resources locals, and local for-loop variables. It is not allowed for fields, method parameters, or return types.

Expression (runnable):

// Java10_VarBasics.java
import java.util.*;
public class Java10_VarBasics {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // var infers type ArrayList<String>
        var list = new ArrayList<String>();
        list.add("apple");
        list.add("banana");

        // var in enhanced for (type inferred as String)
        for (var s : list) {
            System.out.println(s.toUpperCase());
        }

        // var with map stream result (inferred as Map<String, Integer>)
        var counts = new HashMap<String, Integer>();
        counts.put("a", 1);
        counts.put("b", 2);
        System.out.println("counts class: " + counts.getClass().getName());

        // var in try-with-resources (in Java 10)
        try (var scan = new java.util.Scanner("hello world")) {
            while (scan.hasNext()) {
                var tok = scan.next();
                System.out.println("tok:" + tok);
            }
        }
    }
}


Notes / best practices

Use var to reduce verbosity where the right-hand side clearly indicates the type.

Avoid var when it reduces readability (e.g., var x = doSomething(); where the returned type is not obvious).

var does not make Java dynamically typed — everything remains statically typed.

Interview Q&A

Q: Can you use var for method parameters or fields? — A: No. var is only for local variable declarations (including try-with-resources and index variables).

Q: Does var change type inference semantics at runtime? — A: No — inference happens at compile-time; bytecode has concrete types.

2) Parameter: Collection factory/immutability helpers (minor API additions)

What: Java 9 introduced List.of/Set.of/Map.of. Java 10 added complementary API improvements in the standard library (and later Java versions added copyOf and Collectors.toUnmodifiableList family). Regardless of exact method names across releases, the takeaway is: prefer the new factory methods for small immutable collections and prefer unmodifiable collectors when you want an immutable result from a stream.

Expression (runnable and portable):

// Java10_CollectionsImmutability.java
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class Java10_CollectionsImmutability {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // (List.of is Java 9+)
        List<String> fixed = List.of("a", "b", "c"); // immutable
        System.out.println("fixed: " + fixed);

        // From a stream — prefer producing an unmodifiable list at the end
        List<String> names = Stream.of("alice","bob","carol")
                                    .collect(Collectors.collectingAndThen(Collectors.toList(), Collections::unmodifiableList));
        System.out.println("names (unmodifiable): " + names);
        try {
            names.add("d"); // throws UnsupportedOperationException
        } catch (UnsupportedOperationException ex) {
            System.out.println("list is unmodifiable");
        }
    }
}


Notes

Small immutable factory methods (List.of, Set.of, Map.of) are concise and more memory-efficient for small fixed collections.

For stream pipelines you can use Collectors.collectingAndThen to return an unmodifiable collection. (Later JDKs add Collectors.toUnmodifiableList() etc., but the pattern above is portable.)

Interview Q&A

Q: Why choose immutable collections? — A: Immutability prevents accidental mutation, is safer for sharing between threads, and can make reasoning about code simpler.

3) Parameter: Application Class-Data Sharing (AppCDS)

What: AppCDS helps reduce startup time and memory footprint by archiving class metadata (class definitions and metadata used by the Java runtime) into a shared archive that the JVM can load at startup. Java 10 extended CDS to allow application classes (not just the JDK classes) to be included in the archive — useful for server/CLI apps with many classes.

Expression (concept / command-line recipe; not Java source)

# 1) Create a class list (run your app to generate the list)
$ java -Xshare:off -XX:DumpLoadedClassList=classes.lst -cp myapp.jar my.app.Main

# 2) Create the shared archive
$ java -Xshare:dump -XX:SharedClassListFile=classes.lst -XX:SharedArchiveFile=myapp.jsa -cp myapp.jar

# 3) Run app using the shared archive
$ java -XX:SharedArchiveFile=myapp.jsa -cp myapp.jar my.app.Main


Notes / best practices

AppCDS is configured at runtime; the commands above are typical (exact flags may vary by JDK patch level).

Benefits: faster startup, smaller memory footprint when multiple JVMs share the archive.

MNC usage: generate per-version archives as part of your build/release pipeline and use them in containers to speed image startup.

Interview Q&A

Q: What is the main user benefit of AppCDS? — A: Lower startup time and reduced memory usage by sharing class metadata across JVM instances.

Q: When would you not use AppCDS? — A: During rapid development or when the application changes frequently; best used in production images created during build/release.

4) Parameter: Performance / runtime ergonomics (minor improvements)

What: Java 10 included multiple runtime improvements (GC tuning, better performance of certain operations, project-specific JEPs). These are implementation and JVM config matters rather than language changes.

Expression (advice, not code)

Monitor with jcmd, jstat, and Flight Recorder (or your APM).

Consider using AppCDS and JIT-tier profiling for production: collect profiles during warm runs and apply them during startup.

Notes

Nothing to change in application source; these are operational improvements you leverage via JVM options and profiling.

5) Parameter: Local developer tooling — jlink improvements & packaging sensibilities

What: Java 9 introduced jlink and modular runtime images; Java 10 continued building on a smaller, faster release cadence. jlink remains a way to produce minimal run-time images tailored to your modules.

Expression (usage hint):

# create a runtime image containing only required modules (example)
$ jlink --module-path jmods:mods --add-modules com.example.app --output runtime-image


Notes: jlink is especially useful for building small docker images and minimizing attack surface. For Java 10, incremental improvements helped the adoption story.

Quick Java 10 Cheatsheet (summary)

var (local-variable type inference) is the star language feature — reduces boilerplate for obvious local declarations.

AppCDS (Application Class-Data Sharing) allows you to create archives for faster startup & lower memory.

Small API / runtime ergonomics improvements; continue to use List.of, Map.of, and prefer unmodifiable collections where appropriate.

Java 10 set the tone for the faster release cadence and small, focused improvements — many larger features were introduced in later JDKs.


ice — here are 20 small var exercises (Java 10 style) you can use to practice good and bad uses of var.
Each item shows:

Parameter (what to practice),

Expression (a compact runnable snippet or a commented bad example),

Verdict: Good / Bad / Tricky, and

Why / tip.

Run the “Good” snippets with a Java 10+ compiler. The “Bad” snippets are shown commented out — they illustrate compile-time errors or surprising inference behavior.

1 — Obvious concrete type (good)

Parameter: Use var when the RHS clearly reveals the type.

// VarEx01_ConcreteType.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class VarEx01 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var list = new ArrayList<String>(); // inferred ArrayList<String>
        list.add("apple");
        System.out.println(list.get(0).toUpperCase());
    }
}


Why: Readable and reduces redundant generics on RHS.

2 — Enhanced for with var (good)

Parameter: Use var in enhanced for where element type is clear.

// VarEx02_ForEach.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class VarEx02 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var names = List.of("anna","bob");
        for (var name : names) { // inferred as String
            System.out.println(name.length());
        }
    }
}


Why: Cleaner loop variable declaration.

3 — No initializer (bad — won't compile)

Parameter: var requires an initializer.

// VarEx03_NoInitializer.java (Bad - compile error)
// public class VarEx03 {
//     public static void main(String[] args) {
//         var x; // ERROR: cannot use 'var' on variable without initializer
//         x = 10;
//     }
// }


Why: Compiler must infer type at declaration time.

4 — var = null (bad — won't compile)

Parameter: Cannot infer type from null; initializer cannot be null.

// VarEx04_NullInit.java (Bad - compile error)
// public class VarEx04 {
//     public static void main(String[] args) {
//         var x = null; // ERROR: cannot infer type for local variable initialized to null
//     }
// }


Why: null has no type information.

5 — Multiple declarators (bad — won't compile)

Parameter: You cannot declare multiple variables in one statement with var.

// VarEx05_MultiDecl.java (Bad - compile error)
// public class VarEx05 {
//     public static void main(String[] args) {
//         var a = 1, b = 2; // ERROR: 'var' is not allowed in a compound declaration
//     }
// }


Why: Ambiguous typing; var must be used in a single-declaration form.

6 — Try-with-resources (good)

Parameter: var allowed in try-with-resources (since Java 10).

// VarEx06_TryWithResources.java (Good)
import java.io.*;
public class VarEx06 {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        try (var br = new BufferedReader(new StringReader("hello\nworld"))) {
            System.out.println(br.readLine());
        }
    }
}


Why: Reduces boilerplate for local resource variables.

7 — Diamond operator + var (good)

Parameter: Combine var with diamond <> for clean initialization.

// VarEx07_Diamond.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class VarEx07 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var map = new HashMap<String, Integer>(); // inferred HashMap<String,Integer>
        map.put("a", 1);
        System.out.println(map);
    }
}


Why: Avoid repetition of generics types.

8 — Collections.emptyList() pitfall (tricky)

Parameter: var + generic factory returning a raw/empty type — surprising inference.

// VarEx08_EmptyListPitfall.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;

public class VarEx08 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var list = Collections.emptyList(); // inferred List<Object> (no target type)
        // list.add("x"); // runtime: UnsupportedOperationException (emptyList is immutable)
        System.out.println(list.getClass() + " -> " + list);
    }
}


Why: emptyList() returns an empty generic list; without target type, compiler infers List<Object>. Prefer List.of() or explicit type: List<String> list = Collections.emptyList();

9 — Lambda literal RHS (bad in Java 10 — won't compile)

Parameter: var cannot be used to declare a variable initialized to a lambda (no target type).

// VarEx09_LambdaInit.java (Bad - compile error)
// public class VarEx09 {
//     public static void main(String[] args) {
//         var f = x -> x + 1; // ERROR: lambda expression needs an explicit target-type
//     }
// }


Why: Lambdas need a target functional interface type; use Function<Integer,Integer> f = x -> x+1; or cast: var f = (Function<Integer,Integer>) (x -> x+1); (but explicit cast required).

10 — Cast on RHS (good)

Parameter: You can use var with an explicit cast on RHS to provide a target type.

// VarEx10_CastRHS.java (Good)
import java.util.function.*;
public class VarEx10 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var f = (Function<String,Integer>) (s -> s.length()); // explicit cast gives target type
        System.out.println(f.apply("abc"));
    }
}


Why: Cast gives compiler the exact type to infer.

11 — Field/Method parameter use (bad — won't compile)

Parameter: var is not allowed for class fields or method parameters/returns.

// VarEx11_FieldParam.java (Bad - compile error)
// public class VarEx11 {
//     var field = 10; // ERROR: 'var' is not allowed here (only local variables)
//     public void m(var x) {} // ERROR: cannot use 'var' as parameter type
// }


Why: var is only for local variable declarations.

12 — Anonymous inner class (tricky/good)

Parameter: var with an anonymous class — inferred anonymous type (not interface).

// VarEx12_Anonymous.java (Tricky/Good)
public class VarEx12 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var r = new Runnable() { public void run() { System.out.println("run"); } };
        r.run(); // r is the anonymous class type (not just Runnable)
    }
}


Why: The variable's static type is the anonymous class; this matters if you try to assign it to a Runnable variable later — it's allowed but the static type is the specific anonymous type.

13 — Array initializer shorthand (bad — won't compile)

Parameter: var cannot be used with array initializer without new context.

// VarEx13_ArrayInit.java (Bad - compile error)
// public class VarEx13 {
//     public static void main(String[] args) {
//         var a = {1,2,3}; // ERROR: array initializer needs an explicit type or 'new int[]'
//     }
// }


Correct:

// Good:
var a = new int[] {1,2,3};


Why: Array initializers need explicit type or new.

14 — Stream result (good)

Parameter: var with stream terminal result (primitive or collection).

// VarEx14_StreamResult.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;
public class VarEx14 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var sum = IntStream.rangeClosed(1,5).sum(); // int
        var list = Stream.of("a","b").collect(Collectors.toList()); // List<String>
        System.out.println("sum=" + sum + " list=" + list);
    }
}


Why: Clear RHS; type inference obvious.

15 — Ternary with different types (tricky)

Parameter: var + ternary operator where branches have different types — compiler chooses least upper bound.

// VarEx15_Ternary.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class VarEx15 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var x = true ? new ArrayList<String>() : new LinkedList<String>(); // inferred as List<String>
        System.out.println(x.getClass());
    }
}


But:

// Tricky variant:
var y = true ? Collections.emptyList() : new ArrayList<String>();
// y inferred as List<Object> because emptyList() has no contextual type


Why: Be careful — if one branch loses generic information (emptyList), overall inferred type can be surprising.

16 — var and numeric literal assignment (good/tricky)

Parameter: var fixes a type at declaration time — mixing numeric literals later fails.

// VarEx16_NumericTypes.java (Tricky)
public class VarEx16 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var i = 1; // inferred as int
        // i = 1L; // ERROR: incompatible types: possible lossy conversion from long to int
        System.out.println(((Object)i).getClass().getName()); // java.lang.Integer
    }
}


Why: var infers specific primitive wrapper type for boxed contexts. The variable type is fixed.

17 — var for loop index (good)

Parameter: var in enhanced for or as loop index in C-style? (var allowed in enhanced for, not in traditional for-declaration with multiple init with vars?) You can use var i = 0 in for? Yes var can be used in local for (var i = 0; ... ) valid.

// VarEx17_ForIndex.java (Good)
public class VarEx17 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(i);
        }
    }
}


Why: var is allowed for local variables including index declarations.

18 — var hides generics on API boundaries (bad/tricky)

Parameter: Using var makes the static type the exact RHS type; when you need an interface type for polymorphism, prefer explicit typing.

// VarEx18_APIType.java (Tricky/Bad)
import java.util.*;
public class VarEx18 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var stack = new ArrayDeque<String>(); // inferred ArrayDeque<String>
        // Suppose API expects Deque<String> param: methodThatAcceptsDeque(stack) works due to subtype.
        // But if you later refactor to assign a LinkedList, var would pick LinkedList and some operations might differ in performance.
        System.out.println(stack.getClass());
    }
}


Why: When the static type matters (exposing less-specific interfaces), be explicit: Deque<String> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();

19 — var with annotations? (bad — not allowed on declaration)

Parameter: You cannot use var where annotations are required on the type variable (rare). E.g., local variable with @Nonnull? Java doesn't support annotations on inferred var target type in Java 10. (Some contexts in later Java may allow annotation on var?). Show example:

// VarEx19_AnnotatedVar.java (Bad/tricky)
// import javax.annotation.*;
// public class VarEx19 {
//     public static void main(String[] args) {
//         @Nonnull var s = "hello"; // ERROR in Java 10: annotations on 'var' are not supported like this
//     }
// }


Why: If you need annotations on the type use explicit type.

(Note: Some later JDK versions may relax type annotations in var; avoid in Java10.)

20 — Refactoring exercise (MNC-style good use)

Parameter: When refactoring long declarations, var reduces noise — but keep readability. Show before/after.

Before (explicit):

// VarEx20_BeforeAfter.java (Good practice)
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;
public class VarEx20 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Map<String, List<Integer>>> complex =
            new ArrayList<Map<String, List<Integer>>>();
        // ... fill ...
        System.out.println("Before type explicit: " + complex.getClass());
    }
}


After (use var where RHS is clear):

// After
import java.util.*;
public class VarEx20After {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var complex = new ArrayList<Map<String, List<Integer>>>(); // still readable
        System.out.println("After var: " + complex.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Good when RHS reveals the full type; avoids long repetitive declarations—but avoid var complex = someMethod() if the method return type is not obvious.

Quick rules checklist (summary)

var is only for local variables (including try-with-resources and loop indices).

Must have an initializer — compiler needs RHS to infer type.

Cannot be null initializer or compound declarations (var a=1, b=2).

Avoid var when reading the code later would be unclear (e.g., var obj = someMethod() where return type is not obvious).

Be careful with APIs that return generic/empty values (e.g., Collections.emptyList()): var can lead to List<Object> inference.

var does not change static typing — the variable gets a concrete static type inferred once at compile time.


Copy any runnable snippet into its own .java file and run on Java 10+. For the “Bad” cases the commented code demonstrates compile-time surprises or pitfalls.

1) Empty collection factory without type target
// Ex01_EmptyListInference.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex01 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var list = Collections.emptyList(); // inferred List<Object>
        System.out.println(list.getClass() + " -> " + list);
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — Collections.emptyList() loses generic type without target; prefer List.<String>of() or explicit typing.

2) List.of() preserves generic type
// Ex02_ListOfGood.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex02 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var list = List.of("a", "b"); // List<String>
        System.out.println(list.get(0).toUpperCase());
    }
}


Verdict: Good — List.of retains generic type info.

3) Ternary with same generic type branches
// Ex03_TernarySameGenerics.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex03 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var x = true ? new ArrayList<String>() : new LinkedList<String>(); // inferred List<String>
        System.out.println(x.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Good — both branches share generic type → nice inference.

4) Ternary with emptyList() + concrete generic (surprising)
// Ex04_TernaryEmptyList.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex04 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var x = true ? Collections.emptyList() : new ArrayList<String>(); // inferred List<Object>
        System.out.println(x.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — emptyList() forces raw/Object inference; avoid mixing.

5) Diamond on RHS with var (good)
// Ex05_VarDiamond.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex05 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var m = new HashMap<String, Integer>(); // HashMap<String,Integer>
        m.put("a", 1);
        System.out.println(m);
    }
}


Verdict: Good — diamond + var reduces verbosity.

6) Var with raw type RHS (bad/tricky)
// Ex06_RawType.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex06 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // var x = (List) new ArrayList<String>(); // compiles, but x inferred as List (raw) -> unsafe
        // x.add(123); // possible at compile-time due to raw type
        List raw = new ArrayList<String>();
        var x = raw; // inferred as List (raw) — avoid
        System.out.println(x.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — raw types propagate and remove generics safety.

7) Optional.empty() inference pitfall
// Ex07_OptionalEmpty.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex07 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var o = Optional.empty(); // Optional<Object>
        // o.ifPresent(s -> System.out.println(s)); // fine, but typed to Object
        Optional<String> os = Optional.empty(); // preferred explicit
        System.out.println(o.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — Optional.empty() without a target becomes Optional<Object>.

8) Lambda RHS needs target type (bad)
// Ex08_LambdaNoTarget.java (Bad)
// import java.util.function.*;
// public class Ex08 {
//     public static void main(String[] args) {
//         var f = x -> x + 1; // ERROR: lambda expression needs an explicit target-type
//     }
// }


Verdict: Bad — use explicit functional interface type or cast.

9) Cast to provide target for lambda (good)
// Ex09_LambdaWithCast.java (Good)
import java.util.function.*;
public class Ex09 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var f = (Function<Integer,Integer>)(x -> x + 1);
        System.out.println(f.apply(10));
    }
}


Verdict: Good — explicit cast supplies target type for var inference.

10) Nested generics and var inference
// Ex10_NestedGenerics.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex10 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var complex = new ArrayList<Map<String, List<Integer>>>();
        System.out.println(complex.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Good — RHS explicit nested generics are inferred.

11) Collectors.toList() with var — type preserved
// Ex11_CollectorsToList.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;
public class Ex11 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var list = Stream.of("a","b").collect(Collectors.toList()); // List<String>
        System.out.println(list.get(0).toUpperCase());
    }
}


Verdict: Good — collectors capture generic type.

12) Collectors.toMap() and var with merge -> Map<K,V>
// Ex12_ToMapVar.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;
public class Ex12 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var map = Stream.of("apple","ant","banana")
                .collect(Collectors.toMap(s -> s.charAt(0), s -> s, (a,b)-> a + "|" + b));
        System.out.println(map);
    }
}


Verdict: Good — map generics inferred from collector.

13) Ternary mixing List<String> and List<Object>
// Ex13_TernaryDifferentGenericBranches.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex13 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var a = true ? Arrays.asList("x") : Arrays.<Object>asList(); // inferred List<Object>
        System.out.println(a.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — explicit type hint needed to preserve String type.

14) Diamond + anonymous subclass (type becomes anonymous class)
// Ex14_AnonymousDiamond.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex14 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var obj = new ArrayList<String>() { public String toString(){ return "anon"; } }; // anonymous class type
        System.out.println(obj.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — static type is anonymous class; be careful when you expect a List interface.

15) Var with overloaded method resolution
// Ex15_OverloadAmbiguity.java (Tricky)
public class Ex15 {
    static void m(Number n) { System.out.println("Number"); }
    static void m(Integer i) { System.out.println("Integer"); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var v = 10; // int -> inferred int (primitive), but auto-boxing may pick Integer in overload resolution
        m(v); // calls m(Integer) due to boxing to Integer (compile-time resolution)
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — var infers primitive int, but overload resolution uses boxing; be mindful.

16) var with Map.of entries (immutable)
// Ex16_MapOf.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex16 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var m = Map.of("a", 1, "b", 2); // Map<String,Integer>
        System.out.println(m);
    }
}


Verdict: Good — Map.of returns typed immutable map.

17) Mixed numeric ternary causing widening to Number
// Ex17_TernaryNumeric.java (Tricky)
public class Ex17 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var x = true ? 1 : 2L; // inferred as Number (long promoted), boxed to Long? Actually primitive promoted to long, then boxed to Long -> Number
        System.out.println(x.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — numeric literals with different types produce a common numeric type (long) and then boxed; be explicit when needed.

18) var with method returning raw type
// Ex18_MethodRawReturn.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex18 {
    static List rawReturn(){ return new ArrayList<String>(); }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var x = rawReturn(); // inferred raw List -> no generics safety
        // x.add(123); // allowed at compile-time due to raw type
        System.out.println(x.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — raw-returning methods defeat generic safety.

19) var with wildcard generics (List<?>)
// Ex19_Wildcard.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex19 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> s = List.of("a","b");
        var w = (List<?>) s; // inferred List<?>
        System.out.println(w.get(0));
    }
}


Verdict: Good — you can capture wildcard by casting; note write operations are restricted.

20) var and anonymous Runnable assigned to functional interface
// Ex20_AnonRunnable.java (Tricky)
public class Ex20 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var r = new Runnable() { public void run(){ System.out.println("run"); } }; // type is anonymous class
        r.run();
        // Runnable rr = r; // allowed because anonymous extends Runnable
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — static type is the anonymous class (not just Runnable).

21) var and method reference without context (bad)
// Ex21_MethodRefNoTarget.java (Bad)
// public class Ex21 {
//     public static void main(String[] args) {
//         var x = System.out::println; // ERROR: method reference needs a target type
//     }
// }


Verdict: Bad — method refs need a target functional interface type.

22) var with explicit cast to generic type (good)
// Ex22_CastToGeneric.java (Good)
import java.util.function.*;
public class Ex22 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var f = (Function<String,Integer>) (s -> s.length());
        System.out.println(f.apply("abc"));
    }
}


Verdict: Good — explicit cast helps for lambdas/method refs.

23) var with nested ternary mixing generics -> common supertype
// Ex23_NestedTernary.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex23 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var x = true ? Arrays.asList("a") : Collections.singletonList(1); // List<? extends Serializable> or List<Object> depending
        System.out.println(x.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — inference chooses common supertype; results may be less specific.

24) var with generic method inference
// Ex24_GenericMethod.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex24 {
    static <T> List<T> ofOne(T t){ return Collections.singletonList(t); }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var list = ofOne("x"); // List<String>
        System.out.println(list.get(0).toUpperCase());
    }
}


Verdict: Good — generic method inference works with var.

25) var and chained method calls (type clarity)
// Ex25_ChainedCalls.java (Good)
import java.util.stream.*;
import java.util.*;
public class Ex25 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var map = Stream.of("a","b").collect(Collectors.groupingBy(s -> s.charAt(0)));
        System.out.println(map);
    }
}


Verdict: Good — RHS pipeline makes the type clear.

26) var with method overload choosing ambiguity
// Ex26_OverloadAmbiguous.java (Tricky)
public class Ex26 {
    static void foo(Integer i){ System.out.println("Integer"); }
    static void foo(Long l){ System.out.println("Long"); }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var v = 1; // int
        // foo(v); // compiles -> selects foo(Integer) due to boxing to Integer; surprising sometimes
        foo(v); // prints Integer
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — boxing rules apply; be aware how var interacts with overload resolution.

27) var with wildcard capture from method
// Ex27_WildcardMethodReturn.java (Tricky)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex27 {
    static List<?> wildcard(){ return List.of("x"); }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var x = wildcard(); // List<?>
        // x.add("y"); // compile error — cannot add to List<?>
        System.out.println(x.get(0));
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — wildcard prevents mutation.

28) var with multi-line initializer (good)
// Ex28_MultiLineRHS.java (Good)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex28 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var map = new HashMap<String, List<Integer>>() {{
            put("a", Arrays.asList(1,2));
        }};
        System.out.println(map);
    }
}


Verdict: Good — type inference works even for complex RHS; but this example creates an anonymous subclass (double-brace), so be cautious.

29) var and generic array creation (bad)
// Ex29_GenericArray.java (Bad)
// import java.util.*;
// public class Ex29 {
//     public static void main(String[] args) {
//         var arr = new ArrayList<String>[1]; // ERROR: generic array creation not allowed
//     }
// }


Verdict: Bad — generic arrays are not allowed; use List<String>[] via unsafe cast or use lists-of-lists.

30) var hiding return type in API usage (MNC caution)
// Ex30_APIHide.java (Tricky/Bad if abused)
import java.util.*;
public class Ex30 {
    public static List<String> expensiveCompute() { return new ArrayList<>(); }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var res = expensiveCompute(); // var inferred as List<String>
        // Later, a refactor changes expensiveCompute() to return Collection<String> — var inference changes type silently
        System.out.println(res.getClass());
    }
}


Verdict: Tricky — using var at call sites can hide API changes; favor explicit type in public-facing code (or ensure method return types are stable).

Quick guidance (one-paragraph)

Use var when the right-hand side clearly communicates the static type (constructor, factory method with generics, stream collectors, Map.of, List.of, etc.).

Avoid var when the RHS is ambiguous (Collections.empty*(), Optional.empty(), raw types, method overloads, lambdas/method references without cast).

Be especially cautious with the ternary operator and mixed-branch generics — the compiler finds a common supertype which may be Object or Number, losing type specificity.

When code readability, API stability, or type clarity matters (public method bodies, long-term code maintenance), prefer explicit types.


1) Parameter: record (compact immutable data carrier)

What: Concise syntax for immutable data classes with generated equals/hashCode/toString and accessors.

// Java17_RecordDemo.java
public class Java17_RecordDemo {
    // record declaration (Java 16+; works on Java 17)
    public record Person(String name, int age) { }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Person p = new Person("Alice", 30);
        System.out.println(p.name());   // accessor
        System.out.println(p);          // pretty toString auto-generated
    }
}


Explanation: use record for DTOs/immutable value objects. Records are final and automatically implement equals/hashCode.
Interview tip: records are not just “shorthand POJOs” — they have special semantics (canonical constructor, component-based equals/hashCode).

2) Parameter: Record with compact constructor + validation

What: Add validation or normalization in a compact canonical constructor.

// Java17_RecordValidation.java
public class Java17_RecordValidation {
    public record Email(String localPart, String domain) {
        public Email {
            if (localPart == null || domain == null) throw new IllegalArgumentException("parts required");
            if (!domain.contains(".")) throw new IllegalArgumentException("invalid domain");
            // normalize
            localPart = localPart.toLowerCase();
            domain = domain.toLowerCase();
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Email e = new Email("John.Doe", "Example.COM");
        System.out.println(e); // Email[localPart=john.doe, domain=example.com]
    }
}


Explanation: the compact constructor parameter names are the record component names; assign normalized values if needed.
Interview tip: canonical constructor body parameters are implicitly assigned back to components — you can assign to them.

3) Parameter: sealed classes / interfaces

What: Restrict which classes can extend/implement a type — good for closed hierarchies and exhaustive reasoning.

// Java17_SealedDemo.java
public class Java17_SealedDemo {
    // sealed interface; only permitted implementations may extend it
    public sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Point {}

    public static final class Circle implements Shape {
        public final double r;
        public Circle(double r) { this.r = r; }
    }

    public static final class Rectangle implements Shape {
        public final double w, h;
        public Rectangle(double w, double h) { this.w = w; this.h = h; }
    }

    public static final class Point implements Shape {
        public final double x, y;
        public Point(double x, double y) { this.x = x; this.y = y; }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s = new Circle(2.0);
        System.out.println(s.getClass().getSimpleName()); // Circle
    }
}


Explanation: sealed + permits gives compiler knowledge of the closed set of subtypes — enables exhaustive switch/analysis and safer API evolution.
Interview tip: permitted types must be in the same module (or same package for non-modular code) and be declared final, sealed, or non-sealed.

4) Parameter: Pattern matching for instanceof (concise type test + bind)

What: Do instanceof + cast in one expression.

// Java17_PatternInstanceof.java
public class Java17_PatternInstanceof {
    static String describe(Object o) {
        if (o instanceof String s) {           // pattern binding
            return "String of length " + s.length();
        } else if (o instanceof Integer i) {
            return "Integer " + i;
        } else {
            return "Other: " + o;
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(describe("hello"));
        System.out.println(describe(123));
    }
}


Explanation: the variable (e.g., s) is in scope inside the if block — avoids explicit casts.
Interview tip: pattern variable is implicitly final (effectively final), and scoping rules apply (not visible outside the if).

5) Parameter: Enhanced switch expression (arrow labels, yield)

What: Use switch as an expression that returns a value; supports arrow labels and yield for multi-statement results.

// Java17_SwitchExpression.java
public class Java17_SwitchExpression {
    static String dayType(int dayOfWeek) {
        return switch (dayOfWeek) {
            case 1, 7 -> "Weekend";               // arrow form
            case 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 -> "Weekday";
            default -> {
                System.out.println("Unknown day: " + dayOfWeek);
                yield "Unknown";                 // yield value from block
            }
        };
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(dayType(1)); // Weekend
        System.out.println(dayType(99)); // Unknown
    }
}


Explanation: switch as an expression reduces boilerplate and improves exhaustiveness checks for enums/sealed types.
Interview tip: yield is used when a case block has multiple statements and must return a value.

6) Parameter: Sealed types + exhaustive switch (ADT-style)

What: Combine sealed types and switch expressions to get compile-time exhaustiveness checks.

// Java17_ExprEval.java
public class Java17_ExprEval {
    public sealed interface Expr permits Val, Add {}
    public static record Val(int v) implements Expr {}
    public static record Add(Expr left, Expr right) implements Expr {}

    static int eval(Expr e) {
        return switch (e) {
            case Val v -> v.v();
            case Add a -> eval(a.left()) + eval(a.right());
        };
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Expr expr = new Add(new Val(2), new Val(3));
        System.out.println("eval: " + eval(expr)); // 5
    }
}


Explanation: because Expr is sealed and we covered all permitted subtypes, the switch is exhaustive — no default needed.
Interview tip: sealed + switch expressions are a concise way to model algebraic data types in Java.

7) Parameter: Text blocks (multi-line string literals)

What: Cleaner multi-line string literals and embedded JSON/SQL/HTML.

// Java17_TextBlock.java
public class Java17_TextBlock {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String json = """
            {
              "name": "Alice",
              "age": 30
            }
            """;
        System.out.println(json);
    }
}


Explanation: text blocks preserve indentation more cleanly and avoid escaping many quotes/newlines.
Interview tip: trim/formatting helpers exist (stripIndent, translateEscapes) on String.

8) Parameter: Helpful NullPointerExceptions (diagnostics)

What: Helpful NPE messages (showing exactly which expression evaluated to null) improve debugging — enabled by default in recent JDK builds.

// Java17_HelpfulNPE.java
import java.util.*;
public class Java17_HelpfulNPE {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String[] arr = null;
        try {
            // throw NPE with diagnostics showing "arr" is null
            System.out.println(arr.length);
        } catch (NullPointerException npe) {
            npe.printStackTrace(); // JDK may show helpful message pointing to "arr"
        }
    }
}


Explanation: Java can provide more informative NPE messages when available in the JVM build, making root-cause faster to find.
Interview tip: this is a runtime/JVM feature, not a language change.

9) Parameter: Strong encapsulation of JDK internals (JEP 403)

What: JDK internal classes (sun.*) are strongly encapsulated by default on Java 17; reflective access needs --add-opens if you rely on internals.

# When running reflection-based libraries that access internal packages, you may need:
java --add-opens java.base/java.lang=ALL-UNNAMED -jar yourapp.jar


Explanation: improves security & maintainability; migrate code to supported APIs and avoid depending on sun.*.
Interview tip: know how to open modules for legacy frameworks, but prefer refactoring to supported APIs.

10) Parameter: Compact RAND improvements (note)

What: JDK 17 included enhancements in random number generator APIs (new interfaces and algorithms) — useful in simulations and cryptography (see RandomGenerator API). (If you need sample code for PRNGs I can add.)

11) Parameter: MNC pattern — records + sealed + validation + persistence

What: Put it together: domain model with records + sealed to represent persistent events/messages.

// Java17_MNC_Domain.java
import java.time.*;
import java.util.*;

public class Java17_MNC_Domain {
    public sealed interface Event permits UserCreated, UserDeleted {}
    public static record UserCreated(UUID id, String email, Instant when) implements Event {
        public UserCreated {
            if (!email.contains("@")) throw new IllegalArgumentException("bad email");
        }
    }
    public static record UserDeleted(UUID id, Instant when) implements Event {}

    // simple dispatcher/demux using pattern matching
    static void handle(Event e) {
        if (e instanceof UserCreated uc) {
            System.out.println("User created: " + uc);
        } else if (e instanceof UserDeleted ud) {
            System.out.println("User deleted: " + ud);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Event ev = new UserCreated(UUID.randomUUID(), "alice@example.com", Instant.now());
        handle(ev);
    }
}


Explanation: records + sealed = compact, safe, and declarative domain modeling. Validation in record constructor enforces invariants early.
MNC tip: store Instant for event time; enforce validation at creation; use modules/encapsulation for API boundaries.

12) Parameter: Interop & migration note — module encapsulation + reflection

What: On Java 17, some reflection-based libraries require --add-opens or opens in module-info.java. For migration, prefer MethodHandles, supported public APIs, or update libraries.

Quick checklist: Java 17 essentials

Use records for simple immutable data carriers.

Use sealed types to define closed hierarchies (helps reasoning and exhaustiveness).

Prefer pattern matching for instanceof to reduce casts.

Use switch expressions with yield for concise branching and to work nicely with sealed types.

Use text blocks for multiline literals (JSON/SQL/HTML).

Be aware of strong encapsulation — avoid depending on JDK internals; open modules only when necessary.

Fantastic — below you’ll get 30 runnable Java 17 examples (short, focused classes) grouped by feature, each labeled so you can copy-paste into separate .java files and run on Java 17+. After the examples I’ll walk you through a step-by-step migration from a legacy POJO-heavy codebase to records + sealed classes, with before/after code, pitfalls, and a migration checklist.

Because there are 30 examples, I keep each example compact and focused (basic → tricky → advanced → MNC style across features). If you want any single example expanded into a multi-file mini-project or unit tests, say which one and I’ll produce that immediately.

Part A — 30 Runnable Java 17 Examples

Each block is a self-contained class you can paste into ExampleNN.java and run (javac + java) on Java 17.

Records — concise immutable classes (6 examples)
1) Basic record
// R01_BasicRecord.java
public class R01_BasicRecord {
    public record Person(String name, int age) {}
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Person p = new Person("Alice", 30);
        System.out.println(p.name() + " / " + p.age());
    }
}

2) Record with compact constructor and validation
// R02_RecordValidation.java
public class R02_RecordValidation {
    public record Email(String local, String domain) {
        public Email {
            if (local == null || domain == null) throw new IllegalArgumentException("parts required");
            local = local.trim().toLowerCase();
            domain = domain.trim().toLowerCase();
        }
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Email e = new Email(" John.Doe ", "Example.COM");
        System.out.println(e); // normalized
    }
}

3) Record implementing interface & derived method
// R03_RecordDerived.java
public class R03_RecordDerived {
    public interface Identifiable { String id(); }
    public record Item(String id, String name) implements Identifiable {
        public String display() { return id + ":" + name; }
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Item it = new Item("i1","Toy");
        System.out.println(it.display());
    }
}

4) Record with custom toString (compact canonical constructor + override)
// R04_RecordToString.java
public class R04_RecordToString {
    public record Point(int x, int y) {
        @Override public String toString() { return "(" + x + "," + y + ")"; }
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(new Point(2,3));
    }
}

5) Record and serialization (basic demo)
// R05_RecordSerialization.java
import java.io.*;
public class R05_RecordSerialization {
    public record Pair(String a, String b) implements Serializable {}
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Pair p = new Pair("x","y");
        ByteArrayOutputStream bos = new ByteArrayOutputStream();
        try(ObjectOutputStream o = new ObjectOutputStream(bos)) { o.writeObject(p); }
        try(ObjectInputStream in = new ObjectInputStream(new ByteArrayInputStream(bos.toByteArray()))) {
            Object o = in.readObject();
            System.out.println("Deserialized: " + o);
        }
    }
}

6) Record with derived cached field (non-component)
// R06_RecordDerivedCache.java
public class R06_RecordDerivedCache {
    public record NameParts(String full) {
        private final String[] parts = full.split("\\s+"); // allowed: non-component field
        public String first() { return parts.length > 0 ? parts[0] : ""; }
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var np = new NameParts("John Doe");
        System.out.println(np.first());
    }
}

Sealed types + Pattern matching (6 examples)
7) Basic sealed hierarchy
// S07_SealedBasic.java
public class S07_SealedBasic {
    public sealed interface Expr permits Val, Add {}
    public static final class Val implements Expr { public final int v; public Val(int v){this.v=v;} }
    public static final class Add implements Expr { public final Expr a,b; public Add(Expr a, Expr b){this.a=a;this.b=b;} }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Expr e = new Val(5);
        System.out.println(e.getClass().getSimpleName());
    }
}

8) Pattern matching with instanceof
// S08_PatternInstanceof.java
public class S08_PatternInstanceof {
    static String describe(Object o) {
        if (o instanceof String s) return "str len=" + s.length();
        if (o instanceof Integer i) return "int=" + i;
        return "other";
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(describe("hello"));
        System.out.println(describe(42));
    }
}

9) Sealed + exhaustive switch (ADT eval)
// S09_SealedSwitchEval.java
public class S09_SealedSwitchEval {
    public sealed interface Expr permits Val, Add {}
    public static final record Val(int v) implements Expr {}
    public static final record Add(Expr l, Expr r) implements Expr {}

    static int eval(Expr e) {
        return switch (e) {
            case Val v -> v.v();
            case Add a -> eval(a.l()) + eval(a.r());
        };
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Expr ex = new Add(new Val(2), new Val(3));
        System.out.println(eval(ex));
    }
}

10) Sealed with non-sealed subtype
// S10_SealedNonSealed.java
public class S10_SealedNonSealed {
    public sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, OpenShape {}
    public final class Circle implements Shape {}
    public final class Rectangle implements Shape {}
    public non-sealed interface OpenShape extends Shape {} // open for further extension
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("sealed with non-sealed example");
    }
}

11) Pattern matching + local scope nuance
// S11_PatternScope.java
public class S11_PatternScope {
    static void f(Object o) {
        if (o instanceof String s && s.length() > 3) {
            System.out.println("long string: " + s);
        } else {
            // s not in scope here
            System.out.println("not long string");
        }
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { f("abcd"); f("x"); }
}

12) Sealed hierarchy used for type-safe visitor
// S12_VisitorSealed.java
public class S12_VisitorSealed {
    public sealed interface Cmd permits Start, Stop {}
    public static final record Start(String id) implements Cmd {}
    public static final record Stop(String id) implements Cmd {}
    static String process(Cmd c) {
        return switch (c) {
            case Start s -> "start " + s.id();
            case Stop s  -> "stop " + s.id();
        };
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(process(new Start("x"))); }
}

Switch Expressions & yield (4 examples)
13) Basic switch expression
// SW13_SimpleSwitch.java
public class SW13_SimpleSwitch {
    static String kind(int x) {
        return switch (x) {
            case 1 -> "one";
            case 2 -> "two";
            default -> "many";
        };
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(kind(2)); }
}

14) Switch with block + yield
// SW14_SwitchYield.java
public class SW14_SwitchYield {
    static String classify(int x) {
        return switch (x) {
            case 1 -> "A";
            case 2 -> {
                System.out.println("two case");
                yield "B";
            }
            default -> "C";
        };
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(classify(2)); }
}

15) Enum switch - exhaustiveness check
// SW15_EnumSwitch.java
public class SW15_EnumSwitch {
    public enum Day { MON, TUE, WED, THU, FRI, SAT, SUN }
    static boolean isWeekend(Day d) {
        return switch (d) {
            case SAT, SUN -> true;
            default -> false;
        };
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(isWeekend(Day.SAT)); }
}

16) Switch over sealed records (ADT)
// SW16_SwitchSealedRecords.java
public class SW16_SwitchSealedRecords {
    public sealed interface Expr permits Num, Neg {}
    public static record Num(int v) implements Expr {}
    public static record Neg(Expr e) implements Expr {}
    static int eval(Expr e) {
        return switch (e) {
            case Num n -> n.v();
            case Neg r -> -eval(r.e());
        };
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(eval(new Neg(new Num(5)))); }
}

            // will throw NPE; some JDK builds show detailed message pointing to 'arr'

22) Streams of random numbers

import java.util.random.*;
import java.util.stream.*;
public class RND22_RandomStreams {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        RandomGenerator rnd = RandomGenerator.getDefault();
        IntStream ints = rnd.ints(5, 0, 100);
