    }
}

Process API improvements (2 examples)
23) Process onExit -> CompletableFuture
// P23_ProcessOnExit.java
public class P23_ProcessOnExit {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Process p = new ProcessBuilder("echo", "hello").start();
        p.onExit().thenAccept(proc -> System.out.println("exit: " + proc.exitValue())).get();
    }
}

24) ProcessHandle info
// P24_ProcessHandleInfo.java
public class P24_ProcessHandleInfo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ProcessHandle ph = ProcessHandle.current();
        ph.info().command().ifPresent(cmd -> System.out.println("cmd: " + cmd));
        System.out.println("pid: " + ph.pid());
    }
}

MNC patterns combining records + sealed + practical tasks (4 examples)
25) Event sourcing: record events + handler
// M25_EventSourcing.java
import java.time.*;
import java.util.*;
public class M25_EventSourcing {
    public sealed interface Event permits UserCreated, UserDeleted {}
    public record UserCreated(UUID id, String email, Instant when) implements Event {}
    public record UserDeleted(UUID id, Instant when) implements Event {}

    static void apply(List<String> state, Event e) {
        if (e instanceof UserCreated uc) state.add("user:" + uc.email());
        else if (e instanceof UserDeleted ud) state.remove("user:" + ud.id());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> s = new ArrayList<>();
        apply(s, new UserCreated(UUID.randomUUID(), "a@x.com", Instant.now()));
        System.out.println(s);
    }
}

26) API DTO records + validation pipeline
// M26_DTOValidation.java
public class M26_DTOValidation {
    public record UserDTO(String name, String email) {
        public UserDTO { if (!email.contains("@")) throw new IllegalArgumentException("email"); }
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(new UserDTO("A","a@b.com")); }
}

27) Small interpreter using sealed Expr (MNC)
// M27_SmallInterpreter.java
public class M27_SmallInterpreter {
    public sealed interface Expr permits Lit, Add {}
    public static record Lit(int v) implements Expr {}
    public static record Add(Expr a, Expr b) implements Expr {}

    static int eval(Expr e) {
        return switch (e) {
            case Lit l -> l.v();
            case Add a -> eval(a.a()) + eval(a.b());
        };
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(eval(new Add(new Lit(2), new Lit(3)))); }
}

28) Sealed-driven REST model (DTO + switch)
// M28_RestModel.java
public class M28_RestModel {
    public sealed interface Command permits CreateUser, DeleteUser {}
    public static record CreateUser(String email) implements Command {}
    public static record DeleteUser(String id) implements Command {}

    static String handle(Command c) { return switch (c) {
        case CreateUser cu -> "create:" + cu.email();
        case DeleteUser du -> "delete:" + du.id();
    }; }

    public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(handle(new CreateUser("a@b"))); }
}

Interop & encapsulation / reflection caution (2 examples)
29) Reflection attempt on private JDK member (may fail on Java 17)
// X29_ReflectionFail.java
import java.lang.reflect.*;
public class X29_ReflectionFail {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            Class<?> c = Class.forName("java.lang.String");
            Field f = c.getDeclaredField("value");
            f.setAccessible(true); // may throw InaccessibleObjectException on Java 17 without add-opens
            System.out.println("accessible? " + f.isAccessible());
        } catch (Throwable t) {
            System.out.println("Reflection blocked: " + t);
        }
    }
}

30) Using --add-opens note (not runnable here)
# To allow reflection at runtime (not a .java file)
# java --add-opens java.base/java.lang=ALL-UNNAMED -jar your.jar

Part B — Converting a legacy POJO-heavy codebase to record + sealed — step-by-step

Below is a practical migration guide with code before/after, pitfalls, and a checklist. Follow the steps incrementally and keep tests green.

Example legacy code (before)
Legacy POJOs and inheritance example
// LegacyUser.java  (before)
public class LegacyUser {
    private final String id;
    private String name;
    private String email;

    public LegacyUser(String id, String name, String email) {
        this.id = id;
        this.name = name;
        this.email = email;
    }
    // getters + setters
    public String getId() { return id; }
    public String getName() { return name; }
    public void setName(String n) { this.name = n; }
    public String getEmail() { return email; }
    public void setEmail(String e) { this.email = e; }

    // equals/hashCode/toString (boilerplate)
    @Override public boolean equals(Object o) { ... }
    @Override public int hashCode() { ... }
}

Legacy polymorphic event hierarchy
// LegacyEvent.java (before)
public abstract class LegacyEvent {}
public class UserCreatedEvent extends LegacyEvent {
    private final String userId; private final String email;
    public UserCreatedEvent(String userId, String email) { this.userId=userId; this.email=email; }
    // getters...
}
public class UserDeletedEvent extends LegacyEvent {
    private final String userId;
    public UserDeletedEvent(String userId) { this.userId=userId; }
}


Problems:

Lots of boilerplate for getters, constructors, equals/hashCode.

Unsafe mutation (setters) causing concurrency issues.

Event hierarchy is open (anyone can add subclasses), making exhaustive handling fragile.

Step 1 — Decide which types are value objects (immutable) and which are entities

Value objects: DTOs, small carriers, keys, events (use record).

Entities managed by ORM (JPA) that require mutability/no-arg constructor — do not change to record yet (JPA needs mutable field access or proxies).

Domain ADTs: use sealed to make closed hierarchies when appropriate.

Step 2 — Convert simple DTOs to record

Before:

public class Address {
    private String street; private String city;
    public Address(String s,String c){ this.street=s; this.city=c;}
    public String getStreet(){return street;} public String getCity(){return city;}
}


After:

public record Address(String street, String city) {}


Why:

Removes boilerplate for constructor, getters, equals/hashCode/toString.

Immutable by design — safer for multithreading and caching.

Step 3 — Convert events to record + sealed ADT

Before:

public abstract class Event {}
public class UserCreated extends Event { ... }
public class UserDeleted extends Event { ... }


After:

public sealed interface Event permits UserCreated, UserDeleted {}
public record UserCreated(String id, String email) implements Event {}
public record UserDeleted(String id) implements Event {}


Benefits:

Compiler knows permitted subtypes (safe exhaustive switch).

Records give concise immutable payloads.

Step 4 — Replace instanceof + cast with pattern matching and switch expressions

Before:

if (ev instanceof UserCreated) {
    UserCreated uc = (UserCreated) ev;
    handleCreate(uc.getId(), uc.getEmail());
}


After:

if (ev instanceof UserCreated uc) {
    handleCreate(uc.id(), uc.email());
}

// or with switch:
switch (ev) {
    case UserCreated uc -> handleCreate(uc.id(), uc.email());
    case UserDeleted ud -> handleDelete(ud.id());
}

Step 5 — Add validation in record compact constructors where necessary

Before: validation sprinkled across factories or callers.

After:

public record Email(String local, String domain) {
    public Email {
        if (!domain.contains(".")) throw new IllegalArgumentException("bad domain");
    }
}


Records validate at creation time—ensures invariants in one place.

Step 6 — Migrate APIs & serialization considerations

JSON libraries: Jackson (pre-2.12) required tweaks. Modern Jackson supports records (add jackson-databind 2.12+). Configure ObjectMapper to support records.

JPA / Hibernate: JPA entities are mutable and require a no-arg constructor and proxies—do not convert JPA entities to records unless you are using read-only DTOs. For persistence, prefer DTOs (records) mapped from entities via mappers.

Example mapping:

// convert entity -> DTO record
record UserDto(String id, String name) {}
UserDto dto = new UserDto(entity.getId(), entity.getName());

Step 7 — Replace equality/hashing uses with record semantics

Records already implement equals/hashCode. Remove manual implementations to avoid bug risk and duplication.

Step 8 — Tests & compatibility safety net

Add unit tests for all visible behaviors (serialization, equality, JSON payload shapes).

Keep compatibility layer if public API must remain unchanged — e.g., keep old classes delegating to new records (adapter pattern) while clients migrate.

Adapter example:

public class LegacyUserAdapter {
    private final UserRecord r;
    public LegacyUserAdapter(UserRecord r) { this.r = r; }
    public String getName() { return r.name(); }
    // etc.
}

Step 9 — Migration plan & rollout

Inventory: List all POJOs, identify which can be converted (DTOs, messages) and which cannot (JPA entities, proxied types).

Add tests: Ensure serialization, equals, hash, and other behaviors are covered.

Convert in small batches:

Start with pure DTOs and events.

Convert and update callers.

Run tests and integration tests.

Upgrade frameworks: Ensure Jackson/JPA versions support records or update mappers.

Refactor callers: Replace getters with record accessors (e.g., getName() → name()), or keep adapter during transition.

Deprecate old classes: After migration, remove legacy classes.

Pitfalls & how to handle them

JPA/Hibernate: Entities require no-arg constructors and mutability — keep them as classes; map to records for service boundary DTOs.

Frameworks & reflection: Libraries using reflection to set fields may need updates. Jackson 2.12+ handles records; otherwise add custom serializers/deserializers.

Binary compatibility: Changing public signatures (renaming getters) breaks binary compatibility. Use adapters and deprecation strategy for libraries used by third parties.

Deserialization constructors: Some frameworks expect a bean no-arg constructor—use mix-ins or module upgrades.

Example migration: a small concrete before → after
Before (legacy):
// User.java (legacy)
public class User {
    private String id;
    private String name;
    public User() {} // required by some frameworks
    public User(String id, String name){ this.id=id; this.name=name; }
    public String getId(){return id;}
    public String getName(){return name;}
    public void setName(String name){this.name=name;}
}

After: if it's a DTO only used for API responses
// UserDto.java (record)
public record UserDto(String id, String name) {}

If you must keep bean contract for a while:
// UserDtoAdapter.java
public class UserDtoAdapter {
    private final UserDto r;
    public UserDtoAdapter(UserDto r){ this.r = r; }
    public String getId(){ return r.id(); }
    public String getName(){ return r.name(); }
}

Migration Checklist (summary)

 Inventory: Categorize types (DTO, Entity, Event, Service model).

 Update test suite and add serialization tests.

 Upgrade libraries: Jackson, Hibernate, other frameworks.

 Convert DTOs → records in small PRs; run tests.

 Replace instanceof + cast with pattern matching where clear.

 Introduce sealed interfaces for closed hierarchies (events, commands).

 Add compact constructor validation where necessary.

 Use adapters for backwards compatibility and for frameworks needing legacy beans.

 Monitor runtime (GC, JIT) and do performance tests.

 Gradual deprecation of legacy POJOs; remove after clients migrate.

Quick FAQ (common migration questions)

Q: Can I make a JPA entity a record?
A: Generally no — JPA and many ORMs require a no-arg constructor and mutable fields/proxies. Use records for DTOs, not entities.

Q: Will record break JSON shape?
A: Modern Jackson (2.12+) supports records. If using older JSON libraries, either upgrade or provide custom (de)serializers.

Q: Are records comparable to Lombok @Value?
A: Yes — records are built-in, final, immutable, and generate canonical methods. Lombok @Value was a precursor; using records avoids extra tooling.

Q: How to handle equals/hash changes?
A: Records auto-generate equals/hashCode. Ensure tests cover equality semantics before replacing classes.

Great — below are **50 Java 5 examples** (each short, focused, Java-5–compatible) across **simple → basic → tricky → hard → MNC** levels.
For each: **title → one-line explanation → compact Java5 snippet (runnable where practical) → quick interview note**. These are intentionally compact so you can copy/paste and expand. I grouped them roughly by theme (Collections, OOP, Concurrency, IO/Network, JVM/Advanced, Design Patterns, Servlets/REST, Persistence & Enterprise).

If you want, I can expand any specific example into a full runnable file, tests, or an enterprise-grade snippet next — tell me which numbers to expand.

## Collections & Basics (1–12) — simple → basic → tricky

1. **ArrayList iterate (basic)**
   Use generics + enhanced for.

   ```java
   List<String> l = new ArrayList<String>();
   l.add("a"); l.add("b");
   for (String s : l) System.out.println(s);
   ```

   *Note:* type erasure at runtime; interviewer may ask why `List<String>.class` is invalid.

2. **HashMap frequency counter (simple)**
   Pre-Java8 null checks.

   ```java
   Map<String,Integer> m = new HashMap<String,Integer>();
   for (String w: words) {
     Integer c = m.get(w);
     m.put(w, c==null ? new Integer(1) : new Integer(c.intValue()+1));
   }
   ```

   *Note:* boxing/unboxing and `getOrDefault` absent in 1.5.

3. **LinkedList as queue (basic)**
   Use as FIFO.

   ```java
   Queue<String> q = new LinkedList<String>();
   q.add("x"); System.out.println(q.poll());
   ```

   *Note:* `LinkedList` implements `Deque`.

4. **SubList backed view gotcha (tricky)**
   Modifying original invalidates sublist.

   ```java
   List<String> a = new ArrayList<String>(Arrays.asList("a","b","c"));
   List<String> s = a.subList(0,2);
   a.add("x"); // subsequent ops on s may throw ConcurrentModificationException
   ```

   *Note:* explain structural modification semantics.

5. **LinkedHashMap LRU (advanced)**
   Override `removeEldestEntry`.

   ```java
   class LRU<K,V> extends LinkedHashMap<K,V> {
     private final int cap;
     LRU(int c){ super(c,0.75f,true); cap=c; }
     protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry<K,V> e){ return size()>cap;}
   }
   ```

   *Note:* not thread-safe; discuss synchronization.

6. **EnumSet example (basic/perf)**
   Efficient set for enums.

   ```java
   enum Day { MON,TUE,WED }
   EnumSet<Day> s = EnumSet.of(Day.MON, Day.WED);
   ```

   *Note:* extremely memory- and time-efficient.

7. **TreeMap ordering (simple)**
   Sorted map by natural order or comparator.

   ```java
   SortedMap<String,Integer> m = new TreeMap<String,Integer>();
   m.put("b",2); m.put("a",1);
   ```

8. **Fail-fast iterator (tricky)**
   Structural modify during iteration throws CME.

   ```java
   for (Iterator<String> it = list.iterator(); it.hasNext();) {
       String x = it.next();
       list.add("z"); // ConcurrentModificationException
   }
   ```

9. **CopyOnWriteArrayList for read-heavy workloads (MNC)**
   Safe iteration while writers occur.

   ```java
   CopyOnWriteArrayList<String> cow = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<String>();
   cow.add("a"); for(String s: cow) System.out.println(s);
   ```

10. **ArrayBlockingQueue producer/consumer (basic concurrent)**

    ```java
    ArrayBlockingQueue<String> q = new ArrayBlockingQueue<String>(10);
    q.put("item"); String r = q.take();
    ```

    *Note:* blocking backpressure in producers.

11. **PriorityQueue scheduling (simple)**

    ```java
    PriorityQueue<Integer> pq = new PriorityQueue<Integer>();
    pq.add(5); pq.add(1);
    System.out.println(pq.poll()); // 1
    ```

12. **WeakHashMap for caches (tricky memory)**
    Values removed when key GC'ed.

    ```java
    Map<Object,String> cache = new WeakHashMap<Object,String>();
    Object k = new Object(); cache.put(k,"v"); k=null; // eligible for GC
    ```

---

## OOP, Generics, Types & Common Pitfalls (13–22)

13. **Simple POJO & encapsulation (basic)**

    ```java
    public class Person { private String name; public Person(String n){name=n;} public String getName(){return name;} }
    ```

14. **Equals & hashCode basics (tricky)**

    ```java
    public boolean equals(Object o){ if(this==o) return true; if(!(o instanceof Person)) return false; Person p=(Person)o; return name==null? p.name==null : name.equals(p.name); }
    public int hashCode(){ return name==null?0:name.hashCode(); }
    ```

    *Note:* show contract with HashMap.

15. **Liskov Substitution Violation (concept/tricky)**
    `Square extends Rectangle` example — explain why overriding setters breaks clients.

16. **Generics type erasure example (interview)**

    ```java
    List<String> ls = new ArrayList<String>();
    List<Integer> li = new ArrayList<Integer>();
    System.out.println(ls.getClass() == li.getClass()); // true
    ```

    *Note:* types erased at runtime.

17. **Generic method (basic)**

    ```java
    public static <T> T first(List<T> l){ return l.isEmpty()?null:l.get(0); }
    ```

18. **Varargs usage (Java 5)**

    ```java
    public static void print(String... vals){ for(String s: vals) System.out.println(s); }
    ```

19. **Autoboxing pitfall (tricky)**

    ```java
    Integer a = 128, b = 128; System.out.println(a==b); // false (cache -128..127 only)
    ```

20. **Inner classes & this reference (basic)**

    ```java
    class Outer { class Inner { void f(){ System.out.println(Outer.this); } } }
    ```

21. **Reflection: load class & method invoke (hard)**

    ```java
    Class c = Class.forName("com.example.MyClass");
    Method m = c.getMethod("run", new Class[]{String.class});
    m.invoke(c.newInstance(), new Object[]{"arg"});
    ```

    *Note:* checked exceptions and security.

22. **Annotations usage (basic)**
    Use `@Override`, custom annotation creation available in Java 5.

---

## Concurrency & `java.util.concurrent` (23–36) — critical Java5 additions

23. **Thread + Runnable (simple)**

    ```java
    new Thread(new Runnable(){ public void run(){ System.out.println("run"); }}).start();
    ```

24. **Executors.newFixedThreadPool (basic)**

    ```java
    ExecutorService e = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);
    e.submit(new Runnable(){ public void run(){ System.out.println("task"); }});
    e.shutdown();
    ```

25. **Callable + Future (basic/async)**

    ```java
    Future<Integer> f = e.submit(new Callable<Integer>(){ public Integer call(){ return 42; } });
    Integer v = f.get(); // blocking
    ```

26. **CountDownLatch (coordination)**

    ```java
    CountDownLatch l = new CountDownLatch(3);
    l.countDown(); l.await();
    ```

27. **CyclicBarrier (barrier)**

    ```java
    CyclicBarrier b = new CyclicBarrier(3);
    // threads call b.await();
    ```

28. **Semaphore for throttling**

    ```java
    Semaphore s = new Semaphore(5);
    s.acquire(); s.release();
    ```

29. **ReentrantLock + Condition (hard)**

    ```java
    ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();
    Condition cond = lock.newCondition();
    lock.lock();
    try { cond.await(); } finally { lock.unlock(); }
    ```

30. **AtomicInteger atomic ops (simple)**

    ```java
    AtomicInteger ai = new AtomicInteger(0);
    ai.incrementAndGet();
    ```

31. **ConcurrentHashMap basics (MNC)**

    ```java
    ConcurrentHashMap<String,String> ch = new ConcurrentHashMap<String,String>();
    ch.put("k","v");
    ```

    *Note:* segment locking (in Java5), safe for concurrent reads/writes.

32. **BlockingQueue consumer-producer with timeout (advanced)**

    ```java
    BlockingQueue<String> q = new ArrayBlockingQueue<String>(10);
    String it = q.poll(1, TimeUnit.SECONDS);
    ```

33. **ThreadPool shutdown patterns (MNC)**
    Use `shutdown()` + await termination, optional `shutdownNow()` on timeout.

34. **Producer/Consumer with multiple consumers (hard)**
    Combine `ExecutorService` and `BlockingQueue` for worker pools.

---

## IO, Files, Networking (37–44)

35. **File I/O with Buffered streams (basic)**

    ```java
    BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("in.txt"));
    String line = br.readLine(); br.close();
    ```

36. **try-finally resource management (Java5)**

    ```java
    BufferedReader br = null;
    try { br = new BufferedReader(...); } finally { if(br!=null) br.close(); }
    ```

37. **Socket client (simple networking)**

    ```java
    Socket s = new Socket("example.com",80);
    PrintWriter out = new PrintWriter(s.getOutputStream(), true);
    out.println("GET / HTTP/1.0");
    s.close();
    ```

38. **URLConnection for HTTP (basic)**

    ```java
    URL url = new URL("http://example.com");
    URLConnection conn = url.openConnection();
    InputStream in = conn.getInputStream();
    ```

39. **Object Serialization (tricky)**

    ```java
    ObjectOutputStream o = new ObjectOutputStream(new FileOutputStream("o.bin"));
    o.writeObject(obj); o.close();
    ```

    *Note:* need `serialVersionUID`, security and compatibility issues.

40. **RandomAccessFile for file seek (advanced)**

    ```java
    RandomAccessFile raf = new RandomAccessFile("f.dat","rw");
    raf.seek(100); raf.writeInt(42);
    ```

41. **Jar & ClassLoader basics (hard)**
    Load classes/resources from jars; explain classloader hierarchy and isolation.

42. **Watch out: File locking and NIO absence (Java5)**
    NIO.2 arrived in Java7; show limitations of pre-7 file APIs.

---

## JVM, GC, Memory & Advanced (45–50)

43. **SoftReference for memory-sensitive cache (tricky)**

    ```java
    SoftReference<byte[]> r = new SoftReference<byte[]>(new byte[1024*1024]);
    byte[] b = r.get();
    ```

    *Note:* GC reclaims soft refs under memory pressure.

44. **ReferenceQueue for cleanup (advanced)**
    Pair `WeakReference` with `ReferenceQueue` to detect reclaimed keys.

45. **Class.forName vs ClassLoader (interview)**
    Use `Class.forName("com.X")` vs `Thread.currentThread().getContextClassLoader()` differences.

46. **JMX basics for metrics (MNC)**
    Expose app metrics via `MBean` and `MBeanServer` — discuss monitoring.

47. **SecurityManager basics (hard/interview)**
    Configure `SecurityManager` policies to restrict actions in containers.

48. **Design Pattern: Singleton (double-checked locking) (tricky)**

    ```java
    public class S { private static volatile S inst; private S(){} public static S get(){ if(inst==null){ synchronized(S.class){ if(inst==null) inst=new S();}} return inst; } }
    ```

    *Note:* `volatile` required post Java5 memory model.

49. **Design Pattern: Strategy (simple)**
    Inject behavior via interfaces (see earlier Payment example).

50. **Servlet threading model & safe servlets (MNC hard)**

    ```java
    public class MyServlet extends HttpServlet {
       // avoid instance fields!
       protected void doPost(HttpServletRequest req, HttpServletResponse resp) { /* use local vars*/ }
    }
    ```

    *Note:* explain session vs stateless REST, synchronization pitfalls, DB pooling, idempotency, and use of filters for cross-cutting concerns.

ACID

Sure — here’s a clear, practical explanation of ACID with concrete examples (mostly using an OrderService context so you can quote it in interviews or docs).

ACID — quick definition

ACID is a set of properties that make a database transaction reliable:

Atomicity — a transaction is all-or-nothing.

Consistency — a transaction moves the database from one valid state to another, obeying rules/constraints.

Isolation — concurrent transactions behave as if they ran serially (to some degree).

Durability — once a transaction commits, its effects survive crashes.

Below I explain each property, give an OrderService example, show what can go wrong if the property is violated, and briefly note how it’s implemented or approximated in distributed systems.

1) Atomicity

What it means: Either all operations inside a transaction succeed, or none do. If one step fails, the DB rolls back all changes from that transaction.

OrderService example:
Placing an order might include:

Insert orders row.

Decrease inventory (inventory.reserve(...)).

Charge credit card (payments.charge(...)).

Insert order_events / publish event.

If the payment step fails, atomicity ensures the order row and inventory reservation are not left half-applied — the transaction is rolled back.

SQL pseudo-example:

BEGIN;
INSERT INTO orders (...) VALUES (...);
UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE sku = 'SKU-1';
-- call payment gateway (if synchronous) — if it fails, abort
COMMIT;
-- if payment failed -> ROLLBACK;


If atomicity violated (bad outcome):

Order recorded but payment not captured → customer charged later / inventory blocked incorrectly → inconsistent state and manual reconciliation required.

How it’s implemented: DBs use transaction logs and rollback mechanisms. In distributed systems with multiple services, true atomicity across services requires protocols like 2PC (two-phase commit) — heavy; often replaced by sagas (compensating actions).

2) Consistency

What it means: A transaction must preserve database invariants (constraints, triggers, foreign keys, business rules). If you start in a consistent state, you must end in a consistent state.

OrderService example:

Constraint: order.total >= 0

Constraint: inventory.qty >= 0
A transaction that subtracts inventory must never violate inventory.qty >= 0.

Example of enforcement:

Database CHECK constraints, foreign keys, NOT NULL, unique indexes, and application-level validations ensure consistency.

If consistency violated (bad outcome):

Negative inventory, duplicate order IDs, invoice referencing missing customer — these break downstream processing & reports.

How it’s implemented: Use DB constraints + application validation. For complex business invariants spanning services, enforce invariants via careful orchestration or compensation.

3) Isolation

What it means: Concurrent transactions should not interfere in a way that leads to incorrect outcomes. Isolation comes in levels (trade-offs between correctness and performance).

Common anomalies (with examples):

Dirty Read: T1 writes something but hasn’t committed. T2 reads it. If T1 rolls back, T2 used invalid data.

Example: Temp order appears to analytics, then disappears after rollback.

Non-repeatable Read: T2 reads same row twice within its transaction, and T1 changed it in-between; T2 sees different values.

Phantom Read: T2 runs a query twice and a row appears/disappears because T1 inserted/deleted between reads.

Lost Update: Two transactions read the same value and both write updated value, overwriting each other.

Example: Two checkout processes decrement same inventory and both commit, resulting in qty decreased twice when only one should have applied.

Isolation levels mapping (typical):

READ UNCOMMITTED — allows dirty reads.

READ COMMITTED — prevents dirty reads; allows non-repeatable/phantom.

REPEATABLE READ — prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads (varies by DB); may still allow phantoms.

SERIALIZABLE — strongest, prevents all above anomalies by making concurrent transactions behave like serial execution.

OrderService illustration (lost update):
Transaction A and B both read item qty = 10. Both subtract 1 and write back 9. Ideally result should be 8, but due to lost update result is 9. Use row-level locking or optimistic locking (version column) to prevent that.

Techniques to guarantee isolation:

Row locks, SELECT FOR UPDATE (pessimistic locking).

Optimistic concurrency control: WHERE version = ? updates and retry on conflicts.

Use SERIALIZABLE when absolutely necessary (but watch throughput).

4) Durability

What it means: Once a transaction commits, its result is permanent even if the database crashes immediately after.

OrderService example:
After COMMIT on order placement, the order must not disappear even if the DB server crashes. The payment and order state must be recoverable.

How it’s implemented: DBs flush WAL (write-ahead log) or transaction logs to stable storage before acknowledging commit. On restart, DB replays the log to restore committed transactions.

Durability caveats in distributed world:

If you write to DB but rely on an external message broker or payment gateway, you must ensure end-to-end durability (e.g., outbox pattern + publisher). Otherwise you might persist DB row but fail to publish the event to other services.

Putting it together — a practical OrderService pattern
Typical single-node transaction (ACID inside DB)
BEGIN;
-- validate items available
UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - :n WHERE sku = :sku AND qty >= :n;
INSERT INTO orders(...);
INSERT INTO order_lines(...);
COMMIT;


If these operations are in one DB transaction, ACID ensures atomic updates, preserved constraints, isolated concurrency and durability on commit.

Distributed transactions: 2PC vs Saga

Two-Phase Commit (2PC): Provides atomic commit across multiple transactional resources. Heavy, blocking, and hard to scale. Rarely used across microservices in practice.

Saga/Compensation: Rather than atomic commits, model workflow as a series of local transactions with compensating transactions on failure.

Example: Place order: ReserveInventory (local commit), ChargePayment (local commit). If ChargePayment fails later, run compensating ReleaseInventory. Not ACID across whole process, but eventual consistency.

Outbox pattern (common best-practice):

Write DB + append event to outbox table inside same DB transaction (so Atomic + Durable).

A separate process reads the outbox and publishes to message broker, guaranteeing other services see event even across crashes.

Examples of ACID violations and fixes

Lost update (fix: optimistic lock)

Problem: Two concurrent updates overwrite each other.

Fix: Add version column and update with WHERE version = :v and increment version; on 0 rows affected, retry.

Phantom reads in reporting (fix: SERIALIZABLE or snapshot isolation or repeatable read)

Problem: Summary queries see inconsistent row counts across long-running reads.

Fix: Use snapshot isolation for consistent view, or run reporting on a read-replica.

Partial side-effects (fix: outbox + saga)

Problem: Order saved but event not published because broker down.

Fix: Use transactional outbox (persist event w/ order) and separate reliable publisher.

Practical advice / checklist for building ACID-safe OrderService

Validate invariants in DB (constraints) and in application (business rules).

Keep critical multi-step changes inside a DB transaction when possible.

Use optimistic concurrency (version columns) for high-concurrency updates like inventory adjustments.

Use SELECT FOR UPDATE / row locks for short windows when you need strict serialized access.

For cross-service workflows:

Prefer local transactions + outbox + asynchronous processing.

Use sagas with compensating transactions rather than distributed 2PC in most microservice architectures.

Make operations idempotent (payments, event handlers) to survive retries and duplicates.

Monitor and alert on deadlocks, long-running transactions, and message delivery failures.

Document which operations are strongly consistent (ACID) and which are eventually consistent (sagas).

Short example: Java + Spring pseudo-code (atomic local transaction)
@Service
public class OrderService {
  @Transactional
  public Order placeOrder(OrderRequest req) {
    inventory.reserve(req.items);   // DB update
    Order o = orderRepo.save(...);  // DB insert
    paymentClient.charge(...);      // external call (avoid inside tx or use outbox)
    outbox.save(new OrderPlacedEvent(o.getId())); // same tx
    return o;
  }
}


Note: when calling external services like paymentClient inside a DB transaction, prefer to:

Make payment idempotent and safe to retry, or

Move payment to async step (publish event and let a worker charge), or

Use outbox + worker to trigger external calls.

TL;DR (for interviews)

Atomicity: all-or-nothing — use DB transactions.

Consistency: preserve invariants — enforce with constraints & validations.

Isolation: control how concurrent transactions see/interfere — use appropriate isolation level / locking or optimistic concurrency.

Durability: committed changes survive crashes — rely on DB WAL and durable messaging patterns (outbox).

When you scale to multiple services, ACID for the whole distributed operation is expensive — use sagas + outbox + idempotency to get safe, practical eventual consistency.

If you want, I can:

show a short demo transaction using Postgres SQL + SELECT FOR UPDATE for inventory,

write a Spring Boot snippet implementing the outbox pattern, or

make a one-page cheat-sheet (diagram + common pitfalls) you can print. Which would you like?



Nice — I’ll give you all three:

a compact Postgres + SELECT FOR UPDATE demo showing a safe inventory update,

a focused Spring Boot / JPA transactional outbox example (w/ runner that publishes outbox entries) you can drop into a project, and

a one-page ACID cheat-sheet (diagram-style checklist + common pitfalls and fixes).

I kept each piece practical and ready to copy/paste.

1) Postgres demo: atomic inventory reserve using SELECT … FOR UPDATE

Goal: safely reserve 1 unit of SKU sku_123 while preventing lost-updates / oversell in high concurrency.

-- Example SQL transaction (psql / libpq client)
BEGIN;

-- 1) Lock the inventory row for this SKU (pessimistic lock).
--    This prevents two concurrent txns from reading/updating qty at the same time.
SELECT qty
FROM inventory
WHERE sku = 'sku_123'
FOR UPDATE;

-- Suppose app read qty = 2
-- 2) Check business invariant and update
UPDATE inventory
SET qty = qty - 1
WHERE sku = 'sku_123' AND qty >= 1;

-- Verify update succeeded
-- If row_count = 0 -> not enough inventory or concurrent change; abort
-- In application code, check affected rows count.

-- 3) Insert order record (same tx)
INSERT INTO orders (id, customer_id, total, created_at)
VALUES ('ord-abc-123', 'cust-01', 49.99, now());

-- 4) Append outbox event (write to outbox table in same tx)
INSERT INTO outbox_events (id, aggregate_type, aggregate_id, event_type, payload, created_at, published)
VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), 'order', 'ord-abc-123', 'OrderPlaced', '{"orderId":"ord-abc-123"}', now(), false);

COMMIT;


Notes / behavior

SELECT ... FOR UPDATE obtains a row lock; concurrent transactions block until lock released — safe but may create contention under heavy concurrency.

Check affected rows for the UPDATE to detect insufficient stock (avoid race).

Keep the transaction short: do DB updates first, do not call external services (e.g., charge gateway) inside the transaction — use the outbox to trigger external work after commit.

If an error occurs before COMMIT, the DB rolls back — atomicity guaranteed.

2) Spring Boot + JPA: Transactional Outbox Pattern (simple, runnable sketch)

What this does: inside a single @Transactional method we: (a) persist domain change (Order), and (b) persist an OutboxEvent in same DB transaction. A background publisher picks un-published outbox rows, publishes them (here simulated), and marks them published.

Notes: production systems use idempotent publishing, proper error handling, batching, dead-letter handling, and transactional guarantees to ensure exactly-once or at-least-once semantics.

Key files (Java / Spring Boot)

pom/deps: Spring Boot, spring-boot-starter-data-jpa, H2 or Postgres driver, spring-boot-starter. (You’ll wire DB as you like.)

OutboxEvent entity

@Entity
@Table(name = "outbox_events")
public class OutboxEvent {
    @Id
    private UUID id = UUID.randomUUID();
    private String aggregateType;
    private String aggregateId;
    private String eventType;

    @Lob
    private String payload;

    private Instant createdAt = Instant.now();
    private boolean published = false;

    // getters/setters, constructors
}


Order entity (simplified)

@Entity
@Table(name = "orders")
public class Order {
    @Id
    private String id;
    private String customerId;
    private BigDecimal total;
    private Instant createdAt = Instant.now();
    // getters/setters
}


Repositories

public interface OrderRepository extends JpaRepository<Order, String> {}

public interface OutboxRepository extends JpaRepository<OutboxEvent, UUID> {
    @Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
    @Query("select o from OutboxEvent o where o.published = false order by o.createdAt")
    List<OutboxEvent> findUnpublishedForUpdate(Pageable pageable);
}


OrderService: write domain + outbox in one tx

@Service
public class OrderService {
    private final OrderRepository orderRepo;
    private final OutboxRepository outboxRepo;

    public OrderService(OrderRepository orderRepo, OutboxRepository outboxRepo) {
        this.orderRepo = orderRepo;
        this.outboxRepo = outboxRepo;
    }

    @Transactional
    public Order placeOrder(CreateOrderRequest req) {
        // 1) Do local DB work
        Order order = new Order();
        order.setId(UUID.randomUUID().toString());
        order.setCustomerId(req.getCustomerId());
        order.setTotal(req.getTotal());
        orderRepo.save(order);

        // 2) Create outbox event (persisted atomically with the order)
        OutboxEvent ev = new OutboxEvent();
        ev.setAggregateType("order");
        ev.setAggregateId(order.getId());
        ev.setEventType("OrderPlaced");
        ev.setPayload("{\"orderId\":\"" + order.getId() + "\"}");
        outboxRepo.save(ev);

        return order;
    }
}


OutboxPublisher: background job that publishes events

@Component
public class OutboxPublisher {

    private final OutboxRepository outbox;
    private final PlatformTransactionManager txManager;

    public OutboxPublisher(OutboxRepository outbox, PlatformTransactionManager txManager) {
        this.outbox = outbox;
        this.txManager = txManager;
    }

    // scheduled or invoked in a loop — simplified here with @Scheduled
    @Scheduled(fixedDelayString = "${outbox.poll-ms:1000}")
    public void publishPending() {
        // fetch a small page of unpublished events
        PageRequest page = PageRequest.of(0, 20);
        List<OutboxEvent> events = outbox.findUnpublishedForUpdate(page);

        for (OutboxEvent e : events) {
            try {
                // Simulate publish (replace with Kafka/Rabbit producer)
                publishToBroker(e);

                // mark published in a small transaction (or update in same tx as lock)
                DefaultTransactionDefinition def = new DefaultTransactionDefinition();
                TransactionStatus status = txManager.getTransaction(def);
                try {
                    e.setPublished(true);
                    outbox.save(e);
                    txManager.commit(status);
                } catch (Exception ex) {
                    txManager.rollback(status);
                    throw ex;
                }
            } catch (Exception pubEx) {
                // handle retry/backoff / log
            }
        }
    }

    private void publishToBroker(OutboxEvent e) {
        // Replace with real broker publish (KafkaProducer.send(...).get() etc.)
        System.out.println("Publishing event to broker: " + e.getEventType() + " -> " + e.getPayload());
    }
}


Startup runner for demonstration

@Component
public class DemoRunner implements CommandLineRunner {
    private final OrderService orderService;
    public DemoRunner(OrderService orderService) { this.orderService = orderService; }
    @Override
    public void run(String... args) throws Exception {
        CreateOrderRequest req = new CreateOrderRequest("cust-1", new BigDecimal("19.99"));
        Order o = orderService.placeOrder(req);
        System.out.println("Placed order: " + o.getId());
    }
}


Why this is safe

The order row and outbox event are persisted in one DB transaction (atomic + durable).

The publisher reads outbox records and reliably publishes them; if the publisher crashes, the event remains in DB and will be retried.

Consumers of the broker then react to the published domain event and can update their local state.

Production considerations

Use idempotent consumers and exactly-once or at-least-once semantics depending on requirements.

Build batching, backoff, DLQ, metrics, and monitoring into the outbox publisher.

For scale, partition outbox processing or move to a dedicated outbox dispatcher microservice.

3) One-page ACID cheat-sheet (printable, interview-ready)
ACID — quick recap

Atomicity: transaction is all-or-nothing.
Fix if violated: rollbacks, use DB transactions. For multi-service work, use Sagas (compensate).

Consistency: DB invariants preserved (constraints, FK, business rules).
Fix if violated: add constraints, validate in app and DB.

Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere (use locks, optimistic concurrency, or higher isolation levels).
Fix anomalies: SELECT FOR UPDATE, optimistic version checks, SERIALIZABLE when necessary.

Durability: committed transactions survive crashes (WAL, fsync).
Fix gaps: persistence + outbox + idempotent processing.

Short patterns / recipes

Reserve inventory safely (high concurrency)

Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE or UPDATE ... WHERE qty >= ? and check rows affected; short DB tx.

Or optimistic: UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - :n, version = version+1 WHERE sku = :sku AND version = :v.

Make DB → broker reliable

Use Transactional Outbox: write domain + outbox row in one DB tx; separate publisher reads outbox and publishes to broker; mark published.

Multi-service transaction (avoid 2PC)

Use Saga pattern: chain local txns + compensating txns on failure.

Idempotency

Add idempotency keys for operations that might be retried (payments, publishes). Persist result to dedup.

Common pitfalls & fixes (practical)

Partial side-effects (order saved, email not sent) → use outbox; make external calls async.

Lost updates (two checkouts reduce same inventory) → optimistic locking or FOR UPDATE.

Deadlocks → order DB locks consistently (lock ordering), keep txns short, retry on deadlock.

Long transactions → increase lock contention and risk — break into shorter txns + use outbox/sagas for cross-service actions.

Durability illusion (DB commit but message not published) → outbox + reliable dispatcher.

High contention on hot rows (inventory hot SKU) → sharding, per-SKU queues, or reservation tokens.

Quick decision guide

Single-DB bounded operation? → use DB transaction (ACID).

Cross-service synchronous requirement? → reconsider; prefer async + sagas or 2PC only in exceptional cases.

Need durable publish? → outbox pattern.

Heavy concurrency on a single row? → optimistic concurrency + retry or redesign to reduce hot-spot.

Wrap / Next steps

If you want I can:

Convert the Spring outbox example into a full Maven/Gradle project with build files and a simple JUnit test, or

Produce a Postgres script + small Node/Java client that runs the SELECT FOR UPDATE demo under simulated concurrency to show correctness, or

Produce a one-page printable diagram (PNG / PlantUML) of the outbox + saga flows.



SQL Join,Normalization,SQL Queries 



Perfect — below is a **complete, hands-on learning pack** you can run in **PostgreSQL** to learn core SQL concepts through an **Employee Management** database. It covers:

* schema design & **normalization** (1NF → 3NF),
* DDL to create tables, constraints, indexes,
* sample data (INSERTs),
* lots of **SQL queries** (joins, aggregates, window functions, CTEs, recursive CTE),
* **ACID** demonstration with transaction examples and `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE`,
* common **exercises** and learning path.

You can paste the SQL into psql (or a GUI like DBeaver) and run it. I’ll explain each step and point out what to learn from it.

---

# 0) Quick setup (Postgres)

Assume you have psql connected to a DB called `company`. If not, create DB:

```bash
createdb company
psql -d company
```

Run everything below inside that DB.

---

# 1) Conceptual design & normalization (ER in words)

We want an Employee Management model that supports:

* Employees, Departments, Job Roles
* Salaries (history), Addresses (one or many), Projects
* Employee ⇄ Project assignments with roles and time ranges
* Managers (hierarchical org chart)

Normalized approach:

* **Employees** table (employee core attributes) — 1NF (atomic fields).
* **Departments** separate (no repeating dept info).
* **Roles / JobTitles** separate.
* **SalaryHistory** separate — avoids storing historical payments in Employees (3NF).
* **Addresses** separate — multiple per employee.
* **Projects** and **Assignments** (many-to-many with payload) — normalized.
* **EmployeeManager**: manager\_id in Employees (self-reference) or recursive relationship.

This design avoids redundancy (no repeated department text), supports auditing (salary history), and is amenable to ACID transactions.

---

# 2) DDL — create normalized schema (copy/paste)

```sql
-- Enable uuid extension for IDs (optional)
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS "uuid-ossp";

-- Departments
CREATE TABLE departments (
  dept_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  location TEXT
);

-- Job roles / titles
CREATE TABLE job_titles (
  job_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  title TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  min_salary NUMERIC(12,2),
  max_salary NUMERIC(12,2)
);

-- Employees (core)
CREATE TABLE employees (
  emp_id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
  first_name TEXT NOT NULL,
  last_name TEXT NOT NULL,
  email TEXT UNIQUE,
  phone TEXT,
  hire_date DATE NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
  dept_id INT REFERENCES departments(dept_id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  job_id INT REFERENCES job_titles(job_id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  manager_id UUID REFERENCES employees(emp_id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  active BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Address (employees can have multiple addresses)
CREATE TABLE addresses (
  addr_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  emp_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES employees(emp_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  type TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'home', -- e.g., 'home', 'work'
  line1 TEXT NOT NULL,
  line2 TEXT,
  city TEXT,
  state TEXT,
  postal_code TEXT,
  country TEXT,
  UNIQUE(emp_id, type) -- at most one address of a given type per employee
);

-- Salary history (temporal)
CREATE TABLE salary_history (
  sid SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  emp_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES employees(emp_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  start_date DATE NOT NULL,
  end_date DATE, -- NULL = current
  salary NUMERIC(12,2) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT chk_dates CHECK (end_date IS NULL OR end_date >= start_date)
);

-- Projects
CREATE TABLE projects (
  project_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  description TEXT,
  start_date DATE,
  end_date DATE
);

-- Employee assignments to projects (many-to-many with attributes)
CREATE TABLE assignments (
  assignment_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  project_id INT NOT NULL REFERENCES projects(project_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  emp_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES employees(emp_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  assigned_role TEXT,
  start_date DATE NOT NULL,
  end_date DATE,
  UNIQUE(project_id, emp_id, start_date) -- avoid duplicate assignment rows
);

-- Audit / events table (optional) for demonstration
CREATE TABLE employee_events (
  event_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  emp_id UUID REFERENCES employees(emp_id),
  event_type TEXT NOT NULL,
  payload JSONB,
  created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT now()
);

-- Useful indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_employees_dept ON employees(dept_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_salary_emp ON salary_history(emp_id, start_date DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_assign_emp ON assignments(emp_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_assign_proj ON assignments(project_id);
```

**What to notice (normalization):**

* No repeating groups; each piece of data stored in one table (reduces update anomalies).
* Salary history separated so each salary change is a new row (temporal modeling).
* Assignments table stores the many-to-many relationship with metadata.

---

# 3) Insert sample data (starter dataset)

```sql
-- Departments
INSERT INTO departments(name, location) VALUES
  ('Engineering','NYC'),
  ('HR','San Francisco'),
  ('Sales','Austin');

-- Job titles
INSERT INTO job_titles(title, min_salary, max_salary) VALUES
  ('Software Engineer', 60000, 200000),
  ('Senior Software Engineer', 90000, 240000),
  ('HR Specialist', 40000, 90000),
  ('Sales Rep', 35000, 150000);

-- Employees
INSERT INTO employees(emp_id, first_name, last_name, email, hire_date, dept_id, job_id)
VALUES
  (uuid_generate_v4(), 'Alice', 'Anderson', 'alice@example.com', '2018-03-01', 1, 2),
  (uuid_generate_v4(), 'Bob', 'Brown', 'bob@example.com', '2019-07-15', 1, 1),
  (uuid_generate_v4(), 'Celia', 'Clark', 'celia@example.com', '2020-01-10', 2, 3),
  (uuid_generate_v4(), 'Daniel', 'Dawson', 'daniel@example.com', '2021-11-05', 3, 4);

-- For easier lookups, capture emp ids (in psql you can SELECT them)
-- Salary history (set current salary by end_date NULL)
INSERT INTO salary_history(emp_id, start_date, end_date, salary)
SELECT emp_id, '2018-03-01'::date, NULL, 150000 FROM employees WHERE first_name='Alice';
INSERT INTO salary_history(emp_id, start_date, end_date, salary)
SELECT emp_id, '2019-07-15'::date, NULL, 100000 FROM employees WHERE first_name='Bob';
INSERT INTO salary_history(emp_id, start_date, end_date, salary)
SELECT emp_id, '2020-01-10'::date, NULL, 60000 FROM employees WHERE first_name='Celia';
INSERT INTO salary_history(emp_id, start_date, end_date, salary)
SELECT emp_id, '2021-11-05'::date, NULL, 70000 FROM employees WHERE first_name='Daniel';

-- Projects
INSERT INTO projects(name, description, start_date) VALUES
  ('Website Revamp', 'Rebuild company website', '2022-01-01'),
  ('Recruiting Drive', 'University hiring campaign', '2021-09-01');

-- Assignments (link employees to projects)
-- Get project ids with subselects
INSERT INTO assignments(project_id, emp_id, assigned_role, start_date)
SELECT p.project_id, e.emp_id, 'Lead Developer', '2022-01-10'
FROM projects p JOIN employees e ON p.name='Website Revamp' AND e.first_name='Alice';

INSERT INTO assignments(project_id, emp_id, assigned_role, start_date)
SELECT p.project_id, e.emp_id, 'Developer', '2022-01-10'
FROM projects p JOIN employees e ON p.name='Website Revamp' AND e.first_name='Bob';

INSERT INTO assignments(project_id, emp_id, assigned_role, start_date)
SELECT p.project_id, e.emp_id, 'Coordinator', '2021-09-01'
FROM projects p JOIN employees e ON p.name='Recruiting Drive' AND e.first_name='Celia';
```

---

# 4) SQL queries — learn by example

### Basic SELECTs & filters

```sql
-- All employees
SELECT emp_id, first_name, last_name, email, hire_date FROM employees;

-- Employees in Engineering (dept_id = 1)
SELECT e.first_name, e.last_name, d.name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.name = 'Engineering';
```

### Joins (inner, left, right, full, cross)

```sql
-- INNER JOIN: only matching rows (employee with department)
SELECT e.first_name, e.last_name, d.name AS dept
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;

-- LEFT JOIN: all employees, maybe no department
SELECT e.first_name, e.last_name, d.name AS dept
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;

-- CROSS JOIN: Cartesian product (use rarely)
SELECT e.first_name, p.name
FROM employees e CROSS JOIN projects p
LIMIT 10;
```

### Aggregation and GROUP BY

```sql
-- Count employees per department
SELECT d.name, COUNT(*) AS num_employees
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
GROUP BY d.name
ORDER BY num_employees DESC;

-- Average current salary per department (use salary_history current = end_date IS NULL)
SELECT d.name, AVG(s.salary) AS avg_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
JOIN salary_history s ON s.emp_id = e.emp_id AND s.end_date IS NULL
GROUP BY d.name;
```

### Window functions (rank, running total)

```sql
-- Rank employees by salary within department
SELECT e.first_name, e.last_name, d.name AS dept, s.salary,
RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY d.dept_id ORDER BY s.salary DESC) AS sal_rank
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
JOIN salary_history s ON s.emp_id = e.emp_id AND s.end_date IS NULL
ORDER BY d.name, sal_rank;

-- Running total of salaries across all employees
SELECT first_name, last_name, salary,
SUM(salary) OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total
FROM (
  SELECT e.first_name, e.last_name, s.salary
  FROM employees e
  JOIN salary_history s ON s.emp_id = e.emp_id AND s.end_date IS NULL
) t;
```

### CTEs and recursive CTE (org hierarchy)

```sql
-- CTE: find current salaries and annotate
WITH current_salaries AS (
  SELECT emp_id, salary FROM salary_history WHERE end_date IS NULL
)
SELECT e.first_name, e.last_name, cs.salary
FROM employees e JOIN current_salaries cs USING(emp_id);

-- Recursive CTE: org chart (find manager chain for Alice)
WITH RECURSIVE mgr_chain AS (
  SELECT emp_id, first_name, last_name, manager_id, 0 AS level
  FROM employees WHERE first_name = 'Alice'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT m.emp_id, m.first_name, m.last_name, m.manager_id, mc.level + 1
  FROM employees m
  JOIN mgr_chain mc ON mc.manager_id = m.emp_id
)
SELECT * FROM mgr_chain;
```

### Complex join: employee with projects & roles

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, e.last_name, p.name AS project, a.assigned_role, a.start_date
FROM employees e
JOIN assignments a ON a.emp_id = e.emp_id
JOIN projects p ON p.project_id = a.project_id
ORDER BY e.last_name, a.start_date;
```

### Update salary within a transaction (atomicity + isolation)

```sql
-- Pseudocode: raise salary for Bob, but ensure no race
BEGIN;

-- Option A: pessimistic lock on employee row
SELECT emp_id FROM employees WHERE first_name='Bob' FOR UPDATE;

-- Update salary history: set end_date on current, insert new row
UPDATE salary_history
SET end_date = CURRENT_DATE
WHERE emp_id = (SELECT emp_id FROM employees WHERE first_name='Bob')
  AND end_date IS NULL;

INSERT INTO salary_history(emp_id, start_date, salary)
SELECT emp_id, CURRENT_DATE, 110000
FROM employees WHERE first_name='Bob';

COMMIT;
```

### SELECT ... FOR UPDATE example (prevent lost update)

```sql
-- Reserve 1 unit in inventory pattern (conceptual)
BEGIN;
-- lock inventory table row (assume inventory table exists)
SELECT qty FROM inventory WHERE sku='SKU-1' FOR UPDATE;
-- check qty and update
UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE sku='SKU-1' AND qty >= 1;
COMMIT;
```

---

# 5) ACID demos & explanation (practical exercises)

### Atomicity demo (roll back)

```sql
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO employees(emp_id, first_name, last_name, email, hire_date) VALUES (uuid_generate_v4(), 'Temp', 'User', 'temp@x.com', CURRENT_DATE);
-- Simulate error
SELECT 1/0;  -- causes error
COMMIT;
-- Transaction will be rolled back, no 'Temp User' row exists.
```

**Lesson:** Either all statements commit, or none.

### Isolation demo (serialization anomalies)

* Run two sessions simultaneously:

  * Session A:

    ```sql
    BEGIN;
    SELECT salary FROM salary_history WHERE emp_id = '...Bob...' AND end_date IS NULL;
    -- wait here (do nothing)
    ```
  * Session B:

    ```sql
    BEGIN;
    UPDATE salary_history SET salary = salary + 1000 WHERE emp_id = '...Bob...' AND end_date IS NULL;
    COMMIT;
    ```
  * Session A (resume):

    ```sql
    -- read again depending on isolation level you may see updated salary or not
    SELECT salary FROM salary_history WHERE emp_id = '...Bob...' AND end_date IS NULL;
    COMMIT;
    ```
* Change session isolation via `SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;` to see different behaviors.

**Lesson:** Isolation level changes concurrency guarantees.

### Durability demo (commit + crash)

* After `COMMIT`, stop Postgres server and restart; committed rows persist because of WAL.

---

# 6) Indexes, constraints & performance tips

* Use **primary keys** and **foreign keys** to maintain consistency.
* Add **unique constraints** on emails or business keys.
* Index foreign-key columns used in joins (`employees.dept_id`).
* Use **EXPLAIN ANALYZE** to inspect query plans.
* For heavy text search, use `GIN` indexes on `jsonb` or `tsvector`.
* Avoid too many indexes on write-heavy tables (inserts/updates cost).

---

# 7) Typical learning exercises (do these)

1. Write a query to get employees with salary > department average.
2. Show employees not assigned to any project.
3. Compute number of projects per employee and list top 5 contributors.
4. Use recursive CTE to list all direct and indirect reports for a manager.
5. Implement a safe salary raise that:

   * reads current salary,
   * inserts new salary\_history row,
   * uses optimistic locking or `FOR UPDATE` to prevent races.
6. Add a trigger that writes to `employee_events` whenever an employee is inserted/updated.
7. Create a view `v_current_employees` that shows employee details + current salary.

---

# 8) Example queries you’ll be asked in interviews (and why)

* "How do you get the manager of an employee?" — shows joins and self-joins (or recursive CTEs).

```sql
SELECT m.first_name, m.last_name
FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id
WHERE e.first_name = 'Bob';
```

* "How to find employees who never had a salary increase?" — uses window functions or self-join on salary\_history.
* "How would you prevent two users from simultaneously booking the same resource?" — answer: use `SELECT FOR UPDATE` (pessimistic) or optimistic locking with a version column and retry.

---

# 9) Short cheat-sheet: commands & tips

* Start transaction: `BEGIN;` — commit: `COMMIT;` — rollback: `ROLLBACK;`
* Set isolation: `SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;` (or at session level `SET SESSION CHARACTERISTICS AS TRANSACTION ...`)
* Lock a row: `SELECT * FROM table WHERE id=1 FOR UPDATE;`
* Show indexes: `\d+ table` in psql.
* Explain plan: `EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT ...`

---

# 10) Next steps & resources to practice

* Load the data above into Postgres and run the queries.
* Use `pgAdmin`, `DBeaver`, or `psql`.
* Build small apps or scripts to perform transactions (Java + Spring `@Transactional`, Python + psycopg2).
* Practice concurrency scenarios by opening two psql sessions.
* Try performance tuning: `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`, add an index, compare.

# 11) Bonus — small `v_current_employees` view & trigger example

```sql
-- View for current employees + current salary
CREATE VIEW v_current_employees AS
SELECT e.emp_id, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.email, d.name AS department, s.salary
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
LEFT JOIN LATERAL (
  SELECT salary FROM salary_history sh WHERE sh.emp_id = e.emp_id AND end_date IS NULL
) s ON true;

-- Trigger that logs inserts
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_employee_event() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO employee_events(emp_id, event_type, payload) VALUES (NEW.emp_id, TG_OP, row_to_json(NEW));
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_employee_insert AFTER INSERT ON employees
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION log_employee_event();
```

---

# 1) SQL Commands — Theory + Examples

SQL commands are categorized into **five main types**:

### 🔹 1.1 DDL (Data Definition Language)

Used to define schema (tables, views, indexes).

* `CREATE` → create objects.
* `ALTER` → modify schema.
* `DROP` → delete objects.

**Example:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE departments (
  dept_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE
);

ALTER TABLE departments ADD COLUMN location TEXT;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS temp_table;
```

---

### 🔹 1.2 DML (Data Manipulation Language)

Used to modify data inside tables.

* `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`.

**Example:**

```sql
INSERT INTO departments (name, location) VALUES ('Engineering', 'NYC');
UPDATE departments SET location = 'San Francisco' WHERE name = 'HR';
DELETE FROM departments WHERE name = 'Sales';
```

---

### 🔹 1.3 DQL (Data Query Language)

Used to query/fetch data.

* `SELECT`.

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT first_name, last_name, hire_date FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 1;
```

---

### 🔹 1.4 DCL (Data Control Language)

Used for permissions/security.

* `GRANT`, `REVOKE`.

**Example:**

```sql
GRANT SELECT ON employees TO analyst;
REVOKE UPDATE ON employees FROM analyst;
```

---

### 🔹 1.5 TCL (Transaction Control Language)

Used for managing transactions.

* `BEGIN/START TRANSACTION`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`.

**Example:**

```sql
BEGIN;
UPDATE employees SET dept_id = 2 WHERE emp_id = 'emp-001';
ROLLBACK; -- undo above update
```

---

# 2) SQL Functions

Functions = reusable blocks of SQL logic.

### Types of SQL functions:

1. **Aggregate functions** → operate on groups of rows.

   * `COUNT()`, `SUM()`, `AVG()`, `MIN()`, `MAX()`.

   ```sql
   SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) FROM salary_history WHERE end_date IS NULL GROUP BY dept_id;
   ```
2. **Scalar functions** → operate on single values.

   * `UPPER()`, `LOWER()`, `LENGTH()`, `ROUND()`.

   ```sql
   SELECT UPPER(first_name), LENGTH(last_name) FROM employees;
   ```
3. **Date functions** → manipulate dates.

   * `NOW()`, `AGE()`, `DATE_PART()`.

   ```sql
   SELECT first_name, AGE(hire_date) AS years_worked FROM employees;
   ```
4. **String functions** → manipulate text.

   * `CONCAT()`, `SUBSTRING()`.

   ```sql
   SELECT CONCAT(first_name, ' ', last_name) AS full_name FROM employees;
   ```
5. **User-defined functions (UDFs)** → custom logic, see Section 5.

---

# 3) SQL Joins — Theory + Text Diagrams

**Definition:** Join combines rows from two/more tables based on a related column.

### 🔹 3.1 INNER JOIN

Returns only matching rows.

**Diagram:**

```
Table A ●●●●
Table B   ●●●●
Result   ●● (only overlap)
```

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

---

### 🔹 3.2 LEFT JOIN

Returns all rows from left table + matches from right.

```
A ●●●●
B   ●●
Result = A●●●● (with NULLs if no match in B)
```

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

---

### 🔹 3.3 RIGHT JOIN

Opposite of LEFT JOIN.

```
A ●●
B   ●●●●
Result = B●●●● (with NULLs if no match in A)
```

---

### 🔹 3.4 FULL OUTER JOIN

All rows from both sides.

```
A ●●●
B   ●●●●
Result = All ●●●●●●
```

---

### 🔹 3.5 CROSS JOIN

Cartesian product (all combinations).

```
A (3 rows) × B (2 rows) = 6 rows
```

---

### 🔹 3.6 SELF JOIN

Join table to itself.

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT e.first_name AS employee, m.first_name AS manager
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id;
```

---

# 4) Normalization (with diagrams)

**Definition:** Process of organizing data to reduce redundancy & improve integrity.

### 🔹 1NF (First Normal Form)

* No repeating groups, atomic values.

**Bad (unnormalized):**

```
Employee(emp_id, name, phones[home, work])
```

**1NF:**

```
Employee(emp_id, name)
Phone(emp_id, type, number)
```

---

### 🔹 2NF (Second Normal Form)

* 1NF + no partial dependency on part of a composite key.

**Bad:**

```
Assignment(emp_id, project_id, emp_name) 
```

(emp\_name depends only on emp\_id, not project\_id)

**2NF:**

```
Employee(emp_id, name)
Assignment(emp_id, project_id)
```

---

### 🔹 3NF (Third Normal Form)

* 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key depends on non-key).

**Bad:**

```
Employee(emp_id, dept_id, dept_name)
```

(dept\_name depends on dept\_id, not emp\_id)

**3NF:**

```
Employee(emp_id, dept_id)
Department(dept_id, dept_name)
```

---

# 5) User-Defined Functions (UDFs)

**Definition:** A function written by user to encapsulate logic.

**Example: compute years worked**

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION years_worked(eid UUID)
RETURNS INT AS $$
  SELECT DATE_PART('year', AGE(hire_date))::int
  FROM employees WHERE emp_id = eid;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Usage
SELECT first_name, years_worked(emp_id) FROM employees;
```

---

# 6) Triggers (with text diagram)

**Definition:** Code that executes automatically in response to events (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE).

**Diagram (Trigger workflow):**

```
INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on Table
        ↓
   Trigger fires
        ↓
  Executes Function
        ↓
 Logs/Audit/Validation
```

**Example: log employee inserts into audit table**

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_employee_insert()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO employee_events(emp_id, event_type, payload)
  VALUES (NEW.emp_id, 'INSERT', row_to_json(NEW));
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_employee_insert
AFTER INSERT ON employees
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION log_employee_insert();
```

---

# 7) Stored Procedures (with example)

**Definition:** Stored procedures are routines stored in DB, can contain control logic (loops, IF), and can commit/rollback transactions.

**Example: give all employees in dept a salary raise**

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE raise_salary(dept INT, pct NUMERIC)
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  UPDATE salary_history
  SET salary = salary * (1 + pct/100.0)
  WHERE emp_id IN (SELECT emp_id FROM employees WHERE dept_id = dept)
    AND end_date IS NULL;
END;
$$;

-- Call procedure
CALL raise_salary(1, 10); -- raise by 10% for dept_id=1
```

---

✅ With these concepts, you now have:

* **Theory of all SQL building blocks**
* **Examples tied to Employee Management schema**
* **Diagrams for joins, normalization, trigger workflow**

Do you want me to now create a **single visual cheat-sheet (ASCII/PlantUML diagrams + key SQL syntax)** that you can print and keep for quick revision?


Got it 👍 — you want **more examples** + **better text diagrams inside box-like containers** so it looks structured and easy to revise.

Let’s go **topic by topic** with **multiple examples + container-style text diagrams**.
I’ll expand **Joins, Normalization, Functions, Triggers, and Stored Procedures**.

# 🔹 1) SQL Joins (with container text diagrams + multiple examples)

---

### INNER JOIN

Returns only rows with matching keys in both tables.

```
+-----------+         +-------------+
| Employees |         | Departments |
|-----------|         |-------------|
| emp_id    |         | dept_id     |
| dept_id   |   ⋂     | name        |
+-----------+         +-------------+

Result = overlap (only matches)
```

**Example 1:**

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, e.last_name, d.name AS dept
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

**Example 2:**

```sql
-- Employees working on a project
SELECT e.first_name, p.name AS project
FROM employees e
JOIN assignments a ON e.emp_id = a.emp_id
JOIN projects p ON p.project_id = a.project_id;
```

---

### LEFT JOIN

Returns all rows from left table, matching rows from right, NULL if no match.

```
+-----------+         +-------------+
| Employees |  --->   | Departments |
|-----------|         |-------------|
| ALL ROWS  |   ⋂     | MATCH ONLY  |
+-----------+         +-------------+

Result = all left + matches
```

**Example 1:**

```sql
-- Show all employees even if no dept assigned
SELECT e.first_name, d.name AS dept
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

**Example 2:**

```sql
-- Projects with assigned employees (or NULL if none)
SELECT p.name, e.first_name
FROM projects p
LEFT JOIN assignments a ON p.project_id = a.project_id
LEFT JOIN employees e ON a.emp_id = e.emp_id;
```

---

### RIGHT JOIN

Opposite of LEFT JOIN.

```
+-----------+         +-------------+
| Employees |         | Departments |
|-----------|   ⋂     |-------------|
| MATCH ONLY|  <---   | ALL ROWS    |
+-----------+         +-------------+

Result = all right + matches
```

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT d.name, e.first_name
FROM employees e
RIGHT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

---

### FULL OUTER JOIN

All rows from both sides.

```
+-----------+         +-------------+
| Employees |   ⋃     | Departments |
+-----------+         +-------------+

Result = everything, match + non-match
```

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, d.name
FROM employees e
FULL JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

---

### CROSS JOIN

All combinations (Cartesian product).

```
Employees (3 rows) × Projects (2 rows) = 6 rows
```

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, p.name
FROM employees e
CROSS JOIN projects p;
```

---

### SELF JOIN

Table joined with itself.

```
+-----------+
| Employees |
|-----------|
| emp_id    |
| manager_id|
+-----------+
      ⋈
+-----------+
| Employees |
|-----------|
| emp_id    |
+-----------+
```

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT e.first_name AS employee, m.first_name AS manager
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id;
```

---

# 🔹 2) Normalization (with diagrams + examples)

---

### 1NF (atomic values)

```
❌ Bad:
Employee(emp_id, name, phones[home,work])

✔ Good:
Employee(emp_id, name)
Phone(emp_id, type, number)
```

---

### 2NF (no partial dependency)

```
❌ Bad:
Assignment(emp_id, project_id, emp_name)

✔ Good:
Employee(emp_id, name)
Assignment(emp_id, project_id)
```

---

### 3NF (no transitive dependency)

```
❌ Bad:
Employee(emp_id, dept_id, dept_name)

✔ Good:
Employee(emp_id, dept_id)
Department(dept_id, dept_name)
```

**Example SQL:**

```sql
-- Employee table normalized
CREATE TABLE employees (
  emp_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  first_name TEXT,
  dept_id INT REFERENCES departments(dept_id)
);

-- Department table
CREATE TABLE departments (
  dept_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT
);
```

---

# 🔹 3) SQL Functions

---

### Built-in Function Example (String)

```sql
SELECT first_name, UPPER(last_name) AS last_upper FROM employees;
```

### Built-in Function Example (Date)

```sql
SELECT first_name, AGE(hire_date) AS experience FROM employees;
```

---

### User-Defined Function (UDF)

**Diagram:**

```
+------------+
| Function   |
|------------|
| Input args |
| Logic SQL  |
| Output     |
+------------+
```

**Example:**

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION years_worked(eid INT)
RETURNS INT AS $$
  SELECT DATE_PART('year', AGE(hire_date))::int
  FROM employees WHERE emp_id = eid;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Usage
SELECT first_name, years_worked(emp_id) FROM employees;
```

---

# 🔹 4) Triggers

---

**Diagram:**

```
TABLE EVENT (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)
          │
          ▼
     TRIGGER fires
          │
          ▼
  Calls Function (logic)
          │
          ▼
   Logs / Validates / Changes
```

**Example:** Audit insert

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_employee_insert()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO employee_events(emp_id, event_type, payload)
  VALUES (NEW.emp_id, 'INSERT', row_to_json(NEW));
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_employee_insert
AFTER INSERT ON employees
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION log_employee_insert();
```

---

# 🔹 5) Stored Procedures

---

**Diagram:**

```
+---------------------+
| Stored Procedure    |
|---------------------|
| DECLARE variables   |
| BEGIN ... END block |
| DML statements      |
| COMMIT/ROLLBACK     |
+---------------------+
```

**Example: raise salaries in a department**

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE raise_salary(dept INT, pct NUMERIC)
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  UPDATE salary_history
  SET salary = salary * (1 + pct/100.0)
  WHERE emp_id IN (
    SELECT emp_id FROM employees WHERE dept_id = dept
  ) AND end_date IS NULL;
END;
$$;

-- Call procedure
CALL raise_salary(1, 10);
```

# 🟢 1. SQL Commands

SQL commands are grouped into **5 categories**:

| Category                               | Purpose                | Examples                      |
| -------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| **DDL** (Data Definition Language)     | Define/alter schema    | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`     |
| **DML** (Data Manipulation Language)   | Change data            | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`  |
| **DQL** (Data Query Language)          | Query data             | `SELECT`                      |
| **DCL** (Data Control Language)        | Security & permissions | `GRANT`, `REVOKE`             |
| **TCL** (Transaction Control Language) | Manage transactions    | `BEGIN`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK` |

**Examples:**

```sql
-- DDL: create table
CREATE TABLE departments (
  dept_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  location TEXT
);

-- DML: insert
INSERT INTO departments (name, location) VALUES ('Engineering', 'NYC');

-- DQL: select
SELECT name FROM departments WHERE location = 'NYC';

-- DCL: grant permissions
GRANT SELECT ON employees TO analyst;

-- TCL: transaction rollback
BEGIN;
UPDATE employees SET dept_id = 2 WHERE emp_id = 101;
ROLLBACK; -- undo
```

---

# 🟢 2. SQL Functions

### Built-in Functions

* **Aggregate:** `COUNT()`, `SUM()`, `AVG()`, `MAX()`, `MIN()`
* **Scalar:** `UPPER()`, `LOWER()`, `LENGTH()`
* **Date:** `NOW()`, `AGE()`, `DATE_PART()`

**Examples:**

```sql
-- Count employees per department
SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id;

-- String functions
SELECT UPPER(first_name), LENGTH(last_name) FROM employees;

-- Date function
SELECT first_name, AGE(hire_date) AS years_worked FROM employees;
```

---

# 🟢 3. SQL Joins (Diagrams + Examples)

### INNER JOIN

```
+-----------+         +-------------+
| Employees |   ⋂     | Departments |
+-----------+         +-------------+
Result = only matching rows
```

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, d.name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

---

### LEFT JOIN

```
+-----------+         +-------------+
| Employees |   ⋂     | Departments |
|   ALL     |         |  MATCHING   |
+-----------+         +-------------+
Result = all left + matches
```

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, d.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

---

### RIGHT JOIN

```
+-----------+         +-------------+
| Employees |   ⋂     | Departments |
|  MATCHING |         |    ALL      |
+-----------+         +-------------+
```

```sql
SELECT d.name, e.first_name
FROM employees e
RIGHT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

---

### FULL OUTER JOIN

```
+-----------+         +-------------+
| Employees |   ⋃     | Departments |
+-----------+         +-------------+
Result = all rows from both
```

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, d.name
FROM employees e
FULL JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;
```

---

### CROSS JOIN

```
Employees (3 rows) × Projects (2 rows) = 6 rows
```

```sql
SELECT e.first_name, p.name
FROM employees e
CROSS JOIN projects p;
```

---

### SELF JOIN

```
+-----------+   ⋈   +-----------+
| Employees |       | Employees |
| (emp)     |       | (mgr)     |
+-----------+       +-----------+
```

```sql
SELECT e.first_name AS employee, m.first_name AS manager
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id;
```

---

# 🟢 4. Normalization

Normalization = process of organizing data to reduce redundancy.

### 1NF → Atomic values

```
❌ Bad:
Employee(emp_id, name, phones[home,work])

✔ Good:
Employee(emp_id, name)
Phone(emp_id, type, number)
```

---

### 2NF → No partial dependency

```
❌ Bad:
Assignment(emp_id, project_id, emp_name)

✔ Good:
Employee(emp_id, name)
Assignment(emp_id, project_id)
```

---

### 3NF → No transitive dependency

```
❌ Bad:
Employee(emp_id, dept_id, dept_name)

✔ Good:
Employee(emp_id, dept_id)
Department(dept_id, dept_name)
```

**SQL Example:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE employees (
  emp_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  first_name TEXT,
  dept_id INT REFERENCES departments(dept_id)
);

CREATE TABLE departments (
  dept_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT
);
```

# 🟢 5. User-Defined Functions (UDFs)

**Diagram:**

```
+------------+
| Function   |
|------------|
| Input args |
| Logic SQL  |
| Output     |
+------------+
```

**Example:** Years worked

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION years_worked(eid INT)
RETURNS INT AS $$
  SELECT DATE_PART('year', AGE(hire_date))::int
  FROM employees WHERE emp_id = eid;
$$ LANGUAGE sql;

-- Usage
SELECT first_name, years_worked(emp_id) FROM employees;
```

# 🟢 6. Triggers

**Diagram:**

```
TABLE EVENT (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)
          │
          ▼
     TRIGGER fires
          │
          ▼
  Executes Function
          │
          ▼
   Logs / Validation
```

**Example:** Log employee insert

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_employee_insert()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO employee_events(emp_id, event_type, payload)
  VALUES (NEW.emp_id, 'INSERT', row_to_json(NEW));
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_employee_insert
AFTER INSERT ON employees
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION log_employee_insert();
```

```
+---------------------+
```

**Example:** Raise salaries

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE raise_salary(dept INT, pct NUMERIC)
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  UPDATE salary_history
  SET salary = salary * (1 + pct/100.0)
```
