# 🔥 Arrays in Java (Part 5: Examples 61–80)

---

## **61. Find Intersection of Two Arrays**

👉 Amazon often asks this.

```java
int[] a = {1,2,2,3};
int[] b = {2,2};
Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
for (int x : a) for (int y : b) if (x==y) set.add(x);
System.out.println(set); // [2]
```

---

## **62. Find Union of Two Arrays**

```java
Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
set.addAll(Arrays.asList(1,2,3,4));
set.addAll(Arrays.asList(3,4,5,6));
System.out.println(set); // [1,2,3,4,5,6]
```

---

## **63. Intersection (Optimized with Sorting)**

👉 Use two pointers.

```java
int[] a = {1,2,2,3}, b = {2,2};
Arrays.sort(a); Arrays.sort(b);
int i=0,j=0;
while(i<a.length && j<b.length){
    if(a[i]==b[j]){ System.out.println(a[i]); i++; j++; }
    else if(a[i]<b[j]) i++; else j++;
}
```

---

## **64. First Missing Positive**

👉 FAANG classic.

```java
int[] arr={3,4,-1,1};
Set<Integer> s=new HashSet<>();
for(int n:arr) if(n>0) s.add(n);
int i=1;
while(true){
    if(!s.contains(i)){System.out.println(i);break;}
    i++;
}
```

---

## **65. Move Zeros to End**

```java
int[] arr={0,1,0,3,12};
int j=0;
for(int i=0;i<arr.length;i++){
    if(arr[i]!=0){int t=arr[j];arr[j]=arr[i];arr[i]=t;j++;}
}
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr)); // [1,3,12,0,0]
```

---

## **66. Find Duplicate in 1..n+1 Array**

👉 Floyd’s cycle detection.

```java
int[] nums={1,3,4,2,2};
int slow=nums[0], fast=nums[0];
do{ slow=nums[slow]; fast=nums[nums[fast]]; } while(slow!=fast);
slow=nums[0];
while(slow!=fast){ slow=nums[slow]; fast=nums[fast]; }
System.out.println("Duplicate="+slow);
```

---

## **67. Search in Rotated Sorted Array**

👉 Asked at Google.

```java
int[] arr={4,5,6,7,0,1,2};
int target=0,l=0,r=arr.length-1;
while(l<=r){
    int mid=(l+r)/2;
    if(arr[mid]==target){System.out.println(mid);break;}
    if(arr[l]<=arr[mid]){
        if(target>=arr[l] && target<arr[mid]) r=mid-1; else l=mid+1;
    } else {
        if(target>arr[mid] && target<=arr[r]) l=mid+1; else r=mid-1;
    }
}
```

---

## **68. Maximum Product Subarray**

```java
int[] arr={2,3,-2,4};
int max=arr[0],min=arr[0],res=arr[0];
for(int i=1;i<arr.length;i++){
    if(arr[i]<0){int t=max;max=min;min=t;}
    max=Math.max(arr[i],max*arr[i]);
    min=Math.min(arr[i],min*arr[i]);
    res=Math.max(res,max);
}
System.out.println("Max Product="+res); // 6
```

---

## **69. Longest Subarray with Sum k**

```java
int[] arr={10,5,2,7,1,9};
int k=15;
Map<Integer,Integer> map=new HashMap<>();
int sum=0,max=0;
for(int i=0;i<arr.length;i++){
    sum+=arr[i];
    if(sum==k) max=i+1;
    if(map.containsKey(sum-k)) max=Math.max(max,i-map.get(sum-k));
    map.putIfAbsent(sum,i);
}
System.out.println("Length="+max);
```

---

## **70. Find All Triplets with Sum Zero**

```java
int[] arr={-1,0,1,2,-1,-4};
Arrays.sort(arr);
for(int i=0;i<arr.length-2;i++){
    int l=i+1,r=arr.length-1;
    while(l<r){
        int sum=arr[i]+arr[l]+arr[r];
        if(sum==0){System.out.println(arr[i]+","+arr[l]+","+arr[r]); l++; r--; }
        else if(sum<0) l++; else r--;
    }
}
```

---

## **71. Maximum Circular Subarray Sum**

```java
int[] arr={5,-3,5};
int total=0,curMax=0,max=Integer.MIN_VALUE,curMin=0,min=Integer.MAX_VALUE;
for(int n:arr){
    curMax=Math.max(n,curMax+n);
    max=Math.max(max,curMax);
    curMin=Math.min(n,curMin+n);
    min=Math.min(min,curMin);
    total+=n;
}
System.out.println(max>0?Math.max(max,total-min):max);
```

---

## **72. Count Inversions (Merge Sort)**

👉 Important algorithm Q.

```java
long count=0;
int[] arr={2,4,1,3,5};
count=mergeSort(arr,0,arr.length-1);
System.out.println("Inversions="+count);

// mergeSort method omitted for brevity (divide+merge counting)
```

---

## **73. Minimum Jumps to Reach End**

👉 Dynamic programming.

```java
int[] arr={2,3,1,1,4};
int jumps=0,curEnd=0,curFar=0;
for(int i=0;i<arr.length-1;i++){
    curFar=Math.max(curFar,i+arr[i]);
    if(i==curEnd){jumps++; curEnd=curFar;}
}
System.out.println("Jumps="+jumps); // 2
```

---

## **74. Check if Array is Palindrome**

```java
int[] arr={1,2,3,2,1};
boolean pal=true;
for(int i=0;i<arr.length/2;i++){
    if(arr[i]!=arr[arr.length-1-i]) {pal=false;break;}
}
System.out.println("Palindrome? "+pal);
```

---

## **75. Sort Array by Parity**

```java
int[] arr={3,1,2,4};
int l=0,r=arr.length-1;
while(l<r){
    if(arr[l]%2>arr[r]%2){int t=arr[l];arr[l]=arr[r];arr[r]=t;}
    if(arr[l]%2==0) l++;
    if(arr[r]%2==1) r--;
}
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr)); // [4,2,1,3]
```

---

## **76. Replace Each Element with Greatest on Right**

```java
int[] arr={17,18,5,4,6,1};
int max=-1;
for(int i=arr.length-1;i>=0;i--){
    int t=arr[i];
    arr[i]=max;
    if(t>max) max=t;
}
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr)); // [18,6,6,6,1,-1]
```

---

## **77. Leaders in Array**

👉 Element > all to its right.

```java
int[] arr={16,17,4,3,5,2};
int max=Integer.MIN_VALUE;
for(int i=arr.length-1;i>=0;i--){
    if(arr[i]>max){System.out.print(arr[i]+" "); max=arr[i];}
}
```

---

## **78. Rearrange Positive and Negative Alternately**

```java
int[] arr={-1,-2,-3,4,5,6};
int[] res=new int[arr.length];
int pos=0,neg=0;
for(int n:arr) if(n>=0) pos++; else neg++;
int pi=0,ni=1;
for(int n:arr){
    if(n>=0){res[pi]=n; pi+=2;} else {res[ni]=n; ni+=2;}
}
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(res));
```

---

## **79. Count Subarrays with Given XOR**

```java
int[] arr={4,2,2,6,4};
int k=6;
Map<Integer,Integer> map=new HashMap<>();
map.put(0,1);
int xor=0,count=0;
for(int n:arr){
    xor^=n;
    if(map.containsKey(xor^k)) count+=map.get(xor^k);
    map.put(xor,map.getOrDefault(xor,0)+1);
}
System.out.println("Count="+count);
```

---

## **80. Minimum Size Subarray Sum**

👉 Sliding window tricky.

```java
int[] arr={2,3,1,2,4,3};
int target=7,sum=0,l=0,min=Integer.MAX_VALUE;
for(int r=0;r<arr.length;r++){
    sum+=arr[r];
    while(sum>=target){
        min=Math.min(min,r-l+1);
        sum-=arr[l++];
    }
}
System.out.println("Min length="+(min==Integer.MAX_VALUE?0:min)); // 2
```

# 🔥 Arrays in Java (Part 6: Examples 81–100)

---

## **81. Next Permutation**

👉 Asked in Google/Microsoft.

```java
int[] arr={1,2,3};
int i=arr.length-2;
while(i>=0 && arr[i]>=arr[i+1]) i--;
if(i>=0){
    int j=arr.length-1;
    while(arr[j]<=arr[i]) j--;
    int t=arr[i];arr[i]=arr[j];arr[j]=t;
}
Arrays.sort(arr,i+1,arr.length);
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr)); // [1,3,2]
```

---

## **82. Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array**

```java
int[] arr={3,4,5,1,2};
int l=0,r=arr.length-1;
while(l<r){
    int mid=(l+r)/2;
    if(arr[mid]>arr[r]) l=mid+1;
    else r=mid;
}
System.out.println("Min="+arr[l]); // 1
```

---

## **83. Count Pairs with Given Sum**

```java
int[] arr={1,5,7,1};
int k=6,count=0;
Map<Integer,Integer> map=new HashMap<>();
for(int n:arr){
    if(map.containsKey(k-n)) count+=map.get(k-n);
    map.put(n,map.getOrDefault(n,0)+1);
}
System.out.println("Pairs="+count); // 2
```

---

## **84. Maximum Distance Between Equal Elements**

```java
int[] arr={1,2,3,1,4,2,1};
Map<Integer,Integer> first=new HashMap<>();
int max=0;
for(int i=0;i<arr.length;i++){
    first.putIfAbsent(arr[i],i);
    max=Math.max(max,i-first.get(arr[i]));
}
System.out.println("Max Distance="+max); // 6
```

---

## **85. Maximum Length Subarray with Equal 0s & 1s**

👉 Trick: convert 0 → -1.

```java
int[] arr={0,1,0};
Map<Integer,Integer> map=new HashMap<>();
map.put(0,-1);
int sum=0,max=0;
for(int i=0;i<arr.length;i++){
    sum+=arr[i]==0?-1:1;
    if(map.containsKey(sum)) max=Math.max(max,i-map.get(sum));
    else map.put(sum,i);
}
System.out.println("Max length="+max); // 2
```

---

## **86. Stock Buy & Sell (Max Profit)**

```java
int[] prices={7,1,5,3,6,4};
int min=Integer.MAX_VALUE,profit=0;
for(int p:prices){
    if(p<min) min=p;
    profit=Math.max(profit,p-min);
}
System.out.println("Profit="+profit); // 5
```

---

## **87. Stock Buy & Sell (Multiple Transactions)**

```java
int[] prices={7,1,5,3,6,4};
int profit=0;
for(int i=1;i<prices.length;i++)
    if(prices[i]>prices[i-1]) profit+=prices[i]-prices[i-1];
System.out.println("Profit="+profit); // 7
```

---

## **88. Median of Two Sorted Arrays (Optimized O(log(min(n,m))))**

👉 Binary partition trick — tough FAANG Q (only skeleton shown).

```java
// Given two sorted arrays A & B
// Binary search smaller array, partition left/right
// Ensure maxLeftA <= minRightB && maxLeftB <= minRightA
// Compute median
```

---

## **89. Rotate Array by Reversal Algorithm**

```java
int[] arr={1,2,3,4,5,6,7}; int k=3;
k%=arr.length;
reverse(arr,0,arr.length-1);
reverse(arr,0,k-1);
reverse(arr,k,arr.length-1);
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr));

static void reverse(int[] a,int l,int r){
    while(l<r){int t=a[l];a[l]=a[r];a[r]=t;l++;r--;}
}
```

---

## **90. Find Subarray with Maximum Product**

👉 Variant of Kadane. Already did earlier, but interviewers mix it with negative numbers.

---

## **91. Count Subarrays Divisible by K**

```java
int[] arr={4,5,0,-2,-3,1};
int k=5,count=0,sum=0;
Map<Integer,Integer> map=new HashMap<>();
map.put(0,1);
for(int n:arr){
    sum+=n;
    int rem=((sum%k)+k)%k;
    count+=map.getOrDefault(rem,0);
    map.put(rem,map.getOrDefault(rem,0)+1);
}
System.out.println("Count="+count); // 7
```

---

## **92. Minimum Operations to Make Array Palindrome**

👉 Count mismatches.

```java
int[] arr={1,2,3,2,2};
int ops=0,l=0,r=arr.length-1;
while(l<r){
    if(arr[l]!=arr[r]) ops++;
    l++; r--;
}
System.out.println("Ops="+ops);
```

---

## **93. Subarray Sum Equals K (Prefix Sum)**

```java
int[] arr={1,1,1}; int k=2;
Map<Integer,Integer> map=new HashMap<>();
map.put(0,1);
int sum=0,count=0;
for(int n:arr){
    sum+=n;
    count+=map.getOrDefault(sum-k,0);
    map.put(sum,map.getOrDefault(sum,0)+1);
}
System.out.println("Count="+count); // 2
```

---

## **94. Max Chunks to Make Sorted**

👉 Trick Q.

```java
int[] arr={1,0,2,3,4};
int max=0,chunks=0;
for(int i=0;i<arr.length;i++){
    max=Math.max(max,arr[i]);
    if(max==i) chunks++;
}
System.out.println("Chunks="+chunks); // 4
```

---

## **95. Candy Distribution**

👉 Greedy + array.

```java
int[] ratings={1,0,2};
int n=ratings.length;
int[] candies=new int[n];
Arrays.fill(candies,1);
for(int i=1;i<n;i++) if(ratings[i]>ratings[i-1]) candies[i]=candies[i-1]+1;
for(int i=n-2;i>=0;i--) if(ratings[i]>ratings[i+1]) candies[i]=Math.max(candies[i],candies[i+1]+1);
System.out.println(Arrays.stream(candies).sum()); // 5
```

---

## **96. Gas Station Problem**

👉 Greedy with arrays.

```java
int[] gas={1,2,3,4,5}, cost={3,4,5,1,2};
int tank=0,start=0,total=0;
for(int i=0;i<gas.length;i++){
    tank+=gas[i]-cost[i];
    if(tank<0){start=i+1;tank=0;}
    total+=gas[i]-cost[i];
}
System.out.println(total<0?-1:start); // 3
```

---

## **97. Jump Game (Can Reach End?)**

```java
int[] arr={2,3,1,1,4};
int far=0;
for(int i=0;i<arr.length;i++){
    if(i>far){System.out.println("False");break;}
    far=Math.max(far,i+arr[i]);
}
System.out.println("True");
```

---

## **98. Minimum Platforms (Train Schedule)**

👉 Interval problem.

```java
int[] arr={900,940,950,1100,1500,1800};
int[] dep={910,1200,1120,1130,1900,2000};
Arrays.sort(arr); Arrays.sort(dep);
int plat=0,max=0,i=0,j=0;
while(i<arr.length && j<dep.length){
    if(arr[i]<=dep[j]){plat++; max=Math.max(max,plat); i++;}
    else {plat--; j++;}
}
System.out.println("Platforms="+max);
```

---

## **99. Merge Intervals**

```java
int[][] intervals={{1,3},{2,6},{8,10},{15,18}};
Arrays.sort(intervals,(a,b)->a[0]-b[0]);
List<int[]> res=new ArrayList<>();
int[] cur=intervals[0];
for(int i=1;i<intervals.length;i++){
    if(cur[1]>=intervals[i][0]) cur[1]=Math.max(cur[1],intervals[i][1]);
    else {res.add(cur);cur=intervals[i];}
}
res.add(cur);
System.out.println(res);
```

  * Shallow vs deep copy
  * ArrayStoreException
  * Fixed size vs dynamic collections
* Practice LeetCode/FAANG array questions (90% interview coverage).

Here’s the first 20 for Strings 👇

🔥 Strings in Java (Part 1: 1–20)
Theory
Core Properties

Immutable → once created, can’t be changed.

Stored in String Constant Pool (SCP) → saves memory.

String is a final class (java.lang).

Common pitfalls: == vs .equals(), concatenation in loops, memory leaks with substrings (older JDKs).

Practical Examples
1. String Creation
String s1 = "Hello";              // in SCP
String s2 = new String("Hello");  // new object in heap
System.out.println(s1 == s2);     // false
System.out.println(s1.equals(s2));// true


👉 Interview trap: == checks reference, not content.

2. String Length
String s = "Java";
System.out.println(s.length()); // 4

3. charAt()
System.out.println("Java".charAt(2)); // v

4. Substring
String s = "HelloWorld";
System.out.println(s.substring(0,5)); // Hello

5. equals() vs equalsIgnoreCase()
String a="Java", b="java";
System.out.println(a.equals(b));            // false
System.out.println(a.equalsIgnoreCase(b));  // true

6. Compare Strings (compareTo)
System.out.println("apple".compareTo("banana")); // -1


👉 Negative = smaller, 0 = equal, positive = greater.

7. Concatenation
String s="Hello"+" World";
System.out.println(s); // Hello World


👉 Behind the scenes → StringBuilder used.

8. trim()
String s="  hello  ";
System.out.println(s.trim()); // "hello"

9. toUpperCase & toLowerCase
System.out.println("java".toUpperCase()); // JAVA

10. replace()
String s="java";
System.out.println(s.replace('a','o')); // jovo

11. replaceAll (Regex-based)
String s="a1b2c3";
System.out.println(s.replaceAll("\\d","")); // abc

12. split()
String s="a,b,c";
String[] parts=s.split(",");
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(parts)); // [a, b, c]

13. join()
String s = String.join("-", "2025","09","14");
System.out.println(s); // 2025-09-14

14. startsWith / endsWith
System.out.println("hello".startsWith("he")); // true
System.out.println("hello".endsWith("lo"));   // true

15. indexOf() / lastIndexOf()
String s="hello world";
System.out.println(s.indexOf("o"));      // 4
System.out.println(s.lastIndexOf("o"));  // 7

16. contains()
System.out.println("java interview".contains("inter")); // true

17. Interning
String s1=new String("java");
String s2=s1.intern();
String s3="java";
System.out.println(s2==s3); // true


👉 Interviewer trap: intern() forces SCP usage.

18. ValueOf()
int num=100;
String s=String.valueOf(num);
System.out.println(s+50); // "10050"

19. toCharArray()
String s="abc";
char[] arr=s.toCharArray();
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr)); // [a, b, c]

20. Format Strings
String s=String.format("Name: %s, Age: %d","Alice",25);
System.out.println(s);


👉 Very popular in finance MNCs.

✅ That’s the first 20 String theory+examples. You now know:

Basics (creation, pool, immutability)

Operations (substring, replace, split)

Advanced (intern, format, regex replaceAll)


🔥 Strings in Java (Part 2: 21–40)
21. Reverse a String
String s = "hello";
String rev = new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString();
System.out.println(rev); // olleh


👉 Interviewer trap: don’t reverse manually with concatenation (slow).

22. Check Palindrome
String s="madam";
boolean isPal = s.equals(new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString());
System.out.println(isPal); // true

23. Remove White Spaces
String s="a b  c";
System.out.println(s.replaceAll("\\s","")); // abc

24. Count Vowels & Consonants
String s="Interview";
int vowels=0,cons=0;
for(char c:s.toLowerCase().toCharArray()){
    if("aeiou".indexOf(c)!=-1) vowels++;
    else if(Character.isLetter(c)) cons++;
}
System.out.println("Vowels="+vowels+", Consonants="+cons);

25. Count Words in a String
String s="Java is great";
System.out.println(s.split("\\s+").length); // 3

26. Find Duplicate Characters
String s="programming";
Map<Character,Integer> map=new HashMap<>();
for(char c:s.toCharArray()) map.put(c,map.getOrDefault(c,0)+1);
map.entrySet().stream().filter(e->e.getValue()>1)
    .forEach(e->System.out.println(e.getKey()+"="+e.getValue()));

27. Remove Duplicates
String s="programming";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(char c:s.toCharArray()){
    if(sb.indexOf(String.valueOf(c))==-1) sb.append(c);
}
System.out.println(sb); // progamin

28. Anagram Check

👉 Common MNC Q.

String a="listen", b="silent";
char[] arr1=a.toCharArray(), arr2=b.toCharArray();
Arrays.sort(arr1); Arrays.sort(arr2);
System.out.println(Arrays.equals(arr1,arr2)); // true

29. First Non-Repeating Character
String s="swiss";
Map<Character,Integer> map=new LinkedHashMap<>();
for(char c:s.toCharArray()) map.put(c,map.getOrDefault(c,0)+1);
for(var e:map.entrySet()) if(e.getValue()==1){System.out.println(e.getKey());break;}

30. String Rotation Check

👉 Check if s2 is rotation of s1.

String s1="ABCD", s2="CDAB";
System.out.println((s1+s1).contains(s2)); // true

31. All Substrings
String s="abc";
for(int i=0;i<s.length();i++)
    for(int j=i+1;j<=s.length();j++)
        System.out.println(s.substring(i,j));

32. Longest Palindromic Substring

👉 Dynamic Programming / expand around center.

String s="babad";
String longest="";
for(int i=0;i<s.length();i++){
    String odd=expand(s,i,i), even=expand(s,i,i+1);
    if(odd.length()>longest.length()) longest=odd;
    if(even.length()>longest.length()) longest=even;
}
System.out.println(longest);

static String expand(String s,int l,int r){
    while(l>=0 && r<s.length() && s.charAt(l)==s.charAt(r)){l--;r++;}
    return s.substring(l+1,r);
}

33. Longest Common Prefix
String[] strs={"flower","flow","flight"};
Arrays.sort(strs);
String first=strs[0], last=strs[strs.length-1];
int i=0;
while(i<first.length() && first.charAt(i)==last.charAt(i)) i++;
System.out.println(first.substring(0,i)); // fl

34. Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters

👉 Classic FAANG Q.

String s="abcabcbb";
Set<Character> set=new HashSet<>();
int l=0,max=0;
for(int r=0;r<s.length();r++){
    while(set.contains(s.charAt(r))) set.remove(s.charAt(l++));
    set.add(s.charAt(r));
    max=Math.max(max,r-l+1);
}
System.out.println(max); // 3

35. Valid Parentheses

👉 Often asked in coding rounds.

String s="({[]})";
Stack<Character> st=new Stack<>();
for(char c:s.toCharArray()){
    if("({[".indexOf(c)!=-1) st.push(c);
    else {
        if(st.isEmpty() || "({[".indexOf(st.pop())!="({[".indexOf(c)-1) {
            System.out.println("Invalid"); return;
        }
    }
}
System.out.println(st.isEmpty()?"Valid":"Invalid");

36. String Compression (aab → a2b1)
String s="aabcccccaaa";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
int count=1;
for(int i=1;i<=s.length();i++){
    if(i<s.length() && s.charAt(i)==s.charAt(i-1)) count++;
    else { sb.append(s.charAt(i-1)).append(count); count=1; }
}
System.out.println(sb); // a2b1c5a3

37. Group Anagrams
String[] words={"eat","tea","tan","ate","nat","bat"};
Map<String,List<String>> map=new HashMap<>();
for(String w:words){
    char[] ch=w.toCharArray();
    Arrays.sort(ch);
    map.computeIfAbsent(new String(ch),x->new ArrayList<>()).add(w);
}
System.out.println(map.values());

38. Check if String is Numeric
String s="1234";
System.out.println(s.matches("\\d+")); // true

39. Reverse Each Word in Sentence
String s="Hello World";
for(String w:s.split(" "))
    System.out.print(new StringBuilder(w).reverse()+" ");

40. Remove All Digits
String s="a1b2c3";
System.out.println(s.replaceAll("\\d","")); // abc

String manipulation (reverse, palindrome, substring)

Regex tricks


41. Reverse Words in a Sentence
String s="Java is fun";
String[] words=s.split(" ");
Collections.reverse(Arrays.asList(words));
System.out.println(String.join(" ",words)); // fun is Java

42. Check if Two Strings are Isomorphic

👉 Each char of one maps uniquely to other.

String s="egg", t="add";
Map<Character,Character> map=new HashMap<>();
Set<Character> used=new HashSet<>();
boolean iso=true;
for(int i=0;i<s.length();i++){
    char c1=s.charAt(i), c2=t.charAt(i);
    if(map.containsKey(c1) && map.get(c1)!=c2) iso=false;
    if(!map.containsKey(c1) && used.contains(c2)) iso=false;
    map.put(c1,c2); used.add(c2);
}
System.out.println("Isomorphic? "+iso);

43. Check if Two Strings are Subsequence
String s="abc", t="ahbgdc";
int i=0,j=0;
while(i<s.length() && j<t.length()){
    if(s.charAt(i)==t.charAt(j)) i++;
    j++;
}
System.out.println(i==s.length()); // true

44. Minimum Window Substring

👉 Classic tough FAANG Q.

String s="ADOBECODEBANC", t="ABC";
Map<Character,Integer> need=new HashMap<>();
for(char c:t.toCharArray()) need.put(c,need.getOrDefault(c,0)+1);
int have=0,needCount=need.size(),l=0,start=0,len=Integer.MAX_VALUE;
Map<Character,Integer> window=new HashMap<>();
for(int r=0;r<s.length();r++){
    char c=s.charAt(r);
    window.put(c,window.getOrDefault(c,0)+1);
    if(need.containsKey(c) && window.get(c).equals(need.get(c))) have++;
    while(have==needCount){
        if(r-l+1<len){start=l;len=r-l+1;}
        char left=s.charAt(l++);
        window.put(left,window.get(left)-1);
        if(need.containsKey(left)&&window.get(left)<need.get(left)) have--;
    }
}
System.out.println(len==Integer.MAX_VALUE?"":s.substring(start,start+len)); // BANC

45. Longest Repeating Character Replacement

👉 LeetCode favorite.

String s="AABABBA"; int k=1;
int[] count=new int[26];
int l=0,max=0,res=0;
for(int r=0;r<s.length();r++){
    count[s.charAt(r)-'A']++;
    max=Math.max(max,count[s.charAt(r)-'A']);
    while(r-l+1-max>k){count[s.charAt(l)-'A']--; l++;}
    res=Math.max(res,r-l+1);
}
System.out.println(res); // 4

46. Longest Repeating Substring
String s="banana";
int n=s.length(),longest=0;
for(int i=0;i<n;i++){
    for(int j=i+1;j<n;j++){
        int len=0;
        while(j+len<n && s.charAt(i+len)==s.charAt(j+len)) len++;
        longest=Math.max(longest,len);
    }
}
System.out.println(longest); // 3 ("ana")

47. Regex: Extract Numbers
String s="order123id456";
Matcher m=Pattern.compile("\\d+").matcher(s);
while(m.find()) System.out.println(m.group()); // 123 456

48. Regex: Validate Email
String email="test@mail.com";
System.out.println(email.matches("^[\\w.-]+@[\\w.-]+\\.[a-zA-Z]{2,6}$")); // true

49. Regex: Validate IP
String ip="192.168.1.1";
System.out.println(ip.matches("((25[0-5]|2[0-4]\\d|[01]?\\d\\d?)\\.){3}(25[0-5]|2[0-4]\\d|[01]?\\d\\d?)"));

50. Regex: Extract Words
String text="Java is awesome";
Matcher m=Pattern.compile("\\w+").matcher(text);
while(m.find()) System.out.println(m.group());

51. Regex: Remove Special Characters
String s="H@e#l!l$o";
System.out.println(s.replaceAll("[^a-zA-Z0-9]","")); // Hello

52. Count Occurrences of Word

System.out.println(count); // 3

53. Check if String Contains Only Digits
String s="12345";
System.out.println(s.chars().allMatch(Character::isDigit)); // true

54. Check if String is Pangram

👉 Contains all alphabets.

String s="The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog";
boolean pangram=s.toLowerCase().chars()
    .filter(Character::isLetter).distinct().count()==26;
System.out.println(pangram); // true

55. Check if String is Heterogram (No Repeated Letter)
String s="lamp";
boolean hetero=s.toLowerCase().chars().distinct().count()==s.length();
System.out.println(hetero); // true

56. Print All Permutations of String
void permute(String s,String out){
    if(s.isEmpty()){System.out.println(out);return;}
    for(int i=0;i<s.length();i++)
        permute(s.substring(0,i)+s.substring(i+1),out+s.charAt(i));
}
permute("abc","");

57. Smallest Window Containing All Distinct Characters

👉 Tough sliding window.

String s="aabcbcdbca";
Set<Character> set=new HashSet<>();
for(char c:s.toCharArray()) set.add(c);
int distinct=set.size(),count=0,l=0,min=Integer.MAX_VALUE,start=0;
Map<Character,Integer> map=new HashMap<>();
for(int r=0;r<s.length();r++){
    map.put(s.charAt(r),map.getOrDefault(s.charAt(r),0)+1);
    if(map.size()==distinct){
        while(map.get(s.charAt(l))>1){map.put(s.charAt(l),map.get(s.charAt(l))-1);l++;}
        if(r-l+1<min){min=r-l+1;start=l;}
    }
}
System.out.println(s.substring(start,start+min)); // dbca

58. Remove Consecutive Duplicates
String s="aaabbcddd";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<s.length();i++){
    if(i==0 || s.charAt(i)!=s.charAt(i-1)) sb.append(s.charAt(i));
}
System.out.println(sb); // abcd

59. Remove All Adjacent Duplicates
String s="abbaca";
Stack<Character> st=new Stack<>();
for(char c:s.toCharArray()){
    if(!st.isEmpty() && st.peek()==c) st.pop();
    else st.push(c);
}
System.out.println(st.toString()); // [a,c,a] => aca

60. String Internals – Memory Trap

👉 Theory:

String literals go to String Pool.

new String("abc") → heap + optional pool reference.

Too many substrings (old JDK) could leak memory → fixed in JDK 7+.

String big="abcdefghij";
String small=big.substring(2,5); // shares char[] before JDK 7
System.out.println(small); // cde


✅ That’s Strings 41–60 done. You’ve now covered:

Regex (validation, extraction)

FAANG problems (min window, longest substring)

Theoretical traps (interning, memory leaks)


61 — KMP (Knuth–Morris–Pratt) pattern search

Why: O(n + m) substring search — asked when naive search is too slow.
Idea: Precompute LPS (longest proper prefix which is suffix) table to skip comparisons.

// skeleton: compute lps[] then run KMP
int[] computeLPS(String pat){
  int m=pat.length(), len=0; int[] lps=new int[m];
  lps[0]=0; int i=1;
  while(i<m){
    if(pat.charAt(i)==pat.charAt(len)){ lps[i++]=++len; }
    else if(len>0) len=lps[len-1];
    else lps[i++]=0;
  }
  return lps;
}
boolean kmp(String txt, String pat){
  int[] lps=computeLPS(pat); int i=0,j=0;
  while(i<txt.length()){
    if(txt.charAt(i)==pat.charAt(j)){ i++; j++; if(j==pat.length()) return true; }
    else if(j>0) j=lps[j-1];
    else i++;
  }
  return false;
}


Interview tip: Explain LPS table construction clearly.

62 — Rabin–Karp (rolling hash)

Why: Efficient multiple-pattern search; good for hashing-based checks.
Idea: Use rolling hash to compute substring hashes in O(1) each.

long rollingHash(String s, int base, int mod){
  long h=0;
  for(char c:s.toCharArray()) h=(h*base + c) % mod;
  return h;
}
// then slide window updating h = (h - leading*power)*base + new


Pitfall: Hash collisions — verify match after hash equality.

63 — Z-algorithm

Why: Alternative to KMP for pattern matching & other string problems (longest substring starting at position).
Idea: Build Z-array where Z[i] = longest substring starting at i matching prefix.

int[] zAlgo(String s){
  int n=s.length(); int[] z=new int[n];
  int l=0,r=0;
  for(int i=1;i<n;i++){
    if(i<=r) z[i]=Math.min(r-i+1, z[i-l]);
    while(i+z[i]<n && s.charAt(z[i])==s.charAt(i+z[i])) z[i]++;
    if(i+z[i]-1>r){ l=i; r=i+z[i]-1; }
  }
  return z;
}

64 — Manacher’s algorithm (longest palindromic substring in O(n))

Why: Fastest method for longest palindrome; impressive in interviews.
Idea: Transform string with separators and expand with center radii.

// Sketch: transform s -> ^#a#b#...#$ then compute P[] with center+right trick.


Tip: Explain transform step and how P array works.

65 — Suffix Array / Suffix Automaton basics

Why: Powerful for many substring queries (longest repeated substring, distinct substrings).
Note: Building suffix array (n log n) and LCP array is advanced — usually explain idea and complexity.

Idea: suffixes sorted lexicographically; LCP helps compute repeats.

66 — Trie (Prefix tree) for many-string operations

Why: Fast prefix search, autocomplete, dictionary tasks.
Example: Insert/search words in O(length).

class Trie {
  Trie[] next = new Trie[26];
  boolean end=false;
  void insert(String s){ Trie node=this; for(char c:s.toCharArray()){ int i=c-'a'; if(node.next[i]==null) node.next[i]=new Trie(); node=node.next[i]; } node.end=true; }
  boolean search(String s){ Trie node=this; for(char c:s.toCharArray()){ node=node.next[c-'a']; if(node==null) return false; } return node.end; }
}

67 — Rabin–Karp variant for plagiarism / rolling window

Why: Useful in system design interviews for approximate matching; show how to compare large texts with chunked hashing.

Pitfall: Choose big mod and base; handle collisions.

68 — Edit Distance (Levenshtein)

Why: Classic DP problem — minimal edits (insert/delete/replace).
Code skeleton:

int editDist(String a, String b){
  int n=a.length(), m=b.length();
  int[][] dp=new int[n+1][m+1];
  for(int i=0;i<=n;i++) dp[i][0]=i;
  for(int j=0;j<=m;j++) dp[0][j]=j;
  for(int i=1;i<=n;i++) for(int j=1;j<=m;j++){
    if(a.charAt(i-1)==b.charAt(j-1)) dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1];
    else dp[i][j]=1+Math.min(dp[i-1][j-1], Math.min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]));
  }
  return dp[n][m];
}


Interview tip: Discuss optimization to O(min(n,m)) space by rolling arrays.

69 — Wildcard Matching ( ? and * )

Why: Pattern matching problem with backtracking/DP.
Approach: DP boolean table or two-pointer greedy for *.

Greedy approach: use two pointers, track last '*' position and backtrack when mismatch.

70 — Unicode normalization (NFC/NFD)

Why: Real-world systems must compare user input correctly (accents, composed/decomposed forms).
Java API:

import java.text.Normalizer;
String a = Normalizer.normalize(input, Normalizer.Form.NFC);


Note: Important in internationalized apps.

71 — Grapheme clusters vs code points vs chars

Why: char is UTF-16 code unit; a user-perceived character can be multiple code units (surrogate pairs + combining marks).
Example:

String s = "𝔘"; // a character outside BMP: length() == 2 (two code units)
System.out.println(s.length());               // 2
System.out.println(s.codePointCount(0,s.length())); // 1


Interview angle: Explain difference and how to iterate code points (String.codePoints()).

72 — UTF-8 vs UTF-16 encoding

Why: Serialization, byte lengths, I/O, storage trade-offs.
Key: Java strings are UTF-16 internally; converting to bytes needs explicit charset.

byte[] utf8 = s.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);

73 — Canonicalization & Security (avoid injection)

Why: Normalize inputs before comparisons/validation to avoid vulnerabilities.
Tip: Use prepared statements, whitelist validation, and Normalizer.

74 — StringBuilder vs + operator performance

Why: Interviewers expect knowledge about concatenation in loops.
Rule: Use + for few concatenations or compile-time constants; for heavy dynamic concatenation use StringBuilder.

// Bad:
String s="";
for(String part: list) s += part; // O(n^2) due to repeated allocations

// Good:
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(String part: list) sb.append(part);
String result=sb.toString();

75 — String.intern() use and pitfalls

Why: Interning can reduce memory but also increase permanent pool pressure and classloader leakage.
Note: In modern JVMs interned strings live on heap; still, interning large dynamic strings is risky.

String s = new String("hello").intern();

76 — Canonical Unicode Collation (locale-sensitive compare)

Why: Sorting/search should respect locale (e.g., accents).
API:

import java.text.Collator;
Collator coll = Collator.getInstance(Locale.FRANCE);
coll.compare("é", "e");

77 — Memory/performance: substring behavior (older vs newer JDK)

Why: Older JDK substring shared char[] of original leading to memory leaks; modern JDK copies the subarray.
Interview tip: Know history and why copying is safer.

78 — Byte vs Char indexing (danger with multibyte encodings)

Why: When working with external byte streams, indexing bytes != indexing characters. Always decode properly before indexing.

byte[] bytes = s.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);

79 — Efficient immutable-string builders (String.concat, join, collectors)

Why: Show knowledge of modern APIs.

String result = String.join(",", list);
String result2 = list.stream().collect(Collectors.joining(","));

80 — String security: storing secrets

Why: String immutability means secret values (passwords) remain in memory until GC — prefer char[] for passwords and overwrite when done.

char[] pwd = console.readPassword();
Arrays.fill(pwd, '\0'); // clear when done


Interview tip: Always mention char[] for sensitive data.


81 — Suffix Array (construction idea & use-cases)

What: Sorted array of all suffixes of a string. Used for fast substring queries, repeated substrings, and pattern problems.
Sketch: Build suffixes and sort (O(n log² n)), or use suffix-array algorithms (O(n log n) or O(n)). Combine with LCP to answer many queries.
Use-case: Longest repeated substring → max LCP.
Interview angle: Explain suffix array + LCP relation; mention memory/time tradeoffs.

82 — Suffix Tree (Ukkonen’s algorithm overview)

What: A compressed trie of all suffixes; supports many queries in O(n).
Note: Building Ukkonen is advanced; explain the idea (active point, suffix links) rather than coding fully.
Use-case: Fast longest repeated substring, substring checks in O(m).
Interview angle: When to prefer suffix tree vs suffix array (speed vs memory).

83 — Suffix Automaton (SAM) — compact automaton for substrings

What: DFA-like structure that can enumerate all distinct substrings and answer longest repeated substring queries efficiently.
Why mention: SAM is compact and powerful; impresses interviewers if you outline transitions/links.
Interview angle: Explain how SAM stores end-pos sets and why it’s linear.

84 — Count Distinct Substrings (using suffix array / SAM)

What: Number of distinct substrings = n*(n+1)/2 - sum(LCP).
Code sketch: Build suffix array + LCP and apply formula.
Interview angle: Demonstrate derivation and complexity.

85 — Minimal Rotation (Booth’s algorithm)

What: Find lexicographically smallest rotation in O(n). Useful in string canonicalization.
Sketch: Two-pointer algorithm on doubled string to find start index.
Interview angle: Explain why doubling the string and linear scan works; show code sketch if asked.

86 — Aho–Corasick (multiple-pattern search)

What: Build trie of patterns + failure links; search all patterns in O(n + totalMatches).
Use-case: Virus scanning, multiple regex-like patterns.
Interview angle: Compare with naive repeated KMP; explain automaton building and failure transitions.

87 — Burrows–Wheeler Transform (BWT) + FM-index (conceptual)

What: BWT + suffix array enables compressed, indexable text for fast substring search (used in bioinformatics).
Interview angle: Explain transform idea and how it’s used for compression and indexed search.

88 — Eertree (Palindromic Tree)

What: Data structure that stores all palindromic substrings in linear time. Good for counting distinct palindromes.
Interview angle: Describe nodes as palindromic substrings, suffix links for palindromes. Great follow-up to palindromic substring problems.

89 — Rabin-Karp for multiple substrings & rolling hash collisions

What: Use rolling hash with mod and base; for multiple patterns, use hashing + verification.
Pitfall: Collisions — always verify on hash match. Use double hashing (two MODs) to reduce collision probability.
Interview angle: Discuss hash parameter choices and collision probability.

90 — Minimal Unique Substring / Shortest Unique Substring

What: For a given position, find shortest substring that is unique in the text — solved with suffix array + LCP neighboring values.
Interview angle: Explain LCP role in uniqueness and outline algorithm.

91 — String Compression: Huffman coding (concept)

What: Build frequency table → Huffman tree → variable-length prefix codes.
Use-case: Interviewers ask this for compression/IO efficiency/space tradeoffs.
Interview angle: Explain building Huffman tree and decoding; mention when it helps vs gzip.

92 — Delta Encoding and Run-Length Encoding (RLE)

What: Simple compression methods: store differences (delta) or counts of repeated values (RLE). Useful for repetitive data.
Interview angle: When to use RLE vs when it makes data larger (low repetition).

93 — Base64 / URL Encoding & Decoding (Java APIs)

What: Common in systems: Base64 for binary->text, URL-encoding for safe HTTP.
Java:

byte[] encoded = java.util.Base64.getEncoder().encode(bytes);
byte[] decoded = java.util.Base64.getDecoder().decode(encoded);
String urlEncoded = URLEncoder.encode(s, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
String urlDecoded = URLDecoder.decode(urlEncoded, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);


Interview angle: Know when to use each and security pitfalls.

94 — Canonicalization & Path Traversal Mitigation

What: Normalize inputs (resolve .., symbolic links) before using in file APIs to prevent directory traversal.
Java: Path.normalize() and toRealPath(); validate allowed roots.
Interview angle: Security-aware string handling — crucial for MNC interviews.

95 — String Matching with Automata (DFA via KMP)

What: KMP builds a failure function equivalent to a compact DFA for the pattern; explain how KMP can be seen as a machine moving on characters.
Interview angle: Show state transition concept and how to adapt for streaming input.

96 — Profiling & Benchmarking String Operations

What: When optimizing, measure with microbenchmarks (JMH) rather than guessing.
Example: Use JMH to compare StringBuilder.append vs repeated + in loops.
Interview angle: Emphasize measurement, JVM warm-up, and pitfalls of naive System.nanoTime() timings.

97 — Avoiding String Secrets in Logs / GDPR awareness

What: Never log raw secrets (passwords, tokens). Mask or redact before logging. Use char[] for secrets in memory.
Interview angle: Demonstrate secure coding practices and GDPR compliance sensibilities.

98 — Streaming Large Text — memory vs time tradeoffs

What: For huge files, process with streaming (BufferedReader, streaming parsers) instead of loading whole string into memory.
Java: try (BufferedReader r = Files.newBufferedReader(path)) { r.lines().forEach(...); }
Interview angle: Discuss backpressure, chunking, and when to use memory-mapped files (FileChannel.map) for large read throughput.

99 — Canonical Collation & Locale-aware operations in production

What: Sorting and comparisons must respect locale; using Collator ensures correct user-facing sorting.
Interview angle: Name pitfalls: treating bytes/ASCII order as natural language order; demonstrate collation API usage.

100 — Design question: Large-scale substring search service (system design + strings)

Prompt: “Design a service that, given a large corpus of documents, answers substring search queries in low latency.”
Key points to present:

Indexing: inverted index, n-gram index, or suffix array/FM-index depending on memory/time trade-offs.

Sharding: partition corpus by doc-id or shard by text ranges; use coordinate compression.

Updates: batch indexing vs near-real-time.

Fault tolerance: replicas, consistent hashing.

Security & normalization: normalize inputs (unicode), sanitize, rate limit.

Exact vs fuzzy search: use Levenshtein automata or n-gram + score.
Interview angle: Show ability to connect algorithms (suffix arrays, Aho-Corasick) with system concerns (storage, latency, scaling).


Theory (Core)

StringBuilder is mutable (unlike String).

Provides faster concatenation (append) vs String + in loops.

Not thread-safe — for thread-safe use StringBuffer.

Default capacity = 16 chars, grows automatically ((old*2)+2).

Useful for dynamic text generation, reversing, and string manipulation.

Examples
1. Create a StringBuilder
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Hello");
System.out.println(sb); // Hello

2. Append
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Java");
sb.append(" Rocks");
System.out.println(sb); // Java Rocks

3. Insert
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Jva");
sb.insert(1,"a");
System.out.println(sb); // Java

4. Delete
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Programming");
sb.delete(3,6);
System.out.println(sb); // Proamming

5. Replace
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Java");
sb.replace(0,2,"Py");
System.out.println(sb); // Pyva

6. Reverse
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("abcd");
System.out.println(sb.reverse()); // dcba

7. Capacity & EnsureCapacity
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 16
sb.ensureCapacity(50);
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // >=50

8. Length vs Capacity
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Java");
System.out.println(sb.length());   // 4
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 20 (16+4)

9. Set Length
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Java");
sb.setLength(2);
System.out.println(sb); // Ja

10. CharAt & SetCharAt
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Java");
System.out.println(sb.charAt(2)); // v
sb.setCharAt(2,'X');
System.out.println(sb); // JaXa

11. Substring
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("HelloWorld");
System.out.println(sb.substring(5)); // World

12. Append Different Types
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
sb.append(100).append(" ").append(true).append(" ").append(3.14);
System.out.println(sb); // 100 true 3.14

13. Chaining Methods
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Hi");
sb.append(" ").append("NikhiTech").append("!");
System.out.println(sb); // Hi NikhiTech!

14. DeleteCharAt
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("ABCDE");
sb.deleteCharAt(2);
System.out.println(sb); // ABDE

15. Clear Content
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Clear me");
sb.setLength(0);
System.out.println(sb); // ""

16. Append CodePoint
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
sb.appendCodePoint(65); // Unicode for 'A'
System.out.println(sb); // A

17. Convert to String
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Hello");
String s = sb.toString();
System.out.println(s.toUpperCase()); // HELLO

18. Append in Loops (Performance Demo)
long start=System.nanoTime();
String s="";
for(int i=0;i<10000;i++) s+=i; // slow
long end=System.nanoTime();
System.out.println("String + took:"+(end-start));

start=System.nanoTime();
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<10000;i++) sb.append(i); // fast
end=System.nanoTime();
System.out.println("SB took:"+(end-start));

19. Thread Safety Warning
// StringBuilder is NOT thread-safe
// If multiple threads modify same builder -> race conditions

20. Compare String vs StringBuilder Memory

String "a"+"b"+"c" creates new objects.

StringBuilder .append() reuses the same buffer.

String s = "";
for(int i=0;i<5;i++) s += i; // creates multiple objects
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<5;i++) sb.append(i); // one buffer





🔥 StringBuilder in Java (Part 2: 21–40)
21. Performance in Concatenation

👉 Rule: StringBuilder in loops is O(n), String is O(n²).

// Bad
String s="";
for(int i=0;i<10000;i++) s += i; // very slow

// Good
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<10000;i++) sb.append(i);

22. Capacity Growth Rule

👉 Default = 16; grows (oldCapacity*2)+2.

StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<40;i++) sb.append("a");
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 34


Interview trap: Know resizing formula.

23. TrimToSize()

👉 Reduce capacity to match length.

StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(100);
sb.append("Java");
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 100
sb.trimToSize();
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 4

24. Reversing Words in Sentence
String sentence="Java is fun";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(String w:sentence.split(" "))
    sb.append(new StringBuilder(w).reverse()).append(" ");
System.out.println(sb); // avaJ si nuf

25. Check Palindrome Efficiently
String s="madam";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(s);
System.out.println(s.equals(sb.reverse().toString())); // true

26. Insert Special Characters Dynamically
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("1234567890");
for(int i=3;i<sb.length();i+=4) sb.insert(i,"-");
System.out.println(sb); // 123-4567-890

27. Efficient CSV Builder
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
String[] vals={"Alice","Bob","Charlie"};
for(String v:vals) sb.append(v).append(",");
sb.setLength(sb.length()-1); // remove last comma
System.out.println(sb); // Alice,Bob,Charlie

28. Regex Replacement with AppendReplacement
Pattern p=Pattern.compile("\\d+");
Matcher m=p.matcher("Order123Done456");
StringBuffer out=new StringBuffer();
while(m.find()) m.appendReplacement(out,"#");
m.appendTail(out);
System.out.println(out); // Order#Done#


👉 Here StringBuffer used (Matcher API requires it), but same idea for building.

29. HTML/XML Builder Example
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
sb.append("<html><body>");
sb.append("<h1>").append("Hello").append("</h1>");
sb.append("</body></html>");
System.out.println(sb);

30. SQL Query Builder
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("SELECT * FROM employees WHERE ");
sb.append("salary>").append(5000).append(" AND dept='IT'");
System.out.println(sb);

31. Dynamic JSON Builder
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
sb.append("{")
  .append("\"name\":\"Alice\",")
  .append("\"age\":25")
  .append("}");
System.out.println(sb); // {"name":"Alice","age":25}

32. Logging with StringBuilder

👉 Avoids temporary objects.

StringBuilder log=new StringBuilder();
log.append("Time=").append(System.currentTimeMillis())
   .append(" Event=Login");
System.out.println(log);

33. ReplaceAll Digits
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("abc123xyz");
for(int i=0;i<sb.length();i++)
    if(Character.isDigit(sb.charAt(i))) sb.setCharAt(i,'#');
System.out.println(sb); // abc###xyz

34. Build Repeated Pattern
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<5;i++) sb.append("AB");
System.out.println(sb); // ABABABABAB

35. Efficient Remove Substring
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("abcdef");
sb.delete(2,4); // removes c,d
System.out.println(sb); // abef

36. Reverse Words Order
String s="Java is powerful";
String[] arr=s.split(" ");
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=arr.length-1;i>=0;i--) sb.append(arr[i]).append(" ");
System.out.println(sb.toString().trim()); // powerful is Java

37. Encode/Decode
String text="ABC";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(char c:text.toCharArray()) sb.append((char)(c+3));
System.out.println(sb); // DEF


👉 Caesar cipher demo.

38. Insert at Beginning (Not Efficient in String, Better in SB)
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<5;i++) sb.insert(0,i);
System.out.println(sb); // 43210

39. Chain Multiple Operations
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("Hello");
sb.append(" World").delete(0,6).insert(0,"Hi");
System.out.println(sb); // HiWorld

40. Benchmark: String vs StringBuilder in Multithreading

String immutable, safe but slow.

StringBuilder fast, but not safe.

StringBuffer thread-safe alternative.

StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("a");
Runnable task=()->{for(int i=0;i<1000;i++) sb.append("x");};
new Thread(task).start();
new Thread(task).start();
// Race condition possible!



🔥 StringBuilder in Java (Part 3: 41–60)
41. Dynamic Capacity Tuning

👉 Pre-sizing avoids repeated buffer expansion.

StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(1000);
for(int i=0;i<1000;i++) sb.append("x");
System.out.println(sb.length()); // 1000


Interview Tip: Pre-size when size known (e.g., JSON serialization).

42. Shrink Capacity after Heavy Use
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(5000);
sb.append("Hello");
sb.trimToSize(); 
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 5

43. Build Path Dynamically
String base="C:/Users/";
String folder="NikhiTech";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(base).append(folder).append("/Documents");
System.out.println(sb); // C:/Users/NikhiTech/Documents

44. Backtracking Example (Generate Parentheses)

👉 StringBuilder is efficient for backtracking.

void gen(int n,int open,int close,StringBuilder sb){
    if(sb.length()==2*n){System.out.println(sb);return;}
    if(open<n){sb.append("("); gen(n,open+1,close,sb); sb.deleteCharAt(sb.length()-1);}
    if(close<open){sb.append(")"); gen(n,open,close+1,sb); sb.deleteCharAt(sb.length()-1);}
}
// Call: gen(3,0,0,new StringBuilder());

45. Backtracking Example (Permutations)
void permute(String s, boolean[] used, StringBuilder sb){
    if(sb.length()==s.length()){System.out.println(sb);return;}
    for(int i=0;i<s.length();i++){
        if(used[i]) continue;
        used[i]=true; sb.append(s.charAt(i));
        permute(s,used,sb);
        sb.deleteCharAt(sb.length()-1); used[i]=false;
    }
}
// Call: permute("ABC",new boolean[3],new StringBuilder());

46. Palindrome Partitioning
void partition(String s,int start,List<String> cur){
    if(start==s.length()){System.out.println(cur);return;}
    for(int end=start+1;end<=s.length();end++){
        String sub=s.substring(start,end);
        if(isPal(sub)){
            cur.add(sub);
            partition(s,end,cur);
            cur.remove(cur.size()-1);
        }
    }
}
boolean isPal(String s){return s.equals(new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString());}


👉 StringBuilder can replace substring for efficiency.

47. Undo Last Append (Backtracking Efficiency)
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("abc");
int len=sb.length();
sb.append("xyz");
// undo
sb.setLength(len);
System.out.println(sb); // abc

48. Compare Two StringBuilders
StringBuilder sb1=new StringBuilder("abc");
StringBuilder sb2=new StringBuilder("abc");
System.out.println(sb1.toString().equals(sb2.toString())); // true


Trap: equals() not overridden, so compare with .toString().

49. Streaming Large Data

👉 Build large chunks with StringBuilder before flushing to DB/IO.

StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<100;i++) sb.append("Row ").append(i).append("\n");
Files.writeString(Path.of("output.txt"), sb.toString());

50. Rolling Hash with StringBuilder

👉 For dynamic substring manipulations.

StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("abcd");
sb.deleteCharAt(0).append("e");
System.out.println(sb); // bcde

51. Word Wrapping Example
String text="Java is awesome and powerful.";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
int width=10;
for(String word:text.split(" ")){
    if(sb.length()% (width+1) + word.length()>width) sb.append("\n");
    sb.append(word).append(" ");
}
System.out.println(sb);

52. Custom String Joiner
String[] arr={"one","two","three"};
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(String a:arr) sb.append(a).append("|");
sb.setLength(sb.length()-1);
System.out.println(sb); // one|two|three

53. Efficient Number Formatting
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=1;i<=5;i++) sb.append(i).append(",");
sb.setLength(sb.length()-1);
System.out.println(sb); // 1,2,3,4,5

54. Serialize Object Fields
class Emp{String name="John"; int age=30;}
Emp e=new Emp();
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
sb.append("{\"name\":\"").append(e.name).append("\",\"age\":").append(e.age).append("}");
System.out.println(sb);

55. Deserialization Helper
String json="{\"name\":\"John\",\"age\":30}";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(json);
System.out.println(sb.indexOf("name")); // position of "name"

56. Replace All Vowels
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("Interview");
for(int i=0;i<sb.length();i++)
    if("AEIOUaeiou".indexOf(sb.charAt(i))!=-1) sb.setCharAt(i,'*');
System.out.println(sb); // *nt*rv**w

57. Sliding Window Substring Builder

👉 Instead of creating substrings repeatedly.

String s="abcdef";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(s.substring(0,3));
System.out.println(sb); // abc
for(int i=3;i<s.length();i++){
    sb.deleteCharAt(0).append(s.charAt(i));
    System.out.println(sb); // bcd, cde, def
}

58. Encryption: Reverse + Shift
String text="JAVA";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(text).reverse();
for(int i=0;i<sb.length();i++) sb.setCharAt(i,(char)(sb.charAt(i)+1));
System.out.println(sb); // BWKB

59. Efficient String Repetition
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<5;i++) sb.append("abc");
System.out.println(sb); // abcabcabcabcabc

60. StringBuilder in Recursive DFS (LeetCode Style)

👉 Avoids costly new String creation.

void dfs(int n,int level,StringBuilder path){
    if(level==n){System.out.println(path);return;}
    for(char c:new char[]{'a','b'}){
        path.append(c);
        dfs(n,level+1,path);
        path.deleteCharAt(path.length()-1);
    }
}
// dfs(3,0,new StringBuilder());


✅ That’s 60 StringBuilder concepts + examples done.
Now you know performance, backtracking, serialization, encryption tricks, and algorithmic uses.


61. Multithreading Problem with StringBuilder

👉 Not synchronized → race conditions.

StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("a");
Runnable task = () -> { for(int i=0;i<1000;i++) sb.append("x"); };
new Thread(task).start();
new Thread(task).start();
// Result may be corrupted due to race condition


Interview Tip: Use StringBuffer or external sync for thread-safety.

62. Synchronizing Manually
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("a");
Runnable task=()->{
  for(int i=0;i<1000;i++){
    synchronized(sb){ sb.append("x"); }
  }
};

63. Replace Substring Dynamically
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("I love Java");
int idx=sb.indexOf("Java");
sb.replace(idx, idx+4, "Spring");
System.out.println(sb); // I love Spring

64. Multiple Inserts
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("12345");
sb.insert(0,"A").insert(3,"B").insert(sb.length(),"Z");
System.out.println(sb); // A12B345Z

65. Memory Benchmark vs String
long start=System.currentTimeMillis();
String s="";
for(int i=0;i<100000;i++) s+=i; // slow
System.out.println("String: "+(System.currentTimeMillis()-start));

start=System.currentTimeMillis();
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<100000;i++) sb.append(i);
System.out.println("SB: "+(System.currentTimeMillis()-start));

66. Chunked Writing for Files

👉 Write in chunks instead of many small writes.

StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<1000;i++) sb.append("Line ").append(i).append("\n");
Files.writeString(Path.of("log.txt"), sb.toString());

67. Palindrome Builder

👉 Construct palindrome by appending + reverse.

String base="abc";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(base);
sb.append(new StringBuilder(base).reverse());
System.out.println(sb); // abccba

68. Insert Formatting at Regular Interval
String num="1234567890";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(num);
for(int i=sb.length()-3;i>0;i-=3) sb.insert(i,",");
System.out.println(sb); // 1,234,567,890

69. URL Query Builder
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("https://api.com?");
sb.append("user=").append("Alice").append("&age=").append(25);
System.out.println(sb);

70. Code Template Engine (Basic)
String template="Hello, {{name}}!";
String name="NikhiTech";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(template);
int idx=sb.indexOf("{{name}}");
sb.replace(idx,idx+8,name);
System.out.println(sb); // Hello, NikhiTech!

71. Undo Operation (Custom Editor)
class Editor{
    StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
    Stack<Integer> history=new Stack<>();
    void append(String s){ sb.append(s); history.push(s.length()); }
    void undo(){ sb.setLength(sb.length()-history.pop()); }
}
Editor ed=new Editor();
ed.append("Hello"); ed.append("World");
ed.undo();
System.out.println(ed.sb); // Hello

72. Valid Parentheses Generation with SB

👉 Backtracking + efficient append/delete.

void gen(int n,int open,int close,StringBuilder sb){
    if(sb.length()==2*n){System.out.println(sb);return;}
    if(open<n){sb.append("("); gen(n,open+1,close,sb); sb.deleteCharAt(sb.length()-1);}
    if(close<open){sb.append(")"); gen(n,open,close+1,sb); sb.deleteCharAt(sb.length()-1);}
}

73. Find All Substrings Using SB (Efficient)
String s="abc";
for(int i=0;i<s.length();i++){
    StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
    for(int j=i;j<s.length();j++){
        sb.append(s.charAt(j));
        System.out.println(sb);
    }
}

74. Efficient Caesar Cipher
String text="HELLO";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(char c:text.toCharArray()) sb.append((char)(c+3));
System.out.println(sb); // KHOOR

75. Sliding Window Word Search
String s="hellojava";
int k=4;
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(s.substring(0,k));
System.out.println(sb);
for(int i=k;i<s.length();i++){
    sb.deleteCharAt(0).append(s.charAt(i));
    System.out.println(sb);
}

76. Repeated Concatenation Performance
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<1_000_000;i++) sb.append("x");
System.out.println(sb.length()); // 1 million chars


👉 Shows SB handles massive concatenations well.

77. Reverse Every K Characters
String s="abcdefgh";
int k=2;
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<s.length();i+=k){
    String sub=s.substring(i,Math.min(i+k,s.length()));
    sb.append(new StringBuilder(sub).reverse());
}
System.out.println(sb); // badcfehg

78. Center Align Text
String text="Hi";
int width=10;
int pad=(width-text.length())/2;
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<pad;i++) sb.append(" ");
sb.append(text);
System.out.println("'"+sb+"'");

79. Count Frequency with SB
String s="aaabbccc";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
int count=1;
for(int i=1;i<=s.length();i++){
    if(i<s.length() && s.charAt(i)==s.charAt(i-1)) count++;
    else { sb.append(s.charAt(i-1)).append(count); count=1; }
}
System.out.println(sb); // a3b2c3

80. Rolling Back a Stack Trace String
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("Error:NullPointer->Cause:Line42->Fix:CheckNull");
int idx=sb.lastIndexOf("->");
sb.setLength(idx);
System.out.println(sb); // Error:NullPointer->Cause:Line42


✅ Now we’ve hit 80 StringBuilder use-cases — ranging from multithreading concerns → editors → performance hacks → encryption/backtracking.

🔥 StringBuilder in Java (Part 5: 81–100)
81. DSL (Domain Specific Language) Builder

👉 Using StringBuilder to build SQL/HTML/commands.

StringBuilder query = new StringBuilder()
    .append("SELECT name, age FROM employee WHERE ")
    .append("salary > ").append(5000)
    .append(" AND dept='IT'");
System.out.println(query);


Interview Angle: Demonstrate using SB in builder design pattern.

82. Custom Markup Generator
StringBuilder html = new StringBuilder();
html.append("<ul>");
for(String item:new String[]{"A","B","C"})
    html.append("<li>").append(item).append("</li>");
html.append("</ul>");
System.out.println(html);


👉 SB is ideal for templating engines.

83. Pretty Print JSON
String json="{\"name\":\"NikhiTech\",\"age\":25}";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
int indent=0;
for(char c:json.toCharArray()){
    if(c=='{'||c=='['){ sb.append(c).append("\n"); indent++; sb.append("  ".repeat(indent)); }
    else if(c=='}'||c==']'){ sb.append("\n"); indent--; sb.append("  ".repeat(indent)).append(c); }
    else if(c==','){ sb.append(c).append("\n").append("  ".repeat(indent)); }
    else sb.append(c);
}
System.out.println(sb);

84. Pretty Print XML

👉 Similar to JSON; interviewers like comparing string manipulation.

85. Reverse Each Line in Multiline Input
String input="Hello\nJava\nWorld";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(String line:input.split("\n"))
    sb.append(new StringBuilder(line).reverse()).append("\n");
System.out.println(sb);

86. Build a Stack Trace String
try { throw new Exception("Boom"); }
catch(Exception e){
    StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
    for(StackTraceElement el:e.getStackTrace())
        sb.append(el.toString()).append("\n");
    System.out.println(sb);
}

87. Code Generation Example
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
sb.append("public class Hello{")
  .append("public static void main(String[]a){")
  .append("System.out.println(\"Hi\");")
  .append("}}");
System.out.println(sb);

88. Check if Reversible Word Exists

👉 Efficient scanning.

String[] words={"abc","cba","xyz"};
Set<String> set=new HashSet<>();
for(String w:words){
    if(set.contains(new StringBuilder(w).reverse().toString()))
        System.out.println("Reversible found: "+w);
    set.add(w);
}

89. Pattern Matching with Rolling Window

👉 Use SB instead of costly substring creation.

90. Generate Huge Test Data
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<1_000_000;i++) sb.append("user").append(i).append(",");
System.out.println(sb.substring(0,50)+"...");

91. Custom Encryption (Mirror + Caesar)
String s="SECURE";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(s).reverse();
for(int i=0;i<sb.length();i++) sb.setCharAt(i,(char)(sb.charAt(i)+2));
System.out.println(sb); // Example encrypted

92. Efficient Log Aggregator
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<5;i++) sb.append("Log entry ").append(i).append("\n");
System.out.println(sb);

93. Replace All Spaces with %20 (URL Encoding)
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("Hello World Test");
for(int i=0;i<sb.length();i++)
    if(sb.charAt(i)==' '){ sb.replace(i,i+1,"%20"); i+=2; }
System.out.println(sb); // Hello%20World%20Test

94. SQL IN-Clause Generator
String[] ids={"101","102","103"};
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("SELECT * FROM emp WHERE id IN(");
for(String id:ids) sb.append(id).append(",");
sb.setLength(sb.length()-1);
sb.append(")");
System.out.println(sb);

95. Replace XML Tags Dynamically
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder("<tag>data</tag>");
int start=sb.indexOf("<tag>");
int end=sb.indexOf("</tag>");
sb.replace(start,end+6,"<newTag>"+sb.substring(start+5,end)+"</newTag>");
System.out.println(sb); // <newTag>data</newTag>

96. DNA Reverse Complement (Bioinformatics)
String dna="ATGC";
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
for(char c:dna.toCharArray()){
    sb.append(c=='A'?'T':c=='T'?'A':c=='G'?'C':'G');
}
System.out.println(sb.reverse()); // GCAT

97. Stream Chunk Builder

👉 Build message chunks for network I/O.

98. Using StringBuilder in Builder Pattern
class PersonBuilder{
    StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();
    PersonBuilder setName(String name){ sb.append("Name=").append(name).append(" "); return this; }
    PersonBuilder setAge(int age){ sb.append("Age=").append(age).append(" "); return this; }
    String build(){ return sb.toString(); }
}
System.out.println(new PersonBuilder().setName("NikhiTech").setAge(25).build());

99. Avoiding String Pool Overload

👉 Interview tip: Using StringBuilder avoids littering the String Constant Pool with temporary strings.

String s="";
for(int i=0;i<10000;i++) s+=i; // pollutes SCP

100. Summary (Mastery Point)

StringBuilder = mutable, fast, efficient.

Use for dynamic text, builders, backtracking, log aggregation, serialization.

Know: capacity rules, thread-safety issues, algorithmic use (DFS/Backtracking).

In MNC interviews, they test:

Difference from String/StringBuffer

Performance under loops

Use in parsers, builders, and algorithms


🔥 StringBuffer in Java (Part 1: 1–20)
1. StringBuffer Introduction

StringBuffer = mutable + synchronized string class.

Thread-safe, slower than StringBuilder.

Default capacity = 16.

StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Hello");
System.out.println(sb); // Hello

2. Append
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Java");
sb.append(" Rocks");
System.out.println(sb); // Java Rocks

3. Insert
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Jva");
sb.insert(1,"a");
System.out.println(sb); // Java

4. Delete
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Programming");
sb.delete(3,6);
System.out.println(sb); // Proamming

5. Replace
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Java");
sb.replace(0,2,"Py");
System.out.println(sb); // Pyva

6. Reverse
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("abcd");
System.out.println(sb.reverse()); // dcba

7. Capacity
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 16

8. EnsureCapacity
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();
sb.ensureCapacity(50);
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // >= 50

9. Length vs Capacity
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Java");
System.out.println(sb.length());   // 4
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 20

10. SetLength
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Java");
sb.setLength(2);
System.out.println(sb); // Ja

11. CharAt & SetCharAt
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Java");
System.out.println(sb.charAt(2)); // v
sb.setCharAt(2,'X');
System.out.println(sb); // JaXa

12. Substring
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("HelloWorld");
System.out.println(sb.substring(5)); // World

13. Append Different Types
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();
sb.append(100).append(" ").append(true).append(" ").append(3.14);
System.out.println(sb); // 100 true 3.14

14. DeleteCharAt
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("ABCDE");
sb.deleteCharAt(2);
System.out.println(sb); // ABDE

15. TrimToSize
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer(100);
sb.append("Hello");
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 100
sb.trimToSize();
System.out.println(sb.capacity()); // 5

16. Append CodePoint
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();
sb.appendCodePoint(65); // Unicode for 'A'
System.out.println(sb); // A

17. Convert to String
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Hello");
String s = sb.toString();
System.out.println(s.toUpperCase()); // HELLO

18. Append in Loops (Performance Demo)

👉 Slower than StringBuilder, but safe in multithreaded contexts.

StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();
for(int i=0;i<10000;i++) sb.append(i);
System.out.println(sb.length());

19. Thread Safety (Safe in Multi-threading)
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("a");
Runnable task=()->{for(int i=0;i<1000;i++) sb.append("x");};
Thread t1=new Thread(task),t2=new Thread(task);
t1.start();t2.start();


👉 No corruption because of synchronization.

20. Comparison with StringBuilder

StringBuffer = thread-safe, slower.

StringBuilder = not thread-safe, faster.

Interviewers often ask: “When to use which?”
👉 Answer: Use StringBuilder in single-thread; StringBuffer in multi-thread.

✅ That’s the first 20 StringBuffer basics: creation, modification, capacity, thread-safety.


🔥 StringBuffer in Java (Part 2: 21–40)
21. Multiple Inserts
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("12345");
sb.insert(0,"A").insert(3,"B").insert(sb.length(),"Z");
System.out.println(sb); // A12B345Z

22. Replace Substring Dynamically
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("I love Java");
int idx = sb.indexOf("Java");
sb.replace(idx, idx+4, "Spring");
System.out.println(sb); // I love Spring

23. Compare Two StringBuffers
StringBuffer sb1 = new StringBuffer("abc");
StringBuffer sb2 = new StringBuffer("abc");
System.out.println(sb1.toString().equals(sb2.toString())); // true


👉 .equals() not overridden, so always use .toString().

24. Reverse Words in a Sentence
String sentence="Java is fun";
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(String w:sentence.split(" "))
    sb.append(new StringBuffer(w).reverse()).append(" ");
System.out.println(sb); // avaJ si nuf

25. Thread-Safe Palindrome Check
String word="madam";
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer(word);
System.out.println(word.equals(sb.reverse().toString())); // true

26. CSV Builder
String[] vals={"Alice","Bob","Charlie"};
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(String v:vals) sb.append(v).append(",");
sb.setLength(sb.length()-1);
System.out.println(sb); // Alice,Bob,Charlie

27. HTML/XML Builder
StringBuffer html = new StringBuffer();
html.append("<html><body>");
html.append("<h1>").append("Hello").append("</h1>");
html.append("</body></html>");
System.out.println(html);

28. SQL Query Builder
StringBuffer query = new StringBuffer("SELECT * FROM employee WHERE ");
query.append("salary>").append(5000).append(" AND dept='IT'");
System.out.println(query);

29. Dynamic JSON Builder
StringBuffer json = new StringBuffer();
json.append("{")
    .append("\"name\":\"Alice\",")
    .append("\"age\":25")
    .append("}");
System.out.println(json);

30. Replace Digits
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("abc123xyz");
for(int i=0;i<sb.length();i++)
    if(Character.isDigit(sb.charAt(i))) sb.setCharAt(i,'#');
System.out.println(sb); // abc###xyz

31. Delete Substring
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer("abcdef");
sb.delete(2,4); // removes c,d
System.out.println(sb); // abef

32. Reverse Words Order
String s="Java is powerful";
String[] arr=s.split(" ");
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(int i=arr.length-1;i>=0;i--) sb.append(arr[i]).append(" ");
System.out.println(sb.toString().trim()); // powerful is Java

33. Caesar Cipher with Thread Safety
String text="JAVA";
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(char c:text.toCharArray()) sb.append((char)(c+3));
System.out.println(sb); // MDYD

34. Insert at Beginning
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(int i=0;i<5;i++) sb.insert(0,i);
System.out.println(sb); // 43210

35. Undo Append (Editor Simulation)
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer("abc");
int len=sb.length();
sb.append("xyz");
// undo
sb.setLength(len);
System.out.println(sb); // abc

36. Log Builder
StringBuffer log=new StringBuffer();
log.append("Time=").append(System.currentTimeMillis())
   .append(" Event=Login");
System.out.println(log);

37. Thread-Safe Shared Buffer
StringBuffer shared=new StringBuffer();
Runnable task=()->{
  for(int i=0;i<5;i++) shared.append(Thread.currentThread().getName()+" ");
};
Thread t1=new Thread(task,"T1"), t2=new Thread(task,"T2");
t1.start();t2.start();


👉 Safe because of synchronization.

38. Trim Output
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer("Hello   ");
System.out.println(sb.toString().trim()); // Hello

39. Format Numbers with Comma
String num="1234567890";
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer(num);
for(int i=sb.length()-3;i>0;i-=3) sb.insert(i,",");
System.out.println(sb); // 1,234,567,890

40. Thread-Safe Performance Cost

👉 Benchmark StringBuilder vs StringBuffer.

long start=System.nanoTime();
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(int i=0;i<100000;i++) sb.append(i);
System.out.println("SBuff time:"+(System.nanoTime()-start));

start=System.nanoTime();
StringBuilder sbd=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<100000;i++) sbd.append(i);
System.out.println("SBuild time:"+(System.nanoTime()-start));


👉 Expect StringBuffer slower due to synchronization.

✅ That’s 40 StringBuffer examples done: basics + intermediate with thread-safety demos.


🔥 StringBuffer in Java (Part 3: 41–60)
41. Multi-threaded Logger (Safe Aggregator)
class Logger {
    private final StringBuffer log = new StringBuffer();
    public void log(String msg) {
        log.append(Thread.currentThread().getName())
           .append(": ").append(msg).append("\n");
    }
    public String getLogs() { return log.toString(); }
}
Logger logger = new Logger();
Runnable task=()->{ for(int i=0;i<3;i++) logger.log("Event "+i); };
new Thread(task,"T1").start();
new Thread(task,"T2").start();


👉 Safe logging across threads.

42. Reverse Each Word Safely
String text="Hello Java World";
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(String word:text.split(" "))
    sb.append(new StringBuffer(word).reverse()).append(" ");
System.out.println(sb);

43. Undo Operation with Stack
class Editor {
    StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
    Stack<Integer> hist=new Stack<>();
    void append(String s){ sb.append(s); hist.push(s.length()); }
    void undo(){ sb.setLength(sb.length()-hist.pop()); }
}
Editor ed=new Editor();
ed.append("Hello"); ed.append("World");
ed.undo();
System.out.println(ed.sb); // Hello

44. Thread-Safe Palindrome Partition
String s="aab";
for(int i=1;i<=s.length();i++){
    String part=s.substring(0,i);
    if(part.equals(new StringBuffer(part).reverse().toString())){
        System.out.println("Palindrome part: "+part);
    }
}

45. Generate Parentheses Safely
void gen(int n,int open,int close,StringBuffer sb){
    if(sb.length()==2*n){ System.out.println(sb); return; }
    if(open<n){ sb.append("("); gen(n,open+1,close,sb); sb.deleteCharAt(sb.length()-1);}
    if(close<open){ sb.append(")"); gen(n,open,close+1,sb); sb.deleteCharAt(sb.length()-1);}
}

46. Efficient Substring Replace in Large Buffer
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer("abcabcabc");
int idx;
while((idx=sb.indexOf("abc"))!=-1) sb.replace(idx,idx+3,"X");
System.out.println(sb); // XXX

47. Reverse Lines of File
String text="one\ntwo\nthree";
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(String line:text.split("\n"))
    sb.insert(0,line+"\n");
System.out.println(sb);

48. DNA Complement (Thread-Safe)
String dna="ATGC";
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(char c:dna.toCharArray()){
    sb.append(c=='A'?'T':c=='T'?'A':c=='G'?'C':'G');
}
System.out.println(sb.reverse()); // GCAT

49. Remove Consecutive Duplicates
String s="aaabbcddd";
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(int i=0;i<s.length();i++)
    if(i==0 || s.charAt(i)!=s.charAt(i-1)) sb.append(s.charAt(i));
System.out.println(sb); // abcd

50. Valid Parentheses Check
String s="({[]})";
Stack<Character> stack=new Stack<>();
for(char c:s.toCharArray()){
    if("({[".indexOf(c)!=-1) stack.push(c);
    else {
        char top=stack.pop();
        // Simple matching logic
    }
}
System.out.println(stack.isEmpty());


👉 Could use SB in backtracking generation.

51. Rolling Window with StringBuffer
String s="abcdef";
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer(s.substring(0,3));
System.out.println(sb);
for(int i=3;i<s.length();i++){
    sb.deleteCharAt(0).append(s.charAt(i));
    System.out.println(sb);
}

52. Multi-threaded CSV Collector
StringBuffer csv=new StringBuffer();
Runnable task=( )->{
    for(int i=0;i<3;i++) csv.append(Thread.currentThread().getName())
        .append("_").append(i).append(",");
};
new Thread(task,"T1").start();
new Thread(task,"T2").start();

53. Replace Spaces with %20
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer("Hello World Test");
for(int i=0;i<sb.length();i++)
    if(sb.charAt(i)==' '){ sb.replace(i,i+1,"%20"); i+=2; }
System.out.println(sb); // Hello%20World%20Test

54. Pretty Print JSON

(Same logic as StringBuilder but safe in threads.)
👉 Shows formatting large JSON outputs.

55. Build Log with Timestamps
StringBuffer log=new StringBuffer();
for(int i=0;i<3;i++)
    log.append(System.currentTimeMillis())
       .append(": Event ").append(i).append("\n");
System.out.println(log);

56. Count Character Frequency
String s="programming";
Map<Character,Integer> map=new HashMap<>();
for(char c:s.toCharArray()){
    map.put(c,map.getOrDefault(c,0)+1);
}
System.out.println(map);


👉 Use StringBuffer when constructing string outputs from map.

57. SQL IN Clause Generator
String[] ids={"101","102","103"};
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer("SELECT * FROM emp WHERE id IN(");
for(String id:ids) sb.append(id).append(",");
sb.setLength(sb.length()-1);
sb.append(")");
System.out.println(sb);

58. Build Template Engine
String template="Hello, {{name}}!";
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer(template);
int idx=sb.indexOf("{{name}}");
sb.replace(idx,idx+8,"NikhiTech");
System.out.println(sb); // Hello, NikhiTech!

59. Center Align Text
String text="Hi";
int width=10;
int pad=(width-text.length())/2;
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(int i=0;i<pad;i++) sb.append(" ");
sb.append(text);
System.out.println("'"+sb+"'");

60. Benchmark vs StringBuilder in Multi-thread
long start=System.nanoTime();
StringBuffer sb=new StringBuffer();
for(int i=0;i<100000;i++) sb.append(i);
System.out.println("StringBuffer:"+(System.nanoTime()-start));

start=System.nanoTime();
StringBuilder sbd=new StringBuilder();
for(int i=0;i<100000;i++) sbd.append(i);
System.out.println("StringBuilder:"+(System.nanoTime()-start));


👉 Expect StringBuffer slower, but safe in threads.

✅ That’s 60 StringBuffer examples done: multi-threaded loggers, undo editors, JSON builders, rolling windows, and safe builders.


🔥 StringBuffer in Java (Part 4: 61–80)
61 — Concurrent Append with Multiple Threads (safe aggregator)

Why: Collect logs/events from threads safely without external locks.

StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();
Runnable task = () -> {
  for (int i=0;i<100;i++) sb.append(Thread.currentThread().getName()).append("-").append(i).append("\n");
};
Thread t1=new Thread(task,"T1"), t2=new Thread(task,"T2");
t1.start(); t2.start();
t1.join(); t2.join();
System.out.println(sb.length()); // combined safely

62 — Producer-Consumer Buffering (simple)

Why: Use SB as a shared buffer between producer and consumer threads (still careful with coordination).

final StringBuffer buf = new StringBuffer();
Thread producer = new Thread(() -> { synchronized(buf){ buf.append("data"); buf.notify(); }});
Thread consumer = new Thread(() -> { synchronized(buf){ while(buf.length()==0) buf.wait(); System.out.println(buf.toString()); }});
producer.start(); consumer.start();

63 — Atomic Snapshot (toString() usage)

Why: toString() produces a snapshot; safe to call while other threads append.

StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("start");
sb.append("-m1");
String snapshot = sb.toString(); // consistent view at call time

64 — Synchronized Search & Replace

Why: Searching and modifying a shared buffer must be synchronized to avoid races.

synchronized(sb){
  int idx = sb.indexOf("old");
  if(idx!=-1) sb.replace(idx, idx+"old".length(), "new");
}

65 — Safe Append + Trim in Background Thread

Why: Keep buffer size bounded for long-running services.

synchronized(sb){
  sb.append("entry\n");
  if(sb.length()>10_000) sb.delete(0, sb.length()/2);
}

66 — StringBuffer as a Mutable Key Builder

Why: Build query or cache keys safely in multi-threaded contexts.

synchronized(sb){
  sb.setLength(0);
  sb.append("user:").append(userId).append(":").append(action);
  String key = sb.toString();
  // use key
}

67 — Safe HTML Escaping & Building

Why: Build escaped HTML in multi-threaded servers.

synchronized(sb){
  for(char c: input.toCharArray()){
    if(c=='<') sb.append("&lt;");
    else if(c=='>') sb.append("&gt;");
    else sb.append(c);
  }
}

68 — Thread-safe Tokenizer + Builder

Why: Parse and append sanitized tokens into a shared buffer.

StringTokenizer st = new StringTokenizer(line);
synchronized(sb){
  while(st.hasMoreTokens()) sb.append(st.nextToken()).append(' ');
}

69 — Safe Rolling Log File Prep

Why: Aggregate entries to flush to disk in batches (reduce IO) while threads safely append.

synchronized(sb){
  if(sb.length()>8192) { Files.write(path, sb.toString().getBytes(), APPEND); sb.setLength(0); }
}

70 — Concurrent CSV Row Collector

Why: Multiple threads produce CSV rows. SB holds rows before persistence.

public void addRow(String[] cols){
  synchronized(sb){
    for(String c:cols) sb.append(c).append(',');
    sb.setCharAt(sb.length()-1,'\n');
  }
}

71 — Safe Template Replacement (multi-thread)

Why: Multiple threads perform templating in parallel using a shared StringBuffer only when guarded.

synchronized(sb){
  sb.setLength(0);
  sb.append(template).replace(sb.indexOf("{{name}}"), sb.indexOf("{{name}}")+8, name);
  out = sb.toString();
}

72 — Coarse-grained vs fine-grained synchronization discussion

Why: Interviewers ask trade-offs: coarse lock (synchronized buffer) simpler but reduces concurrency; fine-grained locking increases complexity but scales better. Be ready to explain.

73 — Safe Metrics Aggregation

Why: Threads append metrics; aggregator periodically snapshots and resets buffer.

String snapshot;
synchronized(sb){ snapshot = sb.toString(); sb.setLength(0); }
process(snapshot);

74 — Use StringBuffer in Legacy APIs

Why: Some older APIs expect StringBuffer (e.g., Matcher.appendReplacement historically used StringBuffer). Know these quirks.

Matcher m = Pattern.compile("\\d+").matcher("a1b2");
StringBuffer out = new StringBuffer();
while(m.find()) m.appendReplacement(out,"#");
m.appendTail(out);

75 — Avoid Blocking the Lock for Long Work

Why: Never hold StringBuffer lock while performing expensive IO/DB — copy snapshot then release lock.

String snapshot;
synchronized(sb){ snapshot = sb.toString(); sb.setLength(0); }
writeToDb(snapshot); // outside synchronized block

76 — Hybrid approach: Thread-local StringBuilders + Global StringBuffer

Why: Reduce contention — threads build locally (fast) then append to shared buffer periodically (synchronized).

ThreadLocal<StringBuilder> local = ThreadLocal.withInitial(StringBuilder::new);
StringBuilder lb = local.get();
lb.append(...);
// occasionally
synchronized(sb){ sb.append(lb.toString()); lb.setLength(0); }

77 — Safe Circular Buffer Implementation Using StringBuffer

Why: Implement a simple bounded message store. Use synchronized blocks to maintain pointers and content. (Sketch only; many edge-cases.)

78 — Performance: When StringBuffer is still reasonable

Why: If concurrency is low and safety matters, StringBuffer is acceptable. Also useful in legacy systems where replacing with StringBuilder is risky.

79 — Monitoring buffer growth & telemetry

Why: Expose metrics for buffer length to detect producer/consumer imbalance.

int len;
synchronized(sb){ len = sb.length(); }
metrics.gauge("sharedBuffer.length", len);

80 — Design Q: Choose StringBuilder vs StringBuffer for a server
