Why: Be ready to justify: prefer StringBuilder in request-scoped logic (each request thread has its own builder); use StringBuffer for genuinely shared mutable buffers. Explain lock contention, throughput, and memory effects.


🔥 StringBuffer in Java (Part 5: 81–100)
81 — Stress Testing StringBuffer Under Load

What: Measure throughput and contention when many threads append.
Why: Shows you understand practical limits.

StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();
Runnable r = () -> { for(int i=0;i<10000;i++) sb.append("x"); };
List<Thread> threads = IntStream.range(0,50).mapToObj(i->new Thread(r)).collect(Collectors.toList());
threads.forEach(Thread::start);
threads.forEach(t->t.join());
System.out.println("Len="+sb.length());


Interview angle: Discuss lock contention, CPU wait, and alternatives (batching).

82 — Migration Strategy: Replace Shared StringBuffer with Thread-Local StringBuilder

Why: Improve throughput with minimal code changes.
Pattern: Each thread writes to ThreadLocal<StringBuilder> then occasionally synchronizes to flush into a shared StringBuffer or persistent store.

83 — Memory Profiling: StringBuffer Retention

What: Large StringBuffer can hold many chars; on heap it prevents GC of contained strings.
Tip: Monitor size and call setLength(0) or swap buffers to allow GC of large temporary content.

84 — Atomic Swap Pattern for Low-Latency Flush

What: Replace content reference with a new buffer under lock, process old buffer outside lock.

synchronized(lock){ StringBuffer old = sb; sb = new StringBuffer(); return old; }
process(old.toString()); // heavy work outside lock


Benefit: Minimizes time spent holding lock.

85 — Using StringBuffer in Legacy API Interop

Why: Some older Java patterns / third-party libs expect StringBuffer (e.g., Matcher.appendReplacement). Know when to keep rather than refactor.

86 — Efficient Search in Large Shared Buffer

Approach: Synchronize only for indexOf / substring extraction; perform heavy processing on the snapshot string.

String snapshot;
synchronized(sb){ int idx=sb.indexOf("ERROR"); snapshot = sb.substring(idx,Math.min(idx+100, sb.length())); }
process(snapshot);

87 — Safe Concurrent Replace with CAS-like Swap

Idea: Use synchronized swap to apply a replace of region safely: build replacement in temp StringBuffer, then in synchronized block do sb.replace(...).

88 — Avoiding Deadlocks when Combining Multiple Buffers

Rule: Always lock multiple buffers in a defined order (e.g., by identity hash) to avoid lock-order inversion.

89 — Using StringBuffer for Audit Trails

Pattern: Append audit lines from many threads to a shared buffer, snapshot and persist periodically. Ensure snapshot + clear is atomic.

90 — Resizing Costs & Predictable Capacity

Tip: If you expect N chars, construct new StringBuffer(N) to avoid repeated expansions and reduce memcopy overhead.

91 — Mixing StringBuffer with I/O (Flush Pattern)

Pattern: Buffer content until threshold then flush to disk/network:

synchronized(sb){
  if(sb.length()>8192){ Files.write(path, sb.toString().getBytes(), APPEND); sb.setLength(0); }
}


Interview angle: Discuss backpressure and bounded buffers.

92 — Using StringBuffer in Concurrent Testing Fixtures

Use-case: Collect concurrent test logs/results into a single structure for verification later — simpler than coordinating many return channels.

93 — Security: Avoid Logging Sensitive Data

Rule: Don’t append secrets into shared buffers. If temporarily required, ensure immediate scrub setLength(0) or overwrite content.

94 — Safe Serialization of Buffer Contents

Pattern: snapshot = sb.toString() then serialize that string — do not serialize StringBuffer object directly to avoid exposing lock internals.

95 — Logging Formatter Pipeline (Thread-Safe)

Design: Multiple producers append formatted events to StringBuffer; a single consumer snapshots and writes to sink. Explain how to avoid head-of-line blocking.

96 — Using StringBuffer with Native Code (JNI) — Caution

Note: If passing strings to native code, convert via toString(); avoid exposing the mutable StringBuffer itself.

97 — Unit Testing StringBuffer Concurrency

Approach: Use stress tests with multiple threads and deterministic seeds; assert final length/contents and absence of corruption patterns.

98 — Replacing StringBuffer with Concurrent Queues

When: If contention is high and order is not strictly required, replace with ConcurrentLinkedQueue<String> for producers and a dedicated consumer that drains and aggregates into a StringBuilder for flush — often outperforms synchronized buffer.

99 — Migration: Safe API Change Checklist

Steps:

Identify thread-shared usage.

Replace per-request StringBuffer → StringBuilder locally.

For global shared buffers, consider batching or use ConcurrentLinkedQueue.

Load test before production rollout.

100 — How to Explain StringBuffer Choices in an Interview

Key talking points to cover:

Why StringBuffer exists: thread-safety via synchronized methods.

When to use: legacy code or simple shared mutable buffer with low contention.

Drawbacks: synchronization overhead, possible contention.

Alternatives: StringBuilder for single-thread, ThreadLocal<StringBuilder> + flush, ConcurrentQueue + consumer, AtomicReference swap patterns.

Real-world concerns: capacity tuning, snapshot & flush, avoid holding locks during IO, avoid logging secrets, monitor buffer length.

✅ Wrap-up for StringBuffer (1–100)

You now have a complete, interview-ready set of 100 theory + practical items for StringBuffer:

basics, API, capacity rules,

concurrency-safe patterns and pitfalls,

migration strategies, profiling & stress testing,

system-level patterns (batching, snapshotting, bounded buffer),

security and testing considerations.


🔥 Java Collections — ArrayList (Part 1: 1–20)
1. What is ArrayList?

Resizable array implementation of List.

Allows random access in O(1).

Maintains insertion order.

Can store null.

List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("Java");
list.add("Spring");
System.out.println(list); // [Java, Spring]

2. Default Capacity

Default = 10.

Grows by ~1.5x when exceeded.

ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
System.out.println(list.size());      // 0
System.out.println(list.isEmpty());   // true

3. Add Elements
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("A");
list.add("B");
System.out.println(list); // [A, B]

4. Insert at Index
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","C"));
list.add(1,"B");
System.out.println(list); // [A, B, C]

5. Get and Set
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B"));
System.out.println(list.get(1)); // B
list.set(1,"X");
System.out.println(list); // [A, X]

6. Remove by Index / Object
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","C"));
list.remove(1);
list.remove("C");
System.out.println(list); // [A]

7. Search Elements
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","A"));
System.out.println(list.indexOf("A"));      // 0
System.out.println(list.lastIndexOf("A"));  // 2
System.out.println(list.contains("B"));     // true

8. Iteration
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3));
for(int i:list) System.out.print(i+" "); // 1 2 3

9. Iterator
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","C"));
Iterator<String> it = list.iterator();
while(it.hasNext()) System.out.println(it.next());

10. ListIterator (Bi-directional)
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","C"));
ListIterator<String> it = list.listIterator();
while(it.hasNext()) System.out.println(it.next());
while(it.hasPrevious()) System.out.println(it.previous());

11. ForEach with Lambda
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","C"));
list.forEach(System.out::println);

12. Sort
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("Banana","Apple","Mango"));
Collections.sort(list);
System.out.println(list); // [Apple, Banana, Mango]

13. Sort with Comparator
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("Banana","Apple","Mango"));
list.sort((a,b)->b.compareTo(a));
System.out.println(list); // [Mango, Banana, Apple]

14. SubList
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4,5));
System.out.println(list.subList(1,4)); // [2, 3, 4]

15. Convert to Array
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B"));
String[] arr = list.toArray(new String[0]);
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr)); // [A, B]

16. Clone
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B"));
ArrayList<String> copy = (ArrayList<String>) list.clone();
System.out.println(copy);

17. Clear
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B"));
list.clear();
System.out.println(list.isEmpty()); // true

18. EnsureCapacity & TrimToSize
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.ensureCapacity(50);
list.add("Java");
list.trimToSize(); // reduce capacity to size

19. Fail-Fast Iterator

👉 Modifying during iteration throws ConcurrentModificationException.

ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","C"));
for(String s:list){
    // list.add("X"); // would throw ConcurrentModificationException
    System.out.println(s);
}

20. CopyOnWriteArrayList (Fail-Safe Alternative)

👉 In concurrent code, use CopyOnWriteArrayList.

CopyOnWriteArrayList<String> list = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>(List.of("A","B"));
for(String s:list){
    list.add("X"); // safe
    System.out.println(s);
}
System.out.println(list); // includes extra Xs


✅ That’s 20 ArrayList items: theory + basics + pitfalls + concurrency tip.


🔥 Java Collections — ArrayList (Part 2: 21–40)
21. Time Complexity of ArrayList

get(int index) → O(1)

add(E e) → O(1) amortized

add(int index,E e) → O(n) (shift)

remove(int index) → O(n)

contains(Object o) → O(n)

👉 Interview Tip: Always mention amortized for append.

22. Internal Resizing

Initial capacity = 10.

When full → new capacity = old * 1.5.

Elements are copied into new array.

ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(10);
for(int i=0;i<15;i++) list.add(i);

23. Null Handling

ArrayList allows multiple null values.

ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add(null); list.add(null);
System.out.println(list); // [null, null]

24. Thread Safety

ArrayList is not synchronized.

Multiple threads adding may cause ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.
👉 Solution: Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<>()).

25. Synchronized Wrapper
List<String> list = Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<>());
list.add("A"); list.add("B");
synchronized(list){ list.forEach(System.out::println); }

26. Fail-Fast Behavior (ConcurrentModificationException)
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3));
for(Integer i:list){
    list.add(4); // throws ConcurrentModificationException
}

27. Fail-Safe Iterator Example
CopyOnWriteArrayList<Integer> list = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3));
for(Integer i:list){
    list.add(4); // no exception
}
System.out.println(list);

28. Difference Between Array and ArrayList

Array → fixed size, can store primitives.

ArrayList → dynamic size, only objects.

int[] arr = {1,2,3};
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3));

29. Convert Array to ArrayList
String[] arr={"A","B"};
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(arr));

30. Convert ArrayList to Array
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("X","Y"));
String[] arr = list.toArray(new String[0]);
System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr));

31. Clone vs Copy Constructor
ArrayList<String> list1 = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B"));
ArrayList<String> list2 = (ArrayList<String>) list1.clone(); // shallow copy
ArrayList<String> list3 = new ArrayList<>(list1);            // new list

32. Deep Copy Problem

Both clone & copy constructor copy references.

Need manual deep copy for mutable objects.

33. RemoveIf (Java 8)
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4,5));
list.removeIf(n->n%2==0);
System.out.println(list); // [1,3,5]

34. ReplaceAll (Java 8)
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("a","b"));
list.replaceAll(String::toUpperCase);
System.out.println(list); // [A, B]

35. RetainAll
ArrayList<String> list1 = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","C"));
ArrayList<String> list2 = new ArrayList<>(List.of("B","C","D"));
list1.retainAll(list2);
System.out.println(list1); // [B, C]

36. RemoveAll
ArrayList<String> list1 = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","C"));
ArrayList<String> list2 = new ArrayList<>(List.of("B","C"));
list1.removeAll(list2);
System.out.println(list1); // [A]

37. Bulk Add with addAll
ArrayList<String> list1 = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B"));
ArrayList<String> list2 = new ArrayList<>(List.of("C","D"));
list1.addAll(list2);
System.out.println(list1); // [A, B, C, D]

38. Stream Operations
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4,5));
list.stream().filter(n->n%2==0).forEach(System.out::println); // 2 4

39. ParallelStream
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4,5));
list.parallelStream().forEach(System.out::println);


👉 Parallel iteration — but unordered.

40. Interview Trick: SubList View

subList() returns backed view → changes reflect in parent.

ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","C","D"));
List<String> sub = list.subList(1,3);
sub.set(0,"X");
System.out.println(list); // [A, X, C, D]


✅ That’s 40 ArrayList items covered: internals, fail-fast, synchronization, streams, tricky subList behavior.

🔥 Java Collections — ArrayList (Part 3: 41–60)
41. Capacity Doubling Trap

ArrayList grows 1.5x → not exponential.

Many insertions = many array copies = costly.
👉 Solution: use ensureCapacity() if size is predictable.

42. Shrinking Does Not Happen Automatically
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
for(int i=0;i<1000;i++) list.add(i);
for(int i=0;i<1000;i++) list.remove(0);
System.out.println(list.size()); // 0
// But capacity still large! Call trimToSize()

43. GC Issue

Even after removing, references may remain in backing array.

Could prevent garbage collection until trimToSize() or reallocation.

44. Boxing Overhead

Stores objects only.

For primitives (int, double), use ArrayList<Integer> → autoboxing overhead.
👉 Alternative: IntStream, or Trove/FastUtil libraries.

45. ArrayList vs LinkedList

ArrayList → O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete.

LinkedList → O(n) access, O(1) insert/delete at ends.
👉 Interview trap: Insertion in ArrayList is not always O(1).

46. When to Use ArrayList

Frequent access.

Appending at end.

Small number of insert/delete operations.

47. When NOT to Use ArrayList

Frequent insert/delete at beginning/middle.

Real-time concurrent access.

48. ArrayList vs Vector

Vector is synchronized (legacy).

ArrayList is faster (unsynchronized).
👉 Prefer ArrayList unless legacy API requires Vector.

49. ArrayList vs CopyOnWriteArrayList

CopyOnWriteArrayList makes new copy on every write.

Good for read-heavy scenarios.

Very costly for frequent writes.

50. Shallow Copy Issue
class Emp { String name; Emp(String n){name=n;} }
ArrayList<Emp> l1 = new ArrayList<>();
l1.add(new Emp("A"));
ArrayList<Emp> l2 = new ArrayList<>(l1);
l2.get(0).name="B";
System.out.println(l1.get(0).name); // B (shallow copy!)


👉 Need deep copy logic.

51. Common Interview Question

👉 Why ArrayList is preferred over LinkedList?

Better cache locality.

Lower object overhead.

O(1) random access.

52. Access Pattern & CPU Cache

ArrayList elements stored contiguously.

Exploits CPU caching → faster iteration vs LinkedList.

53. ModCount in ArrayList

Used to detect structural modification during iteration.

Fail-fast iterators throw ConcurrentModificationException.

54. Custom Capacity Constructor
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(1000);


👉 Useful in batch inserts.

55. AddAll at Specific Index
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","D"));
list.addAll(1,List.of("B","C"));
System.out.println(list); // [A, B, C, D]

56. Java 9+ List.of() vs ArrayList

List.of() → immutable.

ArrayList → mutable.

List<String> fixed = List.of("A","B");
// fixed.add("C"); // UnsupportedOperationException

57. Stream Collectors to ArrayList
List<Integer> list = IntStream.range(1,6).boxed().collect(Collectors.toCollection(ArrayList::new));
System.out.println(list); // [1,2,3,4,5]

58. Parallel Modifications

👉 Multiple threads → unsafe.

List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
Runnable task=()->{ for(int i=0;i<1000;i++) list.add(i); };
new Thread(task).start(); new Thread(task).start();
// May throw ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

59. ArrayList as Stack
ArrayList<Integer> stack = new ArrayList<>();
stack.add(10);
int top = stack.remove(stack.size()-1);
System.out.println(top);


👉 Better to use Deque for real stack.

60. ArrayList as Queue
ArrayList<Integer> queue = new ArrayList<>();
queue.add(1);
int front = queue.remove(0); // costly O(n)


👉 Better: use LinkedList or ArrayDeque.

✅ That’s 60 ArrayList items done: capacity internals, GC, cache, traps, and alternatives.


🔥 Java Collections — ArrayList (Part 4: 61–80)
61. Reverse an ArrayList
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4,5));
Collections.reverse(list);
System.out.println(list); // [5,4,3,2,1]

62. Shuffle Elements
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4,5));
Collections.shuffle(list);
System.out.println(list);

63. Rotate Elements
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4,5));
Collections.rotate(list, 2);
System.out.println(list); // [4,5,1,2,3]

64. Frequency of Elements
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","A","C","A"));
System.out.println(Collections.frequency(list,"A")); // 3

65. Remove Duplicates
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,2,3,4,4,5));
ArrayList<Integer> unique = new ArrayList<>(new HashSet<>(list));
System.out.println(unique);

66. Intersection of Two ArrayLists
ArrayList<Integer> l1 = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4));
ArrayList<Integer> l2 = new ArrayList<>(List.of(3,4,5,6));
l1.retainAll(l2);
System.out.println(l1); // [3,4]

67. Union of Two ArrayLists
ArrayList<Integer> l1 = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3));
ArrayList<Integer> l2 = new ArrayList<>(List.of(3,4,5));
HashSet<Integer> set = new HashSet<>(l1);
set.addAll(l2);
System.out.println(new ArrayList<>(set)); // [1,2,3,4,5]

68. Difference of Two ArrayLists
ArrayList<Integer> l1 = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4));
ArrayList<Integer> l2 = new ArrayList<>(List.of(3,4));
l1.removeAll(l2);
System.out.println(l1); // [1,2]

69. SubList Modification Trap
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("A","B","C","D"));
List<String> sub = list.subList(1,3);
sub.clear();
System.out.println(list); // [A, D]


👉 subList directly affects parent.

70. Sort with Custom Comparator
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(5,2,8,1));
list.sort((a,b)->a-b); 
System.out.println(list); // [1,2,5,8]

71. Find Max and Min
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(5,1,8,3));
System.out.println(Collections.max(list)); // 8
System.out.println(Collections.min(list)); // 1

72. Copy One List to Another
ArrayList<Integer> src = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3));
ArrayList<Integer> dest = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(new Integer[src.size()]));
Collections.copy(dest,src);
System.out.println(dest); // [1,2,3]

73. Binary Search
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,3,5,7,9));
int idx = Collections.binarySearch(list,5);
System.out.println(idx); // 2

74. Immutable ArrayList
List<String> list = List.of("A","B","C");
// list.add("D"); // UnsupportedOperationException

75. ArrayList of Custom Objects
class Emp{String name; Emp(String n){name=n;}}
ArrayList<Emp> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add(new Emp("Alice"));
list.add(new Emp("Bob"));
list.forEach(e->System.out.println(e.name));

76. Sort Custom Objects
class Emp{String name; Emp(String n){name=n;} public String toString(){return name;}}
ArrayList<Emp> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(new Emp("Charlie"),new Emp("Alice")));
list.sort(Comparator.comparing(e->e.name));
System.out.println(list); // [Alice, Charlie]

77. Multi-dimensional ArrayList
ArrayList<ArrayList<Integer>> matrix = new ArrayList<>();
matrix.add(new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3)));
matrix.add(new ArrayList<>(List.of(4,5,6)));
System.out.println(matrix); // [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]

78. Flatten Multi-dimensional ArrayList
ArrayList<ArrayList<Integer>> matrix = new ArrayList<>();
matrix.add(new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3)));
matrix.add(new ArrayList<>(List.of(4,5,6)));
ArrayList<Integer> flat = matrix.stream().flatMap(List::stream).collect(Collectors.toCollection(ArrayList::new));
System.out.println(flat); // [1,2,3,4,5,6]

79. ArrayList as Priority Queue (Inefficient)
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(5,1,3));
Collections.sort(list); // maintain min at index 0
System.out.println(list.remove(0)); // pop min


👉 Better: use PriorityQueue.

80. ArrayList vs LinkedList in Coding Problems

ArrayList faster in: random access, caching.

LinkedList better in: frequent insertion/removal in middle.

Interview Trick: ArrayList often still wins due to cache locality, even when theoretically slower.

✅ That’s 80 ArrayList items done: algorithms, set operations, sublist traps, custom object sorting, and coding tricks.


🔥 Java Collections — ArrayList (Part 5: 81–100 — Advanced / MNC Tough)
81. Iterator.remove() — safe removal during iteration

Why: Proper way to remove while iterating; avoids ConcurrentModificationException.

ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3,4));
Iterator<Integer> it = list.iterator();
while (it.hasNext()) {
  if (it.next() % 2 == 0) it.remove(); // safe
}
System.out.println(list); // [1,3]


Interview angle: Show you know iterator contract vs modifying collection directly.

82. Structural Modification vs Non-structural

Why: modCount increments on structural changes (add/remove) — affects fail-fast behavior.
Tip: Reading/setting existing element (set(i,x)) is non-structural; does not change modCount.

83. ConcurrentModificationException Practical Debug

Why: Detect and explain root cause quickly in interviews.

for (String s : list) {
  if (s.equals("X")) list.remove(s); // throws CME
}


Fixes: Use Iterator.remove(), removeIf(), or collect-then-remove.

84. Bulk Operations and Complexity

Why: addAll(index, c) may move many elements → O(n + m). Use ensureCapacity() before bulk add to reduce reallocations.

list.ensureCapacity(list.size() + other.size());
list.addAll(other);

85. Defensive Copying for API Safety

Why: When exposing a list field, return an unmodifiable copy to avoid external mutation.

public List<String> getTags() { return Collections.unmodifiableList(new ArrayList<>(tags)); }


Interview angle: Talk about immutability, encapsulation, and performance trade-offs.

86. Serialization Pitfalls

Why: Default ArrayList is Serializable because its backing array is serializable; beware of serializing huge lists.
Tip: For long-lived caches, prefer streaming or chunked persistence rather than serializing full ArrayList.

87. Memory Leak via Backing Array (loitering references)

Why: After many removes, backing array still holds references until GC; causes memory pressure.

list.removeAll(...); // elements removed but old references may persist inside internal elementData until overwritten or trimToSize()
list.trimToSize(); // release unused array slots


Interview angle: Explain why trimToSize() or creating new list helps.

88. Spliterator Characteristics & Parallel Streams

Why: ArrayList's Spliterator is SIZED and SUBSIZED — good for efficient parallel streams. But beware of side-effects.

List<Integer> list = IntStream.range(0,1_000_000).boxed().collect(Collectors.toCollection(ArrayList::new));
list.parallelStream().map(x->x+1).reduce(0,Integer::sum);


Tip: Don’t mutate shared ArrayList inside parallel stream.

89. Memory Footprint: Object Overhead vs Primitive Arrays

Why: ArrayList<Integer> stores object references; each Integer has heavy overhead. For big numeric arrays, prefer int[] or primitive collections libs (fastutil), or IntStream.
Interview angle: Discuss per-element memory costs and boxing penalties.

90. Safe Iteration with Concurrent Modifications (Copy-On-Modify approach)

Pattern: For low concurrency and many readers, use copy-on-write pattern: new ArrayList<>(list) snapshot and iterate snapshot.

List<String> snapshot = new ArrayList<>(sharedList);
for (String s : snapshot) process(s);


Trade-off: Writes costlier; reads fast and safe.

91. Detecting High Resize Frequency (Benchmarking)

Why: If many resizes, pre-allocate. Quick microbenchmark: measure elapsed time of many inserts with and without ensureCapacity(). Present results and recommend pre-sizing.

92. Avoid Arrays.asList() Gotcha

Why: Arrays.asList(...) returns fixed-size list backed by array — add() throws UnsupportedOperationException.

List<String> list = Arrays.asList("A","B");
// list.add("C"); // UOE
List<String> mutable = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList("A","B")); // fix


Interview angle: Many candidates trip here.

93. Equals & HashCode for Lists

Why: ArrayList.equals() compares element-wise. When storing lists as keys, beware mutability — do defensive copy or use immutable list as key.

List<String> key = new ArrayList<>(List.of("a","b"));
map.put(Collections.unmodifiableList(key), "value");

94. Using ArrayList as Key in HashMap — why it's risky

Why: If you mutate the list after using it as a key, retrieval fails because hashCode/equals change. Never use mutable collections as map keys unless immutable/defensive-copied.

95. Custom Growth Strategy (subclass hack — theoretical)

Why: You can subclass ArrayList to tweak growth, but it's fragile — better to control via ensureCapacity() or use alternate implementation. Interviewers expect caution here.

96. Hot Path Optimizations — escape analysis & JIT

Why: JIT/escape analysis can optimize short-lived ArrayLists created inside a method; JVM may eliminate allocations in some cases. Demonstrate awareness but don’t claim guaranteed elimination — measurements required.

97. Detecting Concurrent Modification with modCount in Debugging

Why: ConcurrentModificationException sometimes hides which thread or modification caused it. Strategy: log modCount or wrap modifications to add instrumentation in debugging builds.

98. When to Use ArrayList vs ArrayDeque vs LinkedList

Quick rules:

Use ArrayList for random access and append-heavy.

Use ArrayDeque for FIFO/LIFO queue operations (better than LinkedList).

Use LinkedList only when you need constant-time insert/remove at ends and frequent removals by iterators — rare in practice.

99. Profiling Real App — Hotspots often show ArrayList.copyOf/resize

Why: On production profiling, heavy resizing shows as hotspots. Solution: pre-size, reuse lists (clear not reallocate), or use object pools for temporary large lists.

100. How to explain ArrayList choice in system design / interview

Checklist to present:

Explain operation profile (reads vs writes, random access needs).

Memory considerations (boxing, object overhead).

Concurrency model (per-thread builders, synchronized wrappers, copy-on-write).

Resizing & GC impacts (pre-size, trimToSize, defensive copying).

Alternatives & trade-offs (ArrayDeque, primitive collections, LinkedList).

Short example pitch:

“For user IDs used in read-mostly access and occasional append, use ArrayList<Integer> pre-sized to expected users. If IDs are primitive-heavy and huge scale, use int[] or a primitive collection to avoid boxing. For concurrent request-scoped processing, use ArrayList per request (no sync).”

✅ Done — ArrayList (1–100)

You’re now armed with:

100 interview-ready ArrayList facts, patterns, pitfalls, and code snippets,

Practical remediation for concurrency, memory, and performance issues,

System-level trade-offs and how to explain them clearly in interviews.


✅ 50 Mock Interview Q&A on ArrayList (With Model Answers)
Basic Level (1–10)

1. What is ArrayList in Java?
👉 A resizable array implementation of the List interface, storing objects in contiguous memory, maintaining insertion order, and allowing random access in O(1).

2. How does ArrayList differ from an Array?
👉 Array is fixed size, can store primitives. ArrayList is dynamic, stores only objects, and provides many utility methods.

3. What is the default capacity of ArrayList?
👉 10 (Java 8+). Resizes by oldCapacity * 1.5.

4. Is ArrayList synchronized?
👉 No. Multiple threads may corrupt state. Use Collections.synchronizedList() or CopyOnWriteArrayList for thread safety.

5. Can ArrayList contain null values?
👉 Yes. Multiple nulls allowed.

6. What is the time complexity of get(int index)?
👉 O(1) because elements are stored contiguously.

7. What is the time complexity of add(E e) at the end?
👉 Amortized O(1). Occasionally O(n) if resize occurs.

8. What happens when ArrayList is full?
👉 A new array of size ~1.5x is created and elements copied into it.

9. How to make ArrayList read-only?
👉 Wrap with Collections.unmodifiableList(list).

10. What happens if you modify ArrayList while iterating?
👉 ConcurrentModificationException (fail-fast behavior).

Intermediate Level (11–25)

11. How to safely remove elements while iterating?
👉 Use Iterator.remove() or removeIf() (Java 8+).

12. What is subList() in ArrayList?
👉 Returns a view, not a copy. Modifying sublist affects parent list.

13. Is ArrayList good for frequent insertions at the beginning? Why/Why not?
👉 No. Insertion at index 0 requires shifting all elements → O(n).

14. How to increase performance if you know element count in advance?
👉 Use ensureCapacity(n) or constructor new ArrayList<>(n).

15. What’s the difference between clone() and copy constructor?
👉 Both shallow copy. For deep copy, must manually clone contained objects.

16. How to convert ArrayList to Array?
👉 list.toArray(new String[0]).

17. How to convert Array to ArrayList?
👉 new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(arr)).

18. What is the difference between ArrayList and Vector?
👉 Vector is synchronized (legacy). ArrayList is faster, unsynchronized.

19. Explain modCount in ArrayList.
👉 A counter tracking structural modifications. Used by fail-fast iterators.

20. What is CopyOnWriteArrayList and how is it different?
👉 Thread-safe variant. Makes a new copy on every write → costly for writes, efficient for reads.

21. How does ArrayList handle equals() and hashCode()?
👉 Element-wise comparison. [A,B] equals another [A,B] if elements are equal.

22. Why should you not use ArrayList as a key in HashMap?
👉 It’s mutable — if list changes, hashCode changes → retrieval fails.

23. How does ArrayList handle memory after clear()?
👉 Size becomes 0, but underlying array still holds references. Use trimToSize() to release.

24. How to remove duplicates from ArrayList?
👉 Convert to HashSet, or use Java 8 streams with .distinct().

25. What is the complexity of contains(Object o)?
👉 O(n), since it scans linearly.

Advanced Level (26–40)

26. ArrayList vs LinkedList — which is faster?
👉 ArrayList → random access, better cache locality.
LinkedList → frequent insert/remove at ends.
But in real-world, ArrayList often wins due to cache performance.

27. Why is ArrayList better for iteration than LinkedList?
👉 Contiguous memory improves CPU cache hits.

28. How to safely share ArrayList between threads?
👉 Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<>()) or CopyOnWriteArrayList.

29. How to sort an ArrayList of custom objects?
👉 Implement Comparable or use Comparator.

list.sort(Comparator.comparing(Emp::getName));


30. How to use ArrayList in Streams?
👉

list.stream().filter(x->x>10).collect(Collectors.toList());


31. What is the difference between remove(int) and remove(Object)?
👉 remove(int) removes at index, remove(Object) removes first occurrence of element.

32. How to reverse an ArrayList?
👉 Collections.reverse(list).

33. How to rotate elements in ArrayList?
👉 Collections.rotate(list, k).

34. How to check frequency of element?
👉 Collections.frequency(list, "A").

35. Is ArrayList serializable?
👉 Yes, but elements must also be Serializable.

36. What is the internal data structure of ArrayList?
👉 Object[] array (elementData).

37. What is load factor in ArrayList?
👉 Trick question — ArrayList has no load factor (HashMap does). It resizes on demand.

38. How to implement stack using ArrayList?
👉 Use add() for push, remove(size-1) for pop.

39. How to implement queue using ArrayList?
👉 Use add() for enqueue, remove(0) for dequeue (inefficient, O(n)).

40. How to find intersection/union of two ArrayLists?
👉 retainAll() for intersection, addAll()+HashSet for union.

MNC / Tricky Level (41–50)

41. What happens when you call trimToSize()?
👉 Shrinks capacity to match current size, freeing memory.

42. Can ArrayList grow indefinitely?
👉 No. Max size = Integer.MAX_VALUE - 8 (approx. 2^31).

43. How to avoid ConcurrentModificationException without CopyOnWrite?
👉 Iterate over a snapshot (new ArrayList<>(list)) or use streams.

44. Why is ArrayList faster than LinkedList in most practical cases, even for insertions?
👉 CPU cache locality, fewer allocations, better branch prediction.

45. How to implement an LRU cache with ArrayList?
👉 Bad idea — removal from front is O(n). Use LinkedHashMap.

46. How to create an immutable ArrayList?
👉 List<String> l = List.of("A","B");

47. Can ArrayList be subclassed?
👉 Yes, but discouraged (fragile internal implementation).

48. When would you choose ArrayList over ArrayDeque?
👉 When random access is needed. ArrayDeque is better for queues/stacks.

49. Can ArrayList have heterogeneous objects?
👉 Yes (if declared raw ArrayList). But generics are recommended.

50. Real-World Example Question:
"Suppose you are implementing a leaderboard with frequent reads and occasional inserts. Would you use ArrayList or LinkedList?"
👉 ArrayList — fast random access and iteration. Pre-size to expected number of players for efficiency.

✅ Summary

You now have 50 interview Q&A for ArrayList:

Basics (1–10),

Intermediate (11–25),

Advanced (26–40),

MNC-level tricky (41–50).

This is enough to answer confidently in any interview.


✅ HashMap — 50 Mock Interview Q&A (Part 1: 1–20 Basics)
1. What is a HashMap in Java?

👉 A collection that stores key–value pairs. It allows null keys (1) and multiple null values. It is unordered and does not guarantee insertion order.

2. How does HashMap work internally?

👉 It uses an array of buckets (Node[] table). Each bucket stores entries with same hash using a linked list or a tree (since Java 8). Hash of key → index in array.

3. What is the default initial capacity and load factor?

👉 Capacity = 16, Load factor = 0.75.

4. What is load factor?

👉 Ratio of entries to capacity. When exceeded, map resizes (capacity * 2). Default 0.75 means resize when 75% full.

5. Can HashMap have null keys and values?

👉 Yes:

One null key allowed.

Multiple null values allowed.

6. Is HashMap synchronized?

👉 No. Multiple threads may corrupt HashMap. Use Collections.synchronizedMap() or ConcurrentHashMap for thread safety.

7. What is the time complexity of HashMap operations?

👉 Average: O(1) for put/get/remove.
Worst-case: O(n) if hash collisions chain badly (but O(log n) in Java 8+ due to tree bins).

8. What happens when two keys have same hashCode?

👉 Collision occurs → entries are placed in the same bucket. Initially via linked list; converted to a balanced tree when bucket size > 8.

9. What data structures are used inside HashMap in Java 8?

👉 Array + Linked List + Balanced Tree (Red-Black Tree).

10. How is the index of a key calculated in HashMap?

👉 index = (hash(key) & (n-1)) where n = array size. Ensures uniform distribution.

11. What is rehashing?

👉 When load factor threshold is exceeded, a new array of double capacity is created and existing entries are re-distributed (rehashing).

12. Difference between HashMap and Hashtable?

HashMap is unsynchronized, allows null keys/values.

Hashtable is synchronized (legacy), does not allow null keys or values.

13. Difference between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap?

HashMap → not thread-safe, may cause infinite loop before Java 8 in resize.

ConcurrentHashMap → thread-safe with segment/stripe locking, no nulls.

14. Can two different keys have same hashCode?

👉 Yes, hash collisions are possible. HashMap uses equals() to resolve key uniqueness.

15. What methods must be overridden in a custom key class?

👉 Always override hashCode() and equals().
If only one is overridden, map may behave incorrectly (duplicate keys or missing lookups).

16. How does HashMap handle null keys?

👉 A special case: all null keys map to bucket[0].

17. What happens if two keys are equal?

👉 If equals() returns true, the new value replaces the old one (no duplicates in keys).

18. What is the difference between size() and capacity()?

👉 size() = number of entries.
Capacity = length of internal table array.

19. What is TreeifyThreshold in HashMap?

👉 If a bucket’s entries exceed 8, HashMap converts LinkedList into a Red-Black Tree for performance.

20. What is UntreeifyThreshold?

👉 If a tree-bucket size falls below 6 after removals, it is converted back to a LinkedList.

✅ That’s 20 HashMap basics Q&A done: covers fundamentals, internal working, null handling, load factor, and collisions.

✅ HashMap — 50 Mock Interview Q&A (Part 2: 21–40 Intermediate)
21. What happens during HashMap resizing?

👉 Capacity doubles, entries are rehashed, and moved into new buckets. In Java 8, tree bins remain as trees if needed.

22. Why is HashMap capacity always a power of 2?

👉 So that hash & (n-1) distributes keys uniformly across buckets, avoiding modulo cost.

23. What is the difference between hashCode() and equals()?

👉 hashCode() decides bucket. equals() decides if two keys are same inside the bucket.

24. What if hashCode() is implemented badly (e.g., constant)?

👉 All keys go into same bucket → O(n) lookups (degrades to linked list traversal).

25. What if equals() is inconsistent with hashCode()?

👉 Map may lose entries or fail to find them. Violates contract → unpredictable behavior.

26. How does HashMap use final fields in keys (immutability)?

👉 Keys should be immutable, otherwise their hashCode may change after insertion, making them “lost” inside map.

27. Can HashMap store duplicate values?

👉 Yes, values can repeat. Keys must be unique.

28. What are entrySet(), keySet(), and values()?

👉 Views into the map:

keySet() → set of keys.

values() → collection of values.

entrySet() → set of key–value pairs.

29. How do you iterate through a HashMap?
for (Map.Entry<K,V> e : map.entrySet())
    System.out.println(e.getKey()+"="+e.getValue());

30. What is fail-fast behavior of HashMap iterators?

👉 If map is structurally modified during iteration, iterator throws ConcurrentModificationException.

31. Why is fail-fast not guaranteed?

👉 Because it’s a best-effort check using modCount. It’s not a strict guarantee (esp. in multi-threaded code).

32. What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators?

Fail-fast: HashMap, ArrayList.

Fail-safe: CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap (iterate over snapshot).

33. What is the threshold for treeification?

👉 8 entries in a bucket (treeifyThreshold). But actual treeification requires table capacity ≥ 64.

34. Why does HashMap not immediately treeify at 8 elements?

👉 It first tries to resize (expand table) to avoid unnecessary tree creation if collisions are due to low capacity.

35. What is the role of equals() in collision resolution?

👉 After finding bucket, equals() is used to compare candidate keys to ensure uniqueness.

36. What is a hash function in HashMap?

👉 hash = (h = key.hashCode()) ^ (h >>> 16)
Mixes higher bits into lower bits for uniform distribution.

37. Can HashMap’s order change after rehashing?

👉 Yes. HashMap does not guarantee iteration order — resizing changes bucket positions.

38. Difference between HashMap and LinkedHashMap?

HashMap → no order.

LinkedHashMap → maintains insertion order or access order.

39. Difference between HashMap and TreeMap?

HashMap → unordered, O(1) average.

TreeMap → sorted keys, O(log n).

TreeMap does not allow null keys.

40. How to synchronize a HashMap?

👉

Map<K,V> syncMap = Collections.synchronizedMap(new HashMap<>());


✅ That’s 40 HashMap Q&A done: covers collisions, iteration, fail-fast, treeification, and comparisons with other maps.


✅ HashMap — 50 Mock Interview Q&A (Part 3: 41–70 Advanced)
41. What changed in HashMap from Java 7 to Java 8?

👉 In Java 7: collisions handled only via linked list → worst case O(n).
👉 In Java 8: buckets switch to red-black trees when chain > 8 → worst case O(log n).

42. Why did Java 8 introduce tree bins?

👉 To improve worst-case performance from O(n) to O(log n) when many keys hash to same bucket.

43. What happens when a bucket tree shrinks below 6 entries?

👉 HashMap “untreeifies” — converts back to linked list for lower memory footprint.

44. Can HashMap get into an infinite loop?

👉 In Java 7, yes — during concurrent resize, rehashing could form circular linked lists. Fixed in Java 8 by improved rehashing logic.

45. Why is HashMap not thread-safe?

👉 Multiple threads updating without synchronization may corrupt internal structure (e.g., lost updates, infinite loops, overwritten entries).

46. How does ConcurrentHashMap solve this?

👉 Uses finer-grained locking (segments in Java 7, CAS + synchronized on buckets in Java 8). It avoids full table lock.

47. What is the difference between Hashtable and ConcurrentHashMap?

Hashtable → synchronized, blocks entire map.

ConcurrentHashMap → segment/bucket-level locking, better concurrency.

48. Can you store mutable objects as keys in HashMap?

👉 Dangerous. If key changes (hashCode or equals changes), entry becomes “lost” in map.

49. Why are immutable objects recommended as keys?

👉 Because their hashCode and equals won’t change after insertion. E.g., String is ideal.

50. Can two unequal objects have the same hashCode()?

👉 Yes (hash collision). HashMap resolves via equals() check.

51. What happens if two equal objects have different hashCodes()?

👉 Violates contract → map may store duplicates, retrieval fails.

52. What happens internally when we call put()?

👉 Steps:

Compute hash → index.

If bucket empty, insert new node.

Else traverse bucket (list/tree):

If key exists → replace value.

Else add new node at end.

If size > threshold → resize.

53. What happens internally when we call get()?

👉 Steps:

Compute hash → index.

Traverse bucket (list/tree).

Compare equals() → return value.

54. What happens internally when we call remove()?

👉 Finds bucket → traverse → if equals() matches, unlink node → reduce size → maybe untreeify.

55. What are WeakHashMap and IdentityHashMap?

WeakHashMap: keys are weakly referenced (GC removes them if no strong refs).

IdentityHashMap: compares keys by == (identity), not equals().

56. Difference between HashMap and EnumMap?

EnumMap: optimized map for enums.

Internally array-based, faster than HashMap.

57. How does HashMap ensure distribution of keys?

👉 Mixes high bits of hash into low bits via hash ^ (hash >>> 16).

58. What is hash collision attack?

👉 Many keys with same hashCode cause worst-case O(n).
Java 8 solved with tree bins to limit impact.

59. How to prevent poor hashCode implementation from slowing HashMap?

👉 Always override both equals() and hashCode() consistently. Ensure good distribution in hashCode().

60. How to traverse HashMap in sorted order?

👉 Use TreeMap, or sort entrySet().

map.entrySet().stream()
   .sorted(Map.Entry.comparingByKey())
   .forEach(System.out::println);

61. Why is keySet() a view, not a copy?

👉 Modifications in map reflect in keySet, and vice versa.

map.keySet().remove("X"); // removes from map

62. What happens if we modify HashMap while iterating entrySet()?

👉 Throws ConcurrentModificationException (fail-fast).

63. How does HashMap detect modification during iteration?

👉 Each iterator stores expectedModCount. If modCount changes, throws exception.

64. Why is HashMap iteration order not predictable?

👉 Because entries are distributed across buckets based on hashCode, and rehashing changes order.

65. Which Map should you use if predictable iteration order is required?

👉 LinkedHashMap (insertion or access order maintained).

66. Which Map should you use if sorted keys are required?

👉 TreeMap (O(log n)).

67. What happens if you insert duplicate keys in HashMap?

👉 The old value is replaced with the new one.

68. What happens if load factor is too high? Too low?

High load factor → more collisions, slower performance.

Low load factor → less memory-efficient, but faster lookups.

69. How can you improve performance of HashMap with large data?

👉 Pre-size with expected size, use immutable keys, and distribute hashCodes well.

70. What are common interview pitfalls with HashMap?

Using mutable keys.

Forgetting equals() + hashCode contract.

Expecting ordering.

Using in concurrent env without sync.

Assuming containsKey checks values.


Nice — let’s make HashMap internals **unmistakably clear** with visual diagrams + concise explanations you can use in interviews and whiteboards.

I’ll show:

1. The basic bucket array → linked-list flow
2. How collisions build a linked list in a bucket
3. When + how that list becomes a red-black **tree** (Java 8+)
4. What happens on **resize / rehash**
5. A small concrete example (puts that cause treeify)
6. Key constants and index/hash maths

---

# HashMap internals — visual diagrams & explanations

> Legend: `table` = internal `Node<K,V>[]` array (capacity = power of two).
> A bucket is `table[i]`. Each bucket can be `null`, a linked list of nodes, or a tree root.

---

## 1) Top-level view — bucket array

```
table (length = 16)  ---> indexes 0 .. 15
+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+ ... +-----+
|  0  |  1  |  2  |  3  |  4  | ... | 15  |
+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+ ... +-----+
  |     |     |                    |
  v     v     v                    v
 null  node  null                node -> node -> node
        (bucket)                     (linked list)
```

* `index = (hash(key) ^ (hash>>>16)) & (table.length - 1)` (conceptually).
* Each non-null slot is a bucket: either single Node, LinkedList of Nodes, or a TreeNode (red-black tree).

---

## 2) A bucket with collisions → linked list

```
table[5]  -->  Node(k1,v1, h1)  -->  Node(k2,v2, h2)  -->  Node(k3,v3, h3)  --> null
                (head)               (next)               (next)
```

* On `put(k2,v2)` with `h2` mapping to index 5, HashMap traverses the list:

  * If `equals` matches an existing key: replace value.
  * Else append a new `Node` at the tail (or insert at head in some implementations).

---

## 3) Java 8+: list → tree (treeify)

When a single bucket’s chain length exceeds `TREEIFY_THRESHOLD` (8) **and** the table capacity ≥ `MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY` (64), the bucket is converted to a red-black tree:

```
Before treeify (bucket chain):
table[10] --> N1 -> N2 -> N3 -> N4 -> N5 -> N6 -> N7 -> N8 -> N9 -> null

After treeify (red-black tree rooted at table[10]):
table[10] --> TreeNode(root: T5)
                 /     \
              T2       T8
             /  \     /  \
           T1   T3  T7   T9
```

* Tree nodes are special `TreeNode` extending `Node`.
* Benefit: lookup time becomes O(log n) for that bucket instead of O(n).
* If later removals reduce size below `UNTREEIFY_THRESHOLD` (6), the tree may convert back to a list.

---

## 4) Resize (rehash) — visual

When `size > capacity * loadFactor` HashMap resizes (capacity doubles). Example from capacity 8 → 16:

**Before resize (capacity = 8):**

```
table (8):
idx0: N(a)
idx1: N(b) -> N(c)
idx2: null
idx3: N(d)
...
```

**After resize (capacity = 16):**

* Each node recomputed to index `(hash & (newCap - 1))`.
* Nodes may stay in same index or move to index `oldIndex + oldCap`.

```
table (16):
idx0: N(a)        // stayed
idx1: N(b)       // maybe stayed
idx9: N(c) -> ... // moved if (hash bit at oldCap) == 1
idx3: N(d)
...
```

Resizing re-distributes nodes — iteration order changes and tree/list shapes may change; tree nodes may be split into two trees/lists.

---

## 5) Put / Get sequence — tiny concrete example

Assume capacity 16, loadFactor 0.75.

```text
put("A",1) // hash -> index 5, table[5] == null -> place Node(A)
put("B",2) // hash -> index 5, collision -> append Node(B) to table[5] list
put("C",3) // hash -> index 5, append Node(C) -> list length 3
...
(continue until list length > 8 and table size >= 64)
-> treeify bucket at index 5: bucket becomes TreeNode root
```

`get("B")`:

* compute hash, index = 5
* find bucket: if TreeNode -> tree lookup (compare hashes and keys) O(log m)
* else list traversal O(m)

---

## 6) Typical Node / TreeNode structure (conceptual)

```
class Node<K,V> {
  final int hash;
  final K key;
  V value;
  Node<K,V> next; // linked list
}

class TreeNode<K,V> extends Node<K,V> {
  TreeNode<K,V> parent, left, right;
  boolean red;
  // red-black metadata
}
```

---

## 7) Important constants to remember

* `DEFAULT_INITIAL_CAPACITY = 16`
* `MAXIMUM_CAPACITY = 1 << 30`
* `DEFAULT_LOAD_FACTOR = 0.75f`
* `TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8`
* `UNTREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 6`
* `MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY = 64`
  (list must be at least this many capacity before treeifying; otherwise HashMap tries to resize first)

---

## 8) Hash mixing & index formula (short)

```text
int h = key.hashCode();
int hash = h ^ (h >>> 16);         // mix high bits into low bits
int index = hash & (table.length - 1);
```

Mixing helps avoid poor distribution when hashCode only varies in high bits.

---

## 9) Resize pitfalls (interview talking points)

* Resizing is O(n) (costly), so pre-sizing with constructor `new HashMap<>(expectedSize)` is recommended for large maps.
* Concurrent structural modification during resize caused severe problems in Java 7 (possible infinite loops). Java 8 fixed many issues.
* After resize, iteration order changes — don't rely on it.

---

## 10) Quick flowcharts (Put & Get)

**Put(K,V) — flow**

```
compute hash -> index
if table[index] == null:
   insert new node
else:
   if bucket is TreeNode:
       treeInsert (replace if key found)
   else:
       iterate list:
           if key.equals(node.key): replace
       append new node
   if list length > TREEIFY_THRESHOLD:
       if table.length < MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY: resize()
       else treeify(bucket)
if size > threshold: resize()
```

**Get(K) — flow**

```
compute hash -> index
node = table[index]
if node == null -> return null
if node.hash == hash && node.key.equals(key) -> return node.value
if node instanceof TreeNode -> tree lookup
else:
    traverse list via next until match -> return node.value
```

---

## 11) Whiteboard-ready example: collision → treeify → get

Explain with this short script when on a whiteboard:

1. Start with `table[5] = null`.
2. Put keys `k1..k9` that all map to index 5 (simulate poor hash or adversary data).
3. After inserting the 9th entry:

   * If table capacity >= 64, HashMap converts the bucket to a red-black tree.
   * Lookup complexity for those keys becomes O(log 9) instead of O(9).

Sketch the before/after bucket as shown above.

---

## 12) When to mention this in interviews

* When asked about `HashMap` internals, collisions, performance, or why `hashCode()`/`equals()` matter.
* When discussing **defence against hash-collision attacks** (explain tree bins and how they mitigate worst-case).
* When asked about concurrency: compare with `ConcurrentHashMap`, explain Java 7 vs 8 differences.

---

## 13) Bonus: common interview traps to call out

* Using mutable keys — they can “disappear” from map after mutation.
* Relying on iteration order — HashMap makes no guarantees.
* Not pre-sizing large maps — leads to many resizes and copies.
* Implementing poor `hashCode()` (constant hash) — leads to performance degradation.

---

Nice — I’ll give you a **single, self-contained Java program** you can run locally.
It’s a **toy HashMap visualizer** (simplified, safe, and readable) that:

* Simulates an internal `table[]` of buckets (array of `Node`),
* Uses the same **hash mixing** `h ^ (h >>> 16)` and `index = hash & (capacity-1)`,
* Keeps bucket contents as linked lists and **marks** a bucket as `TREE(n)` when its chain length passes a threshold (toy treeification; *not* a real red-black tree),
* Performs **resizes** when size > capacity \* loadFactor,
* Prints a textual snapshot of `table` after each `put()` showing which bucket each key/value landed in, the chain contents, and treeified buckets,
* Includes a demo in `main()` that inserts normal keys and then forces collisions using a `CollidingKey` helper so you can see linked lists and treeification.

This is intended for education and whiteboard demos — not a production HashMap replacement. Comments explain simplifications.

Copy → compile → run.

```java
/**
 * HashMapVisualizer.java
 *
 * Toy HashMap visualizer for learning HashMap internals.
 * - Not a production HashMap. Simplified for clarity.
 * - Uses hash mixing, bucket array, chaining, resize; "treeify" is simulated.
 *
 * How to run:
 *   javac HashMapVisualizer.java
 *   java HashMapVisualizer
 *
 * Sample demo output printed after each put() call.
 */
import java.util.*;
public class HashMapVisualizer {

    // Toy constants (close to real but smaller thresholds for demo)
    private static final int DEFAULT_INITIAL_CAPACITY = 16;
    private static final float DEFAULT_LOAD_FACTOR = 0.75f;
    private static final int TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 4; // smaller for demo clarity (real HashMap uses 8)
    private static final int MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY = 32; // smaller for demo (real HashMap uses 64)

    // Node representing bucket entry (linked list)
    static class Node {
        final int hash;
        final Object key;
        Object value;
        Node next;
        boolean isTree; // toy flag to mark treeified bucket

        Node(int hash, Object key, Object value, Node next) {
            this.hash = hash;
            this.key = key;
            this.value = value;
            this.next = next;
            this.isTree = false;
        }

        @Override
        public String toString() {
            return key + ":" + value;
        }
    }

    // Toy HashMap fields
    private Node[] table;
    private int size = 0;
    private int threshold;
    private final float loadFactor;

    public HashMapVisualizer() {
        this(DEFAULT_INITIAL_CAPACITY, DEFAULT_LOAD_FACTOR);
    }

    public HashMapVisualizer(int initialCapacity, float loadFactor) {
        if (initialCapacity <= 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Capacity > 0 required");
        // round up to power of two
        int cap = 1;
        while (cap < initialCapacity) cap <<= 1;
        this.table = new Node[cap];
        this.loadFactor = loadFactor;
        this.threshold = (int)(cap * loadFactor);
    }

    // Hash mixing similar to JDK: spread higher bits
    private static int spread(int h) {
        return h ^ (h >>> 16);
    }

    // Put operation (simplified)
    public void put(Object key, Object value) {
        int h = (key == null) ? 0 : spread(key.hashCode());
        int idx = indexFor(h, table.length);
        // If slot empty, insert
        if (table[idx] == null) {
            table[idx] = new Node(h, key, value, null);
            size++;
        } else {
            // traverse existing chain
            Node node = table[idx];
            Node prev = null;
            boolean replaced = false;
            int chainLen = 0;
            while (node != null) {
                chainLen++;
                if (node.hash == h && Objects.equals(node.key, key)) {
                    // replace value
                    node.value = value;
                    replaced = true;
                    break;
                }
                prev = node;
                node = node.next;
            }
            if (!replaced) {
                prev.next = new Node(h, key, value, null);
                chainLen++;
                size++;
                // simulate treeify if chain too long
                if (chainLen >= TREEIFY_THRESHOLD) {
                    // only treeify if table big enough; else try resizing first
                    if (table.length >= MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY) {
                        treeifyBucket(idx);
                    } else {
                        // encourage resize to avoid treeifying - real HashMap does similar
                        resize();
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        // Check resize after insertion
        if (size > threshold) {
            resize();
        }

        // Print a snapshot after each put
        System.out.println("After put(" + key + "," + value + ")  [size=" + size + ", cap=" + table.length + "]");
        printTable();
        System.out.println("--------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n");
    }

    // Convert a bucket into a "tree" (toy: mark nodes isTree=true and condense display)
    private void treeifyBucket(int idx) {
        Node head = table[idx];
        if (head == null) return;
        // Mark all nodes in bucket as tree nodes
        Node cur = head;
        int count = 0;
        while (cur != null) {
            cur.isTree = true;
            count++;
            cur = cur.next;
        }
        System.out.println(">>> Bucket " + idx + " treeified (toy). bucket-size=" + count);
    }

    // Resize: double capacity, rehash nodes - simplified (keeps chain order)
    private void resize() {
        int oldCap = table.length;
        int newCap = oldCap << 1;
        Node[] oldTable = table;
        Node[] newTable = new Node[newCap];
        // rehash entries
        for (int i = 0; i < oldCap; i++) {
            Node node = oldTable[i];
            while (node != null) {
                Node next = node.next;
                node.next = null; // detach
                // recompute index
                int idx = indexFor(node.hash, newCap);
                if (newTable[idx] == null) {
                    newTable[idx] = node;
                } else {
                    // append to tail
                    Node t = newTable[idx];
                    while (t.next != null) t = t.next;
                    t.next = node;
                }
                node = next;
            }
        }
        table = newTable;
        threshold = (int)(newCap * loadFactor);
        System.out.println(">>> RESIZED: capacity " + oldCap + " -> " + newCap + ", newThreshold=" + threshold);
    }

    private static int indexFor(int hash, int length) {
        return hash & (length - 1);
    }

    // Print a textual snapshot of the table
    private void printTable() {
        for (int i = 0; i < table.length; i++) {
            System.out.print(String.format("[%2d]: ", i));
            Node node = table[i];
            if (node == null) {
                System.out.println("null");
                continue;
            }
            // If bucket treeified (toy), print as TREE(...)
            if (node.isTree) {
                // count nodes in this bucket
                int cnt = 0;
                Node t = node;
                StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
                while (t != null) {
                    cnt++;
                    if (sb.length() > 0) sb.append(" -> ");
                    sb.append(t.toString());
                    t = t.next;
                }
                System.out.println("TREE(" + cnt + "): " + sb.toString());
            } else {
                // print linked list
                StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
                while (node != null) {
                    if (sb.length() > 0) sb.append(" -> ");
                    sb.append(node.toString());
                    node = node.next;
                }
                System.out.println(sb.toString());
            }
        }
    }

    // demo usage
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        HashMapVisualizer map = new HashMapVisualizer();

        // Insert normal keys
        for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
            map.put("K" + i, i);
        }

        // Insert keys that intentionally collide by using CollidingKey
        System.out.println("Now inserting colliding keys (all with same hash) to demonstrate chaining and treeify...");
        for (int i = 0; i < 16; i++) {
            // CollidingKey's hashCode() returns a fixed value -> all go into same bucket
            map.put(new CollidingKey("C" + i), "ValC" + i);
        }

        // Insert a few more normal keys to trigger resize and more rehashing
        map.put("X", 100);
        map.put("Y", 200);
    }

    // Helper class to force collisions: every instance returns same hashCode().
    static class CollidingKey {
        private final String id;
        CollidingKey(String id) { this.id = id; }
        @Override
        public int hashCode() {
            return 0x5f3759df; // arbitrary fixed number
        }
        @Override
        public boolean equals(Object o) {
            if (this == o) return true;
            if (!(o instanceof CollidingKey)) return false;
            return Objects.equals(id, ((CollidingKey)o).id);
        }
        @Override
        public String toString() { return "CK[" + id + "]"; }
    }
}
```

---

### What this program demonstrates

* Normal puts into different buckets (using mixed hashes from `String.hashCode()`),
* Then it inserts many `CollidingKey` instances that all produce the **same hash**, forcing them into the same bucket — you’ll see a long chain printed for that bucket,
* When a chain length reaches `TREEIFY_THRESHOLD` (we set a smaller threshold for the toy), the visualizer **marks** the bucket as `TREE(n)` and prints a message,
* Resizes are printed when the map grows past the threshold (and nodes are rehashed to new indexes),
* The textual snapshot shows each bucket index and its contents after each `put()`.

---

### Notes & limitations

* This is a **teaching tool** — it intentionally simplifies treeification (does not implement a real red-black tree). It marks buckets as `TREE(n)` to show where treeification would occur.
* Constants `TREEIFY_THRESHOLD` and `MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY` are reduced for demo speed; real JDK values differ (`TREEIFY_THRESHOLD` = 8, `MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY` = 64).
* The rehash logic preserves chaining order but is simplified for clarity.

---

Great — I’ll deliver a single, self-contained Java program that upgrades the previous toy visualizer to use a real red-black tree for buckets once a chain exceeds the threshold. This is still an educational toy (not a production HashMap), but the tree behavior (BST insert + red-black balancing) will be implemented so you can observe real tree structure, lookups, and splitting on resize.

Key notes / simplifications (so expectations are right):

The visualizer implements a toy HashMap with:

Node (linked list node)

TreeNode (red-black node; extends Node) and red-black insertion/fix-up

real treeified buckets (search & insert use tree operations)

resizing & rehashing similar to real HashMap (trees may be split across new buckets on resize)

Ordering inside the tree is by:

hash (spread/mixed)

If hash equal and keys implement Comparable, use ((Comparable)key).compareTo(otherKey)

Otherwise, fall back to System.identityHashCode(key) and finally toString() compare to ensure total order.

This is not a full JDK HashMap: balancing rules follow standard red-black insertion; tree-to-list conversion and some corner cases are simplified but the tree is real (you can search in O(log n) after treeify).

Tree deletion (untreeify) on removals is not fully implemented — the demo focuses on insertion, treeify, lookup and resize, which is enough to illustrate the structure and behavior.

Thresholds are slightly lowered for demo speed, but constants are clearly marked and can be adjusted.

Copy → compile → run.

/**
 * RBHashMapVisualizer.java
 *
 * Toy HashMap visualizer with REAL red-black tree buckets (simplified).
 *
 * How to run:
 *   javac RBHashMapVisualizer.java
 *   java RBHashMapVisualizer
 *
 * The program inserts normal keys and then many colliding keys
 * to force a bucket to treeify (convert from list -> red-black tree).
 *
 * This code is educational — it implements insert + treeify + lookup + resize
 * including red-black balancing on tree inserts. Deletion/untreeify is not fully covered.
 *
 * Author: ChatGPT (toy educational example)
 */
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicInteger;

public class RBHashMapVisualizer {

    // Demo / toy constants
    private static final int DEFAULT_INITIAL_CAPACITY = 16;
    private static final float DEFAULT_LOAD_FACTOR = 0.75f;
    private static final int TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 4; // lowered for demo (real HashMap uses 8)
    private static final int MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY = 32; // lowered for demo (real HashMap uses 64)
    private static final boolean VERBOSE = true;

    // Basic Node (linked list)
    static class Node {
        final int hash;
        final Object key;
        Object value;
        Node next;

        Node(int hash, Object key, Object value, Node next) {
            this.hash = hash;
            this.key = key;
            this.value = value;
            this.next = next;
        }

        @Override
        public String toString() {
            return String.format("%s:%s", String.valueOf(key), String.valueOf(value));
        }
    }

    // TreeNode implements red-black tree node and extends Node
    static final class TreeNode extends Node {
        TreeNode parent;
        TreeNode left;
        TreeNode right;
        boolean red; // true = red, false = black

        TreeNode(int hash, Object key, Object value, Node next) {
            super(hash, key, value, next);
            this.parent = this.left = this.right = null;
            this.red = true; // new nodes start red
        }

        // convenience for printing
        String nodeStr() {
            return String.format("(%s%s:%s)", red ? "R" : "B", String.valueOf(key), String.valueOf(value));
        }
    }

    // Toy HashMap fields
    private Node[] table;
    private int size = 0;
    private int threshold;
    private final float loadFactor;

    public RBHashMapVisualizer() {
        this(DEFAULT_INITIAL_CAPACITY, DEFAULT_LOAD_FACTOR);
    }

    public RBHashMapVisualizer(int cap, float lf) {
        if (cap <= 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException();
        int c = 1;
        while (c < cap) c <<= 1;
        this.table = new Node[c];
        this.loadFactor = lf;
        this.threshold = (int) (c * lf);
    }

    // hash spread similar to JDK
    private static int spread(int h) {
        return h ^ (h >>> 16);
    }

    // compute index
    private static int indexFor(int hash, int length) {
        return hash & (length - 1);
    }

    // Public put
    public void put(Object key, Object value) {
        int h = (key == null) ? 0 : spread(key.hashCode());
        int idx = indexFor(h, table.length);
        Node bucket = table[idx];

        if (bucket == null) {
            table[idx] = new Node(h, key, value, null);
            size++;
        } else {
            // If bucket is tree (TreeNode head), do tree insert
            if (bucket instanceof TreeNode) {
                TreeNode root = (TreeNode) bucket;
                TreeNode inserted = treePut(root, h, key, value);
                if (inserted == null) {
                    // replaced existing value; size unchanged
                } else {
                    size++;
                }
            } else {
                // linked list path
                Node node = bucket;
                Node prev = null;
                boolean replaced = false;
                int chainLen = 0;
                while (node != null) {
                    chainLen++;
                    if (node.hash == h && Objects.equals(node.key, key)) {
                        node.value = value;
                        replaced = true;
                        break;
                    }
                    prev = node;
                    node = node.next;
                }
                if (!replaced) {
                    prev.next = new Node(h, key, value, null);
                    size++;
                    chainLen++;
                    if (chainLen >= TREEIFY_THRESHOLD) {
                        if (table.length >= MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY) {
                            treeifyBucket(idx);
                        } else {
                            // prefer resize to avoid treeify
                            resize();
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        if (size > threshold) resize();

        if (VERBOSE) {
            System.out.printf("After put(%s,%s) size=%d cap=%d%n", key, value, size, table.length);
            printTable();
            System.out.println("-----------------------------------------------------------------\n");
        }
    }

    // Getter (supports tree or list lookup)
    public Object get(Object key) {
        int h = (key == null) ? 0 : spread(key.hashCode());
        int idx = indexFor(h, table.length);
        Node node = table[idx];
        while (node != null) {
            if (node.hash == h && Objects.equals(node.key, key)) return node.value;
            if (node instanceof TreeNode) {
                // tree lookup
                TreeNode root = (TreeNode) node;
                TreeNode found = treeGet(root, h, key);
                return found == null ? null : found.value;
            }
            node = node.next;
        }
        return null;
    }

    // Convert a linked list bucket into a tree (create TreeNodes and insert into balanced tree)
    private void treeifyBucket(int idx) {
        Node head = table[idx];
        if (head == null || head instanceof TreeNode) return;
        // Build tree by inserting nodes one by one into red-black tree root
        TreeNode root = null;
        Node node = head;
        while (node != null) {
            TreeNode t = new TreeNode(node.hash, node.key, node.value, null);
            if (root == null) {
                root = t;
                root.red = false; // root black
            } else {
                root = treeInsert(root, t);
            }
            node = node.next;
        }
        table[idx] = root;
        System.out.println(">>> Bucket " + idx + " TREEIFIED (real RB tree).");
    }

    // Basic red-black BST insert: insert by ordering (hash, then key ordering), then fix-up
    // Returns new root of tree
    private TreeNode treeInsert(TreeNode root, TreeNode x) {
        // BST insert
        TreeNode p = null;
        TreeNode cur = root;
        int cmp = 0;
        while (cur != null) {
            p = cur;
            cmp = compareKeys(x.hash, x.key, cur.hash, cur.key);
            if (cmp < 0) cur = cur.left;
            else cur = cur.right;
        }
        x.parent = p;
        if (p == null) {
            root = x;
        } else if (cmp < 0) p.left = x;
        else p.right = x;

        // fix red-black properties
        root = balanceInsertion(root, x);
        return root;
    }

    // Insert into tree when bucket already a TreeNode (returns inserted node if new, null if replaced)
    private TreeNode treePut(TreeNode root, int hash, Object key, Object value) {
        // follow BST insertion logic, but if key exists, replace value
        TreeNode cur = root;
        TreeNode parent = null;
        int cmp = 0;
        while (cur != null) {
            parent = cur;
            cmp = compareKeys(hash, key, cur.hash, cur.key);
            if (cmp == 0) {
                if (Objects.equals(cur.key, key)) {
                    cur.value = value; // replace
                    // return null indicates replacement
                    // In our design, tree root remains the same
                    // find actual root
                    while (root.parent != null) root = (TreeNode) root.parent;
                    return null;
                }
                // if keys not equals but ordering same (rare), attempt to disambiguate by identity
                // continue to one side deterministically
                cmp = System.identityHashCode(key) - System.identityHashCode(cur.key);
                if (cmp == 0) cmp = key.toString().compareTo(cur.key.toString());
            }
            if (cmp < 0) cur = cur.left; else cur = cur.right;
        }
        // insert new node
        TreeNode x = new TreeNode(hash, key, value, null);
        x.parent = parent;
        if (cmp < 0) parent.left = x; else parent.right = x;

        TreeNode r = balanceInsertion(root, x);

        // find and return the inserted node within the new root
        while (r.parent != null) r = r.parent;
        treeRootNormalize(r);
        return x;
    }

    // red-black rotation and recolor (standard algorithm)
    private TreeNode balanceInsertion(TreeNode root, TreeNode x) {
        x.red = true;
        while (x != null && x != root && x.parent != null && x.parent.red) {
            TreeNode xp = x.parent;
            TreeNode xpp = xp.parent;
            if (xpp == null) break;
            if (xp == xpp.left) {
                TreeNode y = xpp.right;
                if (y != null && y.red) {
                    xp.red = false;
                    y.red = false;
                    xpp.red = true;
                    x = xpp;
                } else {
                    if (x == xp.right) {
                        x = xp;
                        root = rotateLeft(root, x);
                        xp = x.parent;
                        xpp = xp == null ? null : xp.parent;
                    }
                    if (xp != null) {
                        xp.red = false;
                        if (xpp != null) {
                            xpp.red = true;
                            root = rotateRight(root, xpp);
                        }
                    }
                }
            } else {
                TreeNode y = xpp.left;
                if (y != null && y.red) {
                    xp.red = false;
                    y.red = false;
                    xpp.red = true;
                    x = xpp;
                } else {
                    if (x == xp.left) {
                        x = xp;
                        root = rotateRight(root, x);
                        xp = x.parent;
                        xpp = xp == null ? null : xp.parent;
                    }
                    if (xp != null) {
                        xp.red = false;
                        if (xpp != null) {
                            xpp.red = true;
                            root = rotateLeft(root, xpp);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        // ensure root is black
        while (root.parent != null) root = (TreeNode) root.parent;
        if (root != null) root.red = false;
        return root;
    }

    // left rotate at node p (returns possibly new root)
    private TreeNode rotateLeft(TreeNode root, TreeNode p) {
        if (p == null) return root;
        TreeNode r = p.right;
        if (r == null) return root;
        TreeNode rl = r.left;
        p.right = rl;
        if (rl != null) rl.parent = p;
        r.parent = p.parent;
        if (p.parent == null) root = r;
        else if (p.parent.left == p) p.parent.left = r;
        else p.parent.right = r;
        r.left = p;
        p.parent = r;
        return root;
    }

    // right rotate at node p
    private TreeNode rotateRight(TreeNode root, TreeNode p) {
        if (p == null) return root;
        TreeNode l = p.left;
        if (l == null) return root;
        TreeNode lr = l.right;
        p.left = lr;
        if (lr != null) lr.parent = p;
        l.parent = p.parent;
        if (p.parent == null) root = l;
        else if (p.parent.right == p) p.parent.right = l;
        else p.parent.left = l;
        l.right = p;
        p.parent = l;
        return root;
    }

    // find a node in tree by key/hash
    private TreeNode treeGet(TreeNode root, int hash, Object key) {
        TreeNode p = root;
        while (p != null) {
            int cmp = compareKeys(hash, key, p.hash, p.key);
            if (cmp == 0) {
                if (Objects.equals(p.key, key)) return p;
                // if compare equal but not equals, disambiguate
                int idc = System.identityHashCode(key) - System.identityHashCode(p.key);
                if (idc == 0) {
                    int s = key.toString().compareTo(p.key.toString());
                    if (s == 0) return p;
                    cmp = s;
                } else cmp = idc;
            }
            p = (cmp < 0) ? p.left : p.right;
        }
        return null;
    }

    // Compare helper: returns negative if (h1,k1) < (h2,k2)
    @SuppressWarnings({ "unchecked", "rawtypes" })
    private static int compareKeys(int h1, Object k1, int h2, Object k2) {
        if (h1 != h2) return h1 < h2 ? -1 : 1;
        if (k1 == k2) return 0;
        if (k1 == null) return -1;
        if (k2 == null) return 1;
        if (k1 instanceof Comparable && k2 instanceof Comparable && k1.getClass() == k2.getClass()) {
            try {
                return ((Comparable) k1).compareTo(k2);
            } catch (ClassCastException ex) {
                // fall through
            }
        }
        // fallback to identityHashCode
        int id1 = System.identityHashCode(k1);
        int id2 = System.identityHashCode(k2);
        if (id1 != id2) return id1 - id2;
        // last resort: string compare
        return k1.toString().compareTo(k2.toString());
    }

    // After rotations/balancing, parent pointers might not be fully normalized to root.
    private void treeRootNormalize(TreeNode root) {
        while (root.parent != null) root = (TreeNode) root.parent;
        // mark root black
        if (root != null) root.red = false;
    }

    // Resize: double capacity and rehash nodes (trees are split into new buckets)
    private void resize() {
        int oldCap = table.length;
        int newCap = oldCap << 1;
        Node[] oldTable = table;
        Node[] newTable = new Node[newCap];

        for (int i = 0; i < oldCap; i++) {
            Node node = oldTable[i];
            if (node == null) continue;
            if (node instanceof TreeNode) {
                // traverse tree and re-insert nodes into newTable
                List<TreeNode> nodes = new ArrayList<>();
                collectTreeNodes((TreeNode) node, nodes);
                for (TreeNode t : nodes) {
                    t.left = t.right = t.parent = null;
                    t.red = true;
                    int idx = indexFor(t.hash, newCap);
                    Node existing = newTable[idx];
                    if (existing == null) {
                        newTable[idx] = new TreeNode(t.hash, t.key, t.value, null);
                        ((TreeNode)newTable[idx]).red = false;
                    } else {
                        // if existing is tree, insert into tree; if list, append
                        if (existing instanceof TreeNode) {
                            TreeNode root = (TreeNode) existing;
                            treePut(root, t.hash, t.key, t.value);
                        } else {
                            // append to list
                            Node tail = existing;
                            while (tail.next != null) tail = tail.next;
                            tail.next = new Node(t.hash, t.key, t.value, null);
                        }
                    }
                }
