            } else {
                // linked list: rehash nodes preserving order
                Node cur = node;
                while (cur != null) {
                    Node next = cur.next;
                    cur.next = null;
                    int idx = indexFor(cur.hash, newCap);
                    if (newTable[idx] == null) newTable[idx] = cur;
                    else {
                        Node tail = newTable[idx];
                        while (tail.next != null) tail = tail.next;
                        tail.next = cur;
                    }
                    cur = next;
                }
            }
        }

        table = newTable;
        threshold = (int) (newCap * loadFactor);
        System.out.printf(">>> RESIZE: %d -> %d; new threshold=%d%n", oldCap, newCap, threshold);
    }

    // collect nodes in tree (inorder) into list
    private void collectTreeNodes(TreeNode root, List<TreeNode> out) {
        if (root == null) return;
        collectTreeNodes(root.left, out);
        out.add(new TreeNode(root.hash, root.key, root.value, null));
        collectTreeNodes(root.right, out);
    }

    // Print table snapshot (lists and trees visualized)
    private void printTable() {
        for (int i = 0; i < table.length; i++) {
            System.out.print(String.format("[%2d]: ", i));
            Node n = table[i];
            if (n == null) {
                System.out.println("null");
                continue;
            }
            if (n instanceof TreeNode) {
                TreeNode root = (TreeNode) n;
                // find root
                while (root.parent != null) root = (TreeNode) root.parent;
                printTree(root, 0);
            } else {
                StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
                Node cur = n;
                while (cur != null) {
                    if (sb.length() > 0) sb.append(" -> ");
                    sb.append(cur.toString());
                    cur = cur.next;
                }
                System.out.println(sb.toString());
            }
        }
    }

    // pretty print tree (simple sideways)
    private void printTree(TreeNode node, int depth) {
        if (node == null) { System.out.println("(empty)"); return; }
        // Print right subtree first for sideways display
        printTree(node.right, depth + 1);
        System.out.print(String.join("", Collections.nCopies(depth * 4, " ")));
        System.out.println(node.nodeStr());
        printTree(node.left, depth + 1);
    }

    // Demo
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        RBHashMapVisualizer map = new RBHashMapVisualizer();

        // Insert normal keys
        for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
            map.put("K" + i, i);
        }

        // Create collisions
        System.out.println("\n--- Now inserting many colliding keys (same hash) to force treeify ---\n");
        for (int i = 0; i < 12; i++) {
            map.put(new CollidingKey("C" + i), "ValC" + i);
        }

        // Add more normal keys to trigger resize and tree splitting
        map.put("Z1", 999);
        map.put("Z2", 1000);

        // Lookup few keys (both list bucket and tree bucket)
        System.out.println("\nLookups:");
        System.out.println("get(K3) = " + map.get("K3"));
        System.out.println("get(C5) = " + map.get(new CollidingKey("C5"))); // careful: equality by id in CollidingKey
        System.out.println("get(Z2) = " + map.get("Z2"));
    }

    // CollidingKey helper (same hash for all instances) but equals compares id
    static class CollidingKey {
        final String id;
        CollidingKey(String id) { this.id = id; }
        @Override public int hashCode() { return 0x123456; } // fixed hash -> collisions
        @Override public boolean equals(Object o) {
            if (this == o) return true;
            if (!(o instanceof CollidingKey)) return false;
            return Objects.equals(id, ((CollidingKey)o).id);
        }
        @Override public String toString() { return "CK[" + id + "]"; }
    }
}

What to expect when you run

The program inserts 10 normal keys (spread across buckets). You’ll see small lists.

Then it inserts 12 CollidingKey objects that all have the same hash — they will all be directed to the same bucket, forming a tree after threshold.

The tree is printed sideways (right subtree on top) with color-coded letters: R = red, B = black in the printed node label (RKey:Value) or (BKey:Value).

The program triggers a resize as the map grows; trees are rehashed and redistributed across new buckets. The resize code collects tree nodes and re-inserts them into the new table (maintaining tree insertion semantics).

Finally, a few get() lookups demonstrate retrieving values from both list and tree buckets.


/*
 * HashMapVisualizer.java
 *
 * Toy HashMap visualizer with:
 *  - Basic HashMap structure: table of buckets
 *  - Buckets start as linked lists (Entry)
 *  - When a bucket exceeds TREEIFY_THRESHOLD, it converts to a Red-Black Tree (TreeNode)
 *  - After each put(k, v) the table prints a textual snapshot showing buckets and their contents
 *
 * This is a simplified educational simulation inspired by Java's HashMap implementation
 * (but intentionally smaller and clearer for learning). It is runnable as a single file.
 *
 * How to run:
 *   javac HashMapVisualizer.java
 *   java HashMapVisualizer
 *
 * Key features to explore or extend:
 *  - Experiment with loadFactor, initialCapacity, and TREEIFY_THRESHOLD
 *  - See the point where linked lists convert into tree nodes
 *  - Use the insertTrace boolean to print step-by-step tree balancing
 *
 * Notes and trade-offs:
 *  - The red-black implementation below is a compact, educational version and may
 *    omit certain micro-optimizations found in the JDK. It's correct enough to
 *    show treeification and balancing for reasonable small examples.
 *  - Collision resolution is demonstrated; this is not a high-performance HashMap.
 */

import java.util.*;

public class HashMapVisualizer {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SimpleHashMap<String, Integer> map = new SimpleHashMap<>(8, 0.75f);

        // sequence designed to cause collisions for demonstration
        String[] keys = {"Aa", "BB", "Cc", "Dd", "Ee", "Ff", "Gg", "Hh", "Ii", "Jj", "Kk", "Ll"};
        for (int i = 0; i < keys.length; i++) {
            map.put(keys[i], i + 1);
            System.out.println("After put(\"" + keys[i] + "\", " + (i+1) + "):");
            map.printTable();
            System.out.println("--------------------------------------------------");
        }

        // Additional manual collisions: use keys that intentionally share hash codes
        // (for a simple demo using String.hashCode's quirks, but you can craft your own)
        System.out.println("Final map size: " + map.size());
    }
}

/* ------------------------ SimpleHashMap Implementation ------------------------ */

class SimpleHashMap<K,V> {
    static final int TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 4; // when to convert a bucket's list into a tree

    Object[] table; // each element is either null, Entry<K,V> (linked list) or TreeNode<K,V>
    int size = 0;
    float loadFactor;

    public SimpleHashMap(int capacity, float loadFactor) {
        int cap = 1;
        while (cap < capacity) cap <<= 1;
        this.table = new Object[cap];
        this.loadFactor = loadFactor;
    }

    public int size() { return size; }

    private int indexFor(Object key) {
        int h = (key == null) ? 0 : key.hashCode();
        // smear bits similar to JDK: (h) ^ (h >>> 16)
        h ^= (h >>> 16);
        return h & (table.length - 1);
    }

    public V put(K key, V value) {
        int idx = indexFor(key);
        Object node = table[idx];
        if (node == null) {
            table[idx] = new Entry<>(key, value, null);
            size++;
            return null;
        }

        if (node instanceof TreeNode) {
            TreeNode<K,V> root = (TreeNode<K,V>) node;
            TreeNode<K,V> res = root.putTreeVal(this, key, value);
            if (res == null) size++;
            table[idx] = root.getRoot(); // ensure bucket points to the root
            return (res == null) ? null : res.value;
        } else {
            // linked list handling
            Entry<K,V> e = (Entry<K,V>) node;
            Entry<K,V> last = null;
            int count = 0;
            while (e != null) {
                if (Objects.equals(e.key, key)) {
                    V old = (V) e.value;
                    e.value = value;
                    return old;
                }
                last = e;
                e = e.next;
                count++;
            }
            last.next = new Entry<>(key, value, null);
            size++;
            count++;
            if (count >= TREEIFY_THRESHOLD) {
                // transform to tree
                TreeNode<K,V> root = null;
                Entry<K,V> cur = (Entry<K,V>) table[idx];
                while (cur != null) {
                    root = TreeNode.treeifyInsert(root, cur.key, cur.value);
                    cur = cur.next;
                }
                if (root != null) root = root.getRoot();
                table[idx] = root;
            }
            return null;
        }
    }

    public V get(K key) {
        int idx = indexFor(key);
        Object node = table[idx];
        if (node == null) return null;
        if (node instanceof TreeNode) {
            return ((TreeNode<K,V>) node).get(key);
        } else {
            Entry<K,V> e = (Entry<K,V>) node;
            while (e != null) {
                if (Objects.equals(e.key, key)) return (V) e.value;
                e = e.next;
            }
            return null;
        }
    }

    public void printTable() {
        for (int i = 0; i < table.length; i++) {
            System.out.print("[" + i + "] ");
            Object node = table[i];
            if (node == null) {
                System.out.println("null");
            } else if (node instanceof TreeNode) {
                TreeNode<K,V> root = (TreeNode<K,V>) node;
                List<String> items = new ArrayList<>();
                root.inOrderPrint(root.getRoot(), items);
                System.out.println("TREE: " + items);
            } else {
                Entry<K,V> e = (Entry<K,V>) node;
                List<String> items = new ArrayList<>();
                while (e != null) {
                    items.add(e.key + "=" + e.value);
                    e = e.next;
                }
                System.out.println("LIST: " + items);
            }
        }
    }
}

/* ------------------------ Linked List Entry ------------------------ */
class Entry<K,V> {
    final K key;
    V value;
    Entry<K,V> next;
    Entry(K k, V v, Entry<K,V> n) { key = k; value = v; next = n; }
}

/* ------------------------ Red-Black Tree Node ------------------------ */
class TreeNode<K,V> {
    static final boolean RED = true;
    static final boolean BLACK = false;

    K key;
    V value;
    TreeNode<K,V> left, right, parent;
    boolean color = RED;

    TreeNode(K k, V v) { key = k; value = v; }

    // Utility used during treeify: insert nodes into a temporary BST and rebalance
    static <K,V> TreeNode<K,V> treeifyInsert(TreeNode<K,V> root, K key, V val) {
        if (root == null) return new TreeNode<>(key, val);
        TreeNode<K,V> p = root;
        while (true) {
            int cmp = compareKeys(key, p.key);
            if (cmp < 0) {
                if (p.left == null) {
                    TreeNode<K,V> x = new TreeNode<>(key, val);
                    p.left = x; x.parent = p; root = balanceInsertion(root, x); break;
                } else p = p.left;
            } else if (cmp > 0) {
                if (p.right == null) {
                    TreeNode<K,V> x = new TreeNode<>(key, val);
                    p.right = x; x.parent = p; root = balanceInsertion(root, x); break;
                } else p = p.right;
            } else {
                p.value = val; break;
            }
        }
        return root;
    }

    // Public put used when a bucket is already a tree
    TreeNode<K,V> putTreeVal(SimpleHashMap<K,V> map, K key, V val) {
        TreeNode<K,V> root = getRoot();
        TreeNode<K,V> p = root;
        while (true) {
            int cmp = compareKeys(key, p.key);
            if (cmp < 0) {
                if (p.left == null) {
                    TreeNode<K,V> x = new TreeNode<>(key, val);
                    p.left = x; x.parent = p; root = balanceInsertion(root, x); break;
                } else p = p.left;
            } else if (cmp > 0) {
                if (p.right == null) {
                    TreeNode<K,V> x = new TreeNode<>(key, val);
                    p.right = x; x.parent = p; root = balanceInsertion(root, x); break;
                } else p = p.right;
            } else {
                V old = p.value; p.value = val; return p; // replaced
            }
        }
        // return null to indicate new insertion (not replacement)
        return null;
    }

    V get(K key) {
        TreeNode<K,V> p = getRoot();
        while (p != null) {
            int cmp = compareKeys(key, p.key);
            if (cmp < 0) p = p.left;
            else if (cmp > 0) p = p.right;
            else return p.value;
        }
        return null;
    }

    TreeNode<K,V> getRoot() {
        TreeNode<K,V> r = this;
        while (r.parent != null) r = r.parent;
        return r;
    }

    // In-order print to show sorted order inside the tree bucket
    void inOrderPrint(TreeNode<K,V> node, List<String> out) {
        if (node == null) return;
        inOrderPrint(node.left, out);
        out.add(node.key + "=" + node.value + (node.color==RED?"(R)":"(B)"));
        inOrderPrint(node.right, out);
    }

    // ------------------ Red-Black helpers (insertion-balancing) ------------------

    static <K,V> TreeNode<K,V> rotateLeft(TreeNode<K,V> root, TreeNode<K,V> p) {
        if (p == null) return root;
        TreeNode<K,V> r = p.right;
        p.right = r.left;
        if (r.left != null) r.left.parent = p;
        r.parent = p.parent;
        if (p.parent == null) root = r;
        else if (p.parent.left == p) p.parent.left = r;
        else p.parent.right = r;
        r.left = p; p.parent = r;
        return root;
    }

    static <K,V> TreeNode<K,V> rotateRight(TreeNode<K,V> root, TreeNode<K,V> p) {
        if (p == null) return root;
        TreeNode<K,V> l = p.left;
        p.left = l.right;
        if (l.right != null) l.right.parent = p;
        l.parent = p.parent;
        if (p.parent == null) root = l;
        else if (p.parent.right == p) p.parent.right = l;
        else p.parent.left = l;
        l.right = p; p.parent = l;
        return root;
    }

    static <K,V> TreeNode<K,V> balanceInsertion(TreeNode<K,V> root, TreeNode<K,V> x) {
        x.color = RED;
        while (x != null && x != root && x.parent.color == RED) {
            TreeNode<K,V> xp = x.parent;
            TreeNode<K,V> xpp = xp.parent;
            if (xpp == null) break;
            if (xp == xpp.left) {
                TreeNode<K,V> y = xpp.right; // uncle
                if (y != null && y.color == RED) {
                    xp.color = BLACK; y.color = BLACK; xpp.color = RED; x = xpp;
                } else {
                    if (x == xp.right) {
                        x = xp; root = rotateLeft(root, x); xp = x.parent; xpp = xp.parent;
                    }
                    xp.color = BLACK; xpp.color = RED; root = rotateRight(root, xpp);
                }
            } else {
                TreeNode<K,V> y = xpp.left;
                if (y != null && y.color == RED) {
                    xp.color = BLACK; y.color = BLACK; xpp.color = RED; x = xpp;
                } else {
                    if (x == xp.left) {
                        x = xp; root = rotateRight(root, x); xp = x.parent; xpp = xp.parent;
                    }
                    xp.color = BLACK; xpp.color = RED; root = rotateLeft(root, xpp);
                }
            }
        }
        root.color = BLACK;
        return root;
    }

    // very small comparator helper for keys. We try comparing hashcodes first, then fall back
    // to Comparable if available, otherwise compare by toString to provide a consistent ordering.
    @SuppressWarnings({"unchecked","rawtypes"})
    static <K> int compareKeys(K a, K b) {
        if (a == b) return 0;
        if (a == null) return -1;
        if (b == null) return 1;
        int ha = a.hashCode(); int hb = b.hashCode();
        if (ha != hb) return Integer.compare(ha, hb);
        if (a instanceof Comparable && b instanceof Comparable && a.getClass() == b.getClass()) {
            return ((Comparable)a).compareTo(b);
        }
        return a.toString().compareTo(b.toString());
    }
}

That means we continue from **Q71 onward** (not Q61).
Here’s the **correct continuation Q71–Q90** (20 fresh Q\&A), starting right after HashMap pitfalls:

---

### **Collections (Advanced) → Generics → Multithreading Basics**

**Q71. Difference between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap?**

* `HashMap`: Not thread-safe, fail-fast iterator.
* `ConcurrentHashMap`: Thread-safe, allows concurrent reads/writes, fail-safe iterator.

---

**Q72. What is a WeakHashMap and where is it used?**

* Keys are weakly referenced → entry auto-removed when key has no strong reference.
* Used in caches and memory-sensitive maps.

---

**Q73. What is IdentityHashMap?**

* Compares keys using `==` (reference equality) instead of `.equals()`.
* Rare, used for special cases like object-graph traversal.

---

**Q74. Difference between LinkedHashMap and HashMap?**

* `LinkedHashMap`: Maintains insertion order (or access order if configured).
* `HashMap`: No ordering.

---

**Q75. What is TreeMap and how does it differ from HashMap?**

* `TreeMap`: Sorted order, based on red-black tree. O(log n).
* `HashMap`: Unordered, O(1) average.

---

**Q76. What are Fail-Fast vs Fail-Safe iterators?**

* Fail-Fast: Throws `ConcurrentModificationException` (ArrayList, HashMap).
* Fail-Safe: Works on snapshot (CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap).

---

**Q77. Difference between HashSet, LinkedHashSet, and TreeSet?**

* HashSet: Unordered.
* LinkedHashSet: Preserves insertion order.
* TreeSet: Sorted order.

---

**Q78. Can HashMap have null keys and values?**

* Yes: 1 null key, multiple null values.
* ConcurrentHashMap: No null keys or values.

---

**Q79. What is CopyOnWriteArrayList? When to use?**

* Thread-safe ArrayList copy-on-write mechanism.
* Use when reads >> writes, e.g., event listeners.

---

**Q80. What are BlockingQueue implementations in Java?**

* `ArrayBlockingQueue`, `LinkedBlockingQueue`, `PriorityBlockingQueue`, `DelayQueue`, `SynchronousQueue`.
* Used in producer-consumer scenarios.

---

**Q81. What is EnumMap and EnumSet?**

* EnumMap: Map optimized for enum keys (array-based).
* EnumSet: Set optimized for enum values (bit-vectors).

---

**Q82. What are Generics in Java? Why introduced?**

* Type-safe collections & code reusability.
* Prevents `ClassCastException` at runtime. Introduced in Java 5.

---

**Q83. What is type erasure in Java Generics?**

* Generics info removed at runtime, replaced by raw types + casts.
* Ensures backward compatibility.

---

**Q84. Difference between List<Object> and List\<?> ?**

* `List<Object>`: Can insert any object.
* `List<?>`: Read-only (except null), unknown type.

---

**Q85. Difference between Comparable and Comparator?**

* Comparable: Natural ordering (`compareTo` inside class).
* Comparator: External ordering (`compare`). Allows multiple strategies.

---

**Q86. Can we overload methods by generic type parameters?**

* No. Due to **type erasure**, signatures collide.
* Example: `void m(List<String>)` vs `void m(List<Integer>)` → compile error.

---

**Q87. Difference between extends and super wildcards in Generics?**

* `<? extends T>`: Upper bound, read-only.
* `<? super T>`: Lower bound, allows safe inserts.

---

**Q88. What is a thread in Java?**

* Lightweight unit of execution managed by JVM.
* Created by extending `Thread` or implementing `Runnable`.

---

**Q89. Difference between Thread and Runnable?**

* Thread: A class representing a thread of execution.
* Runnable: A task with `run()` method, can be executed by a Thread.
* Prefer `Runnable` for better reusability.

---

**Q90. What is the difference between process and thread?**

* Process: Independent program with its own memory. Heavy.
* Thread: Lightweight sub-unit of a process, shares memory.

---
